AIF-C01 ordering 12 tables
#1 AIF-C01 Core Ordering Sequences — Mental Anchors
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-001 1 cols 6 rows
▼

The six core workflow sequences tested in AIF-C01 ordering questions. Every ordering question maps to one of these patterns.

Feature Mental Anchor
ML Development Lifecycle Define → Build → Deploy → Watch
Foundation Model Lifecycle Data → Train → Tune → Check → Ship
RAG Offline Pipeline Collect → Chunk → Embed → Index
RAG Online (Query) Pipeline Ask → Embed Query → Retrieve → Augment → Generate
Fine-Tuning Workflow Pick Model → Prep Data → Train → Evaluate
MLOps / CI-CD for ML Train → Register → Approve → Deploy → Monitor
⚠️ Exam Tips

Identify which of the 6 patterns the question tests, then use the mental anchor to order the steps.

Business goal / problem definition is ALWAYS first; monitoring is ALWAYS last.

If the question mentions 'RAG' — retrieval comes BEFORE generation.

Evaluation ALWAYS comes after training, NEVER before.

Deployment ALWAYS comes after evaluation.

Mental Anchor
A short mnemonic phrase that captures the correct step ordering for each core AIF-C01 workflow. Use these anchors to quickly recall the sequence during the exam.
Mental Anchor
ML Development Lifecycle: Define → Build → Deploy → Watch | Foundation Model Lifecycle: Data → Train → Tune → Check → Ship | RAG Offline Pipeline: Collect → Chunk → Embed → Index | RAG Online (Query) Pipeline: Ask → Embed Query → Retrieve → Augment → Generate | Fine-Tuning Workflow: Pick Model → Prep Data → Train → Evaluate | MLOps / CI-CD for ML: Train → Register → Approve → Deploy → Monitor
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

#2 AIF-C01 Ordering Question — Universal Heuristics
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-012 2 cols 8 rows
▼

Golden rules and common exam traps for AIF-C01 ordering questions.

Feature Correct Pattern Common Trap
Golden Rule: Natural Flow Ask: 'What would a human logically do first?' Overthinking and trying to memorize exact sequences
Golden Rule: Input → Process → Output Input → Transform → Output → Monitor Skipping the transform step or putting output before process
Golden Rule: Setup Before Execution Define → Build → Run → Monitor Jumping to execution without setup/definition
Trap: Evaluation Too Early Train → Evaluate (always after training) ❌ Evaluate → Train
Trap: Deployment Before Validation Evaluate → Deploy ❌ Deploy → Evaluate
Trap: Monitoring in the Middle Monitoring is ALWAYS last ❌ Monitor before deployment
Trap: Feature Engineering Confusion Data Prep (cleaning) → Feature Engineering (transforming) ❌ Confusing the two — Prep ≠ Feature Eng
Strategy: Lock First & Last Identify first step (define/input) and last step (monitor) — then order the middle Trying to order all steps simultaneously
⚠️ Exam Tips

Always follow Input → Process → Output — if an answer puts processing before data collection, it’s wrong

Setup always comes before execution — you must prepare data before training, train before deploying

Evaluation always comes before deployment — never deploy a model you haven’t tested

Monitoring is always the LAST step in any production workflow

When unsure, think of the natural flow: gather → prepare → build → test → deploy → monitor

Correct Pattern
The reliable ordering principle that applies across most AWS AI/ML workflows — always follow the natural flow of Input → Process → Output.
Common Trap
The wrong-answer pattern the exam uses to distract. Common traps include skipping validation, deploying before evaluating, or evaluating before training.
Correct Pattern
Golden Rule: Natural Flow: Ask: 'What would a human logically do first?' | Golden Rule: Input → Process → Output: Input → Transform → Output → Monitor | Golden Rule: Setup Before Execution: Define → Build → Run → Monitor | Trap: Evaluation Too Early: Train → Evaluate (always after training) | Trap: Deployment Before Validation: Evaluate → Deploy | Trap: Monitoring in the Middle: Monitoring is ALWAYS last | Trap: Feature Engineering Confusion: Data Prep (cleaning) → Feature Engineering (transforming) | Strategy: Lock First & Last: Identify first step (define/input) and last step (monitor) — then order the middle
Common Trap
Golden Rule: Natural Flow: Overthinking and trying to memorize exact sequences | Golden Rule: Input → Process → Output: Skipping the transform step or putting output before process | Golden Rule: Setup Before Execution: Jumping to execution without setup/definition | Trap: Evaluation Too Early: ❌ Evaluate → Train | Trap: Deployment Before Validation: ❌ Deploy → Evaluate | Trap: Monitoring in the Middle: ❌ Monitor before deployment | Trap: Feature Engineering Confusion: ❌ Confusing the two — Prep ≠ Feature Eng | Strategy: Lock First & Last: Trying to order all steps simultaneously
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

#3 Data Pipeline Order — Correct Ordering
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-008 2 cols 5 rows
▼

The standard AWS data pipeline flow from ingestion to visualization.

Feature Step Number AWS Service Mapping
Data Ingestion 1 Kinesis, S3 uploads, DMS, AppFlow
Data Storage 2 S3 Data Lake, Redshift, DynamoDB
Data Processing (ETL) 3 Glue, EMR, Lambda, Step Functions
Data Analysis 4 Athena, Redshift, EMR (Spark SQL)
Data Visualization 5 QuickSight, Grafana, custom dashboards
💡 Mnemonics

Get → Store → Transform → Analyze → Show

Ingest → Store → Transform → Analyze → Visualize

⚠️ Exam Tips

Data pipelines always start with ingestion and end with visualization or consumption

ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) happens in the middle, after storage but before analysis

AWS Glue is the primary serverless ETL service; EMR is for large-scale processing

Athena queries data in S3 directly; Redshift requires loading data first (unless using Spectrum)

QuickSight sits at the end for dashboards and visualization

Step Number
The correct sequential position in the data pipeline workflow, from ingestion through visualization.
AWS Service Mapping
The AWS services that map to each pipeline stage — e.g., Kinesis/S3 for ingestion, Glue for ETL, Athena/Redshift for analysis, QuickSight for visualization.
Step Number
Data Ingestion: 1 | Data Storage: 2 | Data Processing (ETL): 3 | Data Analysis: 4 | Data Visualization: 5
AWS Service Mapping
Data Ingestion: Kinesis, S3 uploads, DMS, AppFlow | Data Storage: S3 Data Lake, Redshift, DynamoDB | Data Processing (ETL): Glue, EMR, Lambda, Step Functions | Data Analysis: Athena, Redshift, EMR (Spark SQL) | Data Visualization: QuickSight, Grafana, custom dashboards
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

#4 Fine-Tuning Workflow — Correct Ordering
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-006 2 cols 4 rows
▼

The step-by-step process for fine-tuning a foundation model on domain-specific data.

Feature Step Number Details
Select Base Model 1 Choose FM from Bedrock model catalog or Hugging Face based on task requirements
Prepare Training Data 2 Format data as prompt-completion pairs; clean, deduplicate, ensure quality
Fine-Tune / Train 3 Bedrock custom model training or SageMaker fine-tuning job; set hyperparameters
Evaluate Performance 4 Compare fine-tuned vs base using task metrics; check for overfitting, bias, toxicity
💡 Mnemonics

Pick Model → Prep Data → Train → Evaluate

⚠️ Exam Tips

Always select the base model FIRST — fine-tuning adapts an existing model, not building from scratch

Training data must be prepared in the format the base model expects before fine-tuning

Evaluation must happen AFTER fine-tuning to measure improvement over the base model

On the exam, if an answer suggests training data preparation after fine-tuning, it’s wrong

SageMaker JumpStart makes fine-tuning easier; Bedrock supports fine-tuning for select models

Step Number
The correct sequential position in the fine-tuning workflow. The sequence is: Select Base Model → Prepare Training Data → Fine-Tune → Evaluate.
Details
Specific actions and considerations at each step, including data format requirements, hyperparameter selection, and evaluation metrics.
Step Number
Select Base Model: 1 | Prepare Training Data: 2 | Fine-Tune / Train: 3 | Evaluate Performance: 4
Details
Select Base Model: Choose FM from Bedrock model catalog or Hugging Face based on task requirements | Prepare Training Data: Format data as prompt-completion pairs; clean, deduplicate, ensure quality | Fine-Tune / Train: Bedrock custom model training or SageMaker fine-tuning job; set hyperparameters | Evaluate Performance: Compare fine-tuned vs base using task metrics; check for overfitting, bias, toxicity
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

#5 Foundation Model Lifecycle — Correct Ordering
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-003 2 cols 6 rows
▼

The lifecycle for building/customizing a foundation model from data to production.

Feature Step Number Details
Collect & Curate Data 1 Gather large-scale training corpus; filter for quality, bias, and licensing
Pre-Train Model 2 Train on massive compute (weeks/months); learn general language/vision representations
Fine-Tune Model 3 Adapt to specific domain/task with labeled data; instruction tuning, RLHF
Evaluate & Benchmark 4 Measure with ROUGE, BLEU, BERTScore, human eval; check for bias and toxicity
Deploy & Serve 5 SageMaker endpoint, Bedrock API, or self-hosted; set up auto-scaling
Monitor & Iterate 6 Track drift, latency, cost; collect feedback; retrain periodically
💡 Mnemonics

Data → Train → Tune → Check → Ship → Watch

⚠️ Exam Tips

Pre-training requires massive datasets and compute — most organizations skip this step and use pre-trained models

Fine-tuning comes AFTER pre-training, not before — it adapts the pre-trained model to specific tasks

Evaluation and benchmarking must happen before deployment to production

On the exam, distinguish between pre-training (from scratch) and fine-tuning (adapting existing model)

Amazon Bedrock provides pre-trained foundation models so you can skip straight to fine-tuning

Step Number
The correct sequential position in the FM lifecycle. The sequence is: Collect Data → Pre-Train → Fine-Tune → Evaluate → Deploy.
Details
Key activities at each lifecycle stage, from curating massive datasets for pre-training through production deployment with monitoring.
Step Number
Collect & Curate Data: 1 | Pre-Train Model: 2 | Fine-Tune Model: 3 | Evaluate & Benchmark: 4 | Deploy & Serve: 5 | Monitor & Iterate: 6
Details
Collect & Curate Data: Gather large-scale training corpus; filter for quality, bias, and licensing | Pre-Train Model: Train on massive compute (weeks/months); learn general language/vision representations | Fine-Tune Model: Adapt to specific domain/task with labeled data; instruction tuning, RLHF | Evaluate & Benchmark: Measure with ROUGE, BLEU, BERTScore, human eval; check for bias and toxicity | Deploy & Serve: SageMaker endpoint, Bedrock API, or self-hosted; set up auto-scaling | Monitor & Iterate: Track drift, latency, cost; collect feedback; retrain periodically
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

#6 Generative AI / Prompt Workflow — Correct Ordering
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-011 2 cols 6 rows
▼

The runtime flow of a GenAI interaction from user input through refinement.

Feature Step Number Details
User Input (Prompt) 1 User types question or instruction in natural language
Add Context (RAG / Retrieval) 2 System retrieves relevant documents and injects into prompt context
Send to Model 3 Augmented prompt sent to LLM via Bedrock or SageMaker endpoint
Generate Output 4 Model produces response (text, code, image, etc.)
Evaluate Output 5 Check for accuracy, toxicity, hallucination; apply guardrails
Refine Prompt (Iteration) 6 Adjust prompt engineering based on output quality; feedback loop
💡 Mnemonics

Prompt → Context → Model → Output → Evaluate → Improve

⚠️ Exam Tips

If question mentions RAG → retrieval before generation. If 'feedback loop' → refinement is LAST.

Step Number
The correct sequential position in the prompt workflow. The sequence is: User Input → Add Context (RAG) → Send to Model → Generate Output → Evaluate.
Details
What happens at each stage of the generative AI request flow, including how RAG retrieval augments the prompt before model inference.
Step Number
User Input (Prompt): 1 | Add Context (RAG / Retrieval): 2 | Send to Model: 3 | Generate Output: 4 | Evaluate Output: 5 | Refine Prompt (Iteration): 6
Details
User Input (Prompt): User types question or instruction in natural language | Add Context (RAG / Retrieval): System retrieves relevant documents and injects into prompt context | Send to Model: Augmented prompt sent to LLM via Bedrock or SageMaker endpoint | Generate Output: Model produces response (text, code, image, etc.) | Evaluate Output: Check for accuracy, toxicity, hallucination; apply guardrails | Refine Prompt (Iteration): Adjust prompt engineering based on output quality; feedback loop
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

#7 ML Development Lifecycle — Correct Ordering
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-002 2 cols 9 rows
▼

The full 9-step ML lifecycle sequence. Most common ordering question pattern on AIF-C01.

Feature Step Number AWS Service Mapping
Define Problem 1 Business stakeholder alignment — no specific service
Collect Data 2 S3, Kinesis, Data Pipeline, AppFlow
Prepare / Clean Data 3 Glue, Glue DataBrew, SageMaker Data Wrangler
Feature Engineering 4 SageMaker Feature Store, SageMaker Processing
Train Model 5 SageMaker Training, EC2 (GPU)
Evaluate Model 6 SageMaker Clarify, Model Monitor, custom metrics
Tune Model 7 SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning (HPO)
Deploy Model 8 SageMaker Endpoints, Lambda, Fargate
Monitor Model 9 SageMaker Model Monitor, CloudWatch
💡 Mnemonics

Define → Data → Prep → Features → Train → Evaluate → Tune → Deploy → Watch

⚠️ Exam Tips

Prep = cleaning (missing values, duplicates). Feature Engineering = transforming for model (encoding, scaling).

If you see 'business goal' → ALWAYS FIRST. If you see 'monitoring' → ALWAYS LAST.

Evaluation ALWAYS comes after training. They will try to swap these.

Step Number
The correct sequential position in the ML lifecycle. The golden sequence is: Define Problem → Collect Data → Prepare Data → Feature Engineering → Train → Evaluate → Deploy → Monitor.
AWS Service Mapping
AWS services for each stage — SageMaker Data Wrangler for prep, Feature Store for features, SageMaker Training for training, Model Monitor for monitoring.
Step Number
Define Problem: 1 | Collect Data: 2 | Prepare / Clean Data: 3 | Feature Engineering: 4 | Train Model: 5 | Evaluate Model: 6 | Tune Model: 7 | Deploy Model: 8 | Monitor Model: 9
AWS Service Mapping
Define Problem: Business stakeholder alignment — no specific service | Collect Data: S3, Kinesis, Data Pipeline, AppFlow | Prepare / Clean Data: Glue, Glue DataBrew, SageMaker Data Wrangler | Feature Engineering: SageMaker Feature Store, SageMaker Processing | Train Model: SageMaker Training, EC2 (GPU) | Evaluate Model: SageMaker Clarify, Model Monitor, custom metrics | Tune Model: SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning (HPO) | Deploy Model: SageMaker Endpoints, Lambda, Fargate | Monitor Model: SageMaker Model Monitor, CloudWatch
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

#8 MLOps / CI-CD for ML — Correct Ordering
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-007 2 cols 5 rows
▼

The MLOps pipeline from training through production monitoring — the ML equivalent of CI/CD.

Feature Step Number AWS Service Mapping
Train Model 1 SageMaker Training Jobs, SageMaker Pipelines
Register Model 2 SageMaker Model Registry — version and metadata tracking
Approve for Production 3 Manual approval gate in SageMaker Pipelines or CodePipeline
Deploy to Endpoint 4 SageMaker Endpoints (real-time) or Batch Transform
Monitor in Production 5 SageMaker Model Monitor, CloudWatch Alarms, data drift detection
💡 Mnemonics

Train → Register → Approve → Deploy → Monitor

⚠️ Exam Tips

Model Registry is where trained models are versioned and stored before approval

Approval gates (manual or automated) must happen before production deployment

SageMaker Pipelines automates the training → register → approve → deploy flow

Model monitoring is continuous and happens throughout the production lifecycle

If a model drifts, the pipeline should trigger retraining automatically

Step Number
The correct sequential position in the MLOps pipeline. The sequence is: Train → Register → Approve → Deploy → Monitor.
AWS Service Mapping
AWS services for MLOps — SageMaker Pipelines for orchestration, Model Registry for versioning, CodePipeline for CI/CD, Model Monitor for production monitoring.
Step Number
Train Model: 1 | Register Model: 2 | Approve for Production: 3 | Deploy to Endpoint: 4 | Monitor in Production: 5
AWS Service Mapping
Train Model: SageMaker Training Jobs, SageMaker Pipelines | Register Model: SageMaker Model Registry — version and metadata tracking | Approve for Production: Manual approval gate in SageMaker Pipelines or CodePipeline | Deploy to Endpoint: SageMaker Endpoints (real-time) or Batch Transform | Monitor in Production: SageMaker Model Monitor, CloudWatch Alarms, data drift detection
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

#9 Model Deployment Flow — Correct Ordering
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-009 2 cols 6 rows
▼

The deployment sub-lifecycle from trained model to monitored production endpoint.

Feature Step Number Details
Train Model 1 Complete training run with final hyperparameters
Validate / Evaluate 2 Test on held-out data; measure accuracy, F1, ROUGE, etc.
Package Model 3 Create model artifact (tar.gz), container image, or model card
Deploy to Endpoint 4 SageMaker real-time/serverless endpoint, Lambda, or Fargate container
Serve Predictions (Inference) 5 Accept requests, return predictions; auto-scale under load
Monitor Performance 6 Track data drift, model quality, latency; SageMaker Model Monitor + CloudWatch
💡 Mnemonics

Train → Validate → Package → Deploy → Serve → Monitor

⚠️ Exam Tips

Inference ALWAYS happens AFTER deployment. If 'endpoint' is mentioned → deployment step.

Step Number
The correct sequential position in the deployment flow. The sequence is: Train → Validate → Package → Deploy → Serve.
Details
What happens at each deployment stage, from model artifact creation through endpoint configuration and real-time inference serving.
Step Number
Train Model: 1 | Validate / Evaluate: 2 | Package Model: 3 | Deploy to Endpoint: 4 | Serve Predictions (Inference): 5 | Monitor Performance: 6
Details
Train Model: Complete training run with final hyperparameters | Validate / Evaluate: Test on held-out data; measure accuracy, F1, ROUGE, etc. | Package Model: Create model artifact (tar.gz), container image, or model card | Deploy to Endpoint: SageMaker real-time/serverless endpoint, Lambda, or Fargate container | Serve Predictions (Inference): Accept requests, return predictions; auto-scale under load | Monitor Performance: Track data drift, model quality, latency; SageMaker Model Monitor + CloudWatch
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

#10 RAG Offline Pipeline — Correct Ordering
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-004 2 cols 4 rows
▼

The offline (indexing) side of Retrieval-Augmented Generation — preparing the knowledge base.

Feature Step Number AWS Service Mapping
Collect Documents 1 S3, Data sources, web crawlers
Chunk Documents 2 Lambda, Bedrock Knowledge Bases (auto-chunk)
Generate Embeddings 3 Bedrock Embeddings (Titan/Cohere), SageMaker
Store in Vector Index 4 OpenSearch Serverless, Aurora pgvector, Pinecone
💡 Mnemonics

Collect → Chunk → Embed → Index

⚠️ Exam Tips

Chunking comes BEFORE embedding — documents must be split into manageable pieces first

Embeddings convert text chunks into numerical vectors that capture semantic meaning

The vector database stores embeddings for fast similarity search at query time

Bedrock Knowledge Bases can automate the entire offline pipeline (chunk, embed, index)

Chunk size matters: too small loses context, too large dilutes relevance

Step Number
The correct sequential position in the RAG offline (indexing) pipeline. The sequence is: Collect Documents → Chunk → Embed → Store in Vector DB.
AWS Service Mapping
AWS services for RAG indexing — S3 for document storage, Lambda for chunking, Bedrock/SageMaker for embeddings, OpenSearch/Aurora pgvector for vector storage.
Step Number
Collect Documents: 1 | Chunk Documents: 2 | Generate Embeddings: 3 | Store in Vector Index: 4
AWS Service Mapping
Collect Documents: S3, Data sources, web crawlers | Chunk Documents: Lambda, Bedrock Knowledge Bases (auto-chunk) | Generate Embeddings: Bedrock Embeddings (Titan/Cohere), SageMaker | Store in Vector Index: OpenSearch Serverless, Aurora pgvector, Pinecone
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

#11 RAG Online (Query) Pipeline — Correct Ordering
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-005 2 cols 5 rows
▼

The online (query-time) side of RAG — how a user question becomes an augmented answer.

Feature Step Number AWS Service Mapping
User Asks Question 1 Application frontend / API Gateway
Embed the Query 2 Same embedding model as offline (Titan/Cohere)
Retrieve Relevant Chunks 3 Vector DB similarity search (OpenSearch, pgvector)
Augment Prompt with Context 4 Prompt template + retrieved chunks → LLM context window
Generate Answer 5 Bedrock (Claude, Titan), SageMaker endpoint
💡 Mnemonics

Ask → Embed Query → Retrieve → Augment → Generate

⚠️ Exam Tips

If a question mentions RAG, retrieval ALWAYS comes before generation.

Step Number
The correct sequential position in the RAG online (query) pipeline. The sequence is: User Query → Embed Query → Retrieve Chunks → Augment Prompt → Generate Answer.
AWS Service Mapping
AWS services for RAG queries — Bedrock for embedding the query, OpenSearch/Aurora for vector search, Bedrock Knowledge Bases for orchestration, Bedrock for final LLM generation.
Step Number
User Asks Question: 1 | Embed the Query: 2 | Retrieve Relevant Chunks: 3 | Augment Prompt with Context: 4 | Generate Answer: 5
AWS Service Mapping
User Asks Question: Application frontend / API Gateway | Embed the Query: Same embedding model as offline (Titan/Cohere) | Retrieve Relevant Chunks: Vector DB similarity search (OpenSearch, pgvector) | Augment Prompt with Context: Prompt template + retrieved chunks → LLM context window | Generate Answer: Bedrock (Claude, Titan), SageMaker endpoint
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

#12 Responsible AI / Governance Flow — Correct Ordering
AIF-C01 ordering AIF-ORDERING-010 2 cols 6 rows
▼

The responsible AI lifecycle for identifying, measuring, and mitigating bias.

Feature Step Number AWS Service Mapping
Identify Risks / Bias 1 SageMaker Clarify (pre-training bias analysis)
Measure Bias 2 SageMaker Clarify bias metrics (DPL, CI, KL divergence)
Mitigate Bias 3 Data resampling, re-weighting, algorithm changes, Augmented AI (A2I)
Validate Fairness 4 SageMaker Clarify post-training analysis; human evaluation
Deploy with Guardrails 5 Bedrock Guardrails, content filters, toxicity thresholds
Monitor Continuously 6 SageMaker Model Monitor, CloudWatch, bias drift detection
💡 Mnemonics

Detect → Measure → Fix → Validate → Guard → Monitor

⚠️ Exam Tips

If you see 'bias', 'fairness', or 'responsible AI' → this is ALWAYS: Detect → Measure → Fix → Monitor

Step Number
The correct sequential position in the responsible AI workflow. The sequence is: Identify Risks → Measure Bias → Mitigate Bias → Validate Fairness → Deploy with Guardrails.
AWS Service Mapping
AWS services for responsible AI — SageMaker Clarify for bias detection and explainability, Bedrock Guardrails for content filtering, Model Monitor for drift detection, A2I for human review.
Step Number
Identify Risks / Bias: 1 | Measure Bias: 2 | Mitigate Bias: 3 | Validate Fairness: 4 | Deploy with Guardrails: 5 | Monitor Continuously: 6
AWS Service Mapping
Identify Risks / Bias: SageMaker Clarify (pre-training bias analysis) | Measure Bias: SageMaker Clarify bias metrics (DPL, CI, KL divergence) | Mitigate Bias: Data resampling, re-weighting, algorithm changes, Augmented AI (A2I) | Validate Fairness: SageMaker Clarify post-training analysis; human evaluation | Deploy with Guardrails: Bedrock Guardrails, content filters, toxicity thresholds | Monitor Continuously: SageMaker Model Monitor, CloudWatch, bias drift detection
📎 Sources

ordering_questions.md

AI & ML Platforms 7 tables
#13 AI/ML Platform Services Comparison
AI & ML Platforms aiml_platform_services_comparison 3 cols 6 rows
▼

Comparing core AI/ML platforms for building and deploying machine learning solutions

Feature Amazon SageMaker Amazon Bedrock Amazon Q
Primary Use Case Building custom ML models from scratch Building generative AI applications with foundation models Answering questions and assisting with AWS tasks
Customization Level Full control - train custom models Fine-tune foundation models with your data Pre-trained, ready to use
Model Access Bring your own models or use JumpStart Access multiple FM providers via API Built-in AWS knowledge base
Infrastructure Management Managed compute, you choose instance types Fully serverless, no infrastructure to manage Fully serverless
Pricing Model Pay for compute used during training/deployment Pay per token (token-based pricing) Subscription or pay-per-query
Best For ML engineers needing full ML pipeline control Developers building GenAI apps quickly Developers and businesses needing AI assistance
💡 Mnemonics

SAGE = Start with SageMaker for custom ML; BEDROCK = Bedrock for GenAI foundations; Q = Quick answers with Q

⚠️ Exam Tips

SageMaker is for the ML lifecycle; Bedrock is for generative AI via API

Use SageMaker when you need to train custom models from data

Use Bedrock when you want to leverage pre-trained foundation models

Amazon Q is specifically for AWS-related tasks and code assistance

Amazon SageMaker
A fully managed end-to-end machine learning service that enables developers and data scientists to build, train, and deploy ML models at scale with integrated tools for the entire ML lifecycle.
Amazon Bedrock
A fully managed service that provides access to foundation models from Amazon and leading AI startups via a single API, enabling developers to build generative AI applications without managing infrastructure.
Amazon Q
A generative AI-powered assistant designed to help users with AWS tasks, code generation, and business queries through natural language conversations.
Amazon SageMaker
Primary Use Case: Building custom ML models from scratch | Customization Level: Full control - train custom models | Model Access: Bring your own models or use JumpStart | Infrastructure Management: Managed compute, you choose instance types | Pricing Model: Pay for compute used during training/deployment | Best For: ML engineers needing full ML pipeline control
Amazon Bedrock
Primary Use Case: Building generative AI applications with foundation models | Customization Level: Fine-tune foundation models with your data | Model Access: Access multiple FM providers via API | Infrastructure Management: Fully serverless, no infrastructure to manage | Pricing Model: Pay per token (token-based pricing) | Best For: Developers building GenAI apps quickly
Amazon Q
Primary Use Case: Answering questions and assisting with AWS tasks | Customization Level: Pre-trained, ready to use | Model Access: Built-in AWS knowledge base | Infrastructure Management: Fully serverless | Pricing Model: Subscription or pay-per-query | Best For: Developers and businesses needing AI assistance
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#14 AWS ML Platform Services: SageMaker vs Bedrock Comparison
AI & ML Platforms sagemaker_bedrock_comparison 4 cols 9 rows
▼

This table compares AWS services for building and deploying machine learning and generative AI applications. SageMaker is a comprehensive ML platform for building, training, and deploying custom models, while Bedrock provides access to foundation models via API for generative AI applications.

Feature Amazon SageMaker Amazon Bedrock Amazon SageMaker JumpStart Amazon PartyRock
Primary Use Case Build, train, and deploy custom ML models Access pre-trained foundation models via API Quick-start with pre-built ML solutions Learn generative AI through experimentation
ML Expertise Required High (data science knowledge) Low to Medium (API integration) Low to Medium None (code-free)
Model Types Any custom ML model (classification, regression, deep learning) Foundation Models (LLMs, text-to-image, embeddings) Pre-trained models across domains Foundation Models only
Foundation Models Available Via JumpStart (Llama, Falcon, etc.) Titan, Claude, Llama, Stable Diffusion, Jurassic, Command Open-source FMs (Llama, Falcon, etc.) Amazon Titan models
Customization Options Full control: train from scratch, fine-tune, deploy Fine-tuning available for select models, provisioned throughput Pre-trained models can be fine-tuned Prompt engineering only
RAG Support Build custom RAG with vector databases Native Knowledge Bases for RAG Some solutions include RAG patterns Not available
Agents/Autonomous Tasks Build custom agent workflows Native Agents for multi-step task execution Pre-built solution templates Not available
Infrastructure Management Managed notebooks, training clusters, inference endpoints Serverless - no infrastructure to manage Managed deployment on SageMaker Fully managed playground
Pricing Model Notebook instances + training compute + inference endpoints Per token (input/output) + model customization + provisioned throughput Standard SageMaker pricing for deployed resources Free tier available, then per request
⚠️ Exam Tips

SageMaker = build and train custom ML models; Bedrock = consume pre-built foundation models via API

If the question says 'custom model training', the answer is SageMaker

If the question says 'foundation model' or 'generative AI application', the answer is usually Bedrock

SageMaker JumpStart is the bridge — it provides pre-trained models within the SageMaker ecosystem

Bedrock Guardrails is the only service for filtering harmful content from foundation model responses

Amazon SageMaker
Fully managed machine learning platform that provides infrastructure, tools, and workflows for building, training, and deploying custom ML models for any use case.
Amazon Bedrock
Fully managed service that makes foundation models from Amazon and leading AI companies available through a single API for building and scaling generative AI applications.
Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
Machine learning hub within SageMaker providing pre-built solutions, pre-trained models, and example notebooks to accelerate ML adoption with one-click deployment.
Amazon PartyRock
Educational playground for learning generative AI through hands-on, code-free app building to experiment with prompt engineering and foundation model capabilities.
Amazon SageMaker
Primary Use Case: Build, train, and deploy custom ML models | ML Expertise Required: High (data science knowledge) | Model Types: Any custom ML model (classification, regression, deep learning) | Foundation Models Available: Via JumpStart (Llama, Falcon, etc.) | Customization Options: Full control: train from scratch, fine-tune, deploy | RAG Support: Build custom RAG with vector databases | Agents/Autonomous Tasks: Build custom agent workflows | Infrastructure Management: Managed notebooks, training clusters, inference endpoints | Pricing Model: Notebook instances + training compute + inference endpoints
Amazon Bedrock
Primary Use Case: Access pre-trained foundation models via API | ML Expertise Required: Low to Medium (API integration) | Model Types: Foundation Models (LLMs, text-to-image, embeddings) | Foundation Models Available: Titan, Claude, Llama, Stable Diffusion, Jurassic, Command | Customization Options: Fine-tuning available for select models, provisioned throughput | RAG Support: Native Knowledge Bases for RAG | Agents/Autonomous Tasks: Native Agents for multi-step task execution | Infrastructure Management: Serverless - no infrastructure to manage | Pricing Model: Per token (input/output) + model customization + provisioned throughput
Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
Primary Use Case: Quick-start with pre-built ML solutions | ML Expertise Required: Low to Medium | Model Types: Pre-trained models across domains | Foundation Models Available: Open-source FMs (Llama, Falcon, etc.) | Customization Options: Pre-trained models can be fine-tuned | RAG Support: Some solutions include RAG patterns | Agents/Autonomous Tasks: Pre-built solution templates | Infrastructure Management: Managed deployment on SageMaker | Pricing Model: Standard SageMaker pricing for deployed resources
Amazon PartyRock
Primary Use Case: Learn generative AI through experimentation | ML Expertise Required: None (code-free) | Model Types: Foundation Models only | Foundation Models Available: Amazon Titan models | Customization Options: Prompt engineering only | RAG Support: Not available | Agents/Autonomous Tasks: Not available | Infrastructure Management: Fully managed playground | Pricing Model: Free tier available, then per request
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#15 Amazon Bedrock vs Amazon SageMaker
AI & ML Platforms AIF-C01 ordering amazon_bedrock_vs_amazon_sagemaker 2 cols 9 rows
▼

Comparison of AWS's primary AI/ML platforms for building generative AI and machine learning applications

Feature Amazon Bedrock Amazon SageMaker
Primary Use Case Building generative AI applications using pre-trained foundation models (FMs) via API Building custom ML models from scratch, including training and deployment
Model Access Access to multiple third-party FMs (Anthropic, Meta, Cohere, AI21, Mistral) plus AWS Titan models Build custom models or use pre-built algorithms; bring your own model architecture
Customization Options Fine-tuning, RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation), and agents for multi-step tasks Full customization including custom training jobs, hyperparameter tuning, and feature engineering
Infrastructure Management Fully serverless; AWS manages all underlying infrastructure Managed infrastructure but more control over compute resources, instance types, and training configurations
Ease of Use 9/10 - Simple API access, playground environments, quick time to deployment 6/10 - Steeper learning curve but comprehensive capabilities for advanced users
Knowledge Bases Built-in knowledge bases with vector store integration for RAG workloads SageMaker Canvas for no-code ML; JumpStart for pre-trained models
Guardrails & Safety Native guardrails for content filtering, topic constraints, and word filters Requires custom implementation of safety measures in model training/deployment
Cost Model Pay-per-token or per-invocation pricing; no upfront costs Pay for compute resources (instances) used during training and hosting
Typical Exam Scenario Questions about choosing FM access methods, RAG implementation, prompt engineering Questions about ML pipelines, model training, feature stores, model monitoring
💡 Mnemonics

B.R.E.D - Bedrock is for Easy Deployment of generative AI; S.A.G.E - SageMaker is for Advanced GEnerative (and traditional) ML

⚠️ Exam Tips

Use Bedrock when you need quick access to LLMs without managing infrastructure

Use SageMaker when you need to build custom ML models or have specific compliance requirements

Bedrock supports RAG out-of-the-box with its Knowledge Bases feature

SageMaker Clarify helps detect bias in ML models (relevant for Responsible AI domain)

For the AIF-C01 exam, Bedrock features (agents, knowledge bases, guardrails) are high-value topics

Amazon Bedrock
A fully managed service that provides access to foundation models from multiple AI providers through a unified API, enabling rapid development of generative AI applications without managing infrastructure.
Amazon SageMaker
A comprehensive machine learning platform that provides everything needed to build, train, and deploy ML models at scale, from data preparation to model hosting.
Amazon Bedrock
Primary Use Case: Building generative AI applications using pre-trained foundation models (FMs) via API | Model Access: Access to multiple third-party FMs (Anthropic, Meta, Cohere, AI21, Mistral) plus AWS Titan models | Customization Options: Fine-tuning, RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation), and agents for multi-step tasks | Infrastructure Management: Fully serverless; AWS manages all underlying infrastructure | Ease of Use: 9/10 - Simple API access, playground environments, quick time to deployment | Knowledge Bases: Built-in knowledge bases with vector store integration for RAG workloads | Guardrails & Safety: Native guardrails for content filtering, topic constraints, and word filters | Cost Model: Pay-per-token or per-invocation pricing; no upfront costs | Typical Exam Scenario: Questions about choosing FM access methods, RAG implementation, prompt engineering
Amazon SageMaker
Primary Use Case: Building custom ML models from scratch, including training and deployment | Model Access: Build custom models or use pre-built algorithms; bring your own model architecture | Customization Options: Full customization including custom training jobs, hyperparameter tuning, and feature engineering | Infrastructure Management: Managed infrastructure but more control over compute resources, instance types, and training configurations | Ease of Use: 6/10 - Steeper learning curve but comprehensive capabilities for advanced users | Knowledge Bases: SageMaker Canvas for no-code ML; JumpStart for pre-trained models | Guardrails & Safety: Requires custom implementation of safety measures in model training/deployment | Cost Model: Pay for compute resources (instances) used during training and hosting | Typical Exam Scenario: Questions about ML pipelines, model training, feature stores, model monitoring
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#16 Amazon Bedrock vs SageMaker JumpStart vs Amazon Q
AI & ML Platforms AIF-C01 ordering AIF-005 3 cols 8 rows
▼

This is the most heavily tested area for the AIF-C01. These three services are ALL related to Generative AI on AWS, but they serve very different audiences and purposes. The exam will describe a scenario and expect you to select the right service. Bedrock = managed Gen AI platform for building apps. JumpStart = ML model hub for training/deploying. Amazon Q = ready-to-use AI assistant.

Feature Amazon Bedrock Amazon SageMaker JumpStart Amazon Q
Primary User Developers building Gen AI apps ML practitioners / data scientists Business users / non-technical staff
Coding Required? Yes — API calls to build applications Yes — SageMaker SDK and configuration No — plug-and-play assistant
Model Source Curated FMs: Anthropic Claude, Meta Llama, Cohere, AI21, Mistral, Amazon Titan Open-source models: Hugging Face, LLaMA, Falcon, etc. AWS-managed models (internal, not user-selectable)
Key Capability RAG (Knowledge Bases), Agents, Guardrails, Fine-tuning Fine-tune and deploy open-source models at scale Answer questions from connected business data sources
Customization Level High — RAG, fine-tuning, agents, prompt engineering Very High — full model training and customization Low — configure data sources and permissions only
Infrastructure Management None — fully serverless Some — instances deployed for endpoints None — fully managed SaaS
Pricing Model Token-based (pay per input/output token) OR Provisioned Throughput Instance hours for endpoints Per-user subscription
RAG Support Yes — Bedrock Knowledge Bases (native RAG) Must build manually Yes — indexes business documents automatically
⚠️ Exam Tips

Bedrock = API access to foundation models for building Gen AI apps

SageMaker JumpStart = model hub for deploying and fine-tuning models on SageMaker infrastructure

Amazon Q = ready-to-use AI assistant that requires no development

If the scenario describes a business user asking questions — it’s Amazon Q

If the scenario describes a developer building a custom Gen AI app — it’s Bedrock

If the scenario requires training a custom model or hosting on specific compute — it’s SageMaker

Amazon Bedrock
Amazon Bedrock is a fully managed service that gives you access to a curated selection of foundation models (FMs) from AWS and third-party providers (Anthropic, AI21, Cohere, Meta, Stability AI) through a single API — no infrastructure to manage. It is the go-to platform for building custom Gen AI applications on AWS.
Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
JumpStart is a model hub built into SageMaker that provides pre-trained open-source models (from Hugging Face, TensorFlow Hub, etc.) that you can deploy or fine-tune with a few clicks. It is more hands-on than Bedrock — aimed at ML practitioners who want to fine-tune or adapt models.
Amazon Q
Amazon Q is a fully deployed, ready-to-use Gen AI assistant that requires NO development — it is plugged in to your data sources (S3, SharePoint, Confluence) and immediately answers questions about your business. Think of it as 'Gen AI with no code required' for business users.
Amazon Bedrock
Primary User: Developers building Gen AI apps | Coding Required?: Yes — API calls to build applications | Model Source: Curated FMs: Anthropic Claude, Meta Llama, Cohere, AI21, Mistral, Amazon Titan | Key Capability: RAG (Knowledge Bases), Agents, Guardrails, Fine-tuning | Customization Level: High — RAG, fine-tuning, agents, prompt engineering | Infrastructure Management: None — fully serverless | Pricing Model: Token-based (pay per input/output token) OR Provisioned Throughput | RAG Support: Yes — Bedrock Knowledge Bases (native RAG)
Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
Primary User: ML practitioners / data scientists | Coding Required?: Yes — SageMaker SDK and configuration | Model Source: Open-source models: Hugging Face, LLaMA, Falcon, etc. | Key Capability: Fine-tune and deploy open-source models at scale | Customization Level: Very High — full model training and customization | Infrastructure Management: Some — instances deployed for endpoints | Pricing Model: Instance hours for endpoints | RAG Support: Must build manually
Amazon Q
Primary User: Business users / non-technical staff | Coding Required?: No — plug-and-play assistant | Model Source: AWS-managed models (internal, not user-selectable) | Key Capability: Answer questions from connected business data sources | Customization Level: Low — configure data sources and permissions only | Infrastructure Management: None — fully managed SaaS | Pricing Model: Per-user subscription | RAG Support: Yes — indexes business documents automatically
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#17 Amazon Q Business vs Amazon Q Developer Comparison
AI & ML Platforms amazon_q_comparison 2 cols 8 rows
▼

This table compares the two Amazon Q services. Amazon Q Business is a generative AI assistant for enterprise use cases grounded in company data, while Amazon Q Developer assists software developers with coding, testing, and infrastructure optimization.

Feature Amazon Q Business Amazon Q Developer
Target Audience Business users, knowledge workers Software developers, DevOps engineers
Primary Use Case Search company data, generate content, answer business questions Code generation, debugging, infrastructure optimization
Data Sources S3, SharePoint, Confluence, Salesforce, databases IDE context, AWS documentation, code repositories
Integration Points Web interface, Slack, Microsoft Teams VS Code, IntelliJ, AWS Console, command line
Security Model Respects existing data access permissions User identity-based access
Key Features RAG-based answers, document summaries, action execution Code completion, security scans, transformation, chat
Pricing Model Per user subscription Free tier + Professional tier per user
Customization Custom apps, plugins, guardrails Customizations for internal codebases
⚠️ Exam Tips

Q Business connects to enterprise data sources (S3, SharePoint, Confluence) for business Q&A

Q Developer is an IDE-integrated coding assistant for writing, reviewing, and optimizing code

Q Business requires IAM Identity Center for user authentication — it inherits corporate identity

Q Developer can transform code, generate tests, and scan for security vulnerabilities

Both products use Bedrock foundation models under the hood but serve different personas

Amazon Q Business
Generative AI-powered assistant designed for enterprise employees to get answers, summaries, and insights from company data sources while respecting existing access permissions.
Amazon Q Developer
AI-powered assistant for software developers that helps with coding, debugging, testing, upgrading applications, and optimizing AWS resources.
Amazon Q Business
Target Audience: Business users, knowledge workers | Primary Use Case: Search company data, generate content, answer business questions | Data Sources: S3, SharePoint, Confluence, Salesforce, databases | Integration Points: Web interface, Slack, Microsoft Teams | Security Model: Respects existing data access permissions | Key Features: RAG-based answers, document summaries, action execution | Pricing Model: Per user subscription | Customization: Custom apps, plugins, guardrails
Amazon Q Developer
Target Audience: Software developers, DevOps engineers | Primary Use Case: Code generation, debugging, infrastructure optimization | Data Sources: IDE context, AWS documentation, code repositories | Integration Points: VS Code, IntelliJ, AWS Console, command line | Security Model: User identity-based access | Key Features: Code completion, security scans, transformation, chat | Pricing Model: Free tier + Professional tier per user | Customization: Customizations for internal codebases
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#18 Amazon Q Family Comparison
AI & ML Platforms amazon_q_family_comparison 3 cols 8 rows
▼

Comparison of Amazon Q products for different user personas and use cases

Feature Amazon Q Business Amazon Q Developer Amazon Q in QuickSight
Primary Users Business analysts, executives, dashboard viewers Software developers, DevOps engineers, data engineers Business analysts and BI users who create dashboards and reports in Amazon QuickSight
Access Point QuickSight dashboards (embedded in dashboards) VS Code, JetBrains, AWS Console, AWS CLI QuickSight console — natural language query bar embedded in dashboards and analyses
Core Capabilities Natural language queries, ML insights, anomaly detection Code generation, explanation, debugging, refactoring, AWS guidance Natural language to visual — ask questions in plain English, get auto-generated charts, summaries, and data stories
Data Access QuickSight datasets and dashboards Code repositories, documentation, AWS services QuickSight SPICE datasets and connected data sources (Redshift, RDS, S3, Athena, etc.)
RAG Implementation Uses SPICE and QuickSight datasets for answers Indexes code and documentation for context Not RAG-based — uses QuickSight's semantic layer and dataset metadata to interpret natural language queries
Security Inherits QuickSight user permissions Uses IAM permissions, no code stored or trained on Inherits QuickSight row-level and column-level security; IAM permissions; data stays within QuickSight
Pricing Included in QuickSight Enterprise pricing Free tier + \$19/month for Pro features Included in QuickSight Q add-on pricing per user per month on top of standard QuickSight subscription
Use Case Example Asking 'What were our top 3 products last quarter?' in plain English Explaining why an EC2 instance is failing with troubleshooting steps Business user asks 'Show me top 10 products by revenue last quarter' and gets an auto-generated bar chart
💡 Mnemonics

B.D.Q - Business asks questions, Developer codes, QuickSight queries dashboards

⚠️ Exam Tips

Amazon Q Business uses RAG with connectors to enterprise data sources

Amazon Q Developer helps with AWS tasks like infrastructure provisioning and troubleshooting

Amazon Q in QuickSight is included with QuickSight - enables natural language dashboard queries

For AIF-C01: Amazon Q products demonstrate real-world GenAI implementations with responsible AI considerations

All Q products inherit AWS IAM permissions and support corporate identity integration

Amazon Q Business
A generative AI-powered assistant for business users that answers questions, summarizes content, and generates insights from company data and documents.
Amazon Q Developer
An AI coding assistant integrated into IDEs (VS Code, JetBrains), AWS console, and CLI that helps developers write, test, and debug code.
Amazon Q in QuickSight
An AI assistant embedded in QuickSight dashboards that enables natural language querying and automated ML insights for business users.
Amazon Q Business
Primary Users: Business analysts, executives, dashboard viewers | Access Point: QuickSight dashboards (embedded in dashboards) | Core Capabilities: Natural language queries, ML insights, anomaly detection | Data Access: QuickSight datasets and dashboards | RAG Implementation: Uses SPICE and QuickSight datasets for answers | Security: Inherits QuickSight user permissions | Pricing: Included in QuickSight Enterprise pricing | Use Case Example: Asking 'What were our top 3 products last quarter?' in plain English
Amazon Q Developer
Primary Users: Software developers, DevOps engineers, data engineers | Access Point: VS Code, JetBrains, AWS Console, AWS CLI | Core Capabilities: Code generation, explanation, debugging, refactoring, AWS guidance | Data Access: Code repositories, documentation, AWS services | RAG Implementation: Indexes code and documentation for context | Security: Uses IAM permissions, no code stored or trained on | Pricing: Free tier + \$19/month for Pro features | Use Case Example: Explaining why an EC2 instance is failing with troubleshooting steps
Amazon Q in QuickSight
Primary Users: Business analysts and BI users who create dashboards and reports in Amazon QuickSight | Access Point: QuickSight console — natural language query bar embedded in dashboards and analyses | Core Capabilities: Natural language to visual — ask questions in plain English, get auto-generated charts, summaries, and data stories | Data Access: QuickSight SPICE datasets and connected data sources (Redshift, RDS, S3, Athena, etc.) | RAG Implementation: Not RAG-based — uses QuickSight's semantic layer and dataset metadata to interpret natural language queries | Security: Inherits QuickSight row-level and column-level security; IAM permissions; data stays within QuickSight | Pricing: Included in QuickSight Q add-on pricing per user per month on top of standard QuickSight subscription | Use Case Example: Business user asks 'Show me top 10 products by revenue last quarter' and gets an auto-generated bar chart
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#19 SageMaker Components Comparison
AI & ML Platforms AIF-C01 ordering sagemaker_components_comparison 10 cols 8 rows
▼

Amazon SageMaker is a fully managed machine learning service that provides every developer and data scientist with the ability to build, train, and deploy ML models quickly. SageMaker removes the heavy lifting from each step of the machine learning process to make it easier to develop high-quality models. The following components work together to provide a complete ML lifecycle management platform, from data preparation to model deployment and monitoring.

Feature SageMaker Studio SageMaker Training SageMaker Hosting/Endpoints SageMaker Ground Truth SageMaker Model Monitor SageMaker Clarify SageMaker Data Wrangler SageMaker Feature Store SageMaker Pipelines SageMaker JumpStart
Primary Purpose Integrated development environment (IDE) for ML Managed infrastructure for model training Deploy models for real-time inference Data labeling and annotation service Monitor model quality in production Detect bias and explain predictions Visual data preparation tool Centralized storage for ML features Orchestrate ML workflows Pre-built models and solutions library
Target User Data scientists and ML engineers ML engineers and researchers DevOps and ML engineers Data labeling teams ML operations teams Data scientists and compliance teams Data engineers and scientists ML engineers and data scientists ML engineers and MLOps teams All ML practitioners
Key Capabilities Notebooks, debugging, experiment tracking Distributed training, spot instances, debugging Auto-scaling, A/B testing, multi-model endpoints Automated labeling, workforce management Data drift detection, quality metrics Bias detection, SHAP values, model explainability 300+ transformations, data visualization Online/Offline stores, feature discovery CI/CD for ML, step caching, conditional execution Foundation models, solution templates, fine-tuning
Customization Level High - Full code control High - Any ML framework High - Custom inference code Medium - Custom labeling workflows Medium - Custom metrics and baselines Medium - Configurable analysis Medium - Visual + code export High - Custom feature engineering High - Python SDK definition Low to High - Pre-built to customizable
Integration Points All SageMaker components, S3, ECR S3, ECR, CloudWatch, Spot Instances API Gateway, Lambda, Application Auto Scaling S3, Mechanical Turk, Private workforce CloudWatch, SNS, S3 SageMaker Studio, Model Monitor S3, Redshift, Athena, Snowflake SageMaker Training, Processing, Streaming Step Functions, EventBridge, CodePipeline SageMaker Studio, Bedrock
Pricing Model Free IDE, pay for compute instances Per-second compute charges Per-second instance charges Per-label pricing + workforce costs Processing job charges Processing job charges ml.m5.4xlarge minimum, hourly Storage + read/write operations Step execution charges Model deployment charges
Best Use Case End-to-end ML development workflow Large-scale distributed model training Production model serving at scale Creating high-quality training datasets Maintaining model quality over time Regulatory compliance and fairness Rapid data exploration and preparation Feature reuse across teams/models Automated ML CI/CD workflows Quick start with proven solutions
Exam Relevance High - Know as primary IDE High - Spot instances, distributed training High - Multi-model endpoints, auto-scaling Medium - Data labeling scenarios High - Production monitoring Medium - Bias detection questions Medium - Data preparation without code High - Feature management patterns High - MLOps automation Medium - Quick deployment scenarios
⚠️ Exam Tips

SageMaker Studio is the single IDE — all other SageMaker features are accessed through it

Ground Truth creates labeled training data; Data Wrangler prepares and transforms raw data

Feature Store enables feature reuse across teams and provides both online and offline access

Model Monitor detects drift in production; Clarify detects bias before and after deployment

SageMaker Pipelines is the MLOps orchestration tool — it chains all steps into a repeatable workflow

JumpStart accelerates development with pre-trained models that can be deployed or fine-tuned

SageMaker Studio
An integrated development environment (IDE) for ML that provides a single web interface to perform all ML development steps, from data preparation to model deployment and monitoring.
SageMaker Training
Managed training infrastructure that automatically provisions and scales compute resources. Supports built-in algorithms, custom containers, and distributed training across multiple GPU instances.
SageMaker Hosting/Endpoints
Managed real-time inference endpoints that host your trained models. Supports auto-scaling, A/B testing with production variants, and multi-model endpoints for cost efficiency.
SageMaker Ground Truth
A data labeling service that uses a combination of human labelers and active learning to create high-quality training datasets. Supports text, image, video, and 3D point cloud labeling.
SageMaker Model Monitor
Continuously monitors deployed models for data quality issues, model quality degradation, bias drift, and feature attribution drift. Sends alerts when metrics deviate from baselines.
SageMaker Clarify
Detects bias in ML models and provides explainability for predictions using SHAP values. Works during training (pre-deployment) and in production (post-deployment) to ensure fairness and transparency.
SageMaker Data Wrangler
A visual data preparation tool that simplifies data cleaning, transformation, and feature engineering with 300+ built-in transformations. Generates PySpark or Pandas code automatically.
SageMaker Feature Store
A centralized repository for storing, versioning, and sharing ML features across teams. Provides both online (low-latency) and offline (batch) feature retrieval for training and inference.
SageMaker Pipelines
A purpose-built CI/CD service for ML that automates and manages end-to-end ML workflows. Defines steps as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) with built-in experiment tracking.
SageMaker JumpStart
A model hub providing 300+ pre-trained models, including foundation models, that can be fine-tuned and deployed with one click. Includes solution templates for common ML use cases.
SageMaker Studio
Primary Purpose: Integrated development environment (IDE) for ML | Target User: Data scientists and ML engineers | Key Capabilities: Notebooks, debugging, experiment tracking | Customization Level: High - Full code control | Integration Points: All SageMaker components, S3, ECR | Pricing Model: Free IDE, pay for compute instances | Best Use Case: End-to-end ML development workflow | Exam Relevance: High - Know as primary IDE
SageMaker Training
Primary Purpose: Managed infrastructure for model training | Target User: ML engineers and researchers | Key Capabilities: Distributed training, spot instances, debugging | Customization Level: High - Any ML framework | Integration Points: S3, ECR, CloudWatch, Spot Instances | Pricing Model: Per-second compute charges | Best Use Case: Large-scale distributed model training | Exam Relevance: High - Spot instances, distributed training
SageMaker Hosting/Endpoints
Primary Purpose: Deploy models for real-time inference | Target User: DevOps and ML engineers | Key Capabilities: Auto-scaling, A/B testing, multi-model endpoints | Customization Level: High - Custom inference code | Integration Points: API Gateway, Lambda, Application Auto Scaling | Pricing Model: Per-second instance charges | Best Use Case: Production model serving at scale | Exam Relevance: High - Multi-model endpoints, auto-scaling
SageMaker Ground Truth
Primary Purpose: Data labeling and annotation service | Target User: Data labeling teams | Key Capabilities: Automated labeling, workforce management | Customization Level: Medium - Custom labeling workflows | Integration Points: S3, Mechanical Turk, Private workforce | Pricing Model: Per-label pricing + workforce costs | Best Use Case: Creating high-quality training datasets | Exam Relevance: Medium - Data labeling scenarios
SageMaker Model Monitor
Primary Purpose: Monitor model quality in production | Target User: ML operations teams | Key Capabilities: Data drift detection, quality metrics | Customization Level: Medium - Custom metrics and baselines | Integration Points: CloudWatch, SNS, S3 | Pricing Model: Processing job charges | Best Use Case: Maintaining model quality over time | Exam Relevance: High - Production monitoring
SageMaker Clarify
Primary Purpose: Detect bias and explain predictions | Target User: Data scientists and compliance teams | Key Capabilities: Bias detection, SHAP values, model explainability | Customization Level: Medium - Configurable analysis | Integration Points: SageMaker Studio, Model Monitor | Pricing Model: Processing job charges | Best Use Case: Regulatory compliance and fairness | Exam Relevance: Medium - Bias detection questions
SageMaker Data Wrangler
Primary Purpose: Visual data preparation tool | Target User: Data engineers and scientists | Key Capabilities: 300+ transformations, data visualization | Customization Level: Medium - Visual + code export | Integration Points: S3, Redshift, Athena, Snowflake | Pricing Model: ml.m5.4xlarge minimum, hourly | Best Use Case: Rapid data exploration and preparation | Exam Relevance: Medium - Data preparation without code
SageMaker Feature Store
Primary Purpose: Centralized storage for ML features | Target User: ML engineers and data scientists | Key Capabilities: Online/Offline stores, feature discovery | Customization Level: High - Custom feature engineering | Integration Points: SageMaker Training, Processing, Streaming | Pricing Model: Storage + read/write operations | Best Use Case: Feature reuse across teams/models | Exam Relevance: High - Feature management patterns
SageMaker Pipelines
Primary Purpose: Orchestrate ML workflows | Target User: ML engineers and MLOps teams | Key Capabilities: CI/CD for ML, step caching, conditional execution | Customization Level: High - Python SDK definition | Integration Points: Step Functions, EventBridge, CodePipeline | Pricing Model: Step execution charges | Best Use Case: Automated ML CI/CD workflows | Exam Relevance: High - MLOps automation
SageMaker JumpStart
Primary Purpose: Pre-built models and solutions library | Target User: All ML practitioners | Key Capabilities: Foundation models, solution templates, fine-tuning | Customization Level: Low to High - Pre-built to customizable | Integration Points: SageMaker Studio, Bedrock | Pricing Model: Model deployment charges | Best Use Case: Quick start with proven solutions | Exam Relevance: Medium - Quick deployment scenarios
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

AI Services 14 tables
#20 AI Specialized Services Comparison
AI Services ai_specialized_services_comparison 4 cols 5 rows
▼

Comparing AWS specialized AI services for recommendations, fraud detection, and enterprise search

Feature Amazon Personalize Amazon Fraud Detector Amazon Kendra Amazon Forecast
Primary Use Case Product/content recommendations Detecting fraudulent activities Enterprise search and information retrieval Time-series forecasting and predictions
Data Input User interactions, item metadata Transaction data, user behavior Documents from various sources Historical time-series data
Output Personalized recommendations Fraud risk scores Relevant document answers Future value predictions
Real-Time Scoring Yes, real-time recommendations Real-time fraud assessment Query-based search Batch or real-time predictions
ML Expertise Required Low - managed service Low - managed service Low - managed service Low - managed service
💡 Mnemonics

PFKF = Personalize for customers, Fraud for bad actors, Kendra for knowledge, Forecast for future

⚠️ Exam Tips

Personalize is for e-commerce recommendations like Amazon.com

Fraud Detector identifies fraudulent transactions and fake accounts

Kendra provides intelligent enterprise search across multiple data sources

Forecast is for business metrics like demand, inventory, and financials

All these services are low-code - minimal ML expertise needed

Amazon Personalize
A machine learning-powered recommendation service that enables developers to build applications with the same recommendation technology used by Amazon.com.
Amazon Fraud Detector
A fully managed fraud detection service that uses machine learning to identify potentially fraudulent online activities such as online payment fraud and fake accounts.
Amazon Kendra
An intelligent enterprise search service powered by machine learning that enables users to find information stored across various enterprise data sources using natural language queries.
Amazon Forecast
A time-series forecasting service that uses ML to deliver accurate predictions, enabling better business planning and resource optimization.
Amazon Personalize
Primary Use Case: Product/content recommendations | Data Input: User interactions, item metadata | Output: Personalized recommendations | Real-Time Scoring: Yes, real-time recommendations | ML Expertise Required: Low - managed service
Amazon Fraud Detector
Primary Use Case: Detecting fraudulent activities | Data Input: Transaction data, user behavior | Output: Fraud risk scores | Real-Time Scoring: Real-time fraud assessment | ML Expertise Required: Low - managed service
Amazon Kendra
Primary Use Case: Enterprise search and information retrieval | Data Input: Documents from various sources | Output: Relevant document answers | Real-Time Scoring: Query-based search | ML Expertise Required: Low - managed service
Amazon Forecast
Primary Use Case: Time-series forecasting and predictions | Data Input: Historical time-series data | Output: Future value predictions | Real-Time Scoring: Batch or real-time predictions | ML Expertise Required: Low - managed service
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#21 AWS AI Services Comparison (AIF-C01 Focus)
AI Services ai_services_comparison 6 cols 8 rows
▼

This table compares AWS managed AI services that provide pre-trained machine learning capabilities accessible via API. These services enable developers to add AI capabilities without building and training custom models.

Feature Amazon Rekognition Amazon Comprehend Amazon Polly Amazon Transcribe Amazon Translate Amazon Lex
AI Capability Computer Vision Natural Language Processing Text-to-Speech Speech-to-Text Machine Translation Conversational AI
Input Type Images (JPG, PNG), Videos (MP4, MOV) Text documents, UTF-8 text Text (plain or SSML) Audio files (MP3, WAV, FLAC), streaming Text (UTF-8) Voice (audio) or text
Output Type Labels, faces, text, content moderation results Entities, sentiment, key phrases, syntax Audio stream (MP3, OGG, PCM) Transcribed text with timestamps Translated text Intent, slots, response
Key Features Object detection, facial analysis, PPE detection, celebrity recognition Sentiment analysis, entity recognition, topic modeling, custom models Neural voices, SSML support, speech marks, lexicons Speaker diarization, custom vocab, real-time streaming, medical Real-time translation, batch translation, custom terminology Intent recognition, slot filling, multi-turn dialogs, Lambda integration
Real-time Support Yes (video streaming) Yes (real-time API) Yes (streaming synthesis) Yes (streaming transcription) Yes (real-time API) Yes (conversational)
Custom Model Support Custom Labels Custom entities, custom classification Custom voices (Brand Voice) Custom language models, custom vocabularies Custom terminology, parallel data for custom models Custom intents, slots, and slot types
Use Cases Content moderation, face authentication, image search Customer feedback analysis, document processing, social media monitoring Voice assistants, audiobooks, accessibility applications Call center analytics, meeting transcription, captioning Website localization, real-time chat translation, document translation Chatbots, virtual assistants, IVR systems
Pricing Model Per image/video analyzed Per unit (100 characters) Per million characters Per second of audio Per million characters Per speech/text request
⚠️ Exam Tips

Comprehend analyzes text — sentiment, entities, key phrases, language detection, PII detection

Rekognition analyzes images and video — objects, faces, celebrities, text, inappropriate content

Transcribe converts speech to text; Polly converts text to speech — they are opposite services

Lex builds chatbots with voice and text conversational interfaces — same technology as Alexa

All managed AI services are pre-trained — no ML expertise required to use them

Kendra provides intelligent enterprise search with natural language understanding

Amazon Rekognition
Computer vision service that makes it easy to add image and video analysis to applications using pre-trained deep learning models for object detection, facial analysis, and content moderation.
Amazon Comprehend
Natural language processing service that uses machine learning to uncover insights and relationships in text, including sentiment analysis, entity recognition, and key phrase extraction.
Amazon Polly
Text-to-speech service that turns text into lifelike speech using advanced deep learning technologies to create applications that talk with dozens of natural-sounding voices.
Amazon Transcribe
Automatic speech recognition service that accurately converts speech to text with features like speaker identification, custom vocabulary, and real-time streaming transcription.
Amazon Translate
Neural machine translation service that delivers fast, high-quality, and affordable language translation using deep learning models to translate text between languages.
Amazon Lex
Service for building conversational interfaces using voice and text, powered by the same deep learning technologies as Alexa, with intent recognition and slot filling capabilities.
Amazon Rekognition
AI Capability: Computer Vision | Input Type: Images (JPG, PNG), Videos (MP4, MOV) | Output Type: Labels, faces, text, content moderation results | Key Features: Object detection, facial analysis, PPE detection, celebrity recognition | Real-time Support: Yes (video streaming) | Custom Model Support: Custom Labels | Use Cases: Content moderation, face authentication, image search | Pricing Model: Per image/video analyzed
Amazon Comprehend
AI Capability: Natural Language Processing | Input Type: Text documents, UTF-8 text | Output Type: Entities, sentiment, key phrases, syntax | Key Features: Sentiment analysis, entity recognition, topic modeling, custom models | Real-time Support: Yes (real-time API) | Custom Model Support: Custom entities, custom classification | Use Cases: Customer feedback analysis, document processing, social media monitoring | Pricing Model: Per unit (100 characters)
Amazon Polly
AI Capability: Text-to-Speech | Input Type: Text (plain or SSML) | Output Type: Audio stream (MP3, OGG, PCM) | Key Features: Neural voices, SSML support, speech marks, lexicons | Real-time Support: Yes (streaming synthesis) | Custom Model Support: Custom voices (Brand Voice) | Use Cases: Voice assistants, audiobooks, accessibility applications | Pricing Model: Per million characters
Amazon Transcribe
AI Capability: Speech-to-Text | Input Type: Audio files (MP3, WAV, FLAC), streaming | Output Type: Transcribed text with timestamps | Key Features: Speaker diarization, custom vocab, real-time streaming, medical | Real-time Support: Yes (streaming transcription) | Custom Model Support: Custom language models, custom vocabularies | Use Cases: Call center analytics, meeting transcription, captioning | Pricing Model: Per second of audio
Amazon Translate
AI Capability: Machine Translation | Input Type: Text (UTF-8) | Output Type: Translated text | Key Features: Real-time translation, batch translation, custom terminology | Real-time Support: Yes (real-time API) | Custom Model Support: Custom terminology, parallel data for custom models | Use Cases: Website localization, real-time chat translation, document translation | Pricing Model: Per million characters
Amazon Lex
AI Capability: Conversational AI | Input Type: Voice (audio) or text | Output Type: Intent, slots, response | Key Features: Intent recognition, slot filling, multi-turn dialogs, Lambda integration | Real-time Support: Yes (conversational) | Custom Model Support: Custom intents, slots, and slot types | Use Cases: Chatbots, virtual assistants, IVR systems | Pricing Model: Per speech/text request
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#22 AWS Managed AI Services Comparison
AI Services AIF-009 6 cols 14 rows
▼

AWS provides a comprehensive suite of natural language processing services. Amazon Comprehend offers text analysis and NLP. Amazon Translate provides neural machine translation. Amazon Transcribe converts speech to text. Amazon Polly converts text to speech. Amazon Lex builds conversational interfaces. Amazon Kendra provides intelligent enterprise search. Understanding the specific use cases and capabilities of each service is essential for exam success.

Feature Amazon Rekognition Amazon Comprehend Amazon Transcribe Amazon Translate Amazon Polly Amazon Kendra
Key Capabilities Intent recognition, slot filling, multi-turn dialogs, fulfillment hooks Entity recognition, sentiment analysis, topic modeling, PII detection, document classification Speaker diarization, custom vocabularies, medical transcription, call analytics 75+ language pairs, custom terminology, real-time translation Multiple voices, SSML support, lexicons, neural voices Natural language queries, semantic search, relevance tuning, FAQs
Input Type Voice or text user inputs Text documents, social media, emails Audio files (MP3, WAV, FLAC, Ogg) Text content in source language Plain text or SSML Documents from multiple data sources
Output Type Structured intent/slot responses Entities, sentiment, syntax, key phrases, PII Transcript text with timestamps, speaker labels Translated text in target language Audio stream (MP3, Ogg, PCM) Relevant search results with excerpts
Pre-trained Models Yes - labels, faces, celebrities Yes - entities, sentiment, PII Yes - multiple languages Yes - 75+ languages Yes — pre-trained neural TTS voices for 30+ languages with no training required Yes — pre-trained NLP models for intelligent document search and ranking
Custom Training Custom labels, face collections Custom entity recognition, classification Custom vocabulary Custom terminology Limited — supports Brand Voice (custom neural voice) for enterprise customers Yes — supports custom document enrichment, FAQ ingestion, and relevance tuning
Real-time Support Yes - streaming video Yes - real-time analysis Yes - streaming audio Yes - real-time translation Yes — real-time text-to-speech synthesis with streaming audio output Yes — real-time search queries with sub-second response times
Common Use Cases Customer service bots, application interfaces, voice assistants Social media monitoring, document analysis, PII redaction Call center analytics, meeting transcription, captioning Website localization, real-time communication translation IVR systems, audiobooks, accessibility features Enterprise knowledge search, self-service portals
SageMaker Alternative Custom CV models Custom NLP models Custom speech models Custom translation models Custom TTS model training on SageMaker (rarely needed due to Polly's quality) Build custom search with SageMaker embeddings + OpenSearch (more effort, more control)
Primary Purpose Image and video analysis — object detection, facial analysis, content moderation, text-in-image (OCR) Natural language understanding and text analysis Convert speech/audio to text Neural machine translation between languages Convert text to lifelike speech Intelligent enterprise search
Customization Options Custom Labels for domain-specific object detection; configurable confidence thresholds for moderation Custom entity recognition, custom classification, Flywheel Custom vocabularies, language models, content redaction Custom terminology, parallel data for customization Custom lexicons, voice cloning (Neural voices) Custom data sources, relevance tuning, synonyms
Language Support Language-agnostic for visual analysis; text detection supports Latin-script languages Multiple languages for different features 100+ languages and variants 75+ languages supported 30+ languages, various voices per language English (primary), limited multi-language
Integration Points S3, Lambda, Kinesis Video Streams, Step Functions, SNS for async notifications S3, Kinesis, Lambda, SNS S3, Kinesis, Lambda, Amazon Chime SDK S3, Lambda, real-time API Lambda, S3, real-time streaming S3, SharePoint, Confluence, databases, custom sources
Pricing Model Pay-per-image/video analyzed; Custom Labels charged per training hour and inference hour Per-unit text processed (characters/units) Per-second of audio processed Per-million characters translated Per-million characters converted to speech Per-hour index usage + query charges
Exam Relevance AIF-C01 Domain 2 — know as the go-to service for image/video ML; distinguish from Textract (documents) and Comprehend (text NLP) High - NLP tasks, PII detection High - Call analytics, transcription Medium - Multi-language scenarios Medium - TTS requirements High - Enterprise search vs Elasticsearch
💡 Mnemonics

RECOGNIZE-COMPREHEND-TRANSCRIBE-TRANSLATE: Recognize sees, Comprehend understands, Transcribe listens, Translate converts

Working with images/video? Rekognition. Working with text analysis? Comprehend. Working with audio? Transcribe. Need language conversion? Translate.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Rekognition can detect faces, objects, text in images, and celebrities

Comprehend provides sentiment analysis without ML expertise

Transcribe supports automatic language identification

Translate supports custom terminology for domain-specific terms

All services have pre-trained models - no ML expertise required

Use these managed services instead of building custom models when possible

Amazon Rekognition
A fully managed computer vision service that analyzes images and videos to detect objects, scenes, faces, text, and activities. Provides pre-trained models for common tasks plus custom labels capability, enabling content moderation and face recognition without ML expertise.
Amazon Comprehend
A natural language processing service that extracts insights from text including entities, sentiment, key phrases, and PII. Offers pre-trained models for immediate use plus custom classification and entity recognition for domain-specific needs.
Amazon Transcribe
A speech-to-text service that converts audio to text with automatic punctuation and speaker identification. Supports batch processing and real-time streaming, with custom vocabulary for domain-specific terminology like medical or legal terms.
Amazon Translate
A neural machine translation service supporting 75+ languages with high-quality, natural-sounding translations. Supports custom terminology for consistent translation of brand names and domain-specific terms across languages.
Amazon Rekognition
Key Capabilities: Intent recognition, slot filling, multi-turn dialogs, fulfillment hooks | Input Type: Voice or text user inputs | Output Type: Structured intent/slot responses | Pre-trained Models: Yes - labels, faces, celebrities | Custom Training: Custom labels, face collections | Real-time Support: Yes - streaming video | Common Use Cases: Customer service bots, application interfaces, voice assistants | SageMaker Alternative: Custom CV models | Primary Purpose: Image and video analysis — object detection, facial analysis, content moderation, text-in-image (OCR) | Customization Options: Custom Labels for domain-specific object detection; configurable confidence thresholds for moderation | Language Support: Language-agnostic for visual analysis; text detection supports Latin-script languages | Integration Points: S3, Lambda, Kinesis Video Streams, Step Functions, SNS for async notifications | Pricing Model: Pay-per-image/video analyzed; Custom Labels charged per training hour and inference hour | Exam Relevance: AIF-C01 Domain 2 — know as the go-to service for image/video ML; distinguish from Textract (documents) and Comprehend (text NLP)
Amazon Comprehend
