Key Capabilities: Entity recognition, sentiment analysis, topic modeling, PII detection, document classification | Input Type: Text documents, social media, emails | Output Type: Entities, sentiment, syntax, key phrases, PII | Pre-trained Models: Yes - entities, sentiment, PII | Custom Training: Custom entity recognition, classification | Real-time Support: Yes - real-time analysis | Common Use Cases: Social media monitoring, document analysis, PII redaction | SageMaker Alternative: Custom NLP models | Primary Purpose: Natural language understanding and text analysis | Customization Options: Custom entity recognition, custom classification, Flywheel | Language Support: Multiple languages for different features | Integration Points: S3, Kinesis, Lambda, SNS | Pricing Model: Per-unit text processed (characters/units) | Exam Relevance: High - NLP tasks, PII detection
Amazon Transcribe
Key Capabilities: Speaker diarization, custom vocabularies, medical transcription, call analytics | Input Type: Audio files (MP3, WAV, FLAC, Ogg) | Output Type: Transcript text with timestamps, speaker labels | Pre-trained Models: Yes - multiple languages | Custom Training: Custom vocabulary | Real-time Support: Yes - streaming audio | Common Use Cases: Call center analytics, meeting transcription, captioning | SageMaker Alternative: Custom speech models | Primary Purpose: Convert speech/audio to text | Customization Options: Custom vocabularies, language models, content redaction | Language Support: 100+ languages and variants | Integration Points: S3, Kinesis, Lambda, Amazon Chime SDK | Pricing Model: Per-second of audio processed | Exam Relevance: High - Call analytics, transcription
Amazon Translate
Key Capabilities: 75+ language pairs, custom terminology, real-time translation | Input Type: Text content in source language | Output Type: Translated text in target language | Pre-trained Models: Yes - 75+ languages | Custom Training: Custom terminology | Real-time Support: Yes - real-time translation | Common Use Cases: Website localization, real-time communication translation | SageMaker Alternative: Custom translation models | Primary Purpose: Neural machine translation between languages | Customization Options: Custom terminology, parallel data for customization | Language Support: 75+ languages supported | Integration Points: S3, Lambda, real-time API | Pricing Model: Per-million characters translated | Exam Relevance: Medium - Multi-language scenarios
Amazon Polly
Key Capabilities: Multiple voices, SSML support, lexicons, neural voices | Input Type: Plain text or SSML | Output Type: Audio stream (MP3, Ogg, PCM) | Pre-trained Models: Yes — pre-trained neural TTS voices for 30+ languages with no training required | Custom Training: Limited — supports Brand Voice (custom neural voice) for enterprise customers | Real-time Support: Yes — real-time text-to-speech synthesis with streaming audio output | Common Use Cases: IVR systems, audiobooks, accessibility features | SageMaker Alternative: Custom TTS model training on SageMaker (rarely needed due to Polly's quality) | Primary Purpose: Convert text to lifelike speech | Customization Options: Custom lexicons, voice cloning (Neural voices) | Language Support: 30+ languages, various voices per language | Integration Points: Lambda, S3, real-time streaming | Pricing Model: Per-million characters converted to speech | Exam Relevance: Medium - TTS requirements
Amazon Kendra
Key Capabilities: Natural language queries, semantic search, relevance tuning, FAQs | Input Type: Documents from multiple data sources | Output Type: Relevant search results with excerpts | Pre-trained Models: Yes — pre-trained NLP models for intelligent document search and ranking | Custom Training: Yes — supports custom document enrichment, FAQ ingestion, and relevance tuning | Real-time Support: Yes — real-time search queries with sub-second response times | Common Use Cases: Enterprise knowledge search, self-service portals | SageMaker Alternative: Build custom search with SageMaker embeddings + OpenSearch (more effort, more control) | Primary Purpose: Intelligent enterprise search | Customization Options: Custom data sources, relevance tuning, synonyms | Language Support: English (primary), limited multi-language | Integration Points: S3, SharePoint, Confluence, databases, custom sources | Pricing Model: Per-hour index usage + query charges | Exam Relevance: High - Enterprise search vs Elasticsearch
📎 Sources

#23 AWS Managed AI Services — The Pre-Built Tool Kit
AI Services AIF-003 7 cols 5 rows
▼

AWS offers a suite of fully managed AI services that let you add AI capabilities to applications WITHOUT building or training models yourself. The exam tests whether you can match a business requirement to the correct pre-built service. These services are distinct from SageMaker (which lets you BUILD models) — they come ready-made.

Feature Amazon Transcribe Amazon Polly Amazon Translate Amazon Comprehend Amazon Lex Amazon Rekognition Amazon Textract
Input Audio / Video Text Text Text Text / Voice Image / Video Scanned documents / PDFs
Output Text transcript Audio (speech) Translated text Sentiment, entities, topics, language Conversation response / intent Labels, faces, objects, unsafe content flags Extracted text, tables, and form key-value pairs
AI Category Speech Recognition Text-to-Speech (TTS) Machine Translation / NLP NLP / Text Analytics Conversational AI / NLP Computer Vision Computer Vision + OCR
Key Use Case Call center transcription, meeting notes, subtitles Voice assistants, audiobooks, accessibility Multilingual content, localization Sentiment analysis, topic modeling, PII detection Customer chatbots, Alexa Skills, IVR systems Facial ID, content moderation, video surveillance Invoice processing, form digitization, medical records
Requires Model Training? No — fully managed No — choose from pre-built voices No — fully managed No for standard; Custom Comprehend for custom classification No for standard intents; configure dialog flows No — pre-trained; Custom Labels for specific objects No — fully managed
⚠️ Exam Tips

These services require NO ML expertise — they are API calls with pre-trained models

Rekognition = vision, Comprehend = NLP, Transcribe = speech-to-text, Polly = text-to-speech

Textract goes beyond OCR — it understands document structure (tables, forms, key-value pairs)

Translate supports 75+ languages with custom terminology dictionaries

The exam tests your ability to match a business need to the correct pre-built service

Personalize, Forecast, and Fraud Detector are specialized ML services (not NLP/vision)

Amazon Transcribe
Converts speech (audio/video) to text automatically — think of it as a robot stenographer. Used for call center transcripts, meeting notes, closed captions, and video subtitles.
Amazon Polly
Converts text to lifelike speech — the exact opposite of Transcribe. Used for voice assistants, audio books, accessibility features, and any app that needs to 'speak' to users.
Amazon Translate
Machine translation between languages — think Google Translate but as an AWS API. Used for multilingual websites, customer support in multiple languages, and content localization.
Amazon Comprehend
Natural Language Processing (NLP) service that extracts meaning from text — it understands sentiment, key entities, topics, and language. Used for social media analysis, support ticket categorization, and document analysis.
Amazon Lex
Builds conversational chatbots using the same AI technology that powers Alexa. Used for customer service bots, IVR (phone menu) systems, and any interactive text/voice chatbot.
Amazon Rekognition
Computer vision service that analyzes images and videos — detects objects, faces, text, and unsafe content. Used for facial recognition, content moderation, and security cameras.
Amazon Textract
Extracts text AND structured data (like tables and forms) from scanned documents — it goes beyond simple OCR to understand document structure. Used for processing invoices, tax forms, and medical records.
Amazon Transcribe
Input: Audio / Video | Output: Text transcript | AI Category: Speech Recognition | Key Use Case: Call center transcription, meeting notes, subtitles | Requires Model Training?: No — fully managed
Amazon Polly
Input: Text | Output: Audio (speech) | AI Category: Text-to-Speech (TTS) | Key Use Case: Voice assistants, audiobooks, accessibility | Requires Model Training?: No — choose from pre-built voices
Amazon Translate
Input: Text | Output: Translated text | AI Category: Machine Translation / NLP | Key Use Case: Multilingual content, localization | Requires Model Training?: No — fully managed
Amazon Comprehend
Input: Text | Output: Sentiment, entities, topics, language | AI Category: NLP / Text Analytics | Key Use Case: Sentiment analysis, topic modeling, PII detection | Requires Model Training?: No for standard; Custom Comprehend for custom classification
Amazon Lex
Input: Text / Voice | Output: Conversation response / intent | AI Category: Conversational AI / NLP | Key Use Case: Customer chatbots, Alexa Skills, IVR systems | Requires Model Training?: No for standard intents; configure dialog flows
Amazon Rekognition
Input: Image / Video | Output: Labels, faces, objects, unsafe content flags | AI Category: Computer Vision | Key Use Case: Facial ID, content moderation, video surveillance | Requires Model Training?: No — pre-trained; Custom Labels for specific objects
Amazon Textract
Input: Scanned documents / PDFs | Output: Extracted text, tables, and form key-value pairs | AI Category: Computer Vision + OCR | Key Use Case: Invoice processing, form digitization, medical records | Requires Model Training?: No — fully managed
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#24 Amazon Comprehend vs Amazon Lex vs Amazon Polly vs Amazon Transcribe
AI Services amazon_comprehend_vs_amazon_lex_vs_amazon_polly_vs_amazon_tr 4 cols 7 rows
▼

Comparison of AWS natural language processing services for text analysis, chatbots, speech synthesis, and speech recognition

Feature Amazon Comprehend Amazon Lex Amazon Polly Amazon Transcribe
Function Text analysis - extracts insights, sentiment, entities from written text Conversational AI - builds chatbots and voice interfaces Text-to-Speech (TTS) - converts text to natural speech Speech-to-Text (STT) - converts speech to written text
Input Type Text documents, emails, social media posts User text or voice inputs in conversational format Plain text or Speech Synthesis Markup Language (SSML) Audio files (MP3, WAV) or streaming audio
Output Type Insights, sentiment scores, entity lists, key phrases Conversational responses, intent fulfillment Audio stream (MP3, OGG, PCM formats) Text transcripts with timestamps
Customization Custom entity recognition, custom classification models Custom intents, slot types, Lambda integrations Custom neural voices, pronunciation lexicons Custom vocabulary, channel identification, speaker diarization
Use Case Example Analyzing customer feedback for sentiment and key issues Building a customer service chatbot or IVR system Creating voice responses for accessibility applications Transcribing customer service calls for analysis
Serverless Yes - fully managed, pay-per-use Yes - pay-per-request pricing Yes - pay-per-character pricing Yes - pay-per-minute pricing
Real-time Support Async batch processing primarily; real-time via API Yes - designed for real-time conversations Yes - low-latency speech synthesis Both batch and streaming (real-time) modes available
💡 Mnemonics

C.L.P.T - Comprehend reads text, Lex talks, Polly speaks, Transcribe listens

⚠️ Exam Tips

Comprehend is for understanding text; Lex is for creating conversational bots

Polly and Transcribe work together for voice-enabled applications

For chatbots, Lex provides the conversation engine while Lambda handles business logic

Comprehend Medical is a specialized version for healthcare text analysis (out of scope for AIF but relevant for SAA healthcare scenarios)

Amazon Comprehend
A natural language processing (NLP) service that uses machine learning to extract insights from text, including sentiment analysis, entity recognition, and topic modeling.
Amazon Lex
A service for building conversational interfaces (chatbots and voice bots) using the same conversational engine that powers Amazon Alexa.
Amazon Polly
A text-to-speech service that converts text into natural-sounding speech using deep learning technologies.
Amazon Transcribe
An automatic speech recognition (ASR) service that converts speech to text with support for multiple languages and specialized vocabularies.
Amazon Comprehend
Function: Text analysis - extracts insights, sentiment, entities from written text | Input Type: Text documents, emails, social media posts | Output Type: Insights, sentiment scores, entity lists, key phrases | Customization: Custom entity recognition, custom classification models | Use Case Example: Analyzing customer feedback for sentiment and key issues | Serverless: Yes - fully managed, pay-per-use | Real-time Support: Async batch processing primarily; real-time via API
Amazon Lex
Function: Conversational AI - builds chatbots and voice interfaces | Input Type: User text or voice inputs in conversational format | Output Type: Conversational responses, intent fulfillment | Customization: Custom intents, slot types, Lambda integrations | Use Case Example: Building a customer service chatbot or IVR system | Serverless: Yes - pay-per-request pricing | Real-time Support: Yes - designed for real-time conversations
Amazon Polly
Function: Text-to-Speech (TTS) - converts text to natural speech | Input Type: Plain text or Speech Synthesis Markup Language (SSML) | Output Type: Audio stream (MP3, OGG, PCM formats) | Customization: Custom neural voices, pronunciation lexicons | Use Case Example: Creating voice responses for accessibility applications | Serverless: Yes - pay-per-character pricing | Real-time Support: Yes - low-latency speech synthesis
Amazon Transcribe
Function: Speech-to-Text (STT) - converts speech to written text | Input Type: Audio files (MP3, WAV) or streaming audio | Output Type: Text transcripts with timestamps | Customization: Custom vocabulary, channel identification, speaker diarization | Use Case Example: Transcribing customer service calls for analysis | Serverless: Yes - pay-per-minute pricing | Real-time Support: Both batch and streaming (real-time) modes available
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#25 Amazon QuickSight vs AWS Glue
AI Services amazon_quicksight_vs_aws_glue 2 cols 9 rows
▼

Comparison of AWS services for data visualization and data integration

Feature Amazon QuickSight AWS Glue
Primary Function Visualization and reporting - dashboards, charts, ML insights Data integration - ETL, data discovery, cataloging
Data Output Dashboards, visualizations, exported reports Transformed data loaded to data warehouse, data lake, or database
Data Sources S3, RDS, Aurora, Redshift, Athena, third-party databases 50+ sources including S3, RDS, DynamoDB, JDBC, Kafka, etc.
ML Capabilities ML insights, forecasting, anomaly detection in dashboards ML Transforms (FindMatches, De-duplicate), Glue DataBrew for cleaning
Serverless Fully serverless - SPICE in-memory engine Fully serverless - serverless ETL with pay-per-use
Data Catalog Uses Glue Data Catalog for cross-dataset analysis Creates and manages the Glue Data Catalog
Use Case Business analyst dashboards, executive reporting, self-service BI Data lake preparation, data warehouse ETL, data migration
Pricing Per reader per month (with SPICE capacity) Per data processing unit-hour (DPU-hours) + crawler runtime
Integration with Lake Formation Can query data registered with Lake Formation Manages tables in Lake Formation data lake
💡 Mnemonics

Q.G - QuickSight for quick insights (visualization), Glue for data moving (integration)

⚠️ Exam Tips

QuickSight and Glue work together: Glue prepares data, QuickSight visualizes it

QuickSight uses SPICE (Super-fast, Parallel, In-memory Calculation Engine) for fast queries

Glue Data Catalog is the central metadata repository for all data lakes

For data-heavy architectures, use Glue for ETL before loading to Redshift or querying with Athena

QuickSight ML insights automatically find anomalies and forecasts in your data

Amazon QuickSight
A fully managed business intelligence (BI) service that enables interactive dashboards, natural language queries, and ML-powered insights from various data sources.
AWS Glue
A fully managed ETL (extract, transform, load) service that prepares and transforms data for analytics by discovering, cataloging, and moving data between sources.
Amazon QuickSight
Primary Function: Visualization and reporting - dashboards, charts, ML insights | Data Output: Dashboards, visualizations, exported reports | Data Sources: S3, RDS, Aurora, Redshift, Athena, third-party databases | ML Capabilities: ML insights, forecasting, anomaly detection in dashboards | Serverless: Fully serverless - SPICE in-memory engine | Data Catalog: Uses Glue Data Catalog for cross-dataset analysis | Use Case: Business analyst dashboards, executive reporting, self-service BI | Pricing: Per reader per month (with SPICE capacity) | Integration with Lake Formation: Can query data registered with Lake Formation
AWS Glue
Primary Function: Data integration - ETL, data discovery, cataloging | Data Output: Transformed data loaded to data warehouse, data lake, or database | Data Sources: 50+ sources including S3, RDS, DynamoDB, JDBC, Kafka, etc. | ML Capabilities: ML Transforms (FindMatches, De-duplicate), Glue DataBrew for cleaning | Serverless: Fully serverless - serverless ETL with pay-per-use | Data Catalog: Creates and manages the Glue Data Catalog | Use Case: Data lake preparation, data warehouse ETL, data migration | Pricing: Per data processing unit-hour (DPU-hours) + crawler runtime | Integration with Lake Formation: Manages tables in Lake Formation data lake
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#26 Amazon Rekognition vs Amazon Textract
AI Services amazon_rekognition_vs_amazon_textract 2 cols 6 rows
▼

Comparison of AWS computer vision services for image/video analysis and document processing

Feature Amazon Rekognition Amazon Textract
Primary Function Image and video analysis - detects objects, faces, celebrities, inappropriate content Document extraction - pulls text and data from PDFs and images
Input Images (JPEG, PNG) and videos (MP4, MOV) Scanned documents, PDFs, images containing text
Output Labels, bounding boxes, confidence scores, moderation flags Extracted text, table structures, form key-value pairs, query results
Key Capabilities Face detection/analysis, label detection, video clips, PPE detection, celebrity recognition OCR, table extraction, form data extraction, queries, signatures detection
Use Cases Content moderation, user verification, asset cataloging, safety monitoring Invoice processing, contract analysis, data entry automation, compliance document processing
Pricing Per image or per minute of video analyzed Per page processed (with cheaper async option)
💡 Mnemonics

R.I.D.E - Rekognition identifies Images (and video); Textract Reads Everything

⚠️ Exam Tips

Rekognition is for visual content analysis; Textract is for document data extraction

Textract can return structured data (tables, forms) unlike basic OCR services

Both services are serverless and can be invoked via API or integrated with Lambda

Rekognition automatically processes images for labels without custom ML models

Amazon Rekognition
A computer vision service that can identify objects, people, text, scenes, and activities in images and videos, as well as detect inappropriate content.
Amazon Textract
A service that automatically extracts text, handwriting, and data from scanned documents using machine learning, going beyond simple OCR to understand document structure.
Amazon Rekognition
Primary Function: Image and video analysis - detects objects, faces, celebrities, inappropriate content | Input: Images (JPEG, PNG) and videos (MP4, MOV) | Output: Labels, bounding boxes, confidence scores, moderation flags | Key Capabilities: Face detection/analysis, label detection, video clips, PPE detection, celebrity recognition | Use Cases: Content moderation, user verification, asset cataloging, safety monitoring | Pricing: Per image or per minute of video analyzed
Amazon Textract
Primary Function: Document extraction - pulls text and data from PDFs and images | Input: Scanned documents, PDFs, images containing text | Output: Extracted text, table structures, form key-value pairs, query results | Key Capabilities: OCR, table extraction, form data extraction, queries, signatures detection | Use Cases: Invoice processing, contract analysis, data entry automation, compliance document processing | Pricing: Per page processed (with cheaper async option)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#27 Batch Inferencing vs Real-Time Inferencing
AI Services AIF-C01 ordering AIF-004 2 cols 5 rows
▼

Inferencing is what happens AFTER a model is trained — you give it new data and it makes predictions. The two delivery modes — batch and real-time — differ in latency, cost, and use case. Choosing wrong costs money or delivers poor user experience, so the exam tests this frequently.

Feature Batch Inferencing Real-Time Inferencing
Latency High — results may take minutes to hours Low — millisecond to second response
When Results Are Needed NOT immediately — results can wait Immediately — user/system waits for response
Cost Lower — pay only during processing window Higher — endpoint always running
Business Example Nightly product recommendations email, weekly churn prediction reports Fraud detection on card swipe, image moderation on upload, chatbot response
AWS SageMaker Feature SageMaker Batch Transform SageMaker Real-Time Endpoints
⚠️ Exam Tips

Batch inference processes large datasets offline — cheaper but higher latency

Real-time inference processes requests instantly — more expensive but immediate results

Use batch for: scoring entire datasets, nightly predictions, recommendation updates

Use real-time for: chatbots, fraud detection, content moderation, user-facing features

SageMaker supports both: Batch Transform for batch, Endpoints for real-time

Bedrock API calls are always real-time (or near real-time for streaming responses)

Batch Inferencing
Batch inferencing processes a large collection of inputs all at once on a schedule — like running nightly reports. It is cost-effective because compute resources are only used during the job, but results are not instant.
Real-Time Inferencing
Real-time (online) inferencing processes each request as it arrives and returns results immediately — like a fraud check the moment a credit card is swiped. It requires always-on compute which costs more but delivers instant responses.
Batch Inferencing
Latency: High — results may take minutes to hours | When Results Are Needed: NOT immediately — results can wait | Cost: Lower — pay only during processing window | Business Example: Nightly product recommendations email, weekly churn prediction reports | AWS SageMaker Feature: SageMaker Batch Transform
Real-Time Inferencing
Latency: Low — millisecond to second response | When Results Are Needed: Immediately — user/system waits for response | Cost: Higher — endpoint always running | Business Example: Fraud detection on card swipe, image moderation on upload, chatbot response | AWS SageMaker Feature: SageMaker Real-Time Endpoints
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#28 Computer Vision & Document AI Services Comparison
AI Services computer_vision_document_ai_services_comparison 3 cols 5 rows
▼

Comparing AWS services for image/video analysis and document processing

Feature Amazon Rekognition Amazon Textract Amazon Transcribe
Input Type Images and videos Scanned documents, PDFs, forms Audio files (speech)
Output Type Labels, faces, celebrities, unsafe content Text, forms (key-value pairs), tables Text transcripts
Use Case Image/video analysis, facial recognition Document data extraction, OCR Speech-to-text transcription
Customization Custom labels, PPE detection Async queries, IDP for processing pipelines Custom vocabulary, channel identification
Real-Time Support Yes, with Video API Async primarily Streaming (real-time) and batch
💡 Mnemonics

Rekognize images, Textract text, Transcribe speech - RTT for visual/audio content

⚠️ Exam Tips

Rekognition is for visual content - images and videos

Textract is for extracting data from documents - it goes beyond basic OCR

Transcribe is for audio - converts speech to text

For invoices and forms, think Textract for extraction

Rekognition can detect faces, labels, and inappropriate content

Amazon Rekognition
A computer vision service that makes it easy to add image and video analysis to applications using proven, highly scalable deep learning technology.
Amazon Textract
A machine learning service that automatically extracts text, handwriting, and data from scanned documents, forms, and tables using OCR technology.
Amazon Transcribe
An automatic speech recognition (ASR) service that makes it easy to add speech-to-text capabilities to applications with powerful neural network models.
Amazon Rekognition
Input Type: Images and videos | Output Type: Labels, faces, celebrities, unsafe content | Use Case: Image/video analysis, facial recognition | Customization: Custom labels, PPE detection | Real-Time Support: Yes, with Video API
Amazon Textract
Input Type: Scanned documents, PDFs, forms | Output Type: Text, forms (key-value pairs), tables | Use Case: Document data extraction, OCR | Customization: Async queries, IDP for processing pipelines | Real-Time Support: Async primarily
Amazon Transcribe
Input Type: Audio files (speech) | Output Type: Text transcripts | Use Case: Speech-to-text transcription | Customization: Custom vocabulary, channel identification | Real-Time Support: Streaming (real-time) and batch
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#29 Managed AI Services for Language and Speech
AI Services aif_d1_managed_ai_services_nlp_speech 5 cols 6 rows
▼
Feature Amazon Comprehend Amazon Lex Amazon Polly Amazon Transcribe Amazon Translate
Input Text Text or voice conversation Text Audio Text
Output Insights about text Bot responses / intents Speech audio Transcript text Translated text
Best exam clue Sentiment, entity extraction, text analysis Chatbot or conversational assistant Read text aloud Generate transcript from speech Translate text across languages
Primary modality NLP understanding Conversation Speech synthesis Speech recognition Language translation
Great for Text analytics Bots Voice output Meeting/audio transcripts Multilingual messaging
Common trap Using it as translation Confusing it with text analytics Confusing it with transcription Confusing it with text-to-speech Confusing it with sentiment or chatbot logic
⚠️ Exam Tips

Comprehend analyzes text for sentiment, entities, and key phrases — no model training needed

Transcribe converts speech to text with automatic language identification for 100+ languages

Translate provides neural machine translation between 75+ language pairs

Polly converts text to lifelike speech with neural TTS voices for natural-sounding output

Custom vocabulary in Transcribe improves accuracy for domain-specific terminology

Amazon Comprehend
Comprehend extracts meaning from text, such as sentiment, entities, and key phrases. In exam language, it is the text understanding service.
Amazon Lex
Lex is for conversational interfaces like chatbots and voice bots. If the prompt involves dialog flow or intent capture, Lex is the clue.
Amazon Polly
Polly converts text into speech. It is the output voice service, not the speech-to-text one.
Amazon Transcribe
Transcribe converts spoken audio into text. It is the speech-to-text service for transcripts and voice analysis pipelines.
Amazon Translate
Translate converts text from one language to another. When the requirement is multilingual text translation, this is the managed answer.
Amazon Comprehend
Input: Text | Output: Insights about text | Best exam clue: Sentiment, entity extraction, text analysis | Primary modality: NLP understanding | Great for: Text analytics | Common trap: Using it as translation
Amazon Lex
Input: Text or voice conversation | Output: Bot responses / intents | Best exam clue: Chatbot or conversational assistant | Primary modality: Conversation | Great for: Bots | Common trap: Confusing it with text analytics
Amazon Polly
Input: Text | Output: Speech audio | Best exam clue: Read text aloud | Primary modality: Speech synthesis | Great for: Voice output | Common trap: Confusing it with transcription
Amazon Transcribe
Input: Audio | Output: Transcript text | Best exam clue: Generate transcript from speech | Primary modality: Speech recognition | Great for: Meeting/audio transcripts | Common trap: Confusing it with text-to-speech
Amazon Translate
Input: Text | Output: Translated text | Best exam clue: Translate text across languages | Primary modality: Language translation | Great for: Multilingual messaging | Common trap: Confusing it with sentiment or chatbot logic
🎯 Why This Table Matters

AIF Domain 1 asks you to recognize practical AI use cases and the capabilities of AWS managed AI/ML services. This table helps you map the service to the modality fast.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

#30 Managed AI Services for Vision, Documents, Search, and Personalization
AI Services aif_d1_managed_ai_services_vision_docs_search 5 cols 5 rows
▼
Feature Amazon Rekognition Amazon Textract Amazon Kendra Amazon Personalize Amazon Fraud Detector
Primary problem solved Visual analysis Document text and structure extraction Enterprise search Recommendations Fraud risk detection
Best exam clue Images or video Forms, PDFs, scanned documents Search and relevance Personalized suggestions Fraud pattern detection
Typical output Detected visual attributes or events Extracted text / document fields Relevant search results / answers Ranked recommendations Fraud likelihood signals
Great for Vision workloads Document automation Knowledge search UX Recommendation systems Risk decision support
Common trap Using it for document field extraction Using it as semantic enterprise search Using it as a recommendation engine Using it as general search Treating it as generic classification for any AI task
⚠️ Exam Tips

Rekognition = image/video analysis; Textract = document data extraction; Kendra = enterprise search

Textract extracts structured data (tables, forms) from documents — not just raw text

Kendra uses NLP to understand natural language queries and return precise answers

Personalize generates real-time recommendations using the same ML as Amazon.com

These services are all fully managed — no infrastructure to provision or models to train

Amazon Rekognition
Rekognition analyzes images and video for visual information. It is the exam clue for image understanding and video-based visual detection tasks.
Amazon Textract
Textract extracts text and structure from documents. It is stronger than plain OCR-style thinking because it understands document content structure relevant to forms and documents.
Amazon Kendra
Kendra is an intelligent search service for enterprise search experiences. If the requirement is search over content and finding relevant answers, Kendra is the signal.
Amazon Personalize
Personalize is built for recommendation use cases. Think content suggestions, ranking, and individualized recommendations.
Amazon Fraud Detector
Fraud Detector is focused on identifying potentially fraudulent activity patterns. When the business problem is fraud scoring or suspicious transaction detection, it is the purpose-built pick.
Amazon Rekognition
Primary problem solved: Visual analysis | Best exam clue: Images or video | Typical output: Detected visual attributes or events | Great for: Vision workloads | Common trap: Using it for document field extraction
Amazon Textract
Primary problem solved: Document text and structure extraction | Best exam clue: Forms, PDFs, scanned documents | Typical output: Extracted text / document fields | Great for: Document automation | Common trap: Using it as semantic enterprise search
Amazon Kendra
Primary problem solved: Enterprise search | Best exam clue: Search and relevance | Typical output: Relevant search results / answers | Great for: Knowledge search UX | Common trap: Using it as a recommendation engine
Amazon Personalize
Primary problem solved: Recommendations | Best exam clue: Personalized suggestions | Typical output: Ranked recommendations | Great for: Recommendation systems | Common trap: Using it as general search
Amazon Fraud Detector
Primary problem solved: Fraud risk detection | Best exam clue: Fraud pattern detection | Typical output: Fraud likelihood signals | Great for: Risk decision support | Common trap: Treating it as generic classification for any AI task
🎯 Why This Table Matters

AIF Domain 1 also covers real-world use cases like vision, recommendation, search, and document extraction. This table prevents you from picking a general-purpose AI service when a purpose-built one is cleaner.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

#31 Natural Language Processing (NLP) Services Comparison
AI Services natural_language_processing_nlp_services_comparison 4 cols 6 rows
▼

Comparing AWS services for text analysis, language processing, and conversational interfaces

Feature Amazon Comprehend Amazon Lex Amazon Translate Amazon Polly
Primary Function Analyze text and extract insights Build chatbots and conversational interfaces Translate text between languages Convert text to speech
Input Type Unstructured text Text or voice input Text in source language Text
Output Type Insights, entities, sentiment scores Conversational responses Translated text Audio stream (MP3/OGG)
Customization Custom entity recognition, classification Custom conversation flows, slot types Custom terminology Custom lexicons, speech marks
Language Support Multiple languages for key features Multiple languages 75+ languages Multiple languages and voices
Real-Time Processing Async (batch) and sync APIs Real-time conversational Sync and async APIs Real-time synthesis
💡 Mnemonics

CLaTP = Comprehend for language insights, Lex for conversation, Translate for languages, Polly for speech

⚠️ Exam Tips

Use Comprehend for analyzing customer feedback and sentiment analysis

Use Lex for building chatbots - it's the engine behind Alexa

Polly + Lex together create voice/text chatbots with speech synthesis

Comprehend CANNOT translate languages - use Translate for that

Amazon Transcribe converts speech to text (opposite of Polly)

Amazon Comprehend
A natural language processing (NLP) service that uses machine learning to find insights and relationships in text, including sentiment analysis, entity extraction, and topic modeling.
Amazon Lex
A service for building conversational interfaces using voice and text, powered by the same technology as Amazon Alexa, supporting sophisticated chatbots and voice assistants.
Amazon Translate
A neural machine translation service that delivers fast, high-quality, and affordable language translation between supported languages.
Amazon Polly
A text-to-speech (TTS) service that converts written text into lifelike speech using deep learning, supporting multiple languages and voices.
Amazon Comprehend
Primary Function: Analyze text and extract insights | Input Type: Unstructured text | Output Type: Insights, entities, sentiment scores | Customization: Custom entity recognition, classification | Language Support: Multiple languages for key features | Real-Time Processing: Async (batch) and sync APIs
Amazon Lex
Primary Function: Build chatbots and conversational interfaces | Input Type: Text or voice input | Output Type: Conversational responses | Customization: Custom conversation flows, slot types | Language Support: Multiple languages | Real-Time Processing: Real-time conversational
Amazon Translate
Primary Function: Translate text between languages | Input Type: Text in source language | Output Type: Translated text | Customization: Custom terminology | Language Support: 75+ languages | Real-Time Processing: Sync and async APIs
Amazon Polly
Primary Function: Convert text to speech | Input Type: Text | Output Type: Audio stream (MP3/OGG) | Customization: Custom lexicons, speech marks | Language Support: Multiple languages and voices | Real-Time Processing: Real-time synthesis
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#32 Personalization and Forecasting Comparison
AI Services personalization_and_forecasting_comparison 3 cols 11 rows
▼

AWS offers specialized services for creating personalized experiences and making predictions. Amazon Personalize creates real-time personalized recommendations similar to Amazon.com's recommendation engine. Amazon Forecast generates accurate time-series forecasts using ML. Amazon Fraud Detector identifies potentially fraudulent online activities. These services are fully managed and designed for specific business use cases without requiring ML expertise.

Feature Amazon Personalize Amazon Forecast Amazon Fraud Detector
Primary Purpose Create real-time personalized recommendations Generate time-series forecasts using ML Detect fraudulent online activities
ML Problem Type Recommendation systems, collaborative filtering Time-series forecasting, demand planning Binary classification, anomaly detection
Input Data Requirements User interactions, item metadata, user metadata Historical time-series data, related time series Historical fraud labels, transaction data
Key Capabilities Real-time recommendations, similar items, personalized rankings, user segmentation Multiple algorithms, automatic model selection, what-if analysis Online fraud detection, account takeover detection, custom models
Real-time Capabilities Yes - Real-time recommendations API No - Batch forecasts only Yes - Real-time fraud predictions
Customization Level High - Custom recipes, domain optimizers Medium - Algorithm selection, hyperparameters High - Custom models, rules, thresholds
Integration Points API Gateway, Lambda, Kinesis, S3 S3, Lambda, QuickSight API Gateway, Lambda, EventBridge
Pricing Model Data ingestion + training + recommendations (TPS-hours) Data storage + training hours + forecast generation Prediction charges + model training + data storage
Cold Start Handling Item cold start with metadata, user cold start with popularity Cold start not applicable - requires history New entity risk scoring with limited data
Best Use Case E-commerce recommendations, content personalization Demand forecasting, resource planning, financial planning Payment fraud, account takeover, fake account detection
Exam Relevance High - Recommendation scenarios Medium - Time-series prediction needs Medium - Fraud detection requirements
⚠️ Exam Tips

Personalize uses collaborative filtering and deep learning for real-time recommendations

Forecast uses AutoML to select the best time-series algorithm automatically

Fraud Detector builds custom fraud detection models using your historical fraud data

All three services are fully managed — bring your data, get predictions

Personalize requires user interaction data (clicks, views, purchases) for training

Amazon Personalize
A fully managed ML service for real-time personalization and recommendations. Uses the same technology as Amazon.com to generate product recommendations, personalized search results, and targeted marketing.
Amazon Forecast
A time-series forecasting service that uses ML to deliver highly accurate forecasts. Up to 50% more accurate than traditional methods, supporting retail demand, inventory planning, and resource capacity planning.
Amazon Fraud Detector
A fully managed fraud detection service that uses ML to identify potentially fraudulent online activities such as fake account creation and online payment fraud. Requires no prior ML experience.
Amazon Personalize
Primary Purpose: Create real-time personalized recommendations | ML Problem Type: Recommendation systems, collaborative filtering | Input Data Requirements: User interactions, item metadata, user metadata | Key Capabilities: Real-time recommendations, similar items, personalized rankings, user segmentation | Real-time Capabilities: Yes - Real-time recommendations API | Customization Level: High - Custom recipes, domain optimizers | Integration Points: API Gateway, Lambda, Kinesis, S3 | Pricing Model: Data ingestion + training + recommendations (TPS-hours) | Cold Start Handling: Item cold start with metadata, user cold start with popularity | Best Use Case: E-commerce recommendations, content personalization | Exam Relevance: High - Recommendation scenarios
Amazon Forecast
Primary Purpose: Generate time-series forecasts using ML | ML Problem Type: Time-series forecasting, demand planning | Input Data Requirements: Historical time-series data, related time series | Key Capabilities: Multiple algorithms, automatic model selection, what-if analysis | Real-time Capabilities: No - Batch forecasts only | Customization Level: Medium - Algorithm selection, hyperparameters | Integration Points: S3, Lambda, QuickSight | Pricing Model: Data storage + training hours + forecast generation | Cold Start Handling: Cold start not applicable - requires history | Best Use Case: Demand forecasting, resource planning, financial planning | Exam Relevance: Medium - Time-series prediction needs
Amazon Fraud Detector
Primary Purpose: Detect fraudulent online activities | ML Problem Type: Binary classification, anomaly detection | Input Data Requirements: Historical fraud labels, transaction data | Key Capabilities: Online fraud detection, account takeover detection, custom models | Real-time Capabilities: Yes - Real-time fraud predictions | Customization Level: High - Custom models, rules, thresholds | Integration Points: API Gateway, Lambda, EventBridge | Pricing Model: Prediction charges + model training + data storage | Cold Start Handling: New entity risk scoring with limited data | Best Use Case: Payment fraud, account takeover, fake account detection | Exam Relevance: Medium - Fraud detection requirements
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#33 Vision AI Services Comparison
AI Services vision_ai_services_comparison 3 cols 10 rows
▼

AWS offers multiple services for computer vision and document processing tasks. Amazon Rekognition provides image and video analysis for object detection, facial recognition, and content moderation. Amazon Textract extracts text and data from documents including forms and tables. Amazon HealthLake Imaging provides medical imaging storage and analysis. Each service is optimized for specific vision-related use cases and has different capabilities for customization and integration.

Feature Amazon Rekognition Amazon Textract Amazon HealthLake Imaging
Primary Purpose Analyze images and videos for content Extract text and data from documents Store and analyze medical DICOM images
Input Types Images (JPG, PNG) and Videos (MOV, MP4) Documents (PDF, TIFF, JPG, PNG) DICOM format medical images
Key Capabilities Object detection, facial analysis, text detection, content moderation, celebrity recognition OCR, form extraction, table extraction, handwriting recognition, query-based extraction DICOM import, image transformation, metadata indexing, HIPAA-compliant storage
Customization Options Custom Labels for custom object detection Custom queries, adapter-based customization Limited - primarily storage and retrieval
API Type Synchronous and Asynchronous APIs Synchronous (single page) and Asynchronous (multi-page) RESTful APIs for imaging operations
Data Privacy/Compliance Supports VPC, encryption at rest/transit HIPAA eligible, SOC compliant HIPAA compliant, designed for PHI
Integration Points S3, Kinesis Video Streams, SNS S3, SNS, Amazon A2I (human review) HealthLake, S3, IAM
Pricing Model Per-image or per-minute video pricing Per-page pricing by feature type Storage + API request charges
Best Use Case Content moderation, facial recognition, scene detection Invoice processing, form digitization, document analysis Medical imaging archives, radiology workflows
Exam Relevance High - Know Custom Labels vs built-in High - OCR alternative, form extraction Medium - Healthcare-specific scenarios
⚠️ Exam Tips

Rekognition handles both images and video — other services like Textract are image/document only

Textract extracts tables and forms from documents, going well beyond simple OCR

Rekognition Custom Labels lets you train custom object detection for your specific use case

HealthLake Imaging is HIPAA-eligible and specifically designed for medical DICOM images

For content moderation (inappropriate images), use Rekognition Content Moderation API

Amazon Rekognition
A computer vision service for image and video analysis. Detects objects, scenes, faces, celebrities, text in images, and inappropriate content. Supports custom label training for domain-specific detection.
Amazon Textract
An OCR-plus service that extracts text, handwriting, tables, and forms from scanned documents. Goes beyond basic OCR by understanding document structure and relationships between extracted fields.
Amazon HealthLake Imaging
A HIPAA-eligible service for storing, transforming, and analyzing medical imaging data at petabyte scale. Supports DICOM format and enables ML-based medical image analysis.
Amazon Rekognition
Primary Purpose: Analyze images and videos for content | Input Types: Images (JPG, PNG) and Videos (MOV, MP4) | Key Capabilities: Object detection, facial analysis, text detection, content moderation, celebrity recognition | Customization Options: Custom Labels for custom object detection | API Type: Synchronous and Asynchronous APIs | Data Privacy/Compliance: Supports VPC, encryption at rest/transit | Integration Points: S3, Kinesis Video Streams, SNS | Pricing Model: Per-image or per-minute video pricing | Best Use Case: Content moderation, facial recognition, scene detection | Exam Relevance: High - Know Custom Labels vs built-in
Amazon Textract
Primary Purpose: Extract text and data from documents | Input Types: Documents (PDF, TIFF, JPG, PNG) | Key Capabilities: OCR, form extraction, table extraction, handwriting recognition, query-based extraction | Customization Options: Custom queries, adapter-based customization | API Type: Synchronous (single page) and Asynchronous (multi-page) | Data Privacy/Compliance: HIPAA eligible, SOC compliant | Integration Points: S3, SNS, Amazon A2I (human review) | Pricing Model: Per-page pricing by feature type | Best Use Case: Invoice processing, form digitization, document analysis | Exam Relevance: High - OCR alternative, form extraction
Amazon HealthLake Imaging
Primary Purpose: Store and analyze medical DICOM images | Input Types: DICOM format medical images | Key Capabilities: DICOM import, image transformation, metadata indexing, HIPAA-compliant storage | Customization Options: Limited - primarily storage and retrieval | API Type: RESTful APIs for imaging operations | Data Privacy/Compliance: HIPAA compliant, designed for PHI | Integration Points: HealthLake, S3, IAM | Pricing Model: Storage + API request charges | Best Use Case: Medical imaging archives, radiology workflows | Exam Relevance: Medium - Healthcare-specific scenarios
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

Generative AI 4 tables
#34 AWS Generative AI Services Comparison
Generative AI AIF-C01 ordering AIF-004_2 4 cols 15 rows
▼

AWS provides multiple services for generative AI capabilities, each designed for different user personas and use cases. Amazon Bedrock provides foundation model access via API for developers building generative AI applications. Amazon Q is a generative AI-powered assistant for business users and developers. PartyRock is a learning playground for experimenting with generative AI without coding. Understanding when to use each service is critical for both the AIF-C01 and SAA-C03 exams.

Feature Amazon Bedrock Amazon SageMaker JumpStart Amazon Q PartyRock
Service Type Managed FMs as a service ML platform with pre-built models Generative AI assistant Bedrock playground (free tier)
Model Access Multiple FMs (Claude, Titan, Llama, etc.) Open source & AWS models Bedrock-powered assistant Bedrock models (sandbox)
Customization Options Fine-tuning, continued pre-training, RAG, Agents Full model customization Custom data sources, application plugins Prompt engineering only
Infrastructure Fully serverless Requires SageMaker instances Fully managed Serverless (free tier)
Coding Required Yes - API/SDK integration Moderate to high No - Natural language interface No - Visual interface
Best Use Case Build production generative AI applications Custom ML workflows Accelerate development and business workflows Learn generative AI concepts hands-on
Data Privacy Data not used to train models, VPC support Full control over data Enterprise-grade security, IAM integration Public service, not for sensitive data
Pricing Model Per-token pricing (input/output) Compute + storage costs Per-user subscription pricing Free tier with credit-based usage
Agents Support Bedrock Agents available Custom agent development Built-in agent capabilities Not available
Primary Purpose Access foundation models via API for app development Hub for discovering, deploying, and fine-tuning foundation models and ML solutions with one-click deployment AI assistant for business and development tasks Learn and experiment with generative AI
Target User Developers and ML engineers ML engineers and data scientists who need pre-built ML solutions and foundation model access within SageMaker Business users, developers, IT professionals Students, hobbyists, AI learners
Foundation Models Available Claude, Llama, Titan, Jurassic, Stable Diffusion, Command Yes — Llama, Falcon, Stability AI, Hugging Face models, plus proprietary models via SageMaker Proprietary AWS models (Titan-based) Curated selection (Claude, Titan, Stable Diffusion)
Key Capabilities Text generation, embeddings, image generation, agents One-click model deployment, transfer learning, built-in algorithms, solution templates, fine-tuning notebooks Code generation, document analysis, AWS expertise, business insights Prompt engineering, app building, model comparison
Integration Points Lambda, API Gateway, Kendra, OpenSearch, S3 SageMaker Studio, SageMaker Endpoints, S3, SageMaker Pipelines, SageMaker Model Registry AWS Console, IDEs (VS Code), business applications Standalone web interface
Exam Relevance Very High - Core generative AI service Know as SageMaker's model hub; compare with Bedrock (serverless API) vs JumpStart (deploy to SageMaker endpoints) High - Know the assistant use cases Low - Learning tool, not production
💡 Mnemonics

BED-SM-Q-PARTY: BEDrock for foundation, SageMaker for Mastery, Q for Quick assistance, PartyRock for Practice

Need production FMs? Bedrock. Building custom models? SageMaker. Want a business assistant? Amazon Q. Learning/testing? PartyRock.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Bedrock is the primary service for using foundation models without managing infrastructure

SageMaker JumpStart is for when you need more control over the model and training

Amazon Q is the 'copilot' style assistant for business productivity

PartyRock is the free playground for testing Bedrock models - great for learning

For fine-tuning: Bedrock for quick fine-tuning, SageMaker for full customization

Bedrock Agents can orchestrate multi-step tasks automatically

Amazon Bedrock
A fully managed service that provides access to multiple foundation models (Claude, Titan, Llama, Mistral, Stable Diffusion) through a unified API. Offers fine-tuning, provisioned throughput, and agents for building production generative AI applications without managing infrastructure.
Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
A feature within SageMaker providing pre-trained models and end-to-end solutions that can be deployed with a few clicks. Offers more customization options and control but requires managing SageMaker infrastructure.
Amazon Q
A generative AI-powered assistant designed for business productivity. Integrates with enterprise data sources to provide contextual assistance for tasks like coding, document analysis, and business intelligence.
PartyRock
A free Amazon Bedrock playground that allows users to experiment with foundation models without writing code. Ideal for learning prompt engineering, testing model capabilities, and prototyping ideas before production deployment.
Amazon Bedrock
Service Type: Managed FMs as a service | Model Access: Multiple FMs (Claude, Titan, Llama, etc.) | Customization Options: Fine-tuning, continued pre-training, RAG, Agents | Infrastructure: Fully serverless | Coding Required: Yes - API/SDK integration | Best Use Case: Build production generative AI applications | Data Privacy: Data not used to train models, VPC support | Pricing Model: Per-token pricing (input/output) | Agents Support: Bedrock Agents available | Primary Purpose: Access foundation models via API for app development | Target User: Developers and ML engineers | Foundation Models Available: Claude, Llama, Titan, Jurassic, Stable Diffusion, Command | Key Capabilities: Text generation, embeddings, image generation, agents | Integration Points: Lambda, API Gateway, Kendra, OpenSearch, S3 | Exam Relevance: Very High - Core generative AI service
Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
Service Type: ML platform with pre-built models | Model Access: Open source & AWS models | Customization Options: Full model customization | Infrastructure: Requires SageMaker instances | Coding Required: Moderate to high | Best Use Case: Custom ML workflows | Data Privacy: Full control over data | Pricing Model: Compute + storage costs | Agents Support: Custom agent development | Primary Purpose: Hub for discovering, deploying, and fine-tuning foundation models and ML solutions with one-click deployment | Target User: ML engineers and data scientists who need pre-built ML solutions and foundation model access within SageMaker | Foundation Models Available: Yes — Llama, Falcon, Stability AI, Hugging Face models, plus proprietary models via SageMaker | Key Capabilities: One-click model deployment, transfer learning, built-in algorithms, solution templates, fine-tuning notebooks | Integration Points: SageMaker Studio, SageMaker Endpoints, S3, SageMaker Pipelines, SageMaker Model Registry | Exam Relevance: Know as SageMaker's model hub; compare with Bedrock (serverless API) vs JumpStart (deploy to SageMaker endpoints)
Amazon Q
Service Type: Generative AI assistant | Model Access: Bedrock-powered assistant | Customization Options: Custom data sources, application plugins | Infrastructure: Fully managed | Coding Required: No - Natural language interface | Best Use Case: Accelerate development and business workflows | Data Privacy: Enterprise-grade security, IAM integration | Pricing Model: Per-user subscription pricing | Agents Support: Built-in agent capabilities | Primary Purpose: AI assistant for business and development tasks | Target User: Business users, developers, IT professionals | Foundation Models Available: Proprietary AWS models (Titan-based) | Key Capabilities: Code generation, document analysis, AWS expertise, business insights | Integration Points: AWS Console, IDEs (VS Code), business applications | Exam Relevance: High - Know the assistant use cases
PartyRock
Service Type: Bedrock playground (free tier) | Model Access: Bedrock models (sandbox) | Customization Options: Prompt engineering only | Infrastructure: Serverless (free tier) | Coding Required: No - Visual interface | Best Use Case: Learn generative AI concepts hands-on | Data Privacy: Public service, not for sensitive data | Pricing Model: Free tier with credit-based usage | Agents Support: Not available | Primary Purpose: Learn and experiment with generative AI | Target User: Students, hobbyists, AI learners | Foundation Models Available: Curated selection (Claude, Titan, Stable Diffusion) | Key Capabilities: Prompt engineering, app building, model comparison | Integration Points: Standalone web interface | Exam Relevance: Low - Learning tool, not production
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#35 Generative AI Builder Tools on AWS
Generative AI AIF-C01 ordering aif_d2_genai_builder_tools 4 cols 5 rows
▼
Feature Amazon Bedrock SageMaker JumpStart PartyRock Amazon Q
Primary role Build generative AI apps with managed FMs Accelerate getting started with models and templates Playground / experiment quickly AI assistant experience
Best exam clue Managed FM access, RAG, agents, gen AI apps Quick-start model experimentation in SageMaker context No-code/low-friction exploration Assistant for productivity or workflow help
Typical user mindset Application builder ML builder getting started faster Experimenter or learner Knowledge worker / builder using an assistant
Production readiness signal High relevance High but model-start focused Prototype-oriented Assistant-oriented
Common trap Treating it as just a playground Confusing it with Bedrock itself Treating it as the core enterprise platform Confusing assistant UX with model-hosting platform
⚠️ Exam Tips

Bedrock is the primary platform — provides API access to foundation models from multiple providers

PartyRock is the no-code playground for experimenting with generative AI concepts

Bedrock Agents can orchestrate multi-step tasks by calling APIs and querying knowledge bases

Bedrock Knowledge Bases provide RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) without custom infrastructure

Bedrock Guardrails filter harmful content, PII, and off-topic responses from model output

Amazon Bedrock
Amazon Bedrock is the managed service for building generative AI applications with foundation models on AWS. On the exam, it is the central answer for managed foundation model access and gen AI application building.
SageMaker JumpStart
SageMaker JumpStart helps accelerate model discovery and getting started with foundation and ML models within the SageMaker ecosystem. It is the exam clue for starting quickly from model and solution templates.
PartyRock
PartyRock is an Amazon Bedrock playground-style experience for experimenting with generative AI ideas quickly. It is about exploration and rapid prototyping, not the main enterprise production control plane.
Amazon Q
Amazon Q is an AI assistant experience rather than a generic foundation model platform. When the scenario is about AI assistance in workflows or productivity contexts, Amazon Q becomes the likely fit.
Amazon Bedrock
Primary role: Build generative AI apps with managed FMs | Best exam clue: Managed FM access, RAG, agents, gen AI apps | Typical user mindset: Application builder | Production readiness signal: High relevance | Common trap: Treating it as just a playground
SageMaker JumpStart
Primary role: Accelerate getting started with models and templates | Best exam clue: Quick-start model experimentation in SageMaker context | Typical user mindset: ML builder getting started faster | Production readiness signal: High but model-start focused | Common trap: Confusing it with Bedrock itself
PartyRock
Primary role: Playground / experiment quickly | Best exam clue: No-code/low-friction exploration | Typical user mindset: Experimenter or learner | Production readiness signal: Prototype-oriented | Common trap: Treating it as the core enterprise platform
Amazon Q
Primary role: AI assistant experience | Best exam clue: Assistant for productivity or workflow help | Typical user mindset: Knowledge worker / builder using an assistant | Production readiness signal: Assistant-oriented | Common trap: Confusing assistant UX with model-hosting platform
🎯 Why This Table Matters

AIF Domain 2 explicitly names the AWS services and experiences used to build or explore generative AI. This table helps separate the managed model platform from the playground and assistant experiences.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

#36 Generative AI Components Comparison
Generative AI AIF-C01 ordering generative_ai_components_comparison 4 cols 5 rows
▼

Comparing components used in building generative AI applications on AWS

Feature RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) Vector Databases Foundation Models Agents for Bedrock
Purpose Ground LLM responses in real data Store and search embeddings Generate content (text, images, code) Execute multi-step tasks autonomously
How It Works Retrieves relevant docs + LLM generates Store vectors, similarity search Pre-trained, respond to prompts Reasoning loop + tool execution
AWS Service Bedrock Knowledge Bases OpenSearch, Aurora, DocumentDB, Kendra Bedrock model access Agents for Amazon Bedrock
Key Benefit Reduces hallucinations, up-to-date info Semantic search capability Pre-trained, adaptable Automates complex workflows
Exam Focus When to use RAG vs fine-tuning Supported services in Bedrock Model selection criteria When agents are appropriate
💡 Mnemonics

RIVFA = Retrieval augmented, Indexing vectors, Foundational models, Autonomous agents

⚠️ Exam Tips

RAG uses vector databases to provide context to LLMs

Vector databases in AWS include OpenSearch, Aurora, DocumentDB, Neptune, RDS PG

Foundation models are the core of GenAI - choose based on cost, capability, and use case

Agents can use tools and take actions - good for multi-step workflows

RAG is generally preferred over fine-tuning for adding current/organizational knowledge

RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)
An architecture that combines retrieval systems with LLMs to generate responses based on retrieved knowledge, reducing hallucinations by grounding responses in actual data.
Vector Databases
Databases optimized for storing and searching vector embeddings, enabling semantic search and similarity matching for AI applications.
Foundation Models
Large pre-trained AI models that can be adapted for multiple tasks through fine-tuning or prompt engineering, the core of generative AI.
Agents for Bedrock
AI agents that can break down complex multi-step tasks and execute actions across various systems using foundation models.
RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)
Purpose: Ground LLM responses in real data | How It Works: Retrieves relevant docs + LLM generates | AWS Service: Bedrock Knowledge Bases | Key Benefit: Reduces hallucinations, up-to-date info | Exam Focus: When to use RAG vs fine-tuning
Vector Databases
Purpose: Store and search embeddings | How It Works: Store vectors, similarity search | AWS Service: OpenSearch, Aurora, DocumentDB, Kendra | Key Benefit: Semantic search capability | Exam Focus: Supported services in Bedrock
Foundation Models
Purpose: Generate content (text, images, code) | How It Works: Pre-trained, respond to prompts | AWS Service: Bedrock model access | Key Benefit: Pre-trained, adaptable | Exam Focus: Model selection criteria
Agents for Bedrock
Purpose: Execute multi-step tasks autonomously | How It Works: Reasoning loop + tool execution | AWS Service: Agents for Amazon Bedrock | Key Benefit: Automates complex workflows | Exam Focus: When agents are appropriate
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#37 Generative AI Concepts and Techniques Comparison
Generative AI AIF-C01 ordering generative_ai_concepts_comparison 9 rows
▼

This table compares key generative AI concepts and techniques for the AIF-C01 exam, including prompt engineering approaches, RAG architecture, fine-tuning methods, and vector databases for AI applications.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Prompt Engineering is the fastest and cheapest way to customize model behavior — no training required

RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) grounds model responses in your data without changing model weights

Fine-tuning modifies model weights using your data — more expensive but better for domain-specific tasks

Pre-training builds models from scratch — requires massive compute and data, rarely done by customers

The exam expects you to choose the LEAST expensive approach that meets the requirement

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

Foundation Models 10 tables
#38 AI vs ML vs Deep Learning vs Generative AI
Foundation Models AIF-001 4 cols 6 rows
▼

This table clarifies the relationship between the four core terms. Think of them as nested layers: AI is the broadest concept, ML lives inside AI, Deep Learning lives inside ML, and Generative AI is a powerful application built on top of Deep Learning. The exam loves to test whether you know which layer a concept belongs to.

Feature Artificial Intelligence (AI) Machine Learning (ML) Deep Learning (DL) Generative AI (Gen AI)
Definition Any technique enabling machines to simulate human intelligence Systems that learn from data without explicit programming ML using multi-layer neural networks for complex pattern recognition DL models that generate new, original content (text, images, audio, code)
Relationship Parent of all — the broadest category Subset of AI Subset of ML Application/use-case built on top of DL
Data Requirement Varies — rule-based AI needs no data Moderate — labeled datasets Large — needs massive datasets Enormous — trained on internet-scale data
AWS Example Service Amazon Rekognition (pre-built AI) Amazon SageMaker (train/deploy models) SageMaker with TensorFlow/PyTorch Amazon Bedrock, Amazon Q
Output Type Decisions, classifications, actions Predictions, classifications, regressions Complex classifications (faces, speech, text) NEW content: text, images, code, audio, video
Human Analogy A thinking machine (broad) Learning from experience A brain with many neurons working together A creative artist inventing something new
⚠️ Exam Tips

AI is the broadest concept — any system that mimics human intelligence

ML is a subset of AI that learns from data without explicit programming

Deep Learning is a subset of ML that uses neural networks with many layers

Generative AI is an application of Deep Learning that creates new content

The nesting order is: AI > ML > Deep Learning > Generative AI

The exam tests whether you can classify technologies into the correct layer

Artificial Intelligence (AI)
AI is the broadest umbrella — it refers to any technique that enables machines to mimic human intelligence, including rule-based systems, logic, and learning. Every other term on this table lives inside AI.
Machine Learning (ML)
ML is a subset of AI where systems learn patterns from data without being explicitly programmed with rules. Instead of hand-coding logic, you feed the system examples and it figures out the rules itself.
Deep Learning (DL)
DL is a subset of ML that uses artificial neural networks with many layers (hence 'deep') to learn complex patterns, especially useful for images, audio, and text where traditional ML struggles.
Generative AI (Gen AI)
Gen AI is an application of Deep Learning that focuses on creating new content — text, images, audio, code — rather than just classifying or predicting. It powers tools like chatbots, image generators, and code assistants.
Artificial Intelligence (AI)
Definition: Any technique enabling machines to simulate human intelligence | Relationship: Parent of all — the broadest category | Data Requirement: Varies — rule-based AI needs no data | AWS Example Service: Amazon Rekognition (pre-built AI) | Output Type: Decisions, classifications, actions | Human Analogy: A thinking machine (broad)
Machine Learning (ML)
Definition: Systems that learn from data without explicit programming | Relationship: Subset of AI | Data Requirement: Moderate — labeled datasets | AWS Example Service: Amazon SageMaker (train/deploy models) | Output Type: Predictions, classifications, regressions | Human Analogy: Learning from experience
Deep Learning (DL)
Definition: ML using multi-layer neural networks for complex pattern recognition | Relationship: Subset of ML | Data Requirement: Large — needs massive datasets | AWS Example Service: SageMaker with TensorFlow/PyTorch | Output Type: Complex classifications (faces, speech, text) | Human Analogy: A brain with many neurons working together
Generative AI (Gen AI)
Definition: DL models that generate new, original content (text, images, audio, code) | Relationship: Application/use-case built on top of DL | Data Requirement: Enormous — trained on internet-scale data | AWS Example Service: Amazon Bedrock, Amazon Q | Output Type: NEW content: text, images, code, audio, video | Human Analogy: A creative artist inventing something new
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#39 FM Evaluation Metrics — ROUGE vs BLEU vs BERTScore
Foundation Models AIF-C01 ordering AIF-008 3 cols 4 rows
▼

When you deploy a foundation model, how do you measure if it is performing well? These three metrics are the standard evaluation tools the exam tests. They each have different strengths: ROUGE is for summarization, BLEU is for translation, and BERTScore is for deeper semantic quality.

Feature ROUGE BLEU BERTScore
Primary Use Text Summarization evaluation Machine Translation evaluation General text quality / semantic similarity
Measures Overlap of n-grams between generated and reference text (recall-focused) Precision of n-gram matches between generated and reference translation Semantic similarity using contextual embeddings from BERT
Understands Meaning? No — only word/phrase overlap No — only exact n-gram matches Yes — captures paraphrases and semantic equivalence
Score Range 0 to 1 (higher = better) 0 to 1 (higher = better) Usually 0.8-1.0 (higher = better semantic match)
⚠️ Exam Tips

ROUGE measures recall — how much of the reference text appears in the generated text (used for summarization)

BLEU measures precision — how much of the generated text matches the reference (used for translation)

BERTScore uses embeddings for semantic similarity — catches paraphrases that word-overlap metrics miss

Perplexity measures how 'surprised' a model is — lower perplexity means better language modeling

For the exam, ROUGE = summarization, BLEU = translation, human evaluation = subjective quality

ROUGE
ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) measures how much of the reference text appears in the model's output — it prioritizes RECALL. It is the standard metric for evaluating TEXT SUMMARIZATION tasks.
BLEU
BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) measures how closely the model's output matches a reference translation — it prioritizes PRECISION of n-gram matches. It is the standard metric for evaluating MACHINE TRANSLATION quality.
BERTScore
BERTScore uses a BERT language model to measure the SEMANTIC SIMILARITY between generated text and reference text — it understands meaning, not just exact word matches. It is more sophisticated and better at catching paraphrasing as correct.
ROUGE
Primary Use: Text Summarization evaluation | Measures: Overlap of n-grams between generated and reference text (recall-focused) | Understands Meaning?: No — only word/phrase overlap | Score Range: 0 to 1 (higher = better)
BLEU
Primary Use: Machine Translation evaluation | Measures: Precision of n-gram matches between generated and reference translation | Understands Meaning?: No — only exact n-gram matches | Score Range: 0 to 1 (higher = better)
BERTScore
Primary Use: General text quality / semantic similarity | Measures: Semantic similarity using contextual embeddings from BERT | Understands Meaning?: Yes — captures paraphrases and semantic equivalence | Score Range: Usually 0.8-1.0 (higher = better semantic match)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#40 Foundation Model Customization Approaches
Foundation Models AIF-C01 ordering aif_d3_customization_approaches 4 cols 17 rows
▼

Comparison of methods for customizing foundation models on AWS

Comparing approaches to customize foundation models - Domain 3 (Applications of Foundation Models)

Feature Prompt Engineering / In-Context Learning RAG Fine-Tuning Pre-Training or Continuous Pre-Training
Relative effort Lowest Low to moderate Moderate to high Highest
Changes model weights? No - purely input modification No - external knowledge accessed at inference Yes - model weights updated for task Yes - weights updated with domain knowledge
Best exam clue Improve output via better prompts/examples Need fresh or proprietary knowledge grounding Need stronger domain/task adaptation Need major domain learning at large scale
Cost tendency Lowest Usually lower than tuning-heavy options Higher Highest
Great for Dynamic knowledge, Q&A, RAG Knowledge assistants and grounded answers Specialized behavior consistency Large-scale custom model capability building
Common trap Expecting it to solve missing knowledge freshness by itself Confusing it with weight updates Using it before trying simpler options Choosing it when business need does not justify the cost
Training Required No training — uses the model as-is with carefully crafted prompts and examples No model training — requires building a knowledge base/vector store for retrieval Task-specific examples (hundreds) Yes — extensive training on large domain-specific corpora to adapt model weights
Cost Level Lowest — only pay for inference tokens; no training compute costs Low to moderate — costs for vector database, embedding generation, and retrieval infrastructure Moderate Highest — massive compute costs for training; requires large GPU clusters over days/weeks
Time to Deploy Minutes — immediate iteration on prompts with no infrastructure setup Hours to days — time to build knowledge base, generate embeddings, and configure retrieval pipeline Days to weeks Weeks to months — requires large dataset curation, extensive training runs, and thorough evaluation
Knowledge Freshness Limited to context window — can include recent info in prompts but constrained by token limits Very fresh — knowledge base can be updated independently without retraining the model Requires retraining for updates Stale after training — knowledge frozen at training cutoff; requires retraining to update
Customization Depth Surface-level — influences output style and format but cannot change model's core knowledge or behavior Moderate — augments model with external knowledge but doesn't change model behavior or reasoning Task behavior Deepest — fundamentally changes model's knowledge and capabilities at the weight level
AWS Service Amazon Bedrock (prompt management), SageMaker JumpStart, any model API Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases, Amazon Kendra, OpenSearch with vector search Bedrock Fine-tuning, SageMaker Amazon SageMaker (custom training jobs with large GPU instances)
Data Requirements None — only well-crafted prompt text; optional few-shot examples within context window Document corpus for knowledge base — PDFs, web pages, databases indexed into vector store Hundreds to thousands of labeled examples in instruction-response or domain-specific format Massive — billions of tokens of domain-specific text data for meaningful adaptation
Compute Cost Minimal — only inference cost per token; no training compute Low — embedding generation and vector search; inference cost includes retrieval overhead Moderate — GPU hours for training; Bedrock fine-tuning abstracts infrastructure Very high — large GPU clusters (p4d/p5 instances) running for days to weeks
Latency Low — standard inference latency; longer prompts slightly increase first-token time Moderate — adds retrieval step (vector search) before inference; typically 100-500ms overhead Low — same as base model inference; fine-tuned weights are baked into the model Low — same as base model inference once training is complete
Best Use Case Quick prototyping, general tasks, format control, when training data is unavailable Enterprise knowledge Q&A, documentation search, when source attribution and freshness matter Domain-specific tone/style, specialized vocabulary, consistent output format, compliance requirements New domain with specialized language (medical, legal, scientific) where base model lacks knowledge
Hallucination Risk Higher — model relies on internal knowledge; no external grounding to verify facts Lower — retrieval provides factual grounding; model can cite sources; but retrieval quality matters Moderate — model learns patterns from training data but can still hallucinate on unseen topics Similar to base model — large-scale training can reduce domain hallucinations but not eliminate them
💡 Mnemonics

RAG-FT-CPT-FPT: RAG for context, Fine-Tuning for tasks, Continued Pre-Training for domains, Full Pre-Training for new models

Need to add knowledge without training? RAG. Need better task performance? Fine-tune. Need domain expertise? Continued pre-training. Building from scratch? Full pre-training.

P.R.F.C - Prompt is quick, Retrieval grounds, Fine-tune fixes, Continuous covers domains

⚠️ Exam Tips

RAG is most cost-effective for adding new knowledge to models

Fine-tuning changes model behavior, not knowledge

RAG allows real-time knowledge updates by updating documents

Fine-tuning requires hundreds to thousands of examples

Continued pre-training is for domain adaptation (legal, medical, etc.)

Full pre-training is rarely needed - use existing foundation models

Start with prompt engineering before more expensive methods

RAG is preferred for knowledge-intensive applications needing current information

Fine-tuning is for when you need consistent task-specific behavior

Cost increases significantly from prompt → RAG → fine-tuning → continuous pre-training

Hybrid approaches combining multiple methods are common in production

Prompt Engineering / In-Context Learning
A no-training approach that enhances model responses by retrieving relevant context from a knowledge base at inference time. Most cost-effective for adding new knowledge, enabling real-time updates by simply updating the source documents without modifying the model.
RAG
RAG adds external knowledge retrieval so the model can answer using fresher or domain-specific information without fully retraining the model. It is often the sweet spot for enterprise knowledge applications.
Fine-Tuning
A supervised learning process that adapts a pre-trained model to specific tasks using labeled examples. Improves model behavior for particular use cases (style, format, domain tasks) but doesn't update the model's core knowledge base.
Pre-Training or Continuous Pre-Training
The complete process of training a foundation model from scratch on massive datasets. Typically requires billions of tokens and significant compute resources. Most organizations should use pre-trained models rather than training from scratch.
Prompt Engineering / In-Context Learning
