Relative effort: Lowest | Changes model weights?: No - purely input modification | Best exam clue: Improve output via better prompts/examples | Cost tendency: Lowest | Great for: Dynamic knowledge, Q&A, RAG | Common trap: Expecting it to solve missing knowledge freshness by itself | Training Required: No training — uses the model as-is with carefully crafted prompts and examples | Cost Level: Lowest — only pay for inference tokens; no training compute costs | Time to Deploy: Minutes — immediate iteration on prompts with no infrastructure setup | Knowledge Freshness: Limited to context window — can include recent info in prompts but constrained by token limits | Customization Depth: Surface-level — influences output style and format but cannot change model's core knowledge or behavior | AWS Service: Amazon Bedrock (prompt management), SageMaker JumpStart, any model API | Data Requirements: None — only well-crafted prompt text; optional few-shot examples within context window | Compute Cost: Minimal — only inference cost per token; no training compute | Latency: Low — standard inference latency; longer prompts slightly increase first-token time | Best Use Case: Quick prototyping, general tasks, format control, when training data is unavailable | Hallucination Risk: Higher — model relies on internal knowledge; no external grounding to verify facts
RAG
Relative effort: Low to moderate | Changes model weights?: No - external knowledge accessed at inference | Best exam clue: Need fresh or proprietary knowledge grounding | Cost tendency: Usually lower than tuning-heavy options | Great for: Knowledge assistants and grounded answers | Common trap: Confusing it with weight updates | Training Required: No model training — requires building a knowledge base/vector store for retrieval | Cost Level: Low to moderate — costs for vector database, embedding generation, and retrieval infrastructure | Time to Deploy: Hours to days — time to build knowledge base, generate embeddings, and configure retrieval pipeline | Knowledge Freshness: Very fresh — knowledge base can be updated independently without retraining the model | Customization Depth: Moderate — augments model with external knowledge but doesn't change model behavior or reasoning | AWS Service: Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases, Amazon Kendra, OpenSearch with vector search | Data Requirements: Document corpus for knowledge base — PDFs, web pages, databases indexed into vector store | Compute Cost: Low — embedding generation and vector search; inference cost includes retrieval overhead | Latency: Moderate — adds retrieval step (vector search) before inference; typically 100-500ms overhead | Best Use Case: Enterprise knowledge Q&A, documentation search, when source attribution and freshness matter | Hallucination Risk: Lower — retrieval provides factual grounding; model can cite sources; but retrieval quality matters
Fine-Tuning
Relative effort: Moderate to high | Changes model weights?: Yes - model weights updated for task | Best exam clue: Need stronger domain/task adaptation | Cost tendency: Higher | Great for: Specialized behavior consistency | Common trap: Using it before trying simpler options | Training Required: Task-specific examples (hundreds) | Cost Level: Moderate | Time to Deploy: Days to weeks | Knowledge Freshness: Requires retraining for updates | Customization Depth: Task behavior | AWS Service: Bedrock Fine-tuning, SageMaker | Data Requirements: Hundreds to thousands of labeled examples in instruction-response or domain-specific format | Compute Cost: Moderate — GPU hours for training; Bedrock fine-tuning abstracts infrastructure | Latency: Low — same as base model inference; fine-tuned weights are baked into the model | Best Use Case: Domain-specific tone/style, specialized vocabulary, consistent output format, compliance requirements | Hallucination Risk: Moderate — model learns patterns from training data but can still hallucinate on unseen topics
Pre-Training or Continuous Pre-Training
Relative effort: Highest | Changes model weights?: Yes - weights updated with domain knowledge | Best exam clue: Need major domain learning at large scale | Cost tendency: Highest | Great for: Large-scale custom model capability building | Common trap: Choosing it when business need does not justify the cost | Training Required: Yes — extensive training on large domain-specific corpora to adapt model weights | Cost Level: Highest — massive compute costs for training; requires large GPU clusters over days/weeks | Time to Deploy: Weeks to months — requires large dataset curation, extensive training runs, and thorough evaluation | Knowledge Freshness: Stale after training — knowledge frozen at training cutoff; requires retraining to update | Customization Depth: Deepest — fundamentally changes model's knowledge and capabilities at the weight level | AWS Service: Amazon SageMaker (custom training jobs with large GPU instances) | Data Requirements: Massive — billions of tokens of domain-specific text data for meaningful adaptation | Compute Cost: Very high — large GPU clusters (p4d/p5 instances) running for days to weeks | Latency: Low — same as base model inference once training is complete | Best Use Case: New domain with specialized language (medical, legal, scientific) where base model lacks knowledge | Hallucination Risk: Similar to base model — large-scale training can reduce domain hallucinations but not eliminate them
🎯 Why This Table Matters

AIF Domain 3 compares cost and capability tradeoffs across prompting, retrieval, tuning, and pre-training approaches. This table gives the exam-style ladder from lightest to heaviest customization.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#41 Foundation Model Inference Parameters Comparison
Foundation Models AIF-C01 ordering inference_parameters_comparison 3 cols 7 rows
▼

This table compares key inference parameters for controlling foundation model outputs. Temperature controls randomness, Top P (nucleus sampling) limits vocabulary based on cumulative probability, and Top K restricts the number of token choices considered.

Feature Temperature Top P (Nucleus Sampling) Top K
What It Controls Overall randomness/creativity Vocabulary breadth based on probability Number of token candidates
Range 0.0 to 1.0 (or 2.0) 0.0 to 1.0 1 to 500+
Low Value Effect More deterministic, focused outputs Smaller vocabulary, more focused Fewer options, more predictable
High Value Effect More creative, diverse outputs Larger vocabulary, more variety More options, more creative
Best For Factual Q&A (low), Creative writing (high) Balanced creativity and coherence Controlling specific vocabulary diversity
How It Works Scales logits before softmax Dynamic cutoff based on cumulative probability Fixed cutoff at K tokens
Example 0.1 for code, 0.7 for chat, 1.0 for brainstorming 0.9 allows tokens summing to 90% probability Top K=40 considers only top 40 tokens
⚠️ Exam Tips

Temperature controls randomness — higher temperature = more creative but less predictable output

Top-p (nucleus sampling) limits token selection to the most probable tokens that sum to p

Top-k limits selection to the k most probable tokens — simpler than top-p but less flexible

Max tokens controls output length — increasing it allows longer responses but costs more

For factual/deterministic responses, use low temperature (0.0-0.3); for creative tasks, use higher (0.7-1.0)

Temperature
Controls the randomness and creativity of model outputs by adjusting the probability distribution - lower values produce more deterministic responses, higher values more creative ones.
Top P (Nucleus Sampling)
Limits token selection to the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds the threshold P, providing dynamic vocabulary restriction based on context.
Top K
Restricts the model to consider only the K most likely next tokens, providing a fixed-size candidate pool for the next word prediction.
Temperature
What It Controls: Overall randomness/creativity | Range: 0.0 to 1.0 (or 2.0) | Low Value Effect: More deterministic, focused outputs | High Value Effect: More creative, diverse outputs | Best For: Factual Q&A (low), Creative writing (high) | How It Works: Scales logits before softmax | Example: 0.1 for code, 0.7 for chat, 1.0 for brainstorming
Top P (Nucleus Sampling)
What It Controls: Vocabulary breadth based on probability | Range: 0.0 to 1.0 | Low Value Effect: Smaller vocabulary, more focused | High Value Effect: Larger vocabulary, more variety | Best For: Balanced creativity and coherence | How It Works: Dynamic cutoff based on cumulative probability | Example: 0.9 allows tokens summing to 90% probability
Top K
What It Controls: Number of token candidates | Range: 1 to 500+ | Low Value Effect: Fewer options, more predictable | High Value Effect: More options, more creative | Best For: Controlling specific vocabulary diversity | How It Works: Fixed cutoff at K tokens | Example: Top K=40 considers only top 40 tokens
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#42 Foundation Model Selection Criteria Comparison
Foundation Models AIF-C01 ordering fm_selection_comparison 4 cols 7 rows
▼

This table compares popular foundation models available on Amazon Bedrock. Each model has different strengths, context windows, license types, and optimal use cases for building generative AI applications.

Feature Claude (Anthropic) Llama (Meta) Mistral AI Amazon Nova
Max Context Window Up to 200K tokens Up to 128K tokens Up to 128K tokens Up to 128K tokens
License Type Proprietary (commercial license) Open source (Llama 3.1: custom license) Open source (Apache 2.0 for some models) Proprietary (AWS-owned)
Key Strengths Reasoning, safety, long context, analysis Open source, flexibility, various sizes Efficiency, multilingual, coding AWS integration, cost-effective, latency
Best For Complex reasoning, document analysis, safety-critical Customization, fine-tuning, on-premise deployment European deployment, multilingual apps, coding Cost-sensitive workloads, AWS-native apps
Modality Text, Vision (some versions) Text, Vision (some versions) Text Text, Vision (Micro to Pro)
Model Sizes Haiku, Sonnet, Opus 8B, 70B, 405B parameters Various sizes including Large 2 Micro, Lite, Pro, Canvas, Reel
RLHF Training Yes (Constitutional AI) Yes Yes Yes
⚠️ Exam Tips

Consider task type first — text generation, code, multimodal, or embeddings

Larger models are more capable but slower and more expensive for inference

Latency requirements may rule out the largest models for real-time applications

Fine-tuning availability varies by model — not all Bedrock models support fine-tuning

Evaluate models on YOUR data before committing — benchmarks don’t always predict domain performance

Claude (Anthropic)
Advanced LLM known for safety, helpfulness, and honesty with strong reasoning capabilities, large context windows, and enterprise-grade reliability.
Llama (Meta)
Open-source LLM family offering strong performance with permissive licensing, available in various sizes for different deployment needs.
Mistral AI
European AI company providing efficient, high-performing models with strong multilingual capabilities and coding proficiency.
Amazon Nova
Amazon's own foundation model family optimized for AWS integration, cost-effectiveness, and enterprise use cases with competitive performance.
Claude (Anthropic)
Max Context Window: Up to 200K tokens | License Type: Proprietary (commercial license) | Key Strengths: Reasoning, safety, long context, analysis | Best For: Complex reasoning, document analysis, safety-critical | Modality: Text, Vision (some versions) | Model Sizes: Haiku, Sonnet, Opus | RLHF Training: Yes (Constitutional AI)
Llama (Meta)
Max Context Window: Up to 128K tokens | License Type: Open source (Llama 3.1: custom license) | Key Strengths: Open source, flexibility, various sizes | Best For: Customization, fine-tuning, on-premise deployment | Modality: Text, Vision (some versions) | Model Sizes: 8B, 70B, 405B parameters | RLHF Training: Yes
Mistral AI
Max Context Window: Up to 128K tokens | License Type: Open source (Apache 2.0 for some models) | Key Strengths: Efficiency, multilingual, coding | Best For: European deployment, multilingual apps, coding | Modality: Text | Model Sizes: Various sizes including Large 2 | RLHF Training: Yes
Amazon Nova
Max Context Window: Up to 128K tokens | License Type: Proprietary (AWS-owned) | Key Strengths: AWS integration, cost-effective, latency | Best For: Cost-sensitive workloads, AWS-native apps | Modality: Text, Vision (Micro to Pro) | Model Sizes: Micro, Lite, Pro, Canvas, Reel | RLHF Training: Yes
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#43 Interpretability vs Explainability in AI Comparison
Foundation Models AIF-C01 ordering interpretability_explainability_comparison 2 cols 8 rows
▼

This table compares two key concepts in responsible AI. Interpretability refers to the ability to understand how a model makes decisions inherently, while explainability involves techniques to explain model decisions post-hoc, especially for complex models.

Feature Interpretability Explainability
Definition Inherent understandability of the model itself Post-hoc explanations of model decisions
Approach Model is transparent by design External methods explain black-box models
Model Types Decision trees, linear regression, rule-based systems Deep learning, ensemble methods, complex models
When to Use Regulated industries, high-stakes decisions, auditing Complex models where performance trumps simplicity
AWS Tools SageMaker Clarify (model insights) SageMaker Clarify (SHAP, LIME), SHAP library
Techniques Feature importance, decision paths, rule extraction SHAP, LIME, attention visualization, counterfactuals
Trade-off May sacrifice some predictive performance Adds overhead to complex, high-performing models
Examples Loan approval rules, medical diagnosis trees Explaining neural network image classifications
⚠️ Exam Tips

Interpretability = understanding HOW a model works internally (white-box models)

Explainability = understanding WHY a model made a specific prediction (can apply to black-box models)

SageMaker Clarify provides SHAP values for feature-level explainability

Simpler models (linear regression, decision trees) are inherently more interpretable

The exam tests whether you know when to use Clarify vs Guardrails vs Model Monitor

Interpretability
The degree to which a human can understand the cause of a decision made by an AI model, focusing on transparent, inherently understandable model structures.
Explainability
Techniques and methods used to explain or interpret the decisions of complex AI models after they have made predictions, often using external tools.
Interpretability
Definition: Inherent understandability of the model itself | Approach: Model is transparent by design | Model Types: Decision trees, linear regression, rule-based systems | When to Use: Regulated industries, high-stakes decisions, auditing | AWS Tools: SageMaker Clarify (model insights) | Techniques: Feature importance, decision paths, rule extraction | Trade-off: May sacrifice some predictive performance | Examples: Loan approval rules, medical diagnosis trees
Explainability
Definition: Post-hoc explanations of model decisions | Approach: External methods explain black-box models | Model Types: Deep learning, ensemble methods, complex models | When to Use: Complex models where performance trumps simplicity | AWS Tools: SageMaker Clarify (SHAP, LIME), SHAP library | Techniques: SHAP, LIME, attention visualization, counterfactuals | Trade-off: Adds overhead to complex, high-performing models | Examples: Explaining neural network image classifications
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#44 Learning Paradigms Comparison
Foundation Models aif_d1_learning_paradigms 3 cols 6 rows
▼
Feature Supervised Learning Unsupervised Learning Reinforcement Learning
Training signal Labeled examples No labels Rewards / penalties
Best exam clue Predict a known target Find groups or hidden patterns Learn actions by trial and reward
Typical tasks Classification, regression Clustering, segmentation Sequential decision making
Needs known answers? Yes No No fixed labels; needs reward framework
Business-style example Fraud classification or demand prediction Customer grouping or anomaly pattern discovery Adaptive strategy optimization
Common trap Confusing it with clustering Expecting direct labeled prediction Using the term for any model that improves over time
⚠️ Exam Tips

Supervised learning requires labeled data — the model learns from input-output pairs

Unsupervised learning finds patterns in unlabeled data — clustering, dimensionality reduction

Reinforcement learning uses rewards/penalties — the agent learns through trial and error

Semi-supervised learning combines small labeled dataset with large unlabeled dataset

For the exam: classification/regression = supervised, clustering = unsupervised, robotics/game AI = reinforcement

Supervised Learning
Supervised learning uses labeled data, so the model learns from examples with known answers. Exam clues include classification, regression, and prediction based on historical labeled outcomes.
Unsupervised Learning
Unsupervised learning works without labels and looks for hidden structure or grouping in data. Clustering and pattern discovery are the classic exam signals.
Reinforcement Learning
Reinforcement learning improves through reward feedback from actions taken in an environment. It appears when the system learns strategies over time rather than from a static labeled dataset.
Supervised Learning
Training signal: Labeled examples | Best exam clue: Predict a known target | Typical tasks: Classification, regression | Needs known answers?: Yes | Business-style example: Fraud classification or demand prediction | Common trap: Confusing it with clustering
Unsupervised Learning
Training signal: No labels | Best exam clue: Find groups or hidden patterns | Typical tasks: Clustering, segmentation | Needs known answers?: No | Business-style example: Customer grouping or anomaly pattern discovery | Common trap: Expecting direct labeled prediction
Reinforcement Learning
Training signal: Rewards / penalties | Best exam clue: Learn actions by trial and reward | Typical tasks: Sequential decision making | Needs known answers?: No fixed labels; needs reward framework | Business-style example: Adaptive strategy optimization | Common trap: Using the term for any model that improves over time
🎯 Why This Table Matters

AIF Domain 1 explicitly tests supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning. This table gives you the clean mental model behind the jargon so you can map use cases quickly.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

#45 ML Algorithm Categories for AIF-C01
Foundation Models ml_algorithm_categories_for_aifc01 3 cols 6 rows
▼

Comparison of machine learning algorithm types and their AWS implementations

Feature Supervised Learning Unsupervised Learning Reinforcement Learning
Training Data Labeled data (features + correct answers) Unlabeled data (features only) Environment with reward signals (no explicit labels)
Output Type Predictions (classification labels or continuous values) Discovered patterns (clusters, associations, dimensions) Actions (sequence of decisions maximizing rewards)
Example Algorithms Linear regression, decision trees, neural networks, SVM K-means clustering, hierarchical clustering, PCA, association rules Q-learning, deep Q-networks (DQN), policy gradients
AWS Services SageMaker built-in algorithms, Comprehend, Rekognition, custom training SageMaker (K-means, PCA), Personalize clustering SageMaker RL (supports TensorFlow, PyTorch, MXNet)
Use Cases Spam detection, image classification, demand forecasting, fraud detection Customer segmentation, anomaly detection, recommendation systems Game AI, robotics, autonomous vehicles, resource optimization
Common AWS Example Comprehend uses supervised learning for sentiment analysis Personalize uses clustering for user segmentation AWS DeepRacer uses RL for autonomous racing
💡 Mnemonics

S.U.R - Supervised has Solutions (labeled), Unsupervised discovers, Reinforcement rewards

⚠️ Exam Tips

For AIF-C01: Regression predicts continuous values; Classification predicts categories

Clustering groups similar data points without predefined labels

RAG is not an ML algorithm - it's a prompting technique using retrieval

Supervised ML requires labeled data; unlabeled data uses unsupervised methods

Reinforcement learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is used to align LLMs with human preferences

Supervised Learning
ML approach where models learn from labeled training data to predict outcomes, with both input features and desired outputs provided during training.
Unsupervised Learning
ML approach where models identify patterns in data without labeled responses, discovering hidden structures and groupings in datasets.
Reinforcement Learning
ML approach where agents learn optimal actions through trial and error, receiving rewards or penalties based on decisions in an environment.
Supervised Learning
Training Data: Labeled data (features + correct answers) | Output Type: Predictions (classification labels or continuous values) | Example Algorithms: Linear regression, decision trees, neural networks, SVM | AWS Services: SageMaker built-in algorithms, Comprehend, Rekognition, custom training | Use Cases: Spam detection, image classification, demand forecasting, fraud detection | Common AWS Example: Comprehend uses supervised learning for sentiment analysis
Unsupervised Learning
Training Data: Unlabeled data (features only) | Output Type: Discovered patterns (clusters, associations, dimensions) | Example Algorithms: K-means clustering, hierarchical clustering, PCA, association rules | AWS Services: SageMaker (K-means, PCA), Personalize clustering | Use Cases: Customer segmentation, anomaly detection, recommendation systems | Common AWS Example: Personalize uses clustering for user segmentation
Reinforcement Learning
Training Data: Environment with reward signals (no explicit labels) | Output Type: Actions (sequence of decisions maximizing rewards) | Example Algorithms: Q-learning, deep Q-networks (DQN), policy gradients | AWS Services: SageMaker RL (supports TensorFlow, PyTorch, MXNet) | Use Cases: Game AI, robotics, autonomous vehicles, resource optimization | Common AWS Example: AWS DeepRacer uses RL for autonomous racing
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#46 Machine Learning Techniques Comparison
Foundation Models AIF-001_2 3 cols 7 rows
▼

Comparing primary ML techniques for different problem types - critical for Domain 1 (Fundamentals of AI and ML)

Feature Regression Classification Clustering
Learning Type Supervised Supervised Unsupervised
Output Type Continuous numerical values Discrete categories/labels No predefined labels (groups)
Use Case Examples Price prediction, sales forecasting, temperature prediction Spam detection, image recognition, fraud detection Customer segmentation, anomaly detection, document grouping
Common Algorithms Linear Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forest, XGBoost Logistic Regression, SVM, Random Forest, Neural Networks K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical Clustering
Training Data Required Labeled continuous values Labeled categories No labels needed
Evaluation Metrics MSE, RMSE, MAE, R² Score Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, AUC-ROC Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin Index, Inertia
AWS Services Amazon SageMaker, Amazon Forecast Amazon SageMaker, Amazon Rekognition, Amazon Comprehend Amazon SageMaker, Amazon Personalize
💡 Mnemonics

RCC - Regression = Real numbers, Classification = Categories, Clustering = Clusters (no labels)

Need to predict a NUMBER? Use Regression. Need to predict a CATEGORY? Use Classification. Don't have labels? Use Clustering.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Regression problems always have continuous output (e.g., predicting house prices)

Classification problems have discrete outputs (binary or multi-class)

Clustering is unsupervised - no historical labels required

If the question mentions 'predicting a value' - think regression

If the question mentions 'identifying type/category' - think classification

If the question mentions 'finding patterns/groups' without labels - think clustering

Regression
A supervised learning technique used to predict continuous numerical outcomes based on input features. It establishes relationships between dependent and independent variables to forecast future values like sales, prices, or quantities.
Classification
A supervised learning method that categorizes data into predefined classes or labels. It learns from labeled training data to predict the category of new, unseen observations, commonly used for binary decisions (spam/not spam) or multi-class problems.
Clustering
An unsupervised learning technique that groups similar data points together without predefined labels. It discovers inherent patterns or structures in data, useful for customer segmentation, anomaly detection, and exploratory data analysis.
Regression
Learning Type: Supervised | Output Type: Continuous numerical values | Use Case Examples: Price prediction, sales forecasting, temperature prediction | Common Algorithms: Linear Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forest, XGBoost | Training Data Required: Labeled continuous values | Evaluation Metrics: MSE, RMSE, MAE, R² Score | AWS Services: Amazon SageMaker, Amazon Forecast
Classification
Learning Type: Supervised | Output Type: Discrete categories/labels | Use Case Examples: Spam detection, image recognition, fraud detection | Common Algorithms: Logistic Regression, SVM, Random Forest, Neural Networks | Training Data Required: Labeled categories | Evaluation Metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, AUC-ROC | AWS Services: Amazon SageMaker, Amazon Rekognition, Amazon Comprehend
Clustering
Learning Type: Unsupervised | Output Type: No predefined labels (groups) | Use Case Examples: Customer segmentation, anomaly detection, document grouping | Common Algorithms: K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical Clustering | Training Data Required: No labels needed | Evaluation Metrics: Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin Index, Inertia | AWS Services: Amazon SageMaker, Amazon Personalize
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json

#47 Supervised vs Unsupervised vs Reinforcement Learning
Foundation Models AIF-002 3 cols 6 rows
▼

These three learning paradigms describe HOW a model learns from data. The exam will present a business scenario and ask you to identify which type of ML is appropriate. The key differentiator is the data: does it have labels? If yes — supervised. If no labels — unsupervised. If the system learns by trial and reward — reinforcement.

Feature Supervised Learning Unsupervised Learning Reinforcement Learning
Data Type Labeled data (input + correct output) Unlabeled data (inputs only) No dataset — agent interacts with environment
Goal Learn to map inputs to known outputs Discover hidden patterns or groupings Maximize cumulative reward over time
Techniques Regression, Classification Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction, Anomaly Detection Q-learning, Policy Gradient, RLHF
AWS Example Use Case Amazon Fraud Detector (fraud/not fraud), Amazon Rekognition (face identification) Amazon Personalize (recommendation groupings), customer segmentation RLHF in Amazon Bedrock fine-tuning, robotics optimization
Business Example Email spam detection (spam/not spam labels provided) Customer segmentation for marketing Self-driving car learns driving by reward signals
Feedback Immediate — correct label known at training time None — model self-organizes Delayed reward signal from environment
⚠️ Exam Tips

Supervised = labeled data + known outputs (classification, regression)

Unsupervised = unlabeled data + discovering patterns (clustering, anomaly detection)

Reinforcement = agent + environment + reward signal (game AI, robotics, recommendation systems)

SageMaker supports all three paradigms with built-in algorithms

Most AWS managed AI services use supervised learning under the hood but hide the complexity

Supervised Learning
The model is trained on labeled data — inputs paired with correct outputs. Think of it as learning with a teacher who corrects you: you see a dog photo labeled 'dog,' and over thousands of examples, the model learns to recognize dogs.
Unsupervised Learning
The model finds patterns in unlabeled data on its own — no correct answers are provided. It discovers hidden structure, like grouping customers by purchasing behavior without being told what the groups should be.
Reinforcement Learning
The model learns by taking actions in an environment and receiving rewards or penalties, like training a dog with treats. It maximizes long-term rewards through trial and error — used in robotics, games, and recommendation systems.
Supervised Learning
Data Type: Labeled data (input + correct output) | Goal: Learn to map inputs to known outputs | Techniques: Regression, Classification | AWS Example Use Case: Amazon Fraud Detector (fraud/not fraud), Amazon Rekognition (face identification) | Business Example: Email spam detection (spam/not spam labels provided) | Feedback: Immediate — correct label known at training time
Unsupervised Learning
Data Type: Unlabeled data (inputs only) | Goal: Discover hidden patterns or groupings | Techniques: Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction, Anomaly Detection | AWS Example Use Case: Amazon Personalize (recommendation groupings), customer segmentation | Business Example: Customer segmentation for marketing | Feedback: None — model self-organizes
Reinforcement Learning
Data Type: No dataset — agent interacts with environment | Goal: Maximize cumulative reward over time | Techniques: Q-learning, Policy Gradient, RLHF | AWS Example Use Case: RLHF in Amazon Bedrock fine-tuning, robotics optimization | Business Example: Self-driving car learns driving by reward signals | Feedback: Delayed reward signal from environment
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

RAG & Vectors 5 tables
#48 AWS Vector Database Services for RAG Applications
RAG & Vectors AIF-C01 ordering AIF-002_2 4 cols 8 rows
▼

Comparing AWS vector database options for Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) - critical for Domain 3 (Applications of Foundation Models)

Feature Amazon OpenSearch Service Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL Amazon Neptune Amazon DocumentDB
Primary Use Case Vector search + full-text search + analytics Relational + vector similarity search Graph database + vector search Document database + vector search
Vector Engine Native vector engine (k-NN plugin) pgvector extension Vector search for graph data Vector search extension
Scalability Auto-scaling, serverless option Aurora Serverless v2, read replicas Auto-scaling storage Auto-scaling storage
Max Dimensions 16,000 dimensions 2,000 dimensions (pgvector) 16,000 dimensions 2,048 dimensions
Query Performance Sub-second, approximate k-NN Exact and approximate search Graph + vector hybrid queries Document + vector hybrid queries
Integration with Bedrock Knowledge Bases native support Via RAG custom implementation Graph-enhanced RAG MongoDB-compatible RAG
Best For General RAG with search requirements Existing Aurora workloads needing vectors Knowledge graphs with vector search Document workloads with vector needs
Pricing Model Per hour + storage + requests Per hour + I/O + storage Per hour + storage + requests Per hour + I/O + storage
💡 Mnemonics

OPEN-AUR-NEP-DOC: OpenSearch for Everything, Aurora for Relational, Neptune for Graphs, DocumentDB for Documents

Need full-text search with vectors? OpenSearch. Already using PostgreSQL? Aurora + pgvector. Building knowledge graphs? Neptune. MongoDB compatibility needed? DocumentDB.

⚠️ Exam Tips

OpenSearch Service is the most common choice for RAG with Knowledge Bases for Bedrock

pgvector is the extension name for vector support in PostgreSQL/Aurora

Neptune is ideal when you need to combine graph relationships with vector similarity

OpenSearch supports the highest dimension count (16,000)

Aurora Serverless v2 can auto-scale for variable RAG workloads

For RAG: Embedding model creates vectors → Vector DB stores them → Retrieved for LLM context

Amazon OpenSearch Service
A fully managed service providing vector database capabilities through its k-NN plugin, ideal for RAG applications requiring both vector similarity search and traditional full-text search. Supports up to 16,000 dimensions with approximate nearest neighbor algorithms for fast retrieval.
Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL
A PostgreSQL-compatible relational database with the pgvector extension for vector similarity search. Best for organizations with existing Aurora workloads that need to add vector capabilities without managing a separate vector database.
Amazon Neptune
A fully managed graph database service with vector search capabilities, enabling hybrid queries that combine graph traversals with vector similarity. Ideal for knowledge graphs where entity relationships enhance retrieval context.
Amazon DocumentDB
A MongoDB-compatible document database with vector search capabilities, suitable for applications already using document-based data models that need to add semantic search functionality.
Amazon OpenSearch Service
Primary Use Case: Vector search + full-text search + analytics | Vector Engine: Native vector engine (k-NN plugin) | Scalability: Auto-scaling, serverless option | Max Dimensions: 16,000 dimensions | Query Performance: Sub-second, approximate k-NN | Integration with Bedrock: Knowledge Bases native support | Best For: General RAG with search requirements | Pricing Model: Per hour + storage + requests
Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL
Primary Use Case: Relational + vector similarity search | Vector Engine: pgvector extension | Scalability: Aurora Serverless v2, read replicas | Max Dimensions: 2,000 dimensions (pgvector) | Query Performance: Exact and approximate search | Integration with Bedrock: Via RAG custom implementation | Best For: Existing Aurora workloads needing vectors | Pricing Model: Per hour + I/O + storage
Amazon Neptune
Primary Use Case: Graph database + vector search | Vector Engine: Vector search for graph data | Scalability: Auto-scaling storage | Max Dimensions: 16,000 dimensions | Query Performance: Graph + vector hybrid queries | Integration with Bedrock: Graph-enhanced RAG | Best For: Knowledge graphs with vector search | Pricing Model: Per hour + storage + requests
Amazon DocumentDB
Primary Use Case: Document database + vector search | Vector Engine: Vector search extension | Scalability: Auto-scaling storage | Max Dimensions: 2,048 dimensions | Query Performance: Document + vector hybrid queries | Integration with Bedrock: MongoDB-compatible RAG | Best For: Document workloads with vector needs | Pricing Model: Per hour + I/O + storage
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json

#49 RAG Architecture Components Comparison
RAG & Vectors AIF-C01 ordering rag_architecture_components_comparison 4 cols 7 rows
▼

Comparison of vector store options and RAG implementation components on AWS

Feature Amazon OpenSearch Service Amazon Aurora Amazon Neptune Amazon DocumentDB
Database Type Search engine with vector support (k-NN plugin) Relational (PostgreSQL/MySQL compatible) with pgvector Graph database (Property Graph and RDF) Document database (MongoDB compatible)
Vector Search Method k-NN search with HNSW or IVF algorithms pgvector extension with HNSW and IVFFlat ML-based graph queries with vector similarity Vector search index using $vectorSearch
Primary Use Case Full-text + semantic search, log analysis, RAG Traditional apps needing vector search, hybrid queries Knowledge graphs, recommendations, fraud detection Flexible schema documents, content management with AI
Scaling Auto scaling with proper instance sizing Auto scaling storage, compute scaling manual Serverless option available, scaling similar to Aurora Auto scaling with instantaneous flexibility
Integration with Bedrock Native support in Bedrock Knowledge Bases Native support in Bedrock Knowledge Bases Native support in Bedrock Knowledge Bases Native support in Bedrock Knowledge Bases
Best For RAG When Need combined keyword and semantic search Already using PostgreSQL, need relational + vector Data has complex relationships to traverse Working with JSON-like document data
Cost Considerations Cluster costs + storage; good for search-heavy workloads Similar to RDS pricing + vector storage overhead Higher cost for graph processing capability Serverless option for cost optimization
💡 Mnemonics

O.A.N.D - OpenSearch for comprehensive search, Aurora for relational, Neptune for network relationships, DocumentDB for documents

⚠️ Exam Tips

All four services support Bedrock Knowledge Bases for RAG implementations

Choose based on existing data structure and query patterns

OpenSearch is preferred for pure RAG workloads with full-text requirements

Aurora pgvector is best when you need SQL queries + vector search

Neptune excels when relationships between data points matter for AI responses

Amazon OpenSearch Service
A managed search service that supports vector search capabilities, commonly used for RAG implementations requiring full-text search combined with semantic search.
Amazon Aurora
A relational database compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL that supports vector storage and search through pgvector extension for AI applications.
Amazon Neptune
A managed graph database service that supports vector search for knowledge graphs and relationship-based AI applications.
Amazon DocumentDB
A MongoDB-compatible document database that supports vector search for storing and querying embedded documents in AI applications.
Amazon OpenSearch Service
Database Type: Search engine with vector support (k-NN plugin) | Vector Search Method: k-NN search with HNSW or IVF algorithms | Primary Use Case: Full-text + semantic search, log analysis, RAG | Scaling: Auto scaling with proper instance sizing | Integration with Bedrock: Native support in Bedrock Knowledge Bases | Best For RAG When: Need combined keyword and semantic search | Cost Considerations: Cluster costs + storage; good for search-heavy workloads
Amazon Aurora
Database Type: Relational (PostgreSQL/MySQL compatible) with pgvector | Vector Search Method: pgvector extension with HNSW and IVFFlat | Primary Use Case: Traditional apps needing vector search, hybrid queries | Scaling: Auto scaling storage, compute scaling manual | Integration with Bedrock: Native support in Bedrock Knowledge Bases | Best For RAG When: Already using PostgreSQL, need relational + vector | Cost Considerations: Similar to RDS pricing + vector storage overhead
Amazon Neptune
Database Type: Graph database (Property Graph and RDF) | Vector Search Method: ML-based graph queries with vector similarity | Primary Use Case: Knowledge graphs, recommendations, fraud detection | Scaling: Serverless option available, scaling similar to Aurora | Integration with Bedrock: Native support in Bedrock Knowledge Bases | Best For RAG When: Data has complex relationships to traverse | Cost Considerations: Higher cost for graph processing capability
Amazon DocumentDB
Database Type: Document database (MongoDB compatible) | Vector Search Method: Vector search index using $vectorSearch | Primary Use Case: Flexible schema documents, content management with AI | Scaling: Auto scaling with instantaneous flexibility | Integration with Bedrock: Native support in Bedrock Knowledge Bases | Best For RAG When: Working with JSON-like document data | Cost Considerations: Serverless option for cost optimization
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#50 RAG vs Fine-Tuning vs Prompt Engineering vs Pre-Training
RAG & Vectors AIF-C01 ordering AIF-006 4 cols 8 rows
▼

This is the MOST WEIGHTED domain and this table covers its core concept: how do you customize or improve a foundation model's output? There are four main strategies, each with different cost, complexity, and use-case profiles. The exam heavily tests your ability to choose the RIGHT approach for a given business scenario.

Feature Prompt Engineering In-Context Learning (Few-Shot) Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) Fine-Tuning
How It Works Write better instructions and structure for the FM Include examples in the prompt at inference time Retrieve relevant docs and inject into prompt at runtime Retrain model weights on new training data
Model Weights Changed? No No No Yes — permanent change to model
Cost Lowest — just API tokens Low — larger prompts = more tokens Medium — vector DB + embedding costs Highest — GPU training costs
Complexity Low — just text editing Low-Medium — curate good examples Medium — build knowledge base, embeddings, vector DB High — data preparation, training, evaluation, deployment
Best For Quick improvements, structured outputs, tone control Teaching a specific output format with examples Accessing private/up-to-date knowledge without retraining Adapting model to a specific domain, style, or task permanently
Hallucination Risk High — FM still guesses from training data Medium — examples help but FM can still hallucinate Low — answers grounded in retrieved real documents Medium — model learns domain but can still hallucinate
AWS Implementation Any Bedrock model via console or API Any Bedrock model — include examples in prompt Bedrock Knowledge Bases + OpenSearch/Aurora/Neptune as vector DB Bedrock Custom Model Fine-Tuning or SageMaker JumpStart
Data Required None — just good prompt text A few examples (3-10 typical) Your private documents/knowledge base Hundreds to thousands of labeled training examples
⚠️ Exam Tips

Prompt Engineering = cheapest and fastest — modify the input prompt to guide model behavior

RAG = add external knowledge at query time without changing model weights — keeps data current

Fine-Tuning = modify model weights with your data — better for domain-specific language/style

Pre-Training = train a model from scratch — requires massive compute, rarely done by customers

The exam expects you to choose the LEAST resource-intensive approach that meets the requirement

RAG is best when you need factual accuracy with current data; fine-tuning is best for style/domain adaptation

Prompt Engineering
Prompt engineering is the craft of writing better input instructions to get better outputs from an FM — no model changes required. Think of it as 'learning to ask better questions.' It is the fastest, cheapest, and simplest customization approach and should always be tried first.
In-Context Learning (Few-Shot)
In-context learning provides examples directly inside the prompt (zero-shot, one-shot, or few-shot) so the model learns the pattern from the examples you give it at inference time — no training or retraining needed. It is more powerful than simple prompting but the examples are forgotten after each conversation.
Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
RAG connects the FM to an external knowledge base (documents, databases) at inference time. When a user asks a question, the system RETRIEVES relevant chunks of your private documents and AUGMENTS the prompt with that context before generating an answer. This is the standard solution for giving FMs access to private or up-to-date information.
Fine-Tuning
Fine-tuning retrains the FM on your own dataset to adapt its behavior, style, or domain knowledge permanently. It is the most expensive and complex approach but produces a model that has internalized domain knowledge rather than just referencing it.
Prompt Engineering
How It Works: Write better instructions and structure for the FM | Model Weights Changed?: No | Cost: Lowest — just API tokens | Complexity: Low — just text editing | Best For: Quick improvements, structured outputs, tone control | Hallucination Risk: High — FM still guesses from training data | AWS Implementation: Any Bedrock model via console or API | Data Required: None — just good prompt text
In-Context Learning (Few-Shot)
How It Works: Include examples in the prompt at inference time | Model Weights Changed?: No | Cost: Low — larger prompts = more tokens | Complexity: Low-Medium — curate good examples | Best For: Teaching a specific output format with examples | Hallucination Risk: Medium — examples help but FM can still hallucinate | AWS Implementation: Any Bedrock model — include examples in prompt | Data Required: A few examples (3-10 typical)
Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
How It Works: Retrieve relevant docs and inject into prompt at runtime | Model Weights Changed?: No | Cost: Medium — vector DB + embedding costs | Complexity: Medium — build knowledge base, embeddings, vector DB | Best For: Accessing private/up-to-date knowledge without retraining | Hallucination Risk: Low — answers grounded in retrieved real documents | AWS Implementation: Bedrock Knowledge Bases + OpenSearch/Aurora/Neptune as vector DB | Data Required: Your private documents/knowledge base
Fine-Tuning
How It Works: Retrain model weights on new training data | Model Weights Changed?: Yes — permanent change to model | Cost: Highest — GPU training costs | Complexity: High — data preparation, training, evaluation, deployment | Best For: Adapting model to a specific domain, style, or task permanently | Hallucination Risk: Medium — model learns domain but can still hallucinate | AWS Implementation: Bedrock Custom Model Fine-Tuning or SageMaker JumpStart | Data Required: Hundreds to thousands of labeled training examples
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#51 Vector Databases for RAG Comparison
RAG & Vectors AIF-C01 ordering vector_databases_for_rag_comparison 4 cols 11 rows
▼

Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) architectures require vector databases to store and search embeddings. AWS offers multiple database services that support vector storage and similarity search. Amazon OpenSearch Service provides full-text search with vector capabilities. Amazon Aurora with pgvector adds vector support to PostgreSQL. Amazon Neptune is a graph database with vector search. Amazon DocumentDB supports vector search for document databases. Understanding the differences helps architects choose the right service for their RAG implementation.

Feature OpenSearch Service Aurora with pgvector Amazon Neptune Amazon DocumentDB
Primary Database Type Search and analytics engine Relational database (PostgreSQL) Graph database Document database (MongoDB-compatible)
Vector Storage Method k-NN plugin for vector fields pgvector extension for vector columns Vector search with graph embeddings Vector search for document embeddings
Similarity Metrics Cosine, Euclidean, Dot Product, L1, L-inf Cosine, Euclidean, Inner Product, L1 Cosine, Euclidean, Dot Product Cosine, Euclidean
Index Types for Vectors HNSW, IVF, Lucene k-NN HNSW, IVFFlat indexing In-memory vector indexing Vector indexes (IVF, HNSW)
Max Vector Dimensions Up to 16,000 dimensions Up to 16,000 dimensions Up to 65,535 dimensions Up to 2,000 dimensions
Hybrid Search (Text + Vector) Excellent - native text search + k-NN Good - Full-text search + vector Good - Graph traversals + vector Limited - Basic text + vector
Use with Bedrock Native integration via Knowledge Bases Supported via Knowledge Bases Supported via Knowledge Bases Supported via Knowledge Bases
Best Use Case Full-text search with semantic capabilities, log analytics with RAG Existing PostgreSQL apps adding RAG, transactional + vector workloads Knowledge graphs with semantic search, relationship-aware RAG Document-centric RAG, JSON data with embeddings
Scaling Options Horizontal (nodes) + Vertical (instance types) Aurora Serverless v2 auto-scaling Read replicas, cluster scaling Horizontal scaling with replicas
Pricing Model Instance-based + storage Aurora pricing (ACU-based for Serverless) Instance-based + I/O charges Instance-based + storage + I/O
Exam Relevance High - Most common for RAG High - PostgreSQL familiarity Medium - Graph-specific scenarios Low-Medium - Document use cases
⚠️ Exam Tips

OpenSearch Service is the most versatile vector store option on AWS, combining full-text and vector search

Aurora with pgvector is ideal when you already use PostgreSQL and want vector capabilities without a new database

Neptune with vector search is best when you need knowledge graphs combined with semantic similarity

Bedrock Knowledge Bases manages the vector store automatically - you do not need to provision one directly

For the exam: know that embeddings must be generated BEFORE storing in a vector database

OpenSearch Service
A managed search and analytics engine with native vector search (k-NN plugin). Best for combining full-text search with vector similarity search, making it ideal for RAG applications that need both keyword and semantic search.
Aurora with pgvector
PostgreSQL-compatible relational database with the pgvector extension for vector storage and similarity search. Best when you already use Aurora/PostgreSQL and want to add vector capabilities without a separate database.
Amazon Neptune
A graph database with vector search capabilities. Best for RAG applications that need to combine knowledge graph traversal with vector similarity search for richer context retrieval.
Amazon DocumentDB
A MongoDB-compatible document database with vector search support. Best for applications already using document-oriented data models that need to add semantic search capabilities.
OpenSearch Service
Primary Database Type: Search and analytics engine | Vector Storage Method: k-NN plugin for vector fields | Similarity Metrics: Cosine, Euclidean, Dot Product, L1, L-inf | Index Types for Vectors: HNSW, IVF, Lucene k-NN | Max Vector Dimensions: Up to 16,000 dimensions | Hybrid Search (Text + Vector): Excellent - native text search + k-NN | Use with Bedrock: Native integration via Knowledge Bases | Best Use Case: Full-text search with semantic capabilities, log analytics with RAG | Scaling Options: Horizontal (nodes) + Vertical (instance types) | Pricing Model: Instance-based + storage | Exam Relevance: High - Most common for RAG
Aurora with pgvector
Primary Database Type: Relational database (PostgreSQL) | Vector Storage Method: pgvector extension for vector columns | Similarity Metrics: Cosine, Euclidean, Inner Product, L1 | Index Types for Vectors: HNSW, IVFFlat indexing | Max Vector Dimensions: Up to 16,000 dimensions | Hybrid Search (Text + Vector): Good - Full-text search + vector | Use with Bedrock: Supported via Knowledge Bases | Best Use Case: Existing PostgreSQL apps adding RAG, transactional + vector workloads | Scaling Options: Aurora Serverless v2 auto-scaling | Pricing Model: Aurora pricing (ACU-based for Serverless) | Exam Relevance: High - PostgreSQL familiarity
Amazon Neptune
Primary Database Type: Graph database | Vector Storage Method: Vector search with graph embeddings | Similarity Metrics: Cosine, Euclidean, Dot Product | Index Types for Vectors: In-memory vector indexing | Max Vector Dimensions: Up to 65,535 dimensions | Hybrid Search (Text + Vector): Good - Graph traversals + vector | Use with Bedrock: Supported via Knowledge Bases | Best Use Case: Knowledge graphs with semantic search, relationship-aware RAG | Scaling Options: Read replicas, cluster scaling | Pricing Model: Instance-based + I/O charges | Exam Relevance: Medium - Graph-specific scenarios
Amazon DocumentDB
Primary Database Type: Document database (MongoDB-compatible) | Vector Storage Method: Vector search for document embeddings | Similarity Metrics: Cosine, Euclidean | Index Types for Vectors: Vector indexes (IVF, HNSW) | Max Vector Dimensions: Up to 2,000 dimensions | Hybrid Search (Text + Vector): Limited - Basic text + vector | Use with Bedrock: Supported via Knowledge Bases | Best Use Case: Document-centric RAG, JSON data with embeddings | Scaling Options: Horizontal scaling with replicas | Pricing Model: Instance-based + storage + I/O | Exam Relevance: Low-Medium - Document use cases
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#52 Vector Store Options Mentioned by the Exam Guide
RAG & Vectors AIF-C01 ordering aif_d3_vector_store_options 4 cols 5 rows
▼
Feature Amazon OpenSearch Service Amazon Aurora / RDS for PostgreSQL Amazon Neptune Amazon DocumentDB
Architectural bias Search-first Relational-first Graph-first Document-first
Best exam clue Semantic search / retrieval experience Stay in relational architecture while adding embeddings Knowledge graph or relationship-heavy reasoning Document-centric application context
Strength lens Search relevance SQL and app continuity Connected-entity modeling Flexible document data model
Great for Search-heavy RAG Apps already built around relational data Graph-rich knowledge representation Document-centric retrieval patterns
Common trap Assuming every vector use case must use search engine first Forgetting vector retrieval can live in relational workflows Choosing graph when relationships are not core Choosing document DB when search relevance is the primary requirement
⚠️ Exam Tips

OpenSearch is the most common vector store choice for RAG on AWS — combines text and vector search

Aurora with pgvector is ideal when you already use PostgreSQL and want to add vector capabilities

Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases can manage vector storage automatically

The exam focuses on knowing WHEN to use vector databases, not the low-level implementation details

Key concept: embeddings are converted to vectors → stored in vector DB → retrieved by similarity search

Amazon OpenSearch Service
OpenSearch Service is a strong search-oriented option for embeddings and retrieval experiences. On the exam, it is the natural pick when search and relevance are first-class concerns.
Amazon Aurora / RDS for PostgreSQL
Aurora or RDS for PostgreSQL can support vector-style retrieval while staying inside relational patterns. They fit when the app already leans relational and wants to extend that architecture.
Amazon Neptune
Neptune is graph-oriented and can be attractive when relationships between entities matter deeply. It is less about being the default vector answer and more about relationship-rich knowledge structures.
Amazon DocumentDB
DocumentDB fits document-oriented application patterns and can be part of retrieval-oriented designs when document data models already dominate. It is not the default answer for every vector problem, but it can align well with document-centric apps.
Amazon OpenSearch Service
Architectural bias: Search-first | Best exam clue: Semantic search / retrieval experience | Strength lens: Search relevance | Great for: Search-heavy RAG | Common trap: Assuming every vector use case must use search engine first
Amazon Aurora / RDS for PostgreSQL
Architectural bias: Relational-first | Best exam clue: Stay in relational architecture while adding embeddings | Strength lens: SQL and app continuity | Great for: Apps already built around relational data | Common trap: Forgetting vector retrieval can live in relational workflows
Amazon Neptune
Architectural bias: Graph-first | Best exam clue: Knowledge graph or relationship-heavy reasoning | Strength lens: Connected-entity modeling | Great for: Graph-rich knowledge representation | Common trap: Choosing graph when relationships are not core
Amazon DocumentDB
Architectural bias: Document-first | Best exam clue: Document-centric application context | Strength lens: Flexible document data model | Great for: Document-centric retrieval patterns | Common trap: Choosing document DB when search relevance is the primary requirement
🎯 Why This Table Matters

AIF Domain 3 explicitly mentions several AWS-backed storage options for embeddings in retrieval workflows. This table helps compare search-first, relational, graph, and document-oriented choices.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

Prompt Engineering 3 tables
#53 Guardrails and Safety Mechanisms
Prompt Engineering AIF-C01 ordering guardrails_and_safety_mechanisms 3 cols 6 rows
▼

Comparison of AWS services and features for AI safety, security, and responsible AI

Feature Bedrock Guardrails SageMaker Clarify Amazon A2I
Primary Purpose Content filtering and topic restrictions for generative AI outputs Bias detection and fairness metrics for ML models Human-in-the-loop review for ML predictions
Use Phase Inference time - filters model outputs Training and post-deployment monitoring Runtime - routes low-confidence predictions to humans
Key Features Content filters, denied topics, word filters, sensitive data filtering Bias detection, explainability, model cards, drift detection Human workflow integration, confidence thresholds, task definitions
Output Blocked, filtered, or allowed model responses Bias reports, SHAP values, fairness metrics Human review tasks, aggregated decisions
Integration Native to Bedrock, API configuration SageMaker pipeline integration, Model Monitor Lambda, SageMaker, Rekognition, Textract integrations
Exam Focus (AIF) High - applies to GenAI applications High - Responsible AI domain coverage Medium - human oversight mechanisms
💡 Mnemonics

B.S.A - Bedrock blocks bad content, SageMaker spots bias, A2I adds human oversight

⚠️ Exam Tips

Guardrails for Bedrock are configured per-application, not per-model

Content filters in Bedrock can filter based on categories: hate speech, sexual content, violence, harassment

Topic restrictions can block responses about sensitive subjects

SageMaker Clarify should be used early in ML development for bias prevention

A2I is critical for healthcare, finance, and other regulated industries requiring human judgment

Bedrock Guardrails
AWS-native safety features that filter harmful content, restrict topics, and control output based on configurable policies for foundation models.
SageMaker Clarify
A tool for detecting and measuring bias in ML models and data throughout the ML lifecycle, with integration for responsible AI reporting.
Amazon A2I
Augmented AI service that combines machine learning with human review for high-stakes decisions requiring human judgment.
Bedrock Guardrails
Primary Purpose: Content filtering and topic restrictions for generative AI outputs | Use Phase: Inference time - filters model outputs | Key Features: Content filters, denied topics, word filters, sensitive data filtering | Output: Blocked, filtered, or allowed model responses | Integration: Native to Bedrock, API configuration | Exam Focus (AIF): High - applies to GenAI applications
SageMaker Clarify
Primary Purpose: Bias detection and fairness metrics for ML models | Use Phase: Training and post-deployment monitoring | Key Features: Bias detection, explainability, model cards, drift detection | Output: Bias reports, SHAP values, fairness metrics | Integration: SageMaker pipeline integration, Model Monitor | Exam Focus (AIF): High - Responsible AI domain coverage
Amazon A2I
Primary Purpose: Human-in-the-loop review for ML predictions | Use Phase: Runtime - routes low-confidence predictions to humans | Key Features: Human workflow integration, confidence thresholds, task definitions | Output: Human review tasks, aggregated decisions | Integration: Lambda, SageMaker, Rekognition, Textract integrations | Exam Focus (AIF): Medium - human oversight mechanisms
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#54 Prompt Engineering Techniques
Prompt Engineering AIF-C01 ordering AIF-003_2 4 cols 12 rows
▼

Comparison of different prompt engineering techniques used in generative AI applications

This table compares different prompting techniques for foundation models. Zero-shot uses no examples, few-shot provides examples, chain-of-thought enables reasoning, and system prompting sets the behavior context for the model.

Feature Zero-Shot Prompting Few-Shot Prompting Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Negative Prompting
Definition Task description + magic phrase (e.g., 'think step by step') Few examples provided in prompt Step-by-step reasoning in prompt Specify what NOT to include
Examples in Prompt dog → canine, cat → feline, bird → ?, horse → ? English: Dog, French: Chien. English: Cat, French:? Solve this step by step: 15 + 27 Exclusion examples
Complexity Simplest approach Moderate complexity Higher complexity, better reasoning Moderate - additive technique
Best Use Case Complex tasks, specific output formats, domain-specific patterns Format-specific tasks, classification Math, logic, multi-step reasoning Safety, content filtering, precision
Token Usage Low - minimal overhead for reasoning trigger Higher (examples add tokens) Highest (reasoning adds tokens) Moderate increase
Model Performance Good for simple tasks Better accuracy than zero-shot Best for reasoning tasks Reduces unwanted outputs
AWS Bedrock Support All models support All models support Claude, Llama support well Supported via guardrails
Risk Level May lack context Token cost tradeoff Higher latency May over-filter if excessive
Description Direct instructions without examples Instructions with example input-output pairs Instructs the model to reason step-by-step, breaking complex problems into intermediate reasoning steps before arriving at an answer Explicitly tells the model what NOT to do or include, constraining output by specifying undesired behaviors or content
Effectiveness Good for simple tasks Better for specific formats High — significantly improves accuracy on multi-step reasoning, math, and logic tasks Moderate — useful for reducing unwanted content but less effective for complex reasoning tasks
Accuracy Low to moderate — depends on task complexity and model capability without examples Moderate to high — examples guide the model toward correct output patterns High — explicit reasoning steps reduce errors in complex tasks Moderate — helps avoid specific mistakes but doesn't directly improve reasoning
Hallucination Risk Higher — no examples to anchor the model, increasing fabrication risk Moderate — examples reduce hallucination but don't eliminate it Lower — step-by-step reasoning makes errors more visible and self-correcting Moderate — constraints can help but model may still fabricate within allowed scope
💡 Mnemonics

ZERO-FEW-COT-NEG: Zero examples, FEW examples, Chain Of Thoughts, NEGative constraints

No examples? Zero-shot. Have examples? Few-shot. Need reasoning? Chain-of-thought. Want to avoid something? Negative prompting.

Z.F.C.Z - Zero has No examples, Few has Few, Chain shows steps, Zero-CoT is magic

⚠️ Exam Tips

Zero-shot is the default - always start here before adding complexity

Few-shot improves accuracy but increases token costs significantly

Chain-of-thought is essential for multi-step reasoning problems

Use negative prompts to prevent hallucinations or unwanted content

Temperature affects creativity: lower (0-0.3) for factual, higher (0.7-1) for creative

Prompt templates help standardize and reuse effective prompts

For AIF-C01, know when to use each technique based on task complexity

Few-shot is preferred for complex or domain-specific tasks

Chain-of-thought significantly improves math and reasoning tasks

Zero-shot + CoT is a cost-effective alternative to few-shot for reasoning

Negative prompts can exclude unwanted content but work differently than regular prompts

Zero-Shot Prompting
A prompting technique where the model is given a task without any example demonstrations. The model relies entirely on its pre-training to understand and complete the task, making it efficient for simple, well-defined problems where examples aren't necessary.
Few-Shot Prompting
A technique that provides the model with a few example input-output pairs within the prompt to guide its behavior. This helps the model understand the desired format and pattern, improving accuracy for tasks where the model needs additional context about the expected output.
Chain-of-Thought (CoT)
An advanced prompting technique that encourages the model to break down complex problems into intermediate reasoning steps. This significantly improves performance on mathematical, logical, and multi-step reasoning tasks by making the model 'show its work.'
Negative Prompting
A technique that explicitly specifies what should NOT be included in the output, helping to prevent unwanted content, reduce hallucinations, and improve output quality by setting clear boundaries for the model's responses.
Zero-Shot Prompting
Definition: Task description + magic phrase (e.g., 'think step by step') | Examples in Prompt: dog → canine, cat → feline, bird → ?, horse → ? | Complexity: Simplest approach | Best Use Case: Complex tasks, specific output formats, domain-specific patterns | Token Usage: Low - minimal overhead for reasoning trigger | Model Performance: Good for simple tasks | AWS Bedrock Support: All models support | Risk Level: May lack context | Description: Direct instructions without examples | Effectiveness: Good for simple tasks | Accuracy: Low to moderate — depends on task complexity and model capability without examples | Hallucination Risk: Higher — no examples to anchor the model, increasing fabrication risk
Few-Shot Prompting
Definition: Few examples provided in prompt | Examples in Prompt: English: Dog, French: Chien. English: Cat, French:? | Complexity: Moderate complexity | Best Use Case: Format-specific tasks, classification | Token Usage: Higher (examples add tokens) | Model Performance: Better accuracy than zero-shot | AWS Bedrock Support: All models support | Risk Level: Token cost tradeoff | Description: Instructions with example input-output pairs | Effectiveness: Better for specific formats | Accuracy: Moderate to high — examples guide the model toward correct output patterns | Hallucination Risk: Moderate — examples reduce hallucination but don't eliminate it
Chain-of-Thought (CoT)
Definition: Step-by-step reasoning in prompt | Examples in Prompt: Solve this step by step: 15 + 27 | Complexity: Higher complexity, better reasoning | Best Use Case: Math, logic, multi-step reasoning | Token Usage: Highest (reasoning adds tokens) | Model Performance: Best for reasoning tasks | AWS Bedrock Support: Claude, Llama support well | Risk Level: Higher latency | Description: Instructs the model to reason step-by-step, breaking complex problems into intermediate reasoning steps before arriving at an answer | Effectiveness: High — significantly improves accuracy on multi-step reasoning, math, and logic tasks | Accuracy: High — explicit reasoning steps reduce errors in complex tasks | Hallucination Risk: Lower — step-by-step reasoning makes errors more visible and self-correcting
Negative Prompting
Definition: Specify what NOT to include | Examples in Prompt: Exclusion examples | Complexity: Moderate - additive technique | Best Use Case: Safety, content filtering, precision | Token Usage: Moderate increase | Model Performance: Reduces unwanted outputs | AWS Bedrock Support: Supported via guardrails | Risk Level: May over-filter if excessive | Description: Explicitly tells the model what NOT to do or include, constraining output by specifying undesired behaviors or content | Effectiveness: Moderate — useful for reducing unwanted content but less effective for complex reasoning tasks | Accuracy: Moderate — helps avoid specific mistakes but doesn't directly improve reasoning | Hallucination Risk: Moderate — constraints can help but model may still fabricate within allowed scope
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#55 Zero-Shot vs One-Shot vs Few-Shot Prompting
Prompt Engineering AIF-C01 ordering AIF-007 3 cols 5 rows
▼

These three prompt engineering techniques differ only in HOW MANY EXAMPLES you include in the prompt. The exam will describe a prompting scenario and ask you to name the technique. Zero = no examples, One = one example, Few = multiple examples. More examples generally yield better results but use more tokens.

Feature Zero-Shot Prompting One-Shot Prompting Few-Shot Prompting
Examples Provided 0 examples 1 example 3-10 examples (sometimes more)
Token Cost Lowest — shortest prompt Medium — one example adds tokens Highest — multiple examples
Output Reliability Lowest — model guesses format Better — model has one reference Best — model learns pattern clearly
Best For Simple, general tasks where format is obvious When you want to show a single example of a format Complex tasks requiring a specific style or output structure
Example Use Case 'Translate this English text to French: [text]' 'Q: What is the capital of France? A: Paris. Q: What is the capital of Germany? A:' 3-5 Q&A pairs before the actual question the model must answer
⚠️ Exam Tips

Zero-shot = no examples in the prompt — relies entirely on the model’s pre-trained knowledge

One-shot = one example provided — shows the model the expected format or style

Few-shot = multiple examples provided — gives the model more context for complex tasks

More examples generally improve accuracy but use more tokens (increase cost and latency)

For the exam: if accuracy is critical and the task is complex, few-shot prompting is preferred

Zero-Shot Prompting
You give the model ONLY the instruction and input with no examples — you rely entirely on the model's pre-trained knowledge to figure out what you want. It works well for simple tasks or highly capable models but may fail for specialized formats.
One-Shot Prompting
You provide exactly ONE example of the desired input-output pattern before your actual request. This single example dramatically improves the model's understanding of what format or style you expect, at the cost of additional tokens.
Few-Shot Prompting
You provide MULTIPLE examples (typically 3 to 10) of input-output pairs before your request. This is the most reliable technique for teaching the model a specific pattern, format, or task, and is widely used in production systems.
Zero-Shot Prompting
Examples Provided: 0 examples | Token Cost: Lowest — shortest prompt | Output Reliability: Lowest — model guesses format | Best For: Simple, general tasks where format is obvious | Example Use Case: 'Translate this English text to French: [text]'
One-Shot Prompting
Examples Provided: 1 example | Token Cost: Medium — one example adds tokens | Output Reliability: Better — model has one reference | Best For: When you want to show a single example of a format | Example Use Case: 'Q: What is the capital of France? A: Paris. Q: What is the capital of Germany? A:'
Few-Shot Prompting
Examples Provided: 3-10 examples (sometimes more) | Token Cost: Highest — multiple examples | Output Reliability: Best — model learns pattern clearly | Best For: Complex tasks requiring a specific style or output structure | Example Use Case: 3-5 Q&A pairs before the actual question the model must answer
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

Responsible AI 4 tables
#56 Responsible AI Services Comparison
Responsible AI AIF-C01 ordering responsible_ai_services_comparison 3 cols 5 rows
▼

Comparing AWS services for responsible AI implementation and monitoring

Feature Amazon SageMaker Clarify Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor
Purpose Detect bias in ML models and data Filter harmful content in GenAI apps Monitor model quality post-deployment
When to Use During model development and training When deploying generative AI applications After model deployment
What It Detects Demographic bias, fairness metrics Hate speech, violence, sexual content Data drift, feature drift, prediction quality
Key Metrics Bias metrics, attribute importance Content filtered/not filtered KOPS, F1, accuracy drift
Integration SageMaker Data Wrangler, training jobs Bedrock API, agents, Knowledge Bases Deployed SageMaker endpoints
💡 Mnemonics

CGM = Clarify for bias, Guardrails for GenAI safety, Monitor for model quality

⚠️ Exam Tips

SageMaker Clarify is essential for detecting bias BEFORE model deployment

Guardrails work at inference time - blocking harmful inputs and outputs

Model Monitor detects when deployed models degrade - critical for production

Responsible AI includes: bias detection, fairness, transparency, safety

Use multiple responsible AI tools together for comprehensive coverage

Amazon SageMaker Clarify
A service that helps detect potential bias in data and models during the ML development process, supporting responsible AI practices and fairness in machine learning.
Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock
A feature that helps implement safeguards for generative AI applications by filtering harmful content in both inputs and outputs of foundation models.
Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor
A service that continuously monitors deployed models for quality and drift, ensuring model predictions remain accurate and reliable over time.
Amazon SageMaker Clarify
Purpose: Detect bias in ML models and data | When to Use: During model development and training | What It Detects: Demographic bias, fairness metrics | Key Metrics: Bias metrics, attribute importance | Integration: SageMaker Data Wrangler, training jobs
Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock
Purpose: Filter harmful content in GenAI apps | When to Use: When deploying generative AI applications | What It Detects: Hate speech, violence, sexual content | Key Metrics: Content filtered/not filtered | Integration: Bedrock API, agents, Knowledge Bases
Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor
Purpose: Monitor model quality post-deployment | When to Use: After model deployment | What It Detects: Data drift, feature drift, prediction quality | Key Metrics: KOPS, F1, accuracy drift | Integration: Deployed SageMaker endpoints
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#57 Responsible AI Tools — Clarify vs Guardrails vs Model Monitor vs A2I
Responsible AI AIF-C01 ordering AIF-009_2 4 cols 5 rows
▼

Responsible AI on AWS is enforced through a suite of monitoring and safety tools. The exam expects you to match a responsible AI concern (bias, safety, drift, human review) to the correct AWS service. These tools collectively ensure AI systems are fair, safe, transparent, and human-supervised.

Feature Amazon SageMaker Clarify Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock SageMaker Model Monitor Amazon Augmented AI (A2I)
Primary Function Detect bias + explain model predictions Filter harmful/unsafe content from FM inputs and outputs Detect data drift and model quality degradation in production Route low-confidence predictions to human reviewers
When Used During training and evaluation (development phase) At inference time (every API call filtered) After deployment — continuous monitoring At inference time — when confidence is below threshold
Responsible AI Pillar Fairness, Explainability Safety, Robustness, Content Compliance Robustness, Reliability Human Oversight, Safety
Works With SageMaker training jobs and endpoints Any Amazon Bedrock FM SageMaker deployed endpoints Rekognition, Textract, or any custom ML model
Addresses Hallucinations? No — focuses on bias, not Gen AI hallucinations Partially — can block clearly wrong or harmful outputs No — detects drift, not individual hallucinations Yes — human can catch and correct AI errors
⚠️ Exam Tips

SageMaker Clarify = bias detection + explainability (SHAP values) for ML models

Bedrock Guardrails = content filtering for foundation models (toxicity, PII, denied topics)

SageMaker Model Monitor = production monitoring for data/model quality drift

Amazon A2I (Augmented AI) = human review workflow for low-confidence ML predictions

Clarify works BEFORE and AFTER deployment; Guardrails works AT inference time; Monitor works IN production

Amazon SageMaker Clarify
SageMaker Clarify detects BIAS in training datasets and ML models, and provides model EXPLAINABILITY reports — it tells you WHY the model made a prediction and flags if certain demographic groups are treated unfairly. Use it during model development and evaluation.
Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock
Bedrock Guardrails is a safety filtering layer that sits in front of foundation models — it blocks harmful content, prevents prompt injection attacks, filters sensitive topics, and enforces content policies. Use it to make deployed Gen AI apps safe.
SageMaker Model Monitor
Model Monitor continuously watches deployed models in production for PERFORMANCE DRIFT — it alerts you when the model's input data distribution or predictions change over time, signaling the model needs retraining. Use it post-deployment.
Amazon Augmented AI (A2I)
Amazon A2I routes low-confidence model predictions to HUMAN REVIEWERS for manual inspection. It creates a 'human in the loop' workflow so that uncertain AI decisions get a human second opinion before action is taken.
Amazon SageMaker Clarify
Primary Function: Detect bias + explain model predictions | When Used: During training and evaluation (development phase) | Responsible AI Pillar: Fairness, Explainability | Works With: SageMaker training jobs and endpoints | Addresses Hallucinations?: No — focuses on bias, not Gen AI hallucinations
Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock
Primary Function: Filter harmful/unsafe content from FM inputs and outputs | When Used: At inference time (every API call filtered) | Responsible AI Pillar: Safety, Robustness, Content Compliance | Works With: Any Amazon Bedrock FM | Addresses Hallucinations?: Partially — can block clearly wrong or harmful outputs
SageMaker Model Monitor
Primary Function: Detect data drift and model quality degradation in production | When Used: After deployment — continuous monitoring | Responsible AI Pillar: Robustness, Reliability | Works With: SageMaker deployed endpoints | Addresses Hallucinations?: No — detects drift, not individual hallucinations
Amazon Augmented AI (A2I)
Primary Function: Route low-confidence predictions to human reviewers | When Used: At inference time — when confidence is below threshold | Responsible AI Pillar: Human Oversight, Safety | Works With: Rekognition, Textract, or any custom ML model | Addresses Hallucinations?: Yes — human can catch and correct AI errors
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#58 Responsible AI and Guardrails
Responsible AI AIF-C01 ordering AIF-005_2 4 cols 8 rows
▼

Comparing responsible AI features and guardrails - critical for Domain 4 (Guidelines for Responsible AI)

Feature Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock Amazon SageMaker Clarify Amazon Augmented AI (A2I) SageMaker Model Monitor
Primary Purpose Content filtering, safety guardrails Bias detection, explainability Human review workflow Model performance monitoring
Content Filtering Configurable filters for harmful content Not primary function Human-in-loop review Not primary function
Bias Detection Limited Comprehensive bias metrics Human bias review Drift detection only
Explainability Not available SHAP values, feature importance Human explanation review Not available
Real-time Protection Yes - inline guardrails Batch analysis Human review triggered Scheduled monitoring
Use Case Production FM safety ML model fairness analysis High-stakes predictions review Production model drift detection
Integration Bedrock API calls SageMaker pipelines Any ML service SageMaker endpoints
Alert Mechanism Block/filter responses Reports and dashboards Human review tasks CloudWatch alarms
💡 Mnemonics

GUARD-CLARIFY-A2I-MONITOR: Guardrails block, Clarify explains, A2I adds humans, Monitor tracks

Need content filtering? Guardrails. Need bias detection? Clarify. Need human review? A2I. Need drift detection? Model Monitor.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Guardrails for Bedrock is the primary tool for FM content safety

SageMaker Clarify provides SHAP-based explainability for model predictions
