A2I is essential when regulatory requirements demand human oversight

Model Monitor detects data drift and model degradation over time

Guardrails can filter: hate speech, violence, sexual content, insults

Prompt injection protection is built into Guardrails for Bedrock

Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock
A safety feature that implements customizable content filters and safeguards for foundation model outputs. Provides real-time protection against harmful content, prompt injection attacks, and jailbreak attempts while allowing organizations to define their own safety policies.
Amazon SageMaker Clarify
A model explainability and bias detection service that provides insights into model predictions through SHAP values and feature importance. Helps identify and mitigate bias in ML models across demographic groups for responsible AI development.
Amazon Augmented AI (A2I)
A human-in-the-loop service that enables human review of ML predictions, especially important for high-stakes decisions or when regulatory compliance requires human oversight. Creates workflows for human reviewers to validate or correct model predictions.
SageMaker Model Monitor
A monitoring service that continuously tracks model performance in production, detecting data drift, concept drift, and prediction quality issues. Alerts teams when model behavior deviates from the baseline established during training.
Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock
Primary Purpose: Content filtering, safety guardrails | Content Filtering: Configurable filters for harmful content | Bias Detection: Limited | Explainability: Not available | Real-time Protection: Yes - inline guardrails | Use Case: Production FM safety | Integration: Bedrock API calls | Alert Mechanism: Block/filter responses
Amazon SageMaker Clarify
Primary Purpose: Bias detection, explainability | Content Filtering: Not primary function | Bias Detection: Comprehensive bias metrics | Explainability: SHAP values, feature importance | Real-time Protection: Batch analysis | Use Case: ML model fairness analysis | Integration: SageMaker pipelines | Alert Mechanism: Reports and dashboards
Amazon Augmented AI (A2I)
Primary Purpose: Human review workflow | Content Filtering: Human-in-loop review | Bias Detection: Human bias review | Explainability: Human explanation review | Real-time Protection: Human review triggered | Use Case: High-stakes predictions review | Integration: Any ML service | Alert Mechanism: Human review tasks
SageMaker Model Monitor
Primary Purpose: Model performance monitoring | Content Filtering: Not primary function | Bias Detection: Drift detection only | Explainability: Not available | Real-time Protection: Scheduled monitoring | Use Case: Production model drift detection | Integration: SageMaker endpoints | Alert Mechanism: CloudWatch alarms
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json

#59 Responsible AI and Trust Controls
Responsible AI AIF-C01 ordering aif_d4_responsible_ai_controls 5 cols 5 rows
▼
Feature Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock SageMaker Clarify SageMaker Model Monitor Amazon Augmented AI (A2I) SageMaker Model Cards
Primary role Safety controls on generative AI interactions Bias and explainability analysis Production monitoring Human review Transparency documentation
Best exam clue Need runtime guardrails for gen AI app Assess bias/fairness Watch model quality after deployment Human-in-the-loop review Document model facts and intended use
Lifecycle stage Application runtime Assessment / validation Post-deployment Operational oversight Governance / documentation
Great for Safer user interactions Fairness and interpretability checks Detecting drift or degradation Escalating uncertain cases to humans Transparency and audit readiness
Common trap Thinking they replace all governance work Using it as runtime prompt filtering Confusing monitoring with documentation Thinking it replaces model evaluation Thinking documentation alone enforces safe behavior
⚠️ Exam Tips

Bias can enter at data collection, model training, or prediction stages — address all three

SageMaker Clarify detects statistical bias metrics like Disparate Impact and Class Imbalance

Bedrock Guardrails is the ONLY tool for filtering harmful foundation model outputs

Model Cards document model purpose, performance, limitations, and ethical considerations

The exam tests whether you know which tool addresses which aspect of responsible AI

Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock
Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock help shape and constrain model behavior in generative AI applications. On the exam, they are the safety and policy-control clue for Bedrock-based experiences.
SageMaker Clarify
Clarify focuses on bias detection and explainability-style analysis. It is the exam answer when fairness, bias analysis, or interpretability checks are front and center.
SageMaker Model Monitor
Model Monitor tracks behavior and quality in production over time. It is the answer when the risk is drift, changing data, or degradation after deployment.
Amazon Augmented AI (A2I)
A2I adds human review steps so edge cases and low-confidence outputs can be checked by people. It is critical when responsible AI requires human oversight.
SageMaker Model Cards
Model Cards document model characteristics, intended use, limitations, and governance details. They support transparency and documentation rather than runtime control.
Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock
Primary role: Safety controls on generative AI interactions | Best exam clue: Need runtime guardrails for gen AI app | Lifecycle stage: Application runtime | Great for: Safer user interactions | Common trap: Thinking they replace all governance work
SageMaker Clarify
Primary role: Bias and explainability analysis | Best exam clue: Assess bias/fairness | Lifecycle stage: Assessment / validation | Great for: Fairness and interpretability checks | Common trap: Using it as runtime prompt filtering
SageMaker Model Monitor
Primary role: Production monitoring | Best exam clue: Watch model quality after deployment | Lifecycle stage: Post-deployment | Great for: Detecting drift or degradation | Common trap: Confusing monitoring with documentation
Amazon Augmented AI (A2I)
Primary role: Human review | Best exam clue: Human-in-the-loop review | Lifecycle stage: Operational oversight | Great for: Escalating uncertain cases to humans | Common trap: Thinking it replaces model evaluation
SageMaker Model Cards
Primary role: Transparency documentation | Best exam clue: Document model facts and intended use | Lifecycle stage: Governance / documentation | Great for: Transparency and audit readiness | Common trap: Thinking documentation alone enforces safe behavior
🎯 Why This Table Matters

AIF Domain 4 focuses on bias, fairness, safety, human oversight, transparency, and monitoring. This table helps separate preventative guardrails from bias analysis, monitoring, human review, and documentation.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

MLOps & Lifecycle 4 tables
#60 AWS AI/ML Development Lifecycle Services Comparison
MLOps & Lifecycle AIF-C01 ordering ai_ml_lifecycle_comparison 5 cols 6 rows
▼

This table compares AWS services across the machine learning development lifecycle, from data preparation to model deployment and monitoring, essential for understanding MLOps on AWS.

Feature SageMaker Data Wrangler SageMaker Feature Store SageMaker Training SageMaker Pipelines SageMaker Model Monitor
ML Lifecycle Stage Data Preparation Feature Engineering / Management Model Training MLOps / Orchestration Model Monitoring
Primary Function Import, visualize, transform data Store and serve features consistently Execute training jobs at scale Automate ML workflows Monitor model performance in production
Code Required No (visual interface) Yes (SDK) Yes (training scripts) Yes (Python SDK) Minimal (configuration)
Key Capabilities 300+ transformations, visual analysis, export to pipeline Feature reuse, point-in-time correctness, online/offline stores Distributed training, spot instances, hyperparameter tuning Conditional steps, caching, parallel execution, scheduling Data quality, model quality, bias drift, explainability
Integration S3, Redshift, Athena, Snowflake, Databricks SageMaker Training, Processing, Pipelines S3, EFS, FSx, Experiments All SageMaker components, Step Functions CloudWatch, SNS, Lambda
Cost Model ml.m5.4xlarge instance cost Storage + read/write operations Per instance-hour of training Based on underlying resources Monitoring schedule instance cost
⚠️ Exam Tips

SageMaker provides tools for every stage: Data Wrangler → Training → Hosting → Monitor

Feature Store enables feature reuse across teams and reduces duplicated data engineering

Ground Truth Plus uses a managed workforce for data labeling when you lack labelers

Model Registry is critical for MLOps — it versions models and tracks lineage

SageMaker Canvas provides no-code ML for business analysts who don’t write code

SageMaker Data Wrangler
Visual data preparation tool that makes it easy to import, prepare, transform, and analyze data for machine learning without writing code.
SageMaker Feature Store
Fully managed repository for storing, sharing, and managing machine learning features with both online (low-latency) and offline (batch) stores.
SageMaker Training
Managed training infrastructure for running ML training jobs at scale with support for distributed training, spot instances, and automatic model tuning.
SageMaker Pipelines
CI/CD service purpose-built for machine learning that enables creation, automation, and management of end-to-end ML workflows.
SageMaker Model Monitor
Continuous monitoring service that automatically detects and alerts on data quality issues and model drift in production.
SageMaker Data Wrangler
ML Lifecycle Stage: Data Preparation | Primary Function: Import, visualize, transform data | Code Required: No (visual interface) | Key Capabilities: 300+ transformations, visual analysis, export to pipeline | Integration: S3, Redshift, Athena, Snowflake, Databricks | Cost Model: ml.m5.4xlarge instance cost
SageMaker Feature Store
ML Lifecycle Stage: Feature Engineering / Management | Primary Function: Store and serve features consistently | Code Required: Yes (SDK) | Key Capabilities: Feature reuse, point-in-time correctness, online/offline stores | Integration: SageMaker Training, Processing, Pipelines | Cost Model: Storage + read/write operations
SageMaker Training
ML Lifecycle Stage: Model Training | Primary Function: Execute training jobs at scale | Code Required: Yes (training scripts) | Key Capabilities: Distributed training, spot instances, hyperparameter tuning | Integration: S3, EFS, FSx, Experiments | Cost Model: Per instance-hour of training
SageMaker Pipelines
ML Lifecycle Stage: MLOps / Orchestration | Primary Function: Automate ML workflows | Code Required: Yes (Python SDK) | Key Capabilities: Conditional steps, caching, parallel execution, scheduling | Integration: All SageMaker components, Step Functions | Cost Model: Based on underlying resources
SageMaker Model Monitor
ML Lifecycle Stage: Model Monitoring | Primary Function: Monitor model performance in production | Code Required: Minimal (configuration) | Key Capabilities: Data quality, model quality, bias drift, explainability | Integration: CloudWatch, SNS, Lambda | Cost Model: Monitoring schedule instance cost
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#61 ML Lifecycle and MLOps Support Tools
MLOps & Lifecycle AIF-C01 ordering aif_d1_ml_lifecycle_tools 6 cols 11 rows
▼

Comparison of SageMaker features across the ML lifecycle

Feature Amazon SageMaker SageMaker Data Wrangler SageMaker Feature Store SageMaker Model Monitor Amazon Augmented AI (A2I) SageMaker Pipelines
Lifecycle role Core ML platform Data prep Feature management Production monitoring Human review Orchestration — defines and automates end-to-end ML workflows from data prep through deployment
Best exam clue Build/train/deploy ML Prepare and transform data Reuse and govern features Detect drift or monitor predictions Human in the loop 'Automate ML workflow', 'CI/CD for ML', 'orchestrate training and deployment steps'
When it shines End-to-end ML workflows Early pipeline preparation Consistent features across teams Production oversight Escalation for uncertain outputs Repeatable ML workflows that need automation, versioning, and audit trails across teams
Not primarily for Manual human review routing Live model serving End-user dashboards Training data transformation Standalone model training Ad-hoc experimentation or notebook-based exploration — better suited for SageMaker Studio notebooks
Common trap Forgetting it is the umbrella platform Confusing it with monitoring Treating it as a raw data lake Using it when the issue is feature reuse Thinking it replaces model evaluation altogether Confusing with Step Functions (general orchestration) or CodePipeline (code CI/CD) — Pipelines is ML-specific
ML Phase All phases — end-to-end ML platform covering data prep, training, tuning, deployment, and monitoring Data preparation — visual interface for data selection, cleaning, transformation, and feature engineering Feature management — centralized repository for storing, sharing, and serving ML features Production - post-deployment monitoring Post-deployment — human review of ML predictions for quality assurance and edge cases Automation - connects all phases
Key Function Build, train, and deploy ML models at scale with managed infrastructure, built-in algorithms, and IDE (Studio) Visual data prep with 300+ built-in transformations; import from S3, Redshift, Athena, Snowflake Store, discover, and serve features for training (offline store) and inference (online store) with low latency Data quality monitoring, feature attribution drift Route low-confidence ML predictions to human reviewers; integrates with Rekognition, Textract, and custom models Workflow orchestration, repeatable training/deployment
Integration S3, ECR, IAM, CloudWatch, Step Functions, Lambda, all SageMaker components S3, Redshift, Athena, Snowflake, SageMaker Feature Store, SageMaker Pipelines SageMaker Studio, SageMaker Pipelines, S3 (offline store), DynamoDB-backed (online store) Integrates with Clarify for bias detection SageMaker, Rekognition, Textract, Lambda, S3 for review results, custom task UIs Uses SageMaker Processing, Training, Evaluation steps
Versioning Model Registry for model versioning; Experiments for tracking training runs Flow files versioned in SageMaker Studio; export to S3 for pipeline reproducibility Built-in feature versioning with timestamps; supports point-in-time queries for training consistency Monitoring schedules and baseline comparisons Workflow definitions versioned; review results stored with timestamps in S3 Pipeline versions for reproducibility
Automation SageMaker Pipelines for workflow automation; Autopilot for automated model building (AutoML) Export data flows to SageMaker Pipelines for automated and scheduled data prep jobs Automated feature ingestion via Pipelines; scheduled batch feature computation Automated monitoring with scheduled jobs Automatically routes low-confidence predictions to human reviewers based on configurable thresholds Fully automated ML workflows
DevOps Equivalent Full IDE + CI/CD platform — like GitHub + Jenkins + Docker combined for ML ETL tool — like Apache Spark or Pandas with a visual UI for ML-specific data prep Feature registry — like a data catalog (Hive Metastore) but purpose-built for ML features Production monitoring (like Datadog) QA review queue — like a human-in-the-loop approval gate in a CI/CD pipeline CI/CD pipeline (like Jenkins, GitLab CI)
💡 Mnemonics

S.P.M - Studio develops, Pipelines automate, Model Monitor watches production

⚠️ Exam Tips

For AIF-C01, understand MLOps as part of the ML lifecycle: experiment, train, deploy, monitor

SageMaker Pipelines provides repeatable ML workflows with model lineage tracking

Model Monitor detects data drift and automatically alerts when model quality degrades

Feature Store (SageMaker Feature Store) centralizes feature management for reuse across teams

MLOps on AWS combines CI/CD principles with ML-specific requirements like data validation

Amazon SageMaker
SageMaker is the core managed ML platform for building, training, and deploying ML solutions. On the exam, treat it as the umbrella service for ML workflows on AWS.
SageMaker Data Wrangler
Data Wrangler focuses on data preparation and transformation for ML workflows. It is the exam clue when the pain point is data shaping rather than model deployment.
SageMaker Feature Store
Feature Store centralizes and manages reusable ML features. It helps with consistency between training and inference data features.
SageMaker Model Monitor
Model Monitor watches deployed model behavior and data quality over time. It is the right answer when performance drift or production monitoring is the issue.
Amazon Augmented AI (A2I)
A2I adds human review into AI/ML workflows. It is useful when confidence thresholds or responsible review loops matter.
SageMaker Pipelines
A CI/CD service for ML that automates the full ML workflow from data preparation to model deployment with reproducible pipelines.
Amazon SageMaker
Lifecycle role: Core ML platform | Best exam clue: Build/train/deploy ML | When it shines: End-to-end ML workflows | Not primarily for: Manual human review routing | Common trap: Forgetting it is the umbrella platform | ML Phase: All phases — end-to-end ML platform covering data prep, training, tuning, deployment, and monitoring | Key Function: Build, train, and deploy ML models at scale with managed infrastructure, built-in algorithms, and IDE (Studio) | Integration: S3, ECR, IAM, CloudWatch, Step Functions, Lambda, all SageMaker components | Versioning: Model Registry for model versioning; Experiments for tracking training runs | Automation: SageMaker Pipelines for workflow automation; Autopilot for automated model building (AutoML) | DevOps Equivalent: Full IDE + CI/CD platform — like GitHub + Jenkins + Docker combined for ML
SageMaker Data Wrangler
Lifecycle role: Data prep | Best exam clue: Prepare and transform data | When it shines: Early pipeline preparation | Not primarily for: Live model serving | Common trap: Confusing it with monitoring | ML Phase: Data preparation — visual interface for data selection, cleaning, transformation, and feature engineering | Key Function: Visual data prep with 300+ built-in transformations; import from S3, Redshift, Athena, Snowflake | Integration: S3, Redshift, Athena, Snowflake, SageMaker Feature Store, SageMaker Pipelines | Versioning: Flow files versioned in SageMaker Studio; export to S3 for pipeline reproducibility | Automation: Export data flows to SageMaker Pipelines for automated and scheduled data prep jobs | DevOps Equivalent: ETL tool — like Apache Spark or Pandas with a visual UI for ML-specific data prep
SageMaker Feature Store
Lifecycle role: Feature management | Best exam clue: Reuse and govern features | When it shines: Consistent features across teams | Not primarily for: End-user dashboards | Common trap: Treating it as a raw data lake | ML Phase: Feature management — centralized repository for storing, sharing, and serving ML features | Key Function: Store, discover, and serve features for training (offline store) and inference (online store) with low latency | Integration: SageMaker Studio, SageMaker Pipelines, S3 (offline store), DynamoDB-backed (online store) | Versioning: Built-in feature versioning with timestamps; supports point-in-time queries for training consistency | Automation: Automated feature ingestion via Pipelines; scheduled batch feature computation | DevOps Equivalent: Feature registry — like a data catalog (Hive Metastore) but purpose-built for ML features
SageMaker Model Monitor
Lifecycle role: Production monitoring | Best exam clue: Detect drift or monitor predictions | When it shines: Production oversight | Not primarily for: Training data transformation | Common trap: Using it when the issue is feature reuse | ML Phase: Production - post-deployment monitoring | Key Function: Data quality monitoring, feature attribution drift | Integration: Integrates with Clarify for bias detection | Versioning: Monitoring schedules and baseline comparisons | Automation: Automated monitoring with scheduled jobs | DevOps Equivalent: Production monitoring (like Datadog)
Amazon Augmented AI (A2I)
Lifecycle role: Human review | Best exam clue: Human in the loop | When it shines: Escalation for uncertain outputs | Not primarily for: Standalone model training | Common trap: Thinking it replaces model evaluation altogether | ML Phase: Post-deployment — human review of ML predictions for quality assurance and edge cases | Key Function: Route low-confidence ML predictions to human reviewers; integrates with Rekognition, Textract, and custom models | Integration: SageMaker, Rekognition, Textract, Lambda, S3 for review results, custom task UIs | Versioning: Workflow definitions versioned; review results stored with timestamps in S3 | Automation: Automatically routes low-confidence predictions to human reviewers based on configurable thresholds | DevOps Equivalent: QA review queue — like a human-in-the-loop approval gate in a CI/CD pipeline
SageMaker Pipelines
Lifecycle role: Orchestration — defines and automates end-to-end ML workflows from data prep through deployment | Best exam clue: 'Automate ML workflow', 'CI/CD for ML', 'orchestrate training and deployment steps' | When it shines: Repeatable ML workflows that need automation, versioning, and audit trails across teams | Not primarily for: Ad-hoc experimentation or notebook-based exploration — better suited for SageMaker Studio notebooks | Common trap: Confusing with Step Functions (general orchestration) or CodePipeline (code CI/CD) — Pipelines is ML-specific | ML Phase: Automation - connects all phases | Key Function: Workflow orchestration, repeatable training/deployment | Integration: Uses SageMaker Processing, Training, Evaluation steps | Versioning: Pipeline versions for reproducibility | Automation: Fully automated ML workflows | DevOps Equivalent: CI/CD pipeline (like Jenkins, GitLab CI)
🎯 Why This Table Matters

AIF Domain 1 includes the ML lifecycle and MLOps concepts. This table separates the core platform from the adjacent data prep, feature storage, monitoring, and human review helpers.

#62 MLOps & Model Management Services Comparison
MLOps & Lifecycle AIF-C01 ordering mlops_model_management_services_comparison 4 cols 4 rows
▼

Comparing AWS services for machine learning operations and model lifecycle management

Feature SageMaker Model Monitor SageMaker Clarify SageMaker Model Cards SageMaker Feature Store
Purpose Monitor deployed model quality Detect bias in data and models Document models for stakeholders Store and share ML features
When to Use After model deployment During development and after training Throughout model lifecycle During feature engineering
Key Metrics Data quality, feature attribution drift Bias metrics across demographics Model info, performance, warnings Feature values, serving latency
Integration With deployed SageMaker endpoints With SageMaker Data Wrangler Part of SageMaker ecosystem Training and inference pipelines
💡 Mnemonics

MCMF = Monitor quality, Clarify bias, Model cards for docs, Features for reuse

⚠️ Exam Tips

Model Monitor detects when model predictions degrade over time

Clarify is key for responsible AI - detects bias before deployment

Model Cards provide standardized documentation for compliance

Feature Store prevents feature inconsistency between training and inference

These services support Domain 4 (Responsible AI) and Domain 5 (Security/Governance)

SageMaker Model Monitor
A capability that continuously monitors model quality over time, detecting data drift and model degradation to maintain prediction accuracy.
SageMaker Clarify
A service that helps detect potential bias during data preparation and after model training, supporting responsible AI practices.
SageMaker Model Cards
A feature that creates standardized documentation for ML models, capturing purpose, performance metrics, and relevant warnings for stakeholders.
SageMaker Feature Store
A centralized repository for storing and serving machine learning features, enabling feature reuse and consistency across training and inference.
SageMaker Model Monitor
Purpose: Monitor deployed model quality | When to Use: After model deployment | Key Metrics: Data quality, feature attribution drift | Integration: With deployed SageMaker endpoints
SageMaker Clarify
Purpose: Detect bias in data and models | When to Use: During development and after training | Key Metrics: Bias metrics across demographics | Integration: With SageMaker Data Wrangler
SageMaker Model Cards
Purpose: Document models for stakeholders | When to Use: Throughout model lifecycle | Key Metrics: Model info, performance, warnings | Integration: Part of SageMaker ecosystem
SageMaker Feature Store
Purpose: Store and share ML features | When to Use: During feature engineering | Key Metrics: Feature values, serving latency | Integration: Training and inference pipelines

#63 MLOps Pipeline Stages
MLOps & Lifecycle AIF-C01 ordering AIF-007_2 6 cols 7 rows
▼

Comparing MLOps pipeline stages and AWS services - Domain 1 (Fundamentals of AI and ML)

Feature Stage Data Collection Feature Engineering Model Training Model Deployment Monitoring
Purpose Gather and store raw data Transform raw data into features Train ML model on features Make model available for inference Track model performance Continuously track model performance, detect drift, and ensure reliability in production
Key Activities Data ingestion, labeling, storage Feature extraction, transformation, selection Algorithm selection, hyperparameter tuning Endpoint creation, scaling, versioning Drift detection, alerting, retraining Track prediction accuracy, detect data/concept drift, monitor latency and throughput, set up alerts for model degradation
AWS Service S3, Kinesis, Glue SageMaker Data Wrangler, Feature Store SageMaker Training Jobs SageMaker Endpoints, Bedrock SageMaker Model Monitor Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor, Amazon CloudWatch, SageMaker Clarify (bias drift)
Data Flow Raw data → S3/Data Lakes Features → Feature Store Model artifacts → S3 Model → Endpoint Metrics → CloudWatch Production inference data captured → compared against baseline → drift metrics and alerts generated
Automation Level Manual to automated pipelines Automated feature pipelines Automated training pipelines CI/CD deployment Continuous monitoring Fully automated — scheduled monitoring jobs with automatic alerting on threshold breaches
Key Metrics Data quality, completeness Feature importance, correlation Loss, accuracy, F1 score Latency, throughput, errors Drift, accuracy degradation Prediction accuracy, data quality, feature drift, bias metrics, latency (p50/p99), error rates
Cost Driver Storage, data transfer Compute for transformation GPU/instance hours Endpoint instance costs Compute for monitoring CloudWatch metrics/alarms, SageMaker Model Monitor processing jobs, data capture storage in S3
💡 Mnemonics

C-F-T-D-M: Collect, Feature-engineer, Train, Deploy, Monitor

MLOps is cyclic: Collect data → Engineer features → Train model → Deploy → Monitor → Retrain (back to start when drift detected)

⚠️ Exam Tips

Feature Store separates feature engineering from training for reusability

SageMaker Data Wrangler provides no-code feature engineering

Model Monitor detects drift and triggers retraining alerts

Multi-model endpoints reduce costs for models with low traffic

Canary deployments gradually shift traffic to new model versions

MLOps aims to make ML development repeatable and scalable

Data Collection
The foundational stage of the MLOps pipeline where raw data is gathered from various sources, labeled if needed, and stored in a data lake (S3) for subsequent processing. Key considerations include data quality, completeness, and governance.
Feature Engineering
The process of transforming raw data into meaningful features that improve model performance. SageMaker Feature Store enables feature reuse across models and teams, while Data Wrangler provides visual tools for feature transformation.
Model Training
The core ML development stage where algorithms learn patterns from prepared features. SageMaker provides managed training jobs with automatic scaling, distributed training, and hyperparameter optimization capabilities.
Model Deployment
Making trained models available for inference through endpoints or batch processing. Options include real-time endpoints, serverless inference, and batch transform jobs, with traffic management for safe rollouts.
Monitoring
Continuous observation of deployed model performance to detect data drift, concept drift, and accuracy degradation. CloudWatch and Model Monitor provide alerts when performance falls below thresholds, triggering potential retraining.
Stage
Purpose: Gather and store raw data | Key Activities: Data ingestion, labeling, storage | AWS Service: S3, Kinesis, Glue | Data Flow: Raw data → S3/Data Lakes | Automation Level: Manual to automated pipelines | Key Metrics: Data quality, completeness | Cost Driver: Storage, data transfer
Data Collection
Purpose: Transform raw data into features | Key Activities: Feature extraction, transformation, selection | AWS Service: SageMaker Data Wrangler, Feature Store | Data Flow: Features → Feature Store | Automation Level: Automated feature pipelines | Key Metrics: Feature importance, correlation | Cost Driver: Compute for transformation
Feature Engineering
Purpose: Train ML model on features | Key Activities: Algorithm selection, hyperparameter tuning | AWS Service: SageMaker Training Jobs | Data Flow: Model artifacts → S3 | Automation Level: Automated training pipelines | Key Metrics: Loss, accuracy, F1 score | Cost Driver: GPU/instance hours
Model Training
Purpose: Make model available for inference | Key Activities: Endpoint creation, scaling, versioning | AWS Service: SageMaker Endpoints, Bedrock | Data Flow: Model → Endpoint | Automation Level: CI/CD deployment | Key Metrics: Latency, throughput, errors | Cost Driver: Endpoint instance costs
Model Deployment
Purpose: Track model performance | Key Activities: Drift detection, alerting, retraining | AWS Service: SageMaker Model Monitor | Data Flow: Metrics → CloudWatch | Automation Level: Continuous monitoring | Key Metrics: Drift, accuracy degradation | Cost Driver: Compute for monitoring
Monitoring
Purpose: Continuously track model performance, detect drift, and ensure reliability in production | Key Activities: Track prediction accuracy, detect data/concept drift, monitor latency and throughput, set up alerts for model degradation | AWS Service: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor, Amazon CloudWatch, SageMaker Clarify (bias drift) | Data Flow: Production inference data captured → compared against baseline → drift metrics and alerts generated | Automation Level: Fully automated — scheduled monitoring jobs with automatic alerting on threshold breaches | Key Metrics: Prediction accuracy, data quality, feature drift, bias metrics, latency (p50/p99), error rates | Cost Driver: CloudWatch metrics/alarms, SageMaker Model Monitor processing jobs, data capture storage in S3
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json

Compute 10 tables
#64 AWS Compute Services Comparison
Compute SAA-003 4 cols 13 rows
▼

Comparing AWS compute services for different workload requirements

This table compares AWS compute options. EC2 provides resizable virtual servers with full control, Lambda enables serverless code execution without managing servers, ECS and EKS offer container orchestration solutions, and Fargate provides serverless compute for containers.

Feature Amazon EC2 AWS Lambda Amazon ECS/Fargate Amazon EKS
Compute Model Virtual machines (you manage OS) Serverless functions (event-driven) Serverless containers (no server management) Managed Kubernetes
Server Management User manages container hosts (or use Fargate) Fully managed, no servers Fully managed, no cluster management AWS manages control plane; user manages worker nodes
Scaling Auto Scaling Groups, manual, scheduled Automatic, event-triggered scaling Automatic scaling based on task count Cluster Autoscaler, Karpenter
Pricing Per second/hour (instance type) Per invocation + execution time Per vCPU-second + memory-second Control plane + worker nodes
Cold Start Minutes (if using EC2); Fast (if using Fargate) Milliseconds to seconds Lower cold starts than Lambda Minutes (node provisioning)
Max Execution Time Unlimited 15 minutes Unlimited Unlimited
State Stateful or stateless Stateless only Stateful or stateless Stateful or stateless
Best For Long-running apps, full control Event-driven, microservices Docker containers, microservices Kubernetes workloads, portability
Container Support Docker on EC2 Container image support Native container support Kubernetes (pod-based)
Billing Model Per second (minimum 60s) when running Per request + duration (ms) Per vCPU and memory per second for Fargate tasks; EC2 launch type bills for underlying instances $0.10/hour per cluster + EC2/Fargate costs
Maximum Runtime No limit (persistent) 15 minutes per invocation No time limit — tasks and services run indefinitely until stopped Unlimited (pod lifetime)
Use Cases Web servers, applications requiring full OS control, legacy apps Event processing, APIs, automation, microservices Containerized microservices, API backends, batch processing, long-running services without managing servers Kubernetes-native applications, multi-cloud portability
Control Level Full control (OS, runtime, networking) Code only (AWS manages everything else) Medium — Fargate abstracts infrastructure; choose vCPU/memory per task; no OS access High — full Kubernetes control plane; manage node groups or use Fargate; custom networking and scheduling
💡 Mnemonics

EC2 = Elastic Compute Cloud, Lambda = Light code execution, ECS = Elastic Container Service, EKS = Elastic Kubernetes Service

Need full control? EC2. Need event-driven? Lambda. Already using Docker? ECS. Already using Kubernetes? EKS. Want serverless containers? Fargate.

ELF = EC2 for Lift-and-shift, Lambda for Lightweight, Fargate for Containers

⚠️ Exam Tips

Lambda is best for event-driven workloads with execution under 15 minutes

Fargate eliminates the need to manage EC2 instances for containers

EC2 provides maximum control but requires more management

EKS is ideal when you need Kubernetes compatibility or portability

Lambda cold starts affect only the first request after idle period

ECS with Fargate = serverless containers without managing instances

Lambda is ideal for event-driven, short-duration tasks

Lambda has a 15-minute maximum execution time - not for long-running processes

Fargate is the serverless option for containers - no EC2 management

ECS/EKS + Fargate = containerized apps without managing servers

For unpredictable traffic with short requests → Lambda; For consistent traffic → EC2

Amazon EC2
A web service providing secure, resizable compute capacity in the cloud. Offers complete control over the operating system and networking, with a wide selection of instance types optimized for different use cases. Supports On-Demand, Reserved, and Spot pricing models.
AWS Lambda
A serverless compute service that runs code in response to events and automatically manages the underlying compute resources. Scales automatically from a few requests per day to thousands per second, with pay-per-use pricing and no server management required.
Amazon ECS/Fargate
A fully managed container orchestration service. ECS provides the orchestration layer, while Fargate is a serverless compute engine that removes the need to provision and manage servers. Ideal for running Docker containers without managing infrastructure.
Amazon EKS
A managed Kubernetes service that makes it easy to run Kubernetes on AWS. Provides a managed control plane with automatic upgrades and patches, supporting both EC2 and Fargate for worker nodes. Best for organizations with existing Kubernetes expertise and workloads.
Amazon EC2
Compute Model: Virtual machines (you manage OS) | Server Management: User manages container hosts (or use Fargate) | Scaling: Auto Scaling Groups, manual, scheduled | Pricing: Per second/hour (instance type) | Cold Start: Minutes (if using EC2); Fast (if using Fargate) | Max Execution Time: Unlimited | State: Stateful or stateless | Best For: Long-running apps, full control | Container Support: Docker on EC2 | Billing Model: Per second (minimum 60s) when running | Maximum Runtime: No limit (persistent) | Use Cases: Web servers, applications requiring full OS control, legacy apps | Control Level: Full control (OS, runtime, networking)
AWS Lambda
Compute Model: Serverless functions (event-driven) | Server Management: Fully managed, no servers | Scaling: Automatic, event-triggered scaling | Pricing: Per invocation + execution time | Cold Start: Milliseconds to seconds | Max Execution Time: 15 minutes | State: Stateless only | Best For: Event-driven, microservices | Container Support: Container image support | Billing Model: Per request + duration (ms) | Maximum Runtime: 15 minutes per invocation | Use Cases: Event processing, APIs, automation, microservices | Control Level: Code only (AWS manages everything else)
Amazon ECS/Fargate
Compute Model: Serverless containers (no server management) | Server Management: Fully managed, no cluster management | Scaling: Automatic scaling based on task count | Pricing: Per vCPU-second + memory-second | Cold Start: Lower cold starts than Lambda | Max Execution Time: Unlimited | State: Stateful or stateless | Best For: Docker containers, microservices | Container Support: Native container support | Billing Model: Per vCPU and memory per second for Fargate tasks; EC2 launch type bills for underlying instances | Maximum Runtime: No time limit — tasks and services run indefinitely until stopped | Use Cases: Containerized microservices, API backends, batch processing, long-running services without managing servers | Control Level: Medium — Fargate abstracts infrastructure; choose vCPU/memory per task; no OS access
Amazon EKS
Compute Model: Managed Kubernetes | Server Management: AWS manages control plane; user manages worker nodes | Scaling: Cluster Autoscaler, Karpenter | Pricing: Control plane + worker nodes | Cold Start: Minutes (node provisioning) | Max Execution Time: Unlimited | State: Stateful or stateless | Best For: Kubernetes workloads, portability | Container Support: Kubernetes (pod-based) | Billing Model: $0.10/hour per cluster + EC2/Fargate costs | Maximum Runtime: Unlimited (pod lifetime) | Use Cases: Kubernetes-native applications, multi-cloud portability | Control Level: High — full Kubernetes control plane; manage node groups or use Fargate; custom networking and scheduling
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#65 AWS Reserved vs Savings Plans vs Spot
Compute SAA-008 4 cols 9 rows
▼

Comparing AWS purchasing options for cost optimization - Domain 4 (Cost-Optimized Architectures)

Feature On-Demand Reserved Instances Savings Plans Spot Instances
Commitment None 1 or 3 years 1 or 3 years None
Discount 0% (base price) Up to 72% off Up to 72% off Up to 90% off
Flexibility Highest - pay per hour Limited - specific instance type High - instance family flexibility Low - can be interrupted
Use Case Variable workloads, testing Steady-state workloads Flexible compute needs Fault-tolerant, flexible workloads
Payment Options Per hour All upfront, partial, no upfront Hourly commitment ($/hour) Per hour (interruptible)
Instance Selection Any instance Specific instance type/size Any instance in family/region Available capacity pools
Cancellation Anytime Cannot cancel (can sell) Cannot cancel 2-minute warning interruption
Best For Unpredictable workloads Long-term, predictable workloads Multiple instance types needed Batch jobs, testing, stateless
Coverage Scope EC2, Lambda, other services EC2, RDS, Redshift, ElastiCache Compute, EC2, SageMaker EC2 only
💡 Mnemonics

OD = On-Demand (flexible), RI = Reserved Instances (specific), SP = Savings Plans (flexible commitment), Spot = Interruptible savings

No commitment needed? On-Demand. Know exact instance type? Reserved Instances. Flexible on instance type? Savings Plans. Can handle interruptions? Spot.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Savings Plans are more flexible than Reserved Instances

Spot Instances can be interrupted with 2-minute warning

Reserved Instances are not cancelled - they are a commitment

Compute Savings Plans apply to EC2, Lambda, and Fargate

Spot Blocks (previously defined duration) are deprecated

Use Spot for stateless, fault-tolerant, or flexible workloads

On-Demand
Pay for compute capacity by the hour or second with no long-term commitments or upfront payments. Ideal for applications with unpredictable workloads, development/testing, or when you need to try new instance types.
Reserved Instances
A commitment to a specific instance type in a particular Availability Zone for a 1 or 3-year term in exchange for significant discounts. Best for steady-state workloads where you know your exact instance requirements.
Savings Plans
A flexible pricing model offering savings in exchange for a commitment to a consistent amount of usage (measured in $/hour) for 1 or 3 years. Applies automatically across instance families, sizes, and regions, providing more flexibility than RIs.
Spot Instances
Unused EC2 capacity available at up to 90% discount. Can be interrupted with a 2-minute warning when AWS needs the capacity back. Ideal for fault-tolerant, flexible, or stateless workloads like batch processing and testing.
On-Demand
Commitment: None | Discount: 0% (base price) | Flexibility: Highest - pay per hour | Use Case: Variable workloads, testing | Payment Options: Per hour | Instance Selection: Any instance | Cancellation: Anytime | Best For: Unpredictable workloads | Coverage Scope: EC2, Lambda, other services
Reserved Instances
Commitment: 1 or 3 years | Discount: Up to 72% off | Flexibility: Limited - specific instance type | Use Case: Steady-state workloads | Payment Options: All upfront, partial, no upfront | Instance Selection: Specific instance type/size | Cancellation: Cannot cancel (can sell) | Best For: Long-term, predictable workloads | Coverage Scope: EC2, RDS, Redshift, ElastiCache
Savings Plans
Commitment: 1 or 3 years | Discount: Up to 72% off | Flexibility: High - instance family flexibility | Use Case: Flexible compute needs | Payment Options: Hourly commitment ($/hour) | Instance Selection: Any instance in family/region | Cancellation: Cannot cancel | Best For: Multiple instance types needed | Coverage Scope: Compute, EC2, SageMaker
Spot Instances
Commitment: None | Discount: Up to 90% off | Flexibility: Low - can be interrupted | Use Case: Fault-tolerant, flexible workloads | Payment Options: Per hour (interruptible) | Instance Selection: Available capacity pools | Cancellation: 2-minute warning interruption | Best For: Batch jobs, testing, stateless | Coverage Scope: EC2 only
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json

#66 Compute Decision Matrix
Compute compute_decision_matrix 5 cols 20 rows
▼

When building AI/ML solutions on AWS, architects must choose between three main approaches: Amazon SageMaker for custom ML model development, Amazon Bedrock for foundation model-based generative AI applications, and Pre-built AI Services (like Rekognition, Comprehend, Transcribe) for specific use cases. Understanding when to use each approach is critical for exam success and real-world architecture decisions.

Feature Amazon EC2 AWS Lambda Amazon ECS Amazon EKS Pre-built AI Services
Customization Level Medium-High - Fine-tuning, RAG, prompt engineering Function (Code execution) Container (Docker workloads) Container (Kubernetes workloads) Low-Medium - Limited to service capabilities
Management Overhead High (OS patching, scaling, monitoring) None (fully managed) Low to Medium (depends on launch type) Medium to High (Kubernetes complexity) None — fully managed API; no infrastructure, scaling, or model management required
Scaling Speed Minutes (ASG scaling) Milliseconds to seconds Seconds to minutes (Fargate faster) Minutes (node provisioning) Instant — auto-scales behind the API; no capacity planning needed
Maximum Runtime Unlimited (persistent) 15 minutes Unlimited (with Fargate or EC2) Unlimited (with Fargate or EC2) Per-request — API call completes in seconds; no long-running process management
Cost Model Per hour/second (always running) Per request + duration (GB-seconds) EC2 costs OR Fargate per-use pricing $0.10/hour + EC2/Fargate costs Pay-per-API-call — charged per image analyzed, per character translated, per minute transcribed, etc.
State Persistence Full persistence (EBS, instance store) Ephemeral (use external storage) Configurable (volumes, EFS) Full persistence options (PV/PVC) Stateless — each API call is independent; results stored by the caller
Multi-Cloud Portability Manual configuration required Low (AWS-specific) Low (AWS-specific) High (standard Kubernetes) Fully managed auto-scaling
Learning Curve Medium (Linux/Windows admin) Low (simple function model) Low to Medium (Docker basics) High (Kubernetes expertise) Lowest — simple REST API calls; no ML expertise needed; SDKs available for all languages
Best For Lift-and-shift, legacy apps, full control Event-driven, APIs, automation, short tasks Containerized microservices, simple orchestration Complex container apps, K8s ecosystem, portability Standard ML tasks (image recognition, NLP, translation, speech) where pre-trained models are sufficient
When to Choose Generative AI needs, natural language tasks, creative applications Event triggers, <15 min runtime, cost optimization Simple containers, AWS-native, quick to market K8s required, complex scheduling, multi-cloud Standard AI tasks, quick deployment, cost predictability
Not Suitable For Rapid scaling needs, minimal management Long-running tasks, persistent connections, ML training Non-container workloads, complex K8s features Simple apps, teams without K8s expertise Custom model architectures, proprietary algorithms, fine-grained model control, domain-specific models requiring custom training
Primary Use Case Full control compute — custom ML environments, GPU-based training, legacy applications, OS-level customization Event-driven compute — data preprocessing, API backends, inference on small models, ETL triggers Container orchestration — microservices, ML serving containers, batch processing with Docker Kubernetes workloads — multi-cloud portability, complex container orchestration, ML platforms on K8s Solve specific AI tasks without model building
ML Expertise Required High — must configure ML frameworks, drivers (CUDA), dependencies, and infrastructure manually Low to moderate — simple inference; limited by 15-min timeout, 10GB memory, and deployment package size Moderate — containerize ML models; manage task definitions, service scaling, and networking High — Kubernetes expertise plus ML; manage pods, GPU scheduling, model serving frameworks Low - Simple API calls
Development Time Longest — manual setup of environment, networking, security; full infrastructure responsibility Short — deploy function code; automatic scaling; minimal infrastructure setup Moderate — create Docker images, task definitions, and service configurations Long — Kubernetes manifests, Helm charts, cluster management, plus ML-specific configuration Hours to days
Time to Production Weeks — AMI creation, Auto Scaling groups, load balancers, monitoring setup Hours — deploy function, configure API Gateway or event source, auto-scaling built-in Days — build container images, configure services, ALB integration, scaling policies Weeks — cluster setup, node groups, ingress controllers, monitoring, plus K8s learning curve Immediate - API ready
Cost Structure Per-instance-hour — pay for provisioned capacity regardless of utilization; Spot for savings Per-request + duration — pay only when code runs; free tier of 1M requests/month Fargate: per vCPU/memory per second; EC2: per instance-hour + ECS service fee (none) $0.10/hr per cluster + EC2/Fargate per node; most expensive orchestration option Per-request or per-unit pricing
Data Requirements Any — full flexibility; local storage, EBS, S3, any data format; handle data pipeline yourself Small payloads — 6MB sync, 256KB async; use S3 or DynamoDB for larger datasets; /tmp is 10GB Container-accessible — mount EFS, EBS, or pull from S3; Fargate supports 200GB ephemeral storage Any — persistent volumes (EBS CSI, EFS CSI), S3, configurable storage classes in Kubernetes No training data needed
Model Ownership Full ownership — deploy any model, any framework; complete control over model artifacts and IP Full ownership — include model in deployment package or load from S3; limited by package size Full ownership — model baked into container image or loaded at runtime from S3/EFS Full ownership — model in container image, PVC, or downloaded from S3; same as any K8s workload No model ownership - managed service
Examples Custom deep learning training on GPU instances (P4d), legacy ML workloads, SageMaker-alternative DIY setups Image thumbnail generation, sentiment analysis on form submissions, lightweight inference APIs TensorFlow Serving containers, ML microservices with Fargate, batch scoring pipelines KubeFlow ML platform, multi-model serving with Triton on K8s, distributed training with Horovod Image recognition (Rekognition), Text analysis (Comprehend), Speech-to-text (Transcribe)
Exam Decision Tips Choose when question mentions 'full control', 'GPU', 'custom environment', 'BYOL', or 'existing on-premises migration' Choose for 'event-driven', 'serverless', 'short-running', 'no infrastructure management', <15 min runtime Choose for 'Docker containers', 'microservices', 'Fargate' (no servers); simpler than EKS Choose for 'Kubernetes', 'multi-cloud', 'existing K8s workloads', 'container orchestration at scale' Choose when question mentions specific task like 'extract text', 'analyze images'
⚠️ Exam Tips

EC2 when you need specific OS, GPU, or long-running processes exceeding Lambda limits

Lambda for event-driven, short-duration tasks under 15 minutes with unpredictable traffic

ECS for container workloads with deep AWS integration; EKS for Kubernetes portability

Pre-built AI services when you need AI capabilities without training or infrastructure

Cost hierarchy: Pre-built services < Lambda < Fargate < ECS/EC2 (management complexity increases left to right)

Amazon EC2
Virtual servers with full OS control and maximum customization. Choose EC2 when you need specific instance types, custom AMIs, GPU access, or long-running processes that exceed Lambda limits.
AWS Lambda
Serverless compute that runs code in response to events with automatic scaling. Best for short-duration tasks (up to 15 minutes), event-driven processing, and workloads with unpredictable traffic patterns.
Amazon ECS
AWS-native container orchestration with deep integration into AWS services like ALB, CloudWatch, and IAM. Simpler to learn than Kubernetes, ideal for teams committed to the AWS ecosystem.
Amazon EKS
Managed Kubernetes service for organizations with existing Kubernetes expertise or multi-cloud requirements. Provides full Kubernetes API compatibility and access to the Kubernetes ecosystem.
Pre-built AI Services
Managed AI/ML APIs (Rekognition, Comprehend, Polly, etc.) that require zero infrastructure management. Best for common AI tasks where custom model training is unnecessary.
Amazon EC2
Customization Level: Medium-High - Fine-tuning, RAG, prompt engineering | Management Overhead: High (OS patching, scaling, monitoring) | Scaling Speed: Minutes (ASG scaling) | Maximum Runtime: Unlimited (persistent) | Cost Model: Per hour/second (always running) | State Persistence: Full persistence (EBS, instance store) | Multi-Cloud Portability: Manual configuration required | Learning Curve: Medium (Linux/Windows admin) | Best For: Lift-and-shift, legacy apps, full control | When to Choose: Generative AI needs, natural language tasks, creative applications | Not Suitable For: Rapid scaling needs, minimal management | Primary Use Case: Full control compute — custom ML environments, GPU-based training, legacy applications, OS-level customization | ML Expertise Required: High — must configure ML frameworks, drivers (CUDA), dependencies, and infrastructure manually | Development Time: Longest — manual setup of environment, networking, security; full infrastructure responsibility | Time to Production: Weeks — AMI creation, Auto Scaling groups, load balancers, monitoring setup | Cost Structure: Per-instance-hour — pay for provisioned capacity regardless of utilization; Spot for savings | Data Requirements: Any — full flexibility; local storage, EBS, S3, any data format; handle data pipeline yourself | Model Ownership: Full ownership — deploy any model, any framework; complete control over model artifacts and IP | Examples: Custom deep learning training on GPU instances (P4d), legacy ML workloads, SageMaker-alternative DIY setups | Exam Decision Tips: Choose when question mentions 'full control', 'GPU', 'custom environment', 'BYOL', or 'existing on-premises migration'
AWS Lambda
Customization Level: Function (Code execution) | Management Overhead: None (fully managed) | Scaling Speed: Milliseconds to seconds | Maximum Runtime: 15 minutes | Cost Model: Per request + duration (GB-seconds) | State Persistence: Ephemeral (use external storage) | Multi-Cloud Portability: Low (AWS-specific) | Learning Curve: Low (simple function model) | Best For: Event-driven, APIs, automation, short tasks | When to Choose: Event triggers, <15 min runtime, cost optimization | Not Suitable For: Long-running tasks, persistent connections, ML training | Primary Use Case: Event-driven compute — data preprocessing, API backends, inference on small models, ETL triggers | ML Expertise Required: Low to moderate — simple inference; limited by 15-min timeout, 10GB memory, and deployment package size | Development Time: Short — deploy function code; automatic scaling; minimal infrastructure setup | Time to Production: Hours — deploy function, configure API Gateway or event source, auto-scaling built-in | Cost Structure: Per-request + duration — pay only when code runs; free tier of 1M requests/month | Data Requirements: Small payloads — 6MB sync, 256KB async; use S3 or DynamoDB for larger datasets; /tmp is 10GB | Model Ownership: Full ownership — include model in deployment package or load from S3; limited by package size | Examples: Image thumbnail generation, sentiment analysis on form submissions, lightweight inference APIs | Exam Decision Tips: Choose for 'event-driven', 'serverless', 'short-running', 'no infrastructure management', <15 min runtime
Amazon ECS
Customization Level: Container (Docker workloads) | Management Overhead: Low to Medium (depends on launch type) | Scaling Speed: Seconds to minutes (Fargate faster) | Maximum Runtime: Unlimited (with Fargate or EC2) | Cost Model: EC2 costs OR Fargate per-use pricing | State Persistence: Configurable (volumes, EFS) | Multi-Cloud Portability: Low (AWS-specific) | Learning Curve: Low to Medium (Docker basics) | Best For: Containerized microservices, simple orchestration | When to Choose: Simple containers, AWS-native, quick to market | Not Suitable For: Non-container workloads, complex K8s features | Primary Use Case: Container orchestration — microservices, ML serving containers, batch processing with Docker | ML Expertise Required: Moderate — containerize ML models; manage task definitions, service scaling, and networking | Development Time: Moderate — create Docker images, task definitions, and service configurations | Time to Production: Days — build container images, configure services, ALB integration, scaling policies | Cost Structure: Fargate: per vCPU/memory per second; EC2: per instance-hour + ECS service fee (none) | Data Requirements: Container-accessible — mount EFS, EBS, or pull from S3; Fargate supports 200GB ephemeral storage | Model Ownership: Full ownership — model baked into container image or loaded at runtime from S3/EFS | Examples: TensorFlow Serving containers, ML microservices with Fargate, batch scoring pipelines | Exam Decision Tips: Choose for 'Docker containers', 'microservices', 'Fargate' (no servers); simpler than EKS
Amazon EKS
Customization Level: Container (Kubernetes workloads) | Management Overhead: Medium to High (Kubernetes complexity) | Scaling Speed: Minutes (node provisioning) | Maximum Runtime: Unlimited (with Fargate or EC2) | Cost Model: $0.10/hour + EC2/Fargate costs | State Persistence: Full persistence options (PV/PVC) | Multi-Cloud Portability: High (standard Kubernetes) | Learning Curve: High (Kubernetes expertise) | Best For: Complex container apps, K8s ecosystem, portability | When to Choose: K8s required, complex scheduling, multi-cloud | Not Suitable For: Simple apps, teams without K8s expertise | Primary Use Case: Kubernetes workloads — multi-cloud portability, complex container orchestration, ML platforms on K8s | ML Expertise Required: High — Kubernetes expertise plus ML; manage pods, GPU scheduling, model serving frameworks | Development Time: Long — Kubernetes manifests, Helm charts, cluster management, plus ML-specific configuration | Time to Production: Weeks — cluster setup, node groups, ingress controllers, monitoring, plus K8s learning curve | Cost Structure: $0.10/hr per cluster + EC2/Fargate per node; most expensive orchestration option | Data Requirements: Any — persistent volumes (EBS CSI, EFS CSI), S3, configurable storage classes in Kubernetes | Model Ownership: Full ownership — model in container image, PVC, or downloaded from S3; same as any K8s workload | Examples: KubeFlow ML platform, multi-model serving with Triton on K8s, distributed training with Horovod | Exam Decision Tips: Choose for 'Kubernetes', 'multi-cloud', 'existing K8s workloads', 'container orchestration at scale'
Pre-built AI Services
Customization Level: Low-Medium - Limited to service capabilities | Management Overhead: None — fully managed API; no infrastructure, scaling, or model management required | Scaling Speed: Instant — auto-scales behind the API; no capacity planning needed | Maximum Runtime: Per-request — API call completes in seconds; no long-running process management | Cost Model: Pay-per-API-call — charged per image analyzed, per character translated, per minute transcribed, etc. | State Persistence: Stateless — each API call is independent; results stored by the caller | Multi-Cloud Portability: Fully managed auto-scaling | Learning Curve: Lowest — simple REST API calls; no ML expertise needed; SDKs available for all languages | Best For: Standard ML tasks (image recognition, NLP, translation, speech) where pre-trained models are sufficient | When to Choose: Standard AI tasks, quick deployment, cost predictability | Not Suitable For: Custom model architectures, proprietary algorithms, fine-grained model control, domain-specific models requiring custom training | Primary Use Case: Solve specific AI tasks without model building | ML Expertise Required: Low - Simple API calls | Development Time: Hours to days | Time to Production: Immediate - API ready | Cost Structure: Per-request or per-unit pricing | Data Requirements: No training data needed | Model Ownership: No model ownership - managed service | Examples: Image recognition (Rekognition), Text analysis (Comprehend), Speech-to-text (Transcribe) | Exam Decision Tips: Choose when question mentions specific task like 'extract text', 'analyze images'
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#67 EC2 Instance Families Comparison
Compute ec2_instance_families_comparison 4 cols 9 rows
▼

Comparison of EC2 instance families for different workload optimization

Feature General Purpose (T, M) Compute Optimized (C) Memory Optimized (R, X) Storage Optimized (D, I, H)
Ratio (vCPU:RAM) 1:2 (T4g) to 1:4 (M6i) depending on instance size 1:2 (C7i, C6i) - more CPU per RAM 1:8 (R7g) to 1:16 (X2gd) - more RAM per CPU 1:2 (D3) to 1:8 (I4i) - balanced with local NVMe
Best For Web servers, development environments, small databases Batch processing, scientific modeling, ML inference, gaming In-memory databases, big data (Spark, Hadoop), caching Data warehousing, log processing, distributed file systems
Cost per vCPU Baseline Higher - optimized for CPU Higher - optimized for RAM Moderate - balanced with storage focus
Burstable Options Yes - T instances have burst CPU capability No - consistent performance No - consistent performance No - consistent performance
Local Storage No (or minimal NVMe on some) Optional NVMe (C7i, C6i) No local (R7g) or NVMe (R6i) Yes - multiple NVMe drives (D3, I4i, H3)
Use in ML Light training, inference, development ML training (especially with GPU instances like C5) ML training with large datasets (R7g with more RAM) ML training data staging, preprocessing
Resource Balance Equal compute, memory, network High CPU, moderate memory Low CPU, very high memory Storage-heavy — high sequential read/write throughput and local NVMe SSD or HDD storage; moderate CPU/memory
Example Use t3.micro, m5.large c5.large, c6i.large r5.large, x2gd.large Data warehousing (D2/D3), high-IOPS NoSQL databases like Cassandra (I3/I4i), distributed file systems (H1), HDFS clusters
Cost Efficiency Good for most workloads Best CPU cost per dollar Best memory cost per dollar Cost-effective for storage-intensive workloads — local storage included in instance price; avoid EBS costs for ephemeral data
💡 Mnemonics

G.C.M.S - General for balanced, Compute for CPU tasks, Memory for RAM needs, Storage for disk-heavy work

GCMsT = General for Good balance, Compute for CPU-heavy, Memory for big data, Storage for IOPS

⚠️ Exam Tips

T instances (T3, T4g) are burstable - CPU credits used for spikes; good for intermittent workloads

For ML training, consider GPU instances (P5, P4, G5) rather than CPU-optimized

Storage optimized instances (D3, I4i) have NVMe drives ideal for ML data loading

R7g instances use Graviton3 processors - cost-effective for memory-intensive workloads

Always check current generation (current is 7th gen for most families)

T instances are burstable - good for unpredictable workloads

C instances have highest CPU performance per core

R instances have highest memory-to-CPU ratio

I and D instances have NVMe SSD local storage (ephemeral)

Graviton instances (M7g, C7g, R7g) offer better price performance using AWS ARM chips

General Purpose (T, M)
Instances providing a balanced mix of compute, memory, and network resources, suitable for diverse workloads including web servers and development environments.
Compute Optimized (C)
Instances designed for compute-intensive workloads requiring high CPU power, ideal for batch processing, scientific modeling, and gaming servers.
Memory Optimized (R, X)
Large memory footprints designed for memory-intensive applications like in-memory caches, real-time big data analytics, and high-performance databases.
Storage Optimized (D, I, H)
High disk throughput and IOPS for workloads requiring fast local storage including NoSQL databases, data warehousing, and log processing.
General Purpose (T, M)
Ratio (vCPU:RAM): 1:2 (T4g) to 1:4 (M6i) depending on instance size | Best For: Web servers, development environments, small databases | Cost per vCPU: Baseline | Burstable Options: Yes - T instances have burst CPU capability | Local Storage: No (or minimal NVMe on some) | Use in ML: Light training, inference, development | Resource Balance: Equal compute, memory, network | Example Use: t3.micro, m5.large | Cost Efficiency: Good for most workloads
Compute Optimized (C)
Ratio (vCPU:RAM): 1:2 (C7i, C6i) - more CPU per RAM | Best For: Batch processing, scientific modeling, ML inference, gaming | Cost per vCPU: Higher - optimized for CPU | Burstable Options: No - consistent performance | Local Storage: Optional NVMe (C7i, C6i) | Use in ML: ML training (especially with GPU instances like C5) | Resource Balance: High CPU, moderate memory | Example Use: c5.large, c6i.large | Cost Efficiency: Best CPU cost per dollar
Memory Optimized (R, X)
Ratio (vCPU:RAM): 1:8 (R7g) to 1:16 (X2gd) - more RAM per CPU | Best For: In-memory databases, big data (Spark, Hadoop), caching | Cost per vCPU: Higher - optimized for RAM | Burstable Options: No - consistent performance | Local Storage: No local (R7g) or NVMe (R6i) | Use in ML: ML training with large datasets (R7g with more RAM) | Resource Balance: Low CPU, very high memory | Example Use: r5.large, x2gd.large | Cost Efficiency: Best memory cost per dollar
Storage Optimized (D, I, H)
Ratio (vCPU:RAM): 1:2 (D3) to 1:8 (I4i) - balanced with local NVMe | Best For: Data warehousing, log processing, distributed file systems | Cost per vCPU: Moderate - balanced with storage focus | Burstable Options: No - consistent performance | Local Storage: Yes - multiple NVMe drives (D3, I4i, H3) | Use in ML: ML training data staging, preprocessing | Resource Balance: Storage-heavy — high sequential read/write throughput and local NVMe SSD or HDD storage; moderate CPU/memory | Example Use: Data warehousing (D2/D3), high-IOPS NoSQL databases like Cassandra (I3/I4i), distributed file systems (H1), HDFS clusters | Cost Efficiency: Cost-effective for storage-intensive workloads — local storage included in instance price; avoid EBS costs for ephemeral data
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#68 EC2 Instance Types Comparison
Compute ec2_instance_types_comparison 10 cols 8 rows
▼

Amazon EC2 offers a wide variety of instance types optimized for different workloads. Understanding the naming convention and characteristics of each family is crucial for the exam. The naming follows a pattern: [Family][Generation][Size] - for example, M6i.xlarge where M=General Purpose, 6=Generation, i=Intel processor, xlarge=size.

Feature T3/T3a (Burstable) M6i/M6g (General Purpose) C6i/C6g (Compute Optimized) R6i/R6g (Memory Optimized) X2idn/X2iedn (Memory Optimized) I4i (Storage Optimized) D3/D3en (Storage Optimized) P4d/P4de (Accelerated) G5/G6 (Accelerated) Inf2 (Accelerated)
vCPU to Memory Ratio 1:1 to 1:4 (varies by size) 1:4 (balanced) 1:2 1:8 1:16 to 1:32 1:8 1:4 1:8 to 1:16 1:4 1:4 to 1:8
Baseline Performance Baseline with CPU credits Consistent performance High compute performance High memory bandwidth Extreme memory performance High IOPS storage High throughput HDD Highest GPU performance Balanced GPU/CPU High ML inference
Specialized Hardware None None None None High memory bandwidth NVMe SSD (up to 75K IOPS) HDD with high throughput NVIDIA A100 GPUs, NVSwitch NVIDIA A10G/L4 GPUs AWS Inferentia2 chips
Best Use Cases Web servers, dev/test, microservices Application servers, small-medium databases Gaming servers, HPC, batch processing In-memory caches, databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL) SAP HANA, large in-memory databases NoSQL (Cassandra, MongoDB), data warehouses Hadoop, data lakes, log processing Deep learning training, HPC simulations ML inference, graphics workstations, gaming Cost-effective ML inference at scale
Network Performance Up to 5 Gbps Up to 50 Gbps Up to 50 Gbps Up to 50 Gbps Up to 100 Gbps Up to 40 Gbps Up to 100 Gbps 400 Gbps (EFA supported) Up to 100 Gbps Up to 100 Gbps
EBS Optimized Yes (default) Yes (default) Yes (default) Yes (default) Yes (default) N/A (instance storage) N/A (instance storage) Yes (default) Yes (default) Yes (default)
Pricing Model Lowest cost, pay per use with unlimited mode option Moderate, balanced price/performance Moderate, compute-focused pricing Higher due to memory Premium for extreme memory Premium for high IOPS storage Moderate for high capacity Highest cost, GPU premium High, but lower than P-series Lowest cost for ML inference
Processor Type Intel Xeon / AMD EPYC Intel Ice Lake / Graviton2/3 Intel Ice Lake / Graviton2/3 Intel Ice Lake / Graviton2/3 Intel Ice Lake Intel Ice Lake Intel Ice Lake Intel Cascade Lake + A100 GPUs AMD EPYC + NVIDIA GPUs AWS Inferentia2 + Intel Xeon
⚠️ Exam Tips

T-series = burstable (web servers, dev); M-series = general purpose (balanced workloads)

C-series = compute optimized (batch, scientific); R-series = memory optimized (databases, caching)

I-series = storage optimized (databases); P/G-series = GPU (ML training/inference)

Inf2 = AWS Inferentia chips specifically designed for cost-effective ML inference

Graviton (g suffix) instances provide up to 40% better price-performance than x86

For the exam, match the workload description to the instance family by its primary characteristic

T3/T3a (Burstable)
General-purpose burstable instances that provide a baseline CPU performance with the ability to burst above the baseline. Cost-effective for workloads that don't consistently use full CPU — web servers, dev/test environments.
M6i/M6g (General Purpose)
Balanced compute, memory, and networking for a broad range of workloads. M6g uses ARM-based Graviton2 processors for up to 40% better price-performance than x86 equivalents.
C6i/C6g (Compute Optimized)
High-performance processors ideal for compute-intensive workloads: batch processing, scientific modeling, gaming servers, machine learning inference, and video encoding.
R6i/R6g (Memory Optimized)
Designed for memory-intensive workloads with a high memory-to-vCPU ratio. Ideal for in-memory databases (Redis, Memcached), real-time big data analytics, and large-scale caching.
X2idn/X2iedn (Memory Optimized)
Extreme memory instances offering up to 4 TB of RAM. Designed for SAP HANA, large in-memory databases, and workloads requiring enormous memory capacity.
I4i (Storage Optimized)
Instances with NVMe SSD storage optimized for high random I/O operations. Ideal for transactional databases, NoSQL databases (Cassandra, MongoDB), and data warehousing.
D3/D3en (Storage Optimized)
Dense HDD storage instances for high sequential throughput. Best for distributed file systems (HDFS), data warehousing, and large-scale MapReduce workloads.
P4d/P4de (Accelerated)
High-performance GPU instances with NVIDIA A100 GPUs for ML training, HPC, and scientific simulations. Provides up to 400 Gbps networking for distributed training.
G5/G6 (Accelerated)
GPU instances optimized for graphics-intensive applications and ML inference. Cost-effective for inference workloads, video transcoding, and game streaming.
Inf2 (Accelerated)
Purpose-built for ML inference using AWS Inferentia2 chips. Delivers the lowest cost per inference and highest throughput for deploying trained models at scale.
T3/T3a (Burstable)
vCPU to Memory Ratio: 1:1 to 1:4 (varies by size) | Baseline Performance: Baseline with CPU credits | Specialized Hardware: None | Best Use Cases: Web servers, dev/test, microservices | Network Performance: Up to 5 Gbps | EBS Optimized: Yes (default) | Pricing Model: Lowest cost, pay per use with unlimited mode option | Processor Type: Intel Xeon / AMD EPYC
M6i/M6g (General Purpose)
vCPU to Memory Ratio: 1:4 (balanced) | Baseline Performance: Consistent performance | Specialized Hardware: None | Best Use Cases: Application servers, small-medium databases | Network Performance: Up to 50 Gbps | EBS Optimized: Yes (default) | Pricing Model: Moderate, balanced price/performance | Processor Type: Intel Ice Lake / Graviton2/3
C6i/C6g (Compute Optimized)
vCPU to Memory Ratio: 1:2 | Baseline Performance: High compute performance | Specialized Hardware: None | Best Use Cases: Gaming servers, HPC, batch processing | Network Performance: Up to 50 Gbps | EBS Optimized: Yes (default) | Pricing Model: Moderate, compute-focused pricing | Processor Type: Intel Ice Lake / Graviton2/3
R6i/R6g (Memory Optimized)
vCPU to Memory Ratio: 1:8 | Baseline Performance: High memory bandwidth | Specialized Hardware: None | Best Use Cases: In-memory caches, databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL) | Network Performance: Up to 50 Gbps | EBS Optimized: Yes (default) | Pricing Model: Higher due to memory | Processor Type: Intel Ice Lake / Graviton2/3
X2idn/X2iedn (Memory Optimized)
vCPU to Memory Ratio: 1:16 to 1:32 | Baseline Performance: Extreme memory performance | Specialized Hardware: High memory bandwidth | Best Use Cases: SAP HANA, large in-memory databases | Network Performance: Up to 100 Gbps | EBS Optimized: Yes (default) | Pricing Model: Premium for extreme memory | Processor Type: Intel Ice Lake
I4i (Storage Optimized)
vCPU to Memory Ratio: 1:8 | Baseline Performance: High IOPS storage | Specialized Hardware: NVMe SSD (up to 75K IOPS) | Best Use Cases: NoSQL (Cassandra, MongoDB), data warehouses | Network Performance: Up to 40 Gbps | EBS Optimized: N/A (instance storage) | Pricing Model: Premium for high IOPS storage | Processor Type: Intel Ice Lake
D3/D3en (Storage Optimized)
vCPU to Memory Ratio: 1:4 | Baseline Performance: High throughput HDD | Specialized Hardware: HDD with high throughput | Best Use Cases: Hadoop, data lakes, log processing | Network Performance: Up to 100 Gbps | EBS Optimized: N/A (instance storage) | Pricing Model: Moderate for high capacity | Processor Type: Intel Ice Lake
P4d/P4de (Accelerated)
vCPU to Memory Ratio: 1:8 to 1:16 | Baseline Performance: Highest GPU performance | Specialized Hardware: NVIDIA A100 GPUs, NVSwitch | Best Use Cases: Deep learning training, HPC simulations | Network Performance: 400 Gbps (EFA supported) | EBS Optimized: Yes (default) | Pricing Model: Highest cost, GPU premium | Processor Type: Intel Cascade Lake + A100 GPUs
G5/G6 (Accelerated)
vCPU to Memory Ratio: 1:4 | Baseline Performance: Balanced GPU/CPU | Specialized Hardware: NVIDIA A10G/L4 GPUs | Best Use Cases: ML inference, graphics workstations, gaming | Network Performance: Up to 100 Gbps | EBS Optimized: Yes (default) | Pricing Model: High, but lower than P-series | Processor Type: AMD EPYC + NVIDIA GPUs
Inf2 (Accelerated)
vCPU to Memory Ratio: 1:4 to 1:8 | Baseline Performance: High ML inference | Specialized Hardware: AWS Inferentia2 chips | Best Use Cases: Cost-effective ML inference at scale | Network Performance: Up to 100 Gbps | EBS Optimized: Yes (default) | Pricing Model: Lowest cost for ML inference | Processor Type: AWS Inferentia2 + Intel Xeon
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#69 EC2 Pricing Models — On-Demand vs Reserved vs Spot vs Savings Plans
Compute SAA-011 4 cols 6 rows
▼

Choosing the right EC2 pricing model is one of the easiest ways to cut AWS bills by up to 90%. The exam will describe a workload's predictability and tolerance for interruption, and ask you to identify the most cost-effective option. Understanding WHEN each model is appropriate is more important than knowing the exact discount percentages.

Feature On-Demand Reserved Instances (RI) Spot Instances Savings Plans
Discount vs On-Demand 0% — baseline price Up to 72% (3-year, All Upfront) Up to 90% — highest possible discount Up to 66% — similar to RI but more flexible
Interruption Risk None — runs until you stop it None — guaranteed capacity HIGH — can be terminated with 2-min warning None — just a billing discount
Commitment None — pay as you go 1 or 3 years (Standard) or flexible (Convertible) None — but no reliability guarantee 1 or 3 years (hourly spend commitment)
Flexibility Maximum — change instance type anytime Low (Standard RI) to Medium (Convertible RI) High — choose any available instance type HIGH — applies across EC2 families, sizes, regions, Lambda, Fargate
Best For Short-term, unpredictable, dev/test workloads Steady-state production databases, always-on apps Batch jobs, ML training, CI/CD pipelines, fault-tolerant apps Flexible commitment — best of both RI savings and flexibility
Capacity Reservation No guarantee — but usually available Yes — Standard RI reserves capacity No — availability depends on market No — billing discount only, no capacity guarantee
⚠️ Exam Tips

On-Demand = no commitment, highest price — use for unpredictable workloads or short experiments

Reserved Instances = 1-3 year commitment for specific instance type — up to 72% savings

Savings Plans = 1-3 year commitment to a $/hour spend level — flexible across instance types

Spot = up to 90% off for interruptible workloads — 2-minute warning before termination

The exam loves asking: use Spot for fault-tolerant batch jobs, Reserved for steady-state workloads

On-Demand
On-Demand is the default — you pay for compute by the second with no commitments. It is the most flexible but most expensive option, ideal for unpredictable workloads, development/testing, or new applications where you do not yet know your baseline usage.
Reserved Instances (RI)
Reserved Instances provide a significant discount (up to 72%) in exchange for a 1 or 3-year commitment to a specific instance type in a specific region. They are best for steady-state, predictable workloads that run continuously — think 'production database servers.'
Spot Instances
Spot Instances allow you to bid on unused EC2 capacity at discounts up to 90% — but AWS can reclaim them with 2 minutes notice when demand rises. They are ideal for fault-tolerant, interruption-tolerant workloads like batch processing, ML training jobs, and data analysis.
Savings Plans
Savings Plans offer the same discounts as Reserved Instances but with MORE FLEXIBILITY — instead of committing to a specific instance type, you commit to a dollar amount of hourly spend. Compute Savings Plans apply across EC2, Lambda, and Fargate automatically.
On-Demand
Discount vs On-Demand: 0% — baseline price | Interruption Risk: None — runs until you stop it | Commitment: None — pay as you go | Flexibility: Maximum — change instance type anytime | Best For: Short-term, unpredictable, dev/test workloads | Capacity Reservation: No guarantee — but usually available
Reserved Instances (RI)
Discount vs On-Demand: Up to 72% (3-year, All Upfront) | Interruption Risk: None — guaranteed capacity | Commitment: 1 or 3 years (Standard) or flexible (Convertible) | Flexibility: Low (Standard RI) to Medium (Convertible RI) | Best For: Steady-state production databases, always-on apps | Capacity Reservation: Yes — Standard RI reserves capacity
Spot Instances
Discount vs On-Demand: Up to 90% — highest possible discount | Interruption Risk: HIGH — can be terminated with 2-min warning | Commitment: None — but no reliability guarantee | Flexibility: High — choose any available instance type | Best For: Batch jobs, ML training, CI/CD pipelines, fault-tolerant apps | Capacity Reservation: No — availability depends on market
Savings Plans
Discount vs On-Demand: Up to 66% — similar to RI but more flexible | Interruption Risk: None — just a billing discount | Commitment: 1 or 3 years (hourly spend commitment) | Flexibility: HIGH — applies across EC2 families, sizes, regions, Lambda, Fargate | Best For: Flexible commitment — best of both RI savings and flexibility | Capacity Reservation: No — billing discount only, no capacity guarantee
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#70 EC2 Purchasing and Cost Models
Compute saa_d4_compute_purchasing 5 cols 12 rows
▼
