This table compares AWS EC2 pricing options. On-Demand provides flexibility with no commitment, Reserved Instances offer significant discounts for long-term commitments, Savings Plans provide flexible discount models, and Spot Instances offer the deepest discounts for fault-tolerant workloads.

Feature On-Demand Instances Reserved Instances Savings Plans Spot Instances Dedicated Hosts
Commitment Period None (pay per hour/second) 1 year fixed commitment High but more flexible None (interruptible) On-Demand or 1/3 year
Best Use Cases Short-term, unpredictable, spiky workloads Production databases, steady applications Dynamic environments with consistent spend Fault-tolerant, flexible, stateless workloads Software licensing (BYOL), compliance
Interruption risk No special interruption model No special interruption model No special interruption model High - 2-min warning before termination None - dedicated physical server
Typical exam clue Need flexibility now Known baseline demand Want discount with broader usage flexibility Batch, CI, analytics, stateless work 'Bring your own license (BYOL)', 'server-bound software licenses', 'compliance requiring physical server isolation', 'per-socket/per-core licensing'
Cost priority ranking Highest flexibility, less discount Better discount, less flexibility Strong discount with flexibility Deep discount with interruption tradeoff Most expensive — per-host pricing regardless of instances launched; premium for physical isolation and license compliance
Common trap Using for steady always-on fleets indefinitely Choosing them when architecture may change a lot Assuming every workload fits equally well Using them for critical non-interruptible workloads Confusing with Dedicated Instances — Dedicated Hosts give visibility to physical server (sockets, cores) for licensing; Dedicated Instances just ensure hardware isolation
Discount vs On-Demand No discount — full price per hour/second; baseline pricing for all other models Up to 72% discount vs On-Demand for 1-year or 3-year commitments; deeper discount with higher upfront payment Up to 72% discount (Compute SP) Up to 90% discount On-Demand or Reserved pricing
Payment Options Pay-as-you-go — per second (Linux) or per hour (Windows); no upfront commitment All Upfront (biggest discount), Partial Upfront, or No Upfront — 1 or 3 year terms All upfront, Partial upfront, No upfront Pay for capacity used until interrupted On-Demand hourly or Reserved
Instance Flexibility Maximum — any instance type, launch/terminate anytime, no commitment Limited — Standard RIs locked to instance type; Convertible RIs allow changing type/OS/tenancy High flexibility - any instance, region Full flexibility - any available capacity Specific physical server
Capacity Guarantee No guarantee — subject to capacity availability; may get InsufficientInstanceCapacity errors Zonal RIs provide capacity reservation in specified AZ; Regional RIs provide discount but no capacity guarantee Yes - but no AZ capacity guarantee No - capacity can be reclaimed Yes - dedicated physical capacity
Marketplace Support Not applicable — On-Demand is always available at standard pricing Yes — sell unused Standard RIs on the Reserved Instance Marketplace (not Convertible) Cannot be sold No — Spot Instances cannot be sold on marketplace; use Spot Fleet for flexible capacity Can be released
Scope Any region and AZ — completely flexible placement with no restrictions Zonal (specific AZ + capacity) or Regional (any AZ in region, no capacity guarantee) Compute (any instance) or EC2 Instance plans Any available spare capacity Specific AZ — host is allocated in a single Availability Zone; tied to physical hardware location
⚠️ Exam Tips

Compute Savings Plans are the most flexible — they apply across EC2, Lambda, and Fargate

EC2 Instance Savings Plans lock to an instance family but allow size flexibility within that family

Standard Reserved Instances offer the biggest discount but least flexibility

Convertible Reserved Instances allow changing instance type but offer smaller discounts

Dedicated Hosts are for licensing compliance (per-socket, per-core licensing requirements)

On-Demand Instances
On-Demand is the no-commitment option with maximum flexibility and the least planning burden. It is the safe answer for uncertain or short-lived workloads.
Reserved Instances
Reserved Instances exchange flexibility for pricing commitment on steady-state usage patterns. On the exam, they fit known long-running workloads where capacity characteristics are predictable.
Savings Plans
Savings Plans are commitment-based discounts with broader flexibility than classic Reserved Instances. If the question values discount plus some agility, Savings Plans are often the sweeter answer.
Spot Instances
Spot Instances are discounted spare capacity with interruption risk. They are perfect for fault-tolerant, flexible, or retryable workloads and a bad fit for interruption-sensitive production tiers.
On-Demand Instances
Commitment Period: None (pay per hour/second) | Best Use Cases: Short-term, unpredictable, spiky workloads | Interruption risk: No special interruption model | Typical exam clue: Need flexibility now | Cost priority ranking: Highest flexibility, less discount | Common trap: Using for steady always-on fleets indefinitely | Discount vs On-Demand: No discount — full price per hour/second; baseline pricing for all other models | Payment Options: Pay-as-you-go — per second (Linux) or per hour (Windows); no upfront commitment | Instance Flexibility: Maximum — any instance type, launch/terminate anytime, no commitment | Capacity Guarantee: No guarantee — subject to capacity availability; may get InsufficientInstanceCapacity errors | Marketplace Support: Not applicable — On-Demand is always available at standard pricing | Scope: Any region and AZ — completely flexible placement with no restrictions
Reserved Instances
Commitment Period: 1 year fixed commitment | Best Use Cases: Production databases, steady applications | Interruption risk: No special interruption model | Typical exam clue: Known baseline demand | Cost priority ranking: Better discount, less flexibility | Common trap: Choosing them when architecture may change a lot | Discount vs On-Demand: Up to 72% discount vs On-Demand for 1-year or 3-year commitments; deeper discount with higher upfront payment | Payment Options: All Upfront (biggest discount), Partial Upfront, or No Upfront — 1 or 3 year terms | Instance Flexibility: Limited — Standard RIs locked to instance type; Convertible RIs allow changing type/OS/tenancy | Capacity Guarantee: Zonal RIs provide capacity reservation in specified AZ; Regional RIs provide discount but no capacity guarantee | Marketplace Support: Yes — sell unused Standard RIs on the Reserved Instance Marketplace (not Convertible) | Scope: Zonal (specific AZ + capacity) or Regional (any AZ in region, no capacity guarantee)
Savings Plans
Commitment Period: High but more flexible | Best Use Cases: Dynamic environments with consistent spend | Interruption risk: No special interruption model | Typical exam clue: Want discount with broader usage flexibility | Cost priority ranking: Strong discount with flexibility | Common trap: Assuming every workload fits equally well | Discount vs On-Demand: Up to 72% discount (Compute SP) | Payment Options: All upfront, Partial upfront, No upfront | Instance Flexibility: High flexibility - any instance, region | Capacity Guarantee: Yes - but no AZ capacity guarantee | Marketplace Support: Cannot be sold | Scope: Compute (any instance) or EC2 Instance plans
Spot Instances
Commitment Period: None (interruptible) | Best Use Cases: Fault-tolerant, flexible, stateless workloads | Interruption risk: High - 2-min warning before termination | Typical exam clue: Batch, CI, analytics, stateless work | Cost priority ranking: Deep discount with interruption tradeoff | Common trap: Using them for critical non-interruptible workloads | Discount vs On-Demand: Up to 90% discount | Payment Options: Pay for capacity used until interrupted | Instance Flexibility: Full flexibility - any available capacity | Capacity Guarantee: No - capacity can be reclaimed | Marketplace Support: No — Spot Instances cannot be sold on marketplace; use Spot Fleet for flexible capacity | Scope: Any available spare capacity
Dedicated Hosts
Commitment Period: On-Demand or 1/3 year | Best Use Cases: Software licensing (BYOL), compliance | Interruption risk: None - dedicated physical server | Typical exam clue: 'Bring your own license (BYOL)', 'server-bound software licenses', 'compliance requiring physical server isolation', 'per-socket/per-core licensing' | Cost priority ranking: Most expensive — per-host pricing regardless of instances launched; premium for physical isolation and license compliance | Common trap: Confusing with Dedicated Instances — Dedicated Hosts give visibility to physical server (sockets, cores) for licensing; Dedicated Instances just ensure hardware isolation | Discount vs On-Demand: On-Demand or Reserved pricing | Payment Options: On-Demand hourly or Reserved | Instance Flexibility: Specific physical server | Capacity Guarantee: Yes - dedicated physical capacity | Marketplace Support: Can be released | Scope: Specific AZ — host is allocated in a single Availability Zone; tied to physical hardware location
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 4 loves tradeoff questions around flexibility versus commitment. This table helps map purchasing choices to predictability, interruption tolerance, and planning horizon.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#71 EC2 vs Lambda vs Fargate vs ECS vs EKS
Compute SAA-004 5 cols 10 rows
▼

Comparison of AWS compute options for different architectural needs

AWS compute services span a spectrum from full control (EC2) to fully serverless (Lambda). The SAA-C03 exam heavily tests your ability to choose the RIGHT compute service based on workload characteristics like duration, traffic patterns, container needs, and operational overhead tolerance. Understanding this spectrum is fundamental.

Feature Amazon EC2 AWS Lambda AWS Fargate Amazon ECS Amazon EKS
Server Management Full control - you manage OS, patches, scaling, capacity No servers to manage - AWS handles everything Containers without managing servers - you manage containers only Optional — EC2 mode (you manage) or Fargate mode (managed) Optional — EC2 mode (you manage) or Fargate mode (managed)
Unit of Deployment Virtual Machine (AMI + instance type) Function (code + handler + runtime) Container (Docker image + task definition) Container (task definition, service) Pod (Kubernetes pod spec)
Max Duration Unlimited — runs as long as instance is on 15 minutes maximum per invocation Unlimited — long-running containers supported Unlimited Unlimited
Scaling Manual or Auto Scaling Groups; capacity planning required Automatic and instant — scales to 1000s of concurrent invocations ECS/EKS handles scheduling; Fargate scales tasks automatically Service Auto Scaling + Fargate auto-provision Horizontal Pod Autoscaler + Cluster Autoscaler
Pricing Model Per second/hour of instance time (minimum 60 seconds in some cases) Per invocation + per GB-second of execution time (first 400K GB-s free) Per vCPU-hour + per GB-hour of container runtime EC2 mode: instance cost. Fargate mode: task resource cost $0.10/hour for control plane + underlying compute
Best Use Case Long-running, stateful, licensed software, full OS control required Event-driven, short-lived tasks, microservices with unpredictable traffic Containerized microservices, batch jobs, long-running containers AWS-native container apps; simpler than Kubernetes Kubernetes workloads, hybrid environments, multi-cloud portability
Operational Complexity Highest — most control, most responsibility Lowest — zero infrastructure concerns Low — just define containers Medium — container orchestration but AWS-native Medium-High — Kubernetes expertise required
Execution Time No limit (hours, days, weeks) Max 15 minutes per execution No limit (up to task definition limits) Unlimited — tasks run indefinitely; no timeout limit Unlimited — pods run indefinitely; no timeout limit
Cold Starts No cold starts - always ready Cold starts possible - can use provisioned concurrency Cold starts possible but typically faster than Lambda Minimal — container image pull time only; pre-warmed with running service tasks Minimal — container image pull time; node auto-scaling may add delay for new nodes
Idempotency Must implement in application code SDK handles retries for transient failures Must implement in application/container code Application responsibility — not enforced by ECS; design tasks for idempotent behavior Application responsibility — not enforced by EKS; Kubernetes Jobs support restart policies
💡 Mnemonics

E.L.F - EC2 gives you Everything, Lambda is Function-only (serverless), Fargate is For containers without servers

⚠️ Exam Tips

Lambda is ideal for event-driven architectures - S3 events, DynamoDB streams, etc.

Lambda has 15-minute timeout - use Step Functions for longer workflows

Fargate is preferred over Lambda for containerized workloads requiring >15 min execution

For cost optimization: Lambda for sporadic traffic, Fargate for steady traffic, EC2 for predictable high-volume

Lambda + API Gateway is a common pattern for serverless REST APIs

Amazon EC2
EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) provides virtual machines in the cloud where you have full control of the OS, runtime, and configuration. You choose the instance type, manage patching and scaling, and pay for uptime. It is the most flexible but most operationally demanding option.
AWS Lambda
Lambda is serverless compute for event-driven functions — you upload code and Lambda runs it in response to triggers (API calls, S3 events, DynamoDB streams) with no servers to manage. It scales instantly to zero and you pay only for execution time, making it ideal for unpredictable or infrequent workloads.
AWS Fargate
Fargate is serverless container compute — you define your container and Fargate manages the underlying EC2 instances automatically. It bridges the gap between ECS/EKS (container orchestration) and Lambda (serverless) by eliminating server management while supporting long-running containers.
Amazon ECS
ECS (Elastic Container Service) is AWS's native container orchestration platform — it manages the scheduling, deployment, and scaling of Docker containers. It runs on either EC2 instances (you manage servers) or Fargate (serverless). Simpler than Kubernetes and AWS-native.
Amazon EKS
EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) is a fully managed Kubernetes service on AWS — it runs the standard Kubernetes control plane for you. It is more complex than ECS but preferred when the team already uses Kubernetes or needs Kubernetes-specific features and ecosystem tools.
Amazon EC2
Server Management: Full control - you manage OS, patches, scaling, capacity | Unit of Deployment: Virtual Machine (AMI + instance type) | Max Duration: Unlimited — runs as long as instance is on | Scaling: Manual or Auto Scaling Groups; capacity planning required | Pricing Model: Per second/hour of instance time (minimum 60 seconds in some cases) | Best Use Case: Long-running, stateful, licensed software, full OS control required | Operational Complexity: Highest — most control, most responsibility | Execution Time: No limit (hours, days, weeks) | Cold Starts: No cold starts - always ready | Idempotency: Must implement in application code
AWS Lambda
Server Management: No servers to manage - AWS handles everything | Unit of Deployment: Function (code + handler + runtime) | Max Duration: 15 minutes maximum per invocation | Scaling: Automatic and instant — scales to 1000s of concurrent invocations | Pricing Model: Per invocation + per GB-second of execution time (first 400K GB-s free) | Best Use Case: Event-driven, short-lived tasks, microservices with unpredictable traffic | Operational Complexity: Lowest — zero infrastructure concerns | Execution Time: Max 15 minutes per execution | Cold Starts: Cold starts possible - can use provisioned concurrency | Idempotency: SDK handles retries for transient failures
AWS Fargate
Server Management: Containers without managing servers - you manage containers only | Unit of Deployment: Container (Docker image + task definition) | Max Duration: Unlimited — long-running containers supported | Scaling: ECS/EKS handles scheduling; Fargate scales tasks automatically | Pricing Model: Per vCPU-hour + per GB-hour of container runtime | Best Use Case: Containerized microservices, batch jobs, long-running containers | Operational Complexity: Low — just define containers | Execution Time: No limit (up to task definition limits) | Cold Starts: Cold starts possible but typically faster than Lambda | Idempotency: Must implement in application/container code
Amazon ECS
Server Management: Optional — EC2 mode (you manage) or Fargate mode (managed) | Unit of Deployment: Container (task definition, service) | Max Duration: Unlimited | Scaling: Service Auto Scaling + Fargate auto-provision | Pricing Model: EC2 mode: instance cost. Fargate mode: task resource cost | Best Use Case: AWS-native container apps; simpler than Kubernetes | Operational Complexity: Medium — container orchestration but AWS-native | Execution Time: Unlimited — tasks run indefinitely; no timeout limit | Cold Starts: Minimal — container image pull time only; pre-warmed with running service tasks | Idempotency: Application responsibility — not enforced by ECS; design tasks for idempotent behavior
Amazon EKS
Server Management: Optional — EC2 mode (you manage) or Fargate mode (managed) | Unit of Deployment: Pod (Kubernetes pod spec) | Max Duration: Unlimited | Scaling: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler + Cluster Autoscaler | Pricing Model: $0.10/hour for control plane + underlying compute | Best Use Case: Kubernetes workloads, hybrid environments, multi-cloud portability | Operational Complexity: Medium-High — Kubernetes expertise required | Execution Time: Unlimited — pods run indefinitely; no timeout limit | Cold Starts: Minimal — container image pull time; node auto-scaling may add delay for new nodes | Idempotency: Application responsibility — not enforced by EKS; Kubernetes Jobs support restart policies
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#72 Elastic Compute and Container Choices
Compute saa_d2_compute_patterns 4 cols 6 rows
▼
Feature Amazon EC2 with Auto Scaling AWS Lambda Amazon ECS on Fargate Amazon EKS
Ops burden Highest of this group Lowest Low Higher than ECS/Fargate
Best exam clue Need instance-level control Event-driven and short execution Containers without server management Kubernetes requirement
Scaling model Scale instances Scale invocations Scale tasks/services Scale pods/nodes
Packaging style Machine images / instances Function packages Containers Containers on Kubernetes
Great for unpredictable bursts Good but slower than Lambda for some patterns Excellent Good Good with more platform overhead
Common mistake Using it when serverless would cut ops cost Using it for long-running or overly stateful workloads Assuming it is Kubernetes Choosing it without a real Kubernetes need
⚠️ Exam Tips

EC2 Auto Scaling Groups maintain desired instance count and scale based on CloudWatch metrics

ECS tasks can use either EC2 launch type or Fargate launch type within the same cluster

EKS pods scheduled on Fargate each get their own isolation boundary

App Runner is the simplest option for deploying containerized web apps — zero infrastructure config

For the exam: if the question mentions Kubernetes, the answer involves EKS

Amazon EC2 with Auto Scaling
EC2 with Auto Scaling gives the most control over the compute environment while still allowing elastic scaling. Use it when the workload needs OS-level customization, special agents, or traditional server patterns.
AWS Lambda
Lambda is event-driven serverless compute with minimal infrastructure management. Exam clues include short-lived execution, variable demand, and fast scaling without server administration.
Amazon ECS on Fargate
ECS on Fargate lets you run containers without managing servers, landing in a sweet spot between control and low ops overhead. It fits microservices and containerized apps when you do not need Kubernetes complexity.
Amazon EKS
EKS is managed Kubernetes for teams that need Kubernetes APIs, portability, or complex orchestration patterns. In exam questions, it wins when the requirement explicitly calls for Kubernetes.
Amazon EC2 with Auto Scaling
Ops burden: Highest of this group | Best exam clue: Need instance-level control | Scaling model: Scale instances | Packaging style: Machine images / instances | Great for unpredictable bursts: Good but slower than Lambda for some patterns | Common mistake: Using it when serverless would cut ops cost
AWS Lambda
Ops burden: Lowest | Best exam clue: Event-driven and short execution | Scaling model: Scale invocations | Packaging style: Function packages | Great for unpredictable bursts: Excellent | Common mistake: Using it for long-running or overly stateful workloads
Amazon ECS on Fargate
Ops burden: Low | Best exam clue: Containers without server management | Scaling model: Scale tasks/services | Packaging style: Containers | Great for unpredictable bursts: Good | Common mistake: Assuming it is Kubernetes
Amazon EKS
Ops burden: Higher than ECS/Fargate | Best exam clue: Kubernetes requirement | Scaling model: Scale pods/nodes | Packaging style: Containers on Kubernetes | Great for unpredictable bursts: Good with more platform overhead | Common mistake: Choosing it without a real Kubernetes need
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 2 and Domain 3 both expect you to choose the right compute pattern for scale, resilience, and operations overhead. This table compares server-based, serverless, and container execution paths.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

#73 Serverless Compute Comparison
Compute serverless_compute_comparison 3 cols 11 rows
▼

AWS offers multiple serverless compute options that eliminate infrastructure management. Understanding when to use Lambda, Fargate, or App Runner helps design efficient serverless architectures.

Feature AWS Lambda AWS Fargate AWS App Runner
Compute Model Function-as-a-Service (FaaS) Container-as-a-Service (CaaS) Application deployment service
Unit of Deployment Function (code package) Container image (Docker/OCI) Source code or container image
Runtime Support Multiple languages + custom runtime Any containerized application Python, Node.js, Java, .NET, Go, Ruby, PHP
Maximum Execution Time 15 minutes (900 seconds) No limit (continuous running) No limit (continuous running)
Cold Start Latency 100ms - 1s (can use Provisioned Concurrency) 30-60 seconds (new task startup) Seconds to minutes (initial deployment)
Scaling Behavior Automatic, near-instant (1000 concurrent default) Service Auto Scaling (slower than Lambda) Automatic scaling with configurable limits
Pricing Model Per request + duration (GB-seconds) Per vCPU + memory per second Per vCPU + memory per hour + per request
VPC Networking Optional (adds cold start latency) Native VPC integration VPC Connector for private resources
Best Use Cases Event-driven, short tasks, APIs, data processing Long-running containers, microservices, batch jobs Web apps, APIs, rapid deployment from source
Infrastructure Control None (fully abstracted) Minimal (task definitions) None (fully managed)
Deployment Complexity Low (zip file or container image) Medium (requires ECS/EKS setup) Lowest (connect to GitHub or push container)
⚠️ Exam Tips

Lambda = functions (event-driven, 15 min max); Fargate = containers (long-running, no time limit)

App Runner is the easiest way to deploy a containerized web app — like Fargate without the complexity

Lambda cold starts can add latency; use Provisioned Concurrency for latency-sensitive workloads

Fargate works with both ECS and EKS — it’s the compute engine, not the orchestrator

Lambda scales to zero and charges only for execution time; Fargate charges for provisioned vCPU/memory

AWS Lambda
Event-driven serverless functions with automatic scaling from zero. Supports 15-minute max execution, 10 GB memory, and integrates with 200+ AWS services as event sources. Pay only for compute time consumed.
AWS Fargate
Serverless compute engine for containers that eliminates the need to manage EC2 instances. Works with both ECS and EKS. You define CPU and memory requirements; Fargate handles infrastructure.
AWS App Runner
Fully managed container service that simplifies deploying web applications and APIs from source code or container images. Automatic builds, deployments, scaling, and load balancing with minimal configuration.
AWS Lambda
Compute Model: Function-as-a-Service (FaaS) | Unit of Deployment: Function (code package) | Runtime Support: Multiple languages + custom runtime | Maximum Execution Time: 15 minutes (900 seconds) | Cold Start Latency: 100ms - 1s (can use Provisioned Concurrency) | Scaling Behavior: Automatic, near-instant (1000 concurrent default) | Pricing Model: Per request + duration (GB-seconds) | VPC Networking: Optional (adds cold start latency) | Best Use Cases: Event-driven, short tasks, APIs, data processing | Infrastructure Control: None (fully abstracted) | Deployment Complexity: Low (zip file or container image)
AWS Fargate
Compute Model: Container-as-a-Service (CaaS) | Unit of Deployment: Container image (Docker/OCI) | Runtime Support: Any containerized application | Maximum Execution Time: No limit (continuous running) | Cold Start Latency: 30-60 seconds (new task startup) | Scaling Behavior: Service Auto Scaling (slower than Lambda) | Pricing Model: Per vCPU + memory per second | VPC Networking: Native VPC integration | Best Use Cases: Long-running containers, microservices, batch jobs | Infrastructure Control: Minimal (task definitions) | Deployment Complexity: Medium (requires ECS/EKS setup)
AWS App Runner
Compute Model: Application deployment service | Unit of Deployment: Source code or container image | Runtime Support: Python, Node.js, Java, .NET, Go, Ruby, PHP | Maximum Execution Time: No limit (continuous running) | Cold Start Latency: Seconds to minutes (initial deployment) | Scaling Behavior: Automatic scaling with configurable limits | Pricing Model: Per vCPU + memory per hour + per request | VPC Networking: VPC Connector for private resources | Best Use Cases: Web apps, APIs, rapid deployment from source | Infrastructure Control: None (fully managed) | Deployment Complexity: Lowest (connect to GitHub or push container)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

Containers 4 tables
#74 Amazon ECS vs Amazon EKS Comparison
Containers ecs_eks_comparison 2 cols 8 rows
▼

This table compares AWS container orchestration services. ECS is AWS-native and deeply integrated with AWS services, while EKS provides managed Kubernetes for portability and ecosystem compatibility.

Feature Amazon ECS Amazon EKS
Orchestration Platform AWS-native (proprietary) Kubernetes (open source)
Control Plane Fully managed by AWS Managed Kubernetes control plane
Learning Curve Lower (AWS-specific concepts) Steeper (Kubernetes knowledge required)
Portability AWS-only Multi-cloud (standard Kubernetes)
AWS Integration Deep integration (IAM, CloudWatch, ALB) Good integration via add-ons
Ecosystem AWS ecosystem Large Kubernetes ecosystem
Scaling ECS Service Auto Scaling Kubernetes-native scaling (HPA, VPA)
Use Cases AWS-native workloads, simpler container deployments Multi-cloud portability, complex Kubernetes apps
⚠️ Exam Tips

ECS is simpler and AWS-native — choose for teams without Kubernetes expertise

EKS provides standard Kubernetes API — choose for portability or existing Kubernetes expertise

Both support Fargate (serverless) and EC2 (self-managed) launch types

ECS uses Task Definitions; EKS uses Pod specifications and Kubernetes manifests

For the exam: ECS for AWS-first teams, EKS for Kubernetes-first teams

Amazon ECS
Fully managed container orchestration service native to AWS that simplifies running Docker containers with deep AWS integration and minimal configuration.
Amazon EKS
Managed Kubernetes service that makes it easy to run Kubernetes on AWS without managing the control plane, offering full Kubernetes compatibility.
Amazon ECS
Orchestration Platform: AWS-native (proprietary) | Control Plane: Fully managed by AWS | Learning Curve: Lower (AWS-specific concepts) | Portability: AWS-only | AWS Integration: Deep integration (IAM, CloudWatch, ALB) | Ecosystem: AWS ecosystem | Scaling: ECS Service Auto Scaling | Use Cases: AWS-native workloads, simpler container deployments
Amazon EKS
Orchestration Platform: Kubernetes (open source) | Control Plane: Managed Kubernetes control plane | Learning Curve: Steeper (Kubernetes knowledge required) | Portability: Multi-cloud (standard Kubernetes) | AWS Integration: Good integration via add-ons | Ecosystem: Large Kubernetes ecosystem | Scaling: Kubernetes-native scaling (HPA, VPA) | Use Cases: Multi-cloud portability, complex Kubernetes apps
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#75 Container Orchestration Services Comparison
Containers container_orchestration_services_comparison 3 cols 6 rows
▼

Comparing AWS container orchestration and management services

Feature Amazon ECS Amazon EKS Amazon ECR
Orchestration AWS-native container platform Kubernetes-native platform Image registry (not orchestration)
API/Interface ECS API, CLI, AWS SDKs Kubernetes API, kubectl compatible Docker CLI, API
Portability AWS-specific Portable (standard Kubernetes) Any container runtime
Use Case AWS-centric workloads, AWS integration Multi-cloud, Kubernetes expertise Store and manage container images
Compute Options EC2 launch type, Fargate EC2 nodes, Fargate, EKS Distro N/A (storage only)
Integration Deep AWS integration (ELB, IAM, VPC) AWS integration via cloud provider plugin Integrated with ECS and EKS
💡 Mnemonics

ECS = Easy Container Service, EKS = Enterprise Kubernetes Service, ECR = Container Registry

⚠️ Exam Tips

ECS is AWS-native - better integration with AWS services

EKS is for organizations using Kubernetes or planning multi-cloud deployments

ECR holds container images; ECS/EKS pull images from ECR

Both ECS and EKS support Fargate for serverless containers

EKS Anywhere runs Kubernetes on-premises for hybrid deployments

Amazon ECS
A fully managed container orchestration service supporting Docker containers, tightly integrated with AWS services and offering both EC2 and Fargate launch types.
Amazon EKS
A fully managed Kubernetes service that makes it easy to deploy, manage, and scale containerized applications using Kubernetes on AWS.
Amazon ECR
A fully managed Docker container registry that makes it easy for developers to store, manage, and deploy container images.
Amazon ECS
Orchestration: AWS-native container platform | API/Interface: ECS API, CLI, AWS SDKs | Portability: AWS-specific | Use Case: AWS-centric workloads, AWS integration | Compute Options: EC2 launch type, Fargate | Integration: Deep AWS integration (ELB, IAM, VPC)
Amazon EKS
Orchestration: Kubernetes-native platform | API/Interface: Kubernetes API, kubectl compatible | Portability: Portable (standard Kubernetes) | Use Case: Multi-cloud, Kubernetes expertise | Compute Options: EC2 nodes, Fargate, EKS Distro | Integration: AWS integration via cloud provider plugin
Amazon ECR
Orchestration: Image registry (not orchestration) | API/Interface: Docker CLI, API | Portability: Any container runtime | Use Case: Store and manage container images | Compute Options: N/A (storage only) | Integration: Integrated with ECS and EKS
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#76 Container Services Comparison
Containers container_services_comparison 5 cols 10 rows
▼

AWS provides multiple container orchestration services. Understanding the differences between ECS, EKS, and ECR, along with their launch types (EC2 vs Fargate), is essential for designing containerized architectures on AWS.

Feature ECS with EC2 ECS with Fargate EKS with EC2 EKS with Fargate ECR
Orchestration Type AWS-native (Docker-based) AWS-native (Docker-based) Kubernetes (open-source) Kubernetes (open-source) Container registry (storage)
Infrastructure Management Customer manages EC2 instances AWS manages infrastructure (serverless) Customer manages EC2 worker nodes AWS manages pods (serverless) Fully managed registry
Serverless Option No - requires EC2 instances Yes - fully serverless No - requires worker nodes Yes - serverless pods N/A - storage service
Pricing Model EC2 costs + no ECS fees Pay per vCPU/memory used $0.10/hour cluster + EC2 costs $0.10/hour cluster + Fargate costs Storage + data transfer fees
Scaling Method Auto Scaling Groups for EC2 + Service Auto Scaling Service Auto Scaling only Cluster Autoscaler + HPA/VPA HPA only (no node scaling) Automatic scaling
Kubernetes Compatibility No - AWS proprietary No - AWS proprietary Yes - native Kubernetes Yes - native Kubernetes Compatible with any container runtime
Control Plane AWS-managed (no charge) AWS-managed (no charge) AWS-managed ($0.10/hour) AWS-managed ($0.10/hour) Not applicable — ECR is a container registry (stores images), not an orchestration/control plane service
Best Use Cases Cost optimization, GPU workloads, existing EC2 expertise Simple container workloads, no infrastructure management Kubernetes expertise, multi-cloud portability, complex workloads Kubernetes without node management, variable workloads Storing, sharing, deploying container images
Learning Curve Low (AWS-native concepts) Lowest (simplest option) High (Kubernetes complexity) High (Kubernetes knowledge required) Low (simple Docker registry)
IAM Integration Task roles + instance profiles Task roles only (more secure) IAM roles for service accounts (IRSA) IAM roles for service accounts (IRSA) IAM for repository access control
⚠️ Exam Tips

ECS with EC2 gives you the most control over the underlying container host instances

ECS with Fargate removes all EC2 management — you just define CPU/memory per task

EKS with Fargate has limitations: no DaemonSets, no GPU pods, no privileged containers

ECR stores container images and scans for vulnerabilities automatically

For the exam: choose Fargate when the question mentions 'no infrastructure management'

ECS with EC2
AWS-native container orchestration running on EC2 instances you manage. Provides maximum control over underlying infrastructure, instance types, and placement strategies. Best for GPU workloads or specific hardware needs.
ECS with Fargate
Serverless ECS where AWS manages the underlying infrastructure. You define task CPU/memory requirements. Best for teams wanting simplicity without managing EC2 instances.
EKS with EC2
Managed Kubernetes control plane with EC2 worker nodes you manage. Full Kubernetes API compatibility with control over node types and configurations. Best for complex Kubernetes workloads.
EKS with Fargate
Managed Kubernetes with serverless compute. Each pod runs in its own isolated environment. Best for Kubernetes workloads that don't need special instance types or DaemonSets.
ECR
Elastic Container Registry — a fully managed Docker container registry for storing, managing, and deploying container images. Supports image scanning for vulnerabilities and cross-region replication.
ECS with EC2
Orchestration Type: AWS-native (Docker-based) | Infrastructure Management: Customer manages EC2 instances | Serverless Option: No - requires EC2 instances | Pricing Model: EC2 costs + no ECS fees | Scaling Method: Auto Scaling Groups for EC2 + Service Auto Scaling | Kubernetes Compatibility: No - AWS proprietary | Control Plane: AWS-managed (no charge) | Best Use Cases: Cost optimization, GPU workloads, existing EC2 expertise | Learning Curve: Low (AWS-native concepts) | IAM Integration: Task roles + instance profiles
ECS with Fargate
Orchestration Type: AWS-native (Docker-based) | Infrastructure Management: AWS manages infrastructure (serverless) | Serverless Option: Yes - fully serverless | Pricing Model: Pay per vCPU/memory used | Scaling Method: Service Auto Scaling only | Kubernetes Compatibility: No - AWS proprietary | Control Plane: AWS-managed (no charge) | Best Use Cases: Simple container workloads, no infrastructure management | Learning Curve: Lowest (simplest option) | IAM Integration: Task roles only (more secure)
EKS with EC2
Orchestration Type: Kubernetes (open-source) | Infrastructure Management: Customer manages EC2 worker nodes | Serverless Option: No - requires worker nodes | Pricing Model: $0.10/hour cluster + EC2 costs | Scaling Method: Cluster Autoscaler + HPA/VPA | Kubernetes Compatibility: Yes - native Kubernetes | Control Plane: AWS-managed ($0.10/hour) | Best Use Cases: Kubernetes expertise, multi-cloud portability, complex workloads | Learning Curve: High (Kubernetes complexity) | IAM Integration: IAM roles for service accounts (IRSA)
EKS with Fargate
Orchestration Type: Kubernetes (open-source) | Infrastructure Management: AWS manages pods (serverless) | Serverless Option: Yes - serverless pods | Pricing Model: $0.10/hour cluster + Fargate costs | Scaling Method: HPA only (no node scaling) | Kubernetes Compatibility: Yes - native Kubernetes | Control Plane: AWS-managed ($0.10/hour) | Best Use Cases: Kubernetes without node management, variable workloads | Learning Curve: High (Kubernetes knowledge required) | IAM Integration: IAM roles for service accounts (IRSA)
ECR
Orchestration Type: Container registry (storage) | Infrastructure Management: Fully managed registry | Serverless Option: N/A - storage service | Pricing Model: Storage + data transfer fees | Scaling Method: Automatic scaling | Kubernetes Compatibility: Compatible with any container runtime | Control Plane: Not applicable — ECR is a container registry (stores images), not an orchestration/control plane service | Best Use Cases: Storing, sharing, deploying container images | Learning Curve: Low (simple Docker registry) | IAM Integration: IAM for repository access control
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#77 ECS vs EKS vs Fargate Comprehensive Comparison
Containers container_platforms_comparison 3 cols 9 rows
▼

This table provides a comprehensive comparison of AWS container deployment options. ECS offers AWS-native orchestration, EKS provides managed Kubernetes, and Fargate delivers serverless containers without managing infrastructure.

Feature Amazon ECS Amazon EKS AWS Fargate
Definition Fully managed container orchestration service Managed Kubernetes service Serverless compute engine for containers
Control Plane Managed by AWS Managed Kubernetes control plane Managed by AWS (when used with ECS/EKS)
Orchestration ECS Task Definitions Kubernetes (Pods, Deployments, Services) ECS or EKS orchestration
Compute Options EC2 instances or Fargate EC2 instances or Fargate Fargate only (no EC2 to manage)
Ease of Use Simpler, AWS-specific More complex, Kubernetes-standard Simplest, no infrastructure management
Scaling Managed scaling with ECS Service Auto Scaling Kubernetes-native scaling Automatic scaling by AWS
Cost Pay for EC2 or Fargate resources $0.10/hour cluster + EC2 or Fargate Pay per vCPU and memory used
Operational Overhead Lower than managing Kubernetes Requires Kubernetes expertise Minimal (abstracts infrastructure)
Use Cases AWS-native workloads, simpler setups Standardized Kubernetes, multi-cloud Serverless, event-driven, unpredictable workloads
⚠️ Exam Tips

ECS is the orchestrator, EKS is the Kubernetes-based orchestrator, Fargate is the compute engine

Fargate is NOT a standalone service — it runs under ECS or EKS

ECS+Fargate is the simplest serverless container option for AWS-native workloads

EKS+EC2 gives maximum Kubernetes control including DaemonSets and custom networking

For cost optimization: Fargate for variable workloads, EC2 with Spot for batch container jobs

Amazon ECS
Fully managed container orchestration service with AWS-native task definitions and deep service integration for simple to complex container deployments.
Amazon EKS
Managed Kubernetes control plane supporting standard Kubernetes APIs and ecosystem tools for portable, complex container orchestration.
AWS Fargate
Serverless compute engine for containers that eliminates infrastructure management, working with both ECS and EKS for truly serverless container deployments.
Amazon ECS
Definition: Fully managed container orchestration service | Control Plane: Managed by AWS | Orchestration: ECS Task Definitions | Compute Options: EC2 instances or Fargate | Ease of Use: Simpler, AWS-specific | Scaling: Managed scaling with ECS Service Auto Scaling | Cost: Pay for EC2 or Fargate resources | Operational Overhead: Lower than managing Kubernetes | Use Cases: AWS-native workloads, simpler setups
Amazon EKS
Definition: Managed Kubernetes service | Control Plane: Managed Kubernetes control plane | Orchestration: Kubernetes (Pods, Deployments, Services) | Compute Options: EC2 instances or Fargate | Ease of Use: More complex, Kubernetes-standard | Scaling: Kubernetes-native scaling | Cost: $0.10/hour cluster + EC2 or Fargate | Operational Overhead: Requires Kubernetes expertise | Use Cases: Standardized Kubernetes, multi-cloud
AWS Fargate
Definition: Serverless compute engine for containers | Control Plane: Managed by AWS (when used with ECS/EKS) | Orchestration: ECS or EKS orchestration | Compute Options: Fargate only (no EC2 to manage) | Ease of Use: Simplest, no infrastructure management | Scaling: Automatic scaling by AWS | Cost: Pay per vCPU and memory used | Operational Overhead: Minimal (abstracts infrastructure) | Use Cases: Serverless, event-driven, unpredictable workloads
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

Storage 11 tables
#78 AWS Hybrid Storage Solutions
Storage SAA-010 4 cols 9 rows
▼

Comparing hybrid and data transfer storage solutions - Domain 3 and 4

Feature AWS Storage Gateway AWS DataSync AWS Snow Family AWS Transfer Family
Primary Purpose Hybrid cloud storage bridge Automated data transfer Offline data migration Managed file transfer
Deployment Location On-premises (VM/hardware) On-premises agent Physical devices AWS managed service
Transfer Mode Real-time sync Scheduled sync Physical shipment SFTP/FTPS/FTP protocols
Data Volume TB to PB TB to PB TB to EB (Snowmobile) Any size
Gateway Types File, Volume, Tape N/A (agent-based) Snowcone, Snowball, Snowmobile N/A (protocol-based)
Connectivity Internet or Direct Connect Internet or Direct Connect Physical shipping Internet or VPC endpoint
Encryption In transit and at rest In transit and at rest Physical + encryption In transit (TLS)
Best For Hybrid applications, backup Ongoing sync, migration Large initial migration SFTP/FTPS file sharing
Cost Driver Storage + data transfer Data transfer amount Device rental + shipping Data transfer + storage
💡 Mnemonics

Storage Gateway = Hybrid bridge, DataSync = Automated sync, Snow = Physical migration, Transfer Family = SFTP/FTPS

Need hybrid storage? Storage Gateway. Need automated transfer? DataSync. Need offline migration? Snow Family. Need SFTP access? Transfer Family.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Storage Gateway caches frequently accessed data on-premises

DataSync can schedule automated transfers (e.g., nightly sync)

Snowball Edge is for 10s of TB, Snowmobile for 100s of PB

Snowcone is the smallest - portable for edge computing

Transfer Family supports SFTP, FTPS, and FTP protocols

Storage Gateway File Gateway uses S3 as backend storage

AWS Storage Gateway
A hybrid storage service that enables on-premises applications to use AWS cloud storage seamlessly. Offers File Gateway (NFS/SMB to S3), Volume Gateway (iSCSI block storage), and Tape Gateway (virtual tape library) for various hybrid scenarios.
AWS DataSync
A data transfer service that simplifies and automates moving data between on-premises storage and AWS. Uses a lightweight agent to efficiently transfer data over the internet or Direct Connect, with scheduling, encryption, and integrity verification.
AWS Snow Family
Physical data migration devices for offline data transfer when network transfer is impractical. Includes Snowcone (8TB, portable edge computing), Snowball Edge (80TB, compute capability), and Snowmobile (100PB, exabyte-scale migrations).
AWS Transfer Family
A fully managed service for file transfers using SFTP, FTPS, and FTP protocols. Enables secure file exchange with third parties without managing infrastructure, with data stored directly in S3 or EFS.
AWS Storage Gateway
Primary Purpose: Hybrid cloud storage bridge | Deployment Location: On-premises (VM/hardware) | Transfer Mode: Real-time sync | Data Volume: TB to PB | Gateway Types: File, Volume, Tape | Connectivity: Internet or Direct Connect | Encryption: In transit and at rest | Best For: Hybrid applications, backup | Cost Driver: Storage + data transfer
AWS DataSync
Primary Purpose: Automated data transfer | Deployment Location: On-premises agent | Transfer Mode: Scheduled sync | Data Volume: TB to PB | Gateway Types: N/A (agent-based) | Connectivity: Internet or Direct Connect | Encryption: In transit and at rest | Best For: Ongoing sync, migration | Cost Driver: Data transfer amount
AWS Snow Family
Primary Purpose: Offline data migration | Deployment Location: Physical devices | Transfer Mode: Physical shipment | Data Volume: TB to EB (Snowmobile) | Gateway Types: Snowcone, Snowball, Snowmobile | Connectivity: Physical shipping | Encryption: Physical + encryption | Best For: Large initial migration | Cost Driver: Device rental + shipping
AWS Transfer Family
Primary Purpose: Managed file transfer | Deployment Location: AWS managed service | Transfer Mode: SFTP/FTPS/FTP protocols | Data Volume: Any size | Gateway Types: N/A (protocol-based) | Connectivity: Internet or VPC endpoint | Encryption: In transit (TLS) | Best For: SFTP/FTPS file sharing | Cost Driver: Data transfer + storage
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json

#79 AWS Object Storage Services Comparison
Storage aws_object_storage_services_comparison 2 cols 7 rows
▼

Comparing object storage services across AWS for different use cases

Feature Amazon S3 Amazon S3 Glacier
Storage Type Object storage - any type of data Archive object storage - cold data
Use Case Hot/warm storage, websites, apps, backups Long-term archives, compliance, backups
Access Time Milliseconds (real-time) Minutes to hours (varies by retrieval option)
Durability 99.999999999% (11 9's) 99.999999999% (11 9's)
Cost Higher storage cost, pay per request Very low storage cost, retrieval fees apply
Tiers Standard, IA, One Zone-IA, Intelligent-Tiering Glacier Instant, Flexible, Deep Archive
Lifecycle Auto-transition between tiers Auto-archive from S3
💡 Mnemonics

S3 = Speed for hot data, Glacier = Great savings for cold data

⚠️ Exam Tips

S3 Standard is most expensive but fastest access

Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering for unpredictable access patterns

Glacier Deep Archive has 12-hour minimum retrieval time

Data can automatically transition from S3 to Glacier via lifecycle policies

S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval offers free Expedited retrievals under certain conditions

Amazon S3
Object storage built to store and retrieve any amount of data from anywhere, offering 11 9's of durability with extensive versioning, lifecycle policies, and cross-region replication capabilities.
Amazon S3 Glacier
Secure, durable, and extremely low-cost cloud storage service for data archiving and long-term backup, with retrieval options ranging from minutes to hours.
Amazon S3
Storage Type: Object storage - any type of data | Use Case: Hot/warm storage, websites, apps, backups | Access Time: Milliseconds (real-time) | Durability: 99.999999999% (11 9's) | Cost: Higher storage cost, pay per request | Tiers: Standard, IA, One Zone-IA, Intelligent-Tiering | Lifecycle: Auto-transition between tiers
Amazon S3 Glacier
Storage Type: Archive object storage - cold data | Use Case: Long-term archives, compliance, backups | Access Time: Minutes to hours (varies by retrieval option) | Durability: 99.999999999% (11 9's) | Cost: Very low storage cost, retrieval fees apply | Tiers: Glacier Instant, Flexible, Deep Archive | Lifecycle: Auto-archive from S3
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#80 AWS Storage Services Comparison
Storage SAA-002 4 cols 11 rows
▼

This table compares the primary storage services on AWS. S3 provides object storage for virtually unlimited scalability, EBS offers block storage for EC2 instances with consistent performance, and EFS delivers managed file storage accessible from multiple instances simultaneously.

Feature Amazon S3 Amazon EBS Amazon EFS Amazon S3 Glacier
Storage Type File Storage (Various Protocols) Block storage File storage (NFS) Object storage (archive)
Access Method HTTP/HTTPS via API Attached to EC2 instances Mount to multiple instances HTTP/HTTPS (with retrieval delay)
Availability Virtually unlimited Up to 64 TB per volume Automatic, petabyte-scale Virtually unlimited
Multi-AZ/AZ-specific Multi-AZ by default AZ-specific Multi-AZ by default Multi-AZ by default
Performance High throughput, variable latency Up to 256,000 IOPS, low latency Low latency, concurrent access Retrieval from minutes to hours
Use Cases Backups, archives, static websites, big data, ML data lakes Databases, boot volumes, transactional workloads Content management, shared storage, DevOps, ML training data Long-term archival, compliance
Pricing Model Per GB stored + requests + data transfer Per GB provisioned + IOPS (if provisioned) Per GB used (pay-as-you-grow) Instant, Flexible, Deep Archive
Durability High (replicated within AZ/backup) 99.8% - 99.9% (replicated within AZ) High (replicated across AZs) 99.999999999% (11 9s)
Encryption SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C EBS encryption (KMS) Encryption at rest (KMS) Same as S3
Access Pattern REST API, SDK, Console Attached to single EC2 instance Multiple EC2 instances simultaneously Infrequent — archival retrieval; Expedited (1-5 min), Standard (3-5 hrs), Bulk (5-12 hrs)
Latency Milliseconds (object retrieval) Sub-millisecond to single-digit ms Low latency (file operations) Minutes to hours depending on retrieval tier (Expedited: 1-5 min, Standard: 3-5 hrs, Bulk: 5-12 hrs)
💡 Mnemonics

S3 = Simple Storage Service, EBS = Elastic Block Store, EFS = Elastic File System, Glacier = Cold storage

Need object storage? S3. Need boot volume or block storage? EBS. Need shared file storage? EFS. Need long-term archive? Glacier.

⚠️ Exam Tips

S3 is object storage - accessed via API, not mounted

EBS is AZ-specific - cannot attach across AZs

EFS is the only service that allows concurrent access from multiple instances

Glacier retrieval times: Instant (milliseconds), Flexible (3-5 hours), Deep (12-48 hours)

S3 Standard-IA has retrieval costs - use for infrequent access

EFS is POSIX-compliant, works with Linux-based applications

Amazon S3
An object storage service offering industry-leading scalability, data availability, security, and performance. Stores unlimited objects up to 5TB each, accessible via HTTP/HTTPS APIs, with multiple storage classes for different access patterns and lifecycle policies.
Amazon EBS
A block storage service providing persistent storage volumes for EC2 instances. Offers high performance with up to 256,000 IOPS and 4,000 MB/s throughput, tied to a specific Availability Zone, ideal for boot volumes, databases, and applications requiring low-latency block access.
Amazon EFS
A fully managed elastic file system that automatically grows and shrinks as files are added and removed. Supports Network File System (NFS) protocol for concurrent access from multiple EC2 instances across AZs, ideal for shared file workloads and lift-and-shift migrations.
Amazon S3 Glacier
A secure, durable, and low-cost storage class for data archiving and long-term backup. Offers three retrieval options (Instant Retrieval, Flexible Retrieval, Deep Archive) to balance cost and access speed, with 99.999999999% durability and comprehensive compliance features.
Amazon S3
Storage Type: File Storage (Various Protocols) | Access Method: HTTP/HTTPS via API | Availability: Virtually unlimited | Multi-AZ/AZ-specific: Multi-AZ by default | Performance: High throughput, variable latency | Use Cases: Backups, archives, static websites, big data, ML data lakes | Pricing Model: Per GB stored + requests + data transfer | Durability: High (replicated within AZ/backup) | Encryption: SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C | Access Pattern: REST API, SDK, Console | Latency: Milliseconds (object retrieval)
Amazon EBS
Storage Type: Block storage | Access Method: Attached to EC2 instances | Availability: Up to 64 TB per volume | Multi-AZ/AZ-specific: AZ-specific | Performance: Up to 256,000 IOPS, low latency | Use Cases: Databases, boot volumes, transactional workloads | Pricing Model: Per GB provisioned + IOPS (if provisioned) | Durability: 99.8% - 99.9% (replicated within AZ) | Encryption: EBS encryption (KMS) | Access Pattern: Attached to single EC2 instance | Latency: Sub-millisecond to single-digit ms
Amazon EFS
Storage Type: File storage (NFS) | Access Method: Mount to multiple instances | Availability: Automatic, petabyte-scale | Multi-AZ/AZ-specific: Multi-AZ by default | Performance: Low latency, concurrent access | Use Cases: Content management, shared storage, DevOps, ML training data | Pricing Model: Per GB used (pay-as-you-grow) | Durability: High (replicated across AZs) | Encryption: Encryption at rest (KMS) | Access Pattern: Multiple EC2 instances simultaneously | Latency: Low latency (file operations)
Amazon S3 Glacier
Storage Type: Object storage (archive) | Access Method: HTTP/HTTPS (with retrieval delay) | Availability: Virtually unlimited | Multi-AZ/AZ-specific: Multi-AZ by default | Performance: Retrieval from minutes to hours | Use Cases: Long-term archival, compliance | Pricing Model: Instant, Flexible, Deep Archive | Durability: 99.999999999% (11 9s) | Encryption: Same as S3 | Access Pattern: Infrequent — archival retrieval; Expedited (1-5 min), Standard (3-5 hrs), Bulk (5-12 hrs) | Latency: Minutes to hours depending on retrieval tier (Expedited: 1-5 min, Standard: 3-5 hrs, Bulk: 5-12 hrs)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#81 Amazon S3 vs EBS vs EFS vs Instance Store
Storage SAA-008_2 4 cols 11 rows
▼

AWS storage services each have a distinct purpose matching different I/O patterns and access needs. The exam will describe a workload's characteristics — persistence, sharing, performance, access pattern — and ask which storage is appropriate. The key dimensions are: object vs block vs file, and shared vs single-instance vs ephemeral.

Feature Amazon S3 Amazon EBS Amazon EFS EC2 Instance Store
Storage Type Object Storage Block Storage File Storage (NFS) Block Storage (ephemeral)
Persistence Permanent — independent of EC2 Persistent (data survives instance stop/reboot/termination) Permanent — independent of EC2 Ephemeral (data lost on stop/hibernate/termination)
Shared Access Yes — accessible from anywhere globally No — single EC2 instance (unless Multi-Attach feature on io1/io2) Yes — multiple EC2 instances mount simultaneously No — only the local instance
Performance High throughput, higher latency (HTTP-based) Low latency, consistent IOPS (gp3: 3,000 IOPS baseline; io2: up to 64,000 IOPS) Moderate — scales with throughput mode HIGHEST IOPS — millions of IOPS, lowest latency
Scalability Virtually unlimited — exabytes of data Up to 64 TB per volume (gp3/io2) Auto-scales — petabyte scale Fixed — limited to physical host capacity
Typical Use Case Backups, data lake, static assets, media storage, ML training data Databases, critical data, applications requiring persistent storage Shared file system for web server farms, CMS, container persistent storage Cache, buffer, temp files, Hadoop HDFS local storage
S3 Storage Classes (bonus) Standard → Standard-IA → One Zone-IA → Glacier Instant → Glacier Flexible → Deep Archive gp3 (general), io2 (high IOPS), st1 (throughput), sc1 (cold HDD) Standard → Infrequent Access (EFS-IA) tiers N/A — no tiers
Data Retention Persistent — data stored until explicitly deleted; survives any instance lifecycle Data remains after instance termination Persistent — data stored until explicitly deleted; independent of EC2 instances Ephemeral — data lost when instance stops, terminates, or hardware fails
Accessibility Global — accessible from anywhere via HTTPS; cross-region replication available Accessible from any compatible EC2 instance Regional — mountable by multiple EC2 instances across AZs in same region; cross-region with replication Local only — accessible only from the attached EC2 instance
Snapshot Support Not applicable — S3 uses versioning instead of snapshots for data protection Yes (point-in-time snapshots to S3) Yes — AWS Backup supports EFS backups; EFS-to-EFS replication also available No — cannot snapshot instance store volumes; must manually copy data elsewhere
Cost Low — starts at ~$0.023/GB/month (Standard); cheaper tiers for infrequent/archival Pay per GB provisioned Moderate — ~$0.30/GB/month (Standard); IA tier available at ~$0.025/GB Included — no additional cost; included with instance types that offer local storage
⚠️ Exam Tips

S3 = object storage (unlimited, 11 nines durability); EBS = block storage (single EC2); EFS = file storage (shared NFS)

Instance Store = ephemeral — ALL DATA LOST when instance stops or terminates

S3 is the default storage answer unless you specifically need block or file semantics

EBS volumes can be attached to ONE EC2 instance (except io1/io2 Multi-Attach in same AZ)

EFS is accessible from multiple instances across AZs — ideal for shared content and CMS

For the exam: 'shared storage' = EFS; 'boot volume' = EBS; 'web assets/data lake' = S3; 'cache/scratch' = Instance Store

Amazon S3
S3 (Simple Storage Service) is object storage — infinitely scalable, durable (11 nines), and accessible from anywhere over HTTP. It is ideal for storing files, backups, static website assets, data lakes, and any data accessed by multiple services or users globally.
Amazon EBS
EBS (Elastic Block Store) is persistent block storage attached to a SINGLE EC2 instance at a time — like a hard drive for your virtual machine. It persists data after the instance stops, supports snapshots, and is ideal for databases, boot volumes, and transactional workloads requiring low-latency I/O.
Amazon EFS
EFS (Elastic File System) is a managed NFS (Network File System) that can be mounted by MULTIPLE EC2 instances simultaneously — it is a shared file system that auto-scales. Ideal for applications that need shared file access across many servers, like web server farms or container workloads.
EC2 Instance Store
Instance Store is ephemeral (temporary) block storage physically attached to the EC2 host. It offers extremely high IOPS and very low latency but ALL data is LOST when the instance stops or terminates. Use only for temporary data: caches, buffers, scratch space.
Amazon S3
Storage Type: Object Storage | Persistence: Permanent — independent of EC2 | Shared Access: Yes — accessible from anywhere globally | Performance: High throughput, higher latency (HTTP-based) | Scalability: Virtually unlimited — exabytes of data | Typical Use Case: Backups, data lake, static assets, media storage, ML training data | S3 Storage Classes (bonus): Standard → Standard-IA → One Zone-IA → Glacier Instant → Glacier Flexible → Deep Archive | Data Retention: Persistent — data stored until explicitly deleted; survives any instance lifecycle | Accessibility: Global — accessible from anywhere via HTTPS; cross-region replication available | Snapshot Support: Not applicable — S3 uses versioning instead of snapshots for data protection | Cost: Low — starts at ~$0.023/GB/month (Standard); cheaper tiers for infrequent/archival
Amazon EBS
Storage Type: Block Storage | Persistence: Persistent (data survives instance stop/reboot/termination) | Shared Access: No — single EC2 instance (unless Multi-Attach feature on io1/io2) | Performance: Low latency, consistent IOPS (gp3: 3,000 IOPS baseline; io2: up to 64,000 IOPS) | Scalability: Up to 64 TB per volume (gp3/io2) | Typical Use Case: Databases, critical data, applications requiring persistent storage | S3 Storage Classes (bonus): gp3 (general), io2 (high IOPS), st1 (throughput), sc1 (cold HDD) | Data Retention: Data remains after instance termination | Accessibility: Accessible from any compatible EC2 instance | Snapshot Support: Yes (point-in-time snapshots to S3) | Cost: Pay per GB provisioned
Amazon EFS
Storage Type: File Storage (NFS) | Persistence: Permanent — independent of EC2 | Shared Access: Yes — multiple EC2 instances mount simultaneously | Performance: Moderate — scales with throughput mode | Scalability: Auto-scales — petabyte scale | Typical Use Case: Shared file system for web server farms, CMS, container persistent storage | S3 Storage Classes (bonus): Standard → Infrequent Access (EFS-IA) tiers | Data Retention: Persistent — data stored until explicitly deleted; independent of EC2 instances | Accessibility: Regional — mountable by multiple EC2 instances across AZs in same region; cross-region with replication | Snapshot Support: Yes — AWS Backup supports EFS backups; EFS-to-EFS replication also available | Cost: Moderate — ~$0.30/GB/month (Standard); IA tier available at ~$0.025/GB
EC2 Instance Store
Storage Type: Block Storage (ephemeral) | Persistence: Ephemeral (data lost on stop/hibernate/termination) | Shared Access: No — only the local instance | Performance: HIGHEST IOPS — millions of IOPS, lowest latency | Scalability: Fixed — limited to physical host capacity | Typical Use Case: Cache, buffer, temp files, Hadoop HDFS local storage | S3 Storage Classes (bonus): N/A — no tiers | Data Retention: Ephemeral — data lost when instance stops, terminates, or hardware fails | Accessibility: Local only — accessible only from the attached EC2 instance | Snapshot Support: No — cannot snapshot instance store volumes; must manually copy data elsewhere | Cost: Included — no additional cost; included with instance types that offer local storage
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#82 Block Storage Comparison
Storage block_storage_comparison 8 cols 14 rows
▼

Comparing block storage and file storage services for compute workloads

AWS block storage provides raw storage volumes that can be formatted with a file system and used like physical hard drives. This comparison covers Amazon EBS volume types and EC2 Instance Store, which are essential for the SAA-C03 exam. Understanding performance characteristics, use cases, and durability differences is critical for designing high-performance and cost-effective storage architectures.

Feature EBS gp3 (General Purpose SSD) EBS gp2 (General Purpose SSD) EBS io2/io2 Block Express (Provisioned IOPS SSD) EBS io1 (Provisioned IOPS SSD) EBS st1 (Throughput Optimized HDD) EBS sc1 (Cold HDD) Instance Store Amazon EBS
Volume Type SSD SSD SSD SSD HDD HDD SSD/NVMe (physical) Multiple types: gp3/gp2 (General Purpose SSD), io2/io1 (Provisioned IOPS SSD), st1 (Throughput HDD), sc1 (Cold HDD)
Max Volume Size 16 TiB 16 TiB 64 TiB (io2), 256 TiB (Block Express) 16 TiB 16 TiB 16 TiB Up to ~60 TiB (instance dependent) 16 TiB for most types; io2 Block Express supports up to 64 TiB
Max IOPS 16,000 16,000 256,000 (Block Express) 64,000 500 250 Millions (NVMe) Up to 256,000 IOPS (io2 Block Express); gp3 up to 16,000; io2 up to 64,000
Max Throughput 1,000 MiB/s 250 MiB/s (burst to 1,000) 4,000 MiB/s (Block Express) 1,000 MiB/s 500 MiB/s 250 MiB/s Up to 10+ GB/s Up to 4,000 MiB/s (io2 Block Express); gp3 up to 1,000 MiB/s; st1 up to 500 MiB/s
Latency Single-digit milliseconds Single-digit milliseconds Sub-millisecond (Block Express) Single-digit milliseconds Tens of milliseconds Tens of milliseconds Microseconds Sub-millisecond for io2 Block Express; 1-4ms for gp3; varies by volume type
Availability 99.8% - 99.9% 99.8% - 99.9% 99.999% (io2) 99.8% - 99.9% 99.8% - 99.9% 99.8% - 99.9% Ephemeral (data lost on stop/terminate) Single AZ (99.999% within AZ)
Multi-Attach Support No No Yes (Nitro instances) Yes No No No — physically attached to a single host; cannot share across instances Yes — io1/io2 volumes support Multi-Attach to up to 16 Nitro instances in same AZ
Boot Volume Support Yes Yes Yes Yes No No No Yes — gp2, gp3, io1, io2 can be used as boot volumes; HDD types (st1, sc1) cannot
Pricing Model Provisioned size + IOPS + throughput Provisioned size only Provisioned size + IOPS Provisioned size + IOPS Provisioned size only Provisioned size only Included in instance cost Pay per usage based on file system type
Best Use Cases Boot volumes, dev/test, low-latency apps, most workloads Legacy workloads (gp3 preferred for new) I/O-intensive databases, mission-critical apps Legacy I/O-intensive workloads (io2 preferred) Big data, data warehouses, log processing Cold data, infrequent access, lowest cost HDD Temporary storage, caches, buffers, high-IOPS needs Web serving, content management, home directories
Storage Type SSD — General Purpose with independently configurable IOPS and throughput SSD — General Purpose with burst credits; IOPS scales with volume size SSD — Provisioned IOPS with 99.999% durability; Block Express for highest performance SSD — Provisioned IOPS; legacy type being replaced by io2 HDD — Throughput optimized for sequential reads; cannot be boot volume HDD — Cold storage; lowest cost EBS volume for infrequent access workloads NVMe SSD or HDD — physically attached to host; type depends on instance family Block storage (like virtual hard drives)
Access Pattern Random and sequential — balanced for boot volumes, dev/test, small-medium databases Random and sequential — similar to gp3 but with burst credit model Random — optimized for high random I/O; critical databases (Oracle, SAP, SQL Server) Random — similar to io2; for I/O-intensive database workloads Sequential — optimized for large sequential reads/writes; big data, log processing, data warehouses Sequential — infrequent access; lowest cost for rarely accessed sequential data Random and sequential — very high IOPS for temporary data; buffer, cache, scratch space Single EC2 instance (except Multi-Attach)
Scaling Elastic — modify volume size, IOPS, and throughput independently without downtime Size-based — IOPS scales linearly with volume size (3 IOPS/GiB); resize online Elastic — independently provision IOPS up to 256K and throughput; modify online Elastic — provision IOPS independently up to 64K; modify volume online Size-based — throughput scales with volume size; resize online Size-based — throughput scales with volume size; resize online Fixed — cannot resize; determined by instance type; must change instance type Manual - attach/detach volumes
Performance 3,000 baseline IOPS + 125 MiB/s; scalable to 16,000 IOPS + 1,000 MiB/s 3 IOPS/GiB (100-16,000); burst to 3,000 IOPS; throughput up to 250 MiB/s Up to 256,000 IOPS + 4,000 MiB/s (Block Express); 99.999% durability Up to 64,000 IOPS + 1,000 MiB/s; 50 IOPS/GiB ratio Up to 500 MiB/s throughput; 500 IOPS max; optimized for large sequential I/O Up to 250 MiB/s throughput; 250 IOPS max; lowest cost per GB Highest raw IOPS — millions of IOPS possible on NVMe; lowest latency; ephemeral Highest - consistent low latency IOPS
💡 Mnemonics

EBE = EBS for boot and databases (single), EFS for shared files (elastic), FSx for specialized file systems

⚠️ Exam Tips

EBS volumes are tied to ONE availability zone - if AZ fails, EBS fails

EFS is accessible from MULTIPLE AZs - great for distributed workloads

Use FSx for Windows-based applications (requires SMB protocol)

FSx for Lustre is optimized for high-performance computing and ML training

EFS uses POSIX permissions; FSx for Windows uses NTFS permissions

Amazon EBS
Feature-rich file storage with support for Windows File Server, Lustre, NetApp ONTAP, OpenZFS, and other file systems, enabling migration of enterprise workloads to AWS.
EBS gp3 (General Purpose SSD)
Volume Type: SSD | Max Volume Size: 16 TiB | Max IOPS: 16,000 | Max Throughput: 1,000 MiB/s | Latency: Single-digit milliseconds | Availability: 99.8% - 99.9% | Multi-Attach Support: No | Boot Volume Support: Yes | Pricing Model: Provisioned size + IOPS + throughput | Best Use Cases: Boot volumes, dev/test, low-latency apps, most workloads | Storage Type: SSD — General Purpose with independently configurable IOPS and throughput | Access Pattern: Random and sequential — balanced for boot volumes, dev/test, small-medium databases | Scaling: Elastic — modify volume size, IOPS, and throughput independently without downtime | Performance: 3,000 baseline IOPS + 125 MiB/s; scalable to 16,000 IOPS + 1,000 MiB/s
EBS gp2 (General Purpose SSD)
Volume Type: SSD | Max Volume Size: 16 TiB | Max IOPS: 16,000 | Max Throughput: 250 MiB/s (burst to 1,000) | Latency: Single-digit milliseconds | Availability: 99.8% - 99.9% | Multi-Attach Support: No | Boot Volume Support: Yes | Pricing Model: Provisioned size only | Best Use Cases: Legacy workloads (gp3 preferred for new) | Storage Type: SSD — General Purpose with burst credits; IOPS scales with volume size | Access Pattern: Random and sequential — similar to gp3 but with burst credit model | Scaling: Size-based — IOPS scales linearly with volume size (3 IOPS/GiB); resize online | Performance: 3 IOPS/GiB (100-16,000); burst to 3,000 IOPS; throughput up to 250 MiB/s
EBS io2/io2 Block Express (Provisioned IOPS SSD)
Volume Type: SSD | Max Volume Size: 64 TiB (io2), 256 TiB (Block Express) | Max IOPS: 256,000 (Block Express) | Max Throughput: 4,000 MiB/s (Block Express) | Latency: Sub-millisecond (Block Express) | Availability: 99.999% (io2) | Multi-Attach Support: Yes (Nitro instances) | Boot Volume Support: Yes | Pricing Model: Provisioned size + IOPS | Best Use Cases: I/O-intensive databases, mission-critical apps | Storage Type: SSD — Provisioned IOPS with 99.999% durability; Block Express for highest performance | Access Pattern: Random — optimized for high random I/O; critical databases (Oracle, SAP, SQL Server) | Scaling: Elastic — independently provision IOPS up to 256K and throughput; modify online | Performance: Up to 256,000 IOPS + 4,000 MiB/s (Block Express); 99.999% durability
EBS io1 (Provisioned IOPS SSD)
Volume Type: SSD | Max Volume Size: 16 TiB | Max IOPS: 64,000 | Max Throughput: 1,000 MiB/s | Latency: Single-digit milliseconds | Availability: 99.8% - 99.9% | Multi-Attach Support: Yes | Boot Volume Support: Yes | Pricing Model: Provisioned size + IOPS | Best Use Cases: Legacy I/O-intensive workloads (io2 preferred) | Storage Type: SSD — Provisioned IOPS; legacy type being replaced by io2 | Access Pattern: Random — similar to io2; for I/O-intensive database workloads | Scaling: Elastic — provision IOPS independently up to 64K; modify volume online | Performance: Up to 64,000 IOPS + 1,000 MiB/s; 50 IOPS/GiB ratio
EBS st1 (Throughput Optimized HDD)
Volume Type: HDD | Max Volume Size: 16 TiB | Max IOPS: 500 | Max Throughput: 500 MiB/s | Latency: Tens of milliseconds | Availability: 99.8% - 99.9% | Multi-Attach Support: No | Boot Volume Support: No | Pricing Model: Provisioned size only | Best Use Cases: Big data, data warehouses, log processing | Storage Type: HDD — Throughput optimized for sequential reads; cannot be boot volume | Access Pattern: Sequential — optimized for large sequential reads/writes; big data, log processing, data warehouses | Scaling: Size-based — throughput scales with volume size; resize online | Performance: Up to 500 MiB/s throughput; 500 IOPS max; optimized for large sequential I/O
EBS sc1 (Cold HDD)
Volume Type: HDD | Max Volume Size: 16 TiB | Max IOPS: 250 | Max Throughput: 250 MiB/s | Latency: Tens of milliseconds | Availability: 99.8% - 99.9% | Multi-Attach Support: No | Boot Volume Support: No | Pricing Model: Provisioned size only | Best Use Cases: Cold data, infrequent access, lowest cost HDD | Storage Type: HDD — Cold storage; lowest cost EBS volume for infrequent access workloads | Access Pattern: Sequential — infrequent access; lowest cost for rarely accessed sequential data | Scaling: Size-based — throughput scales with volume size; resize online | Performance: Up to 250 MiB/s throughput; 250 IOPS max; lowest cost per GB
Instance Store
Volume Type: SSD/NVMe (physical) | Max Volume Size: Up to ~60 TiB (instance dependent) | Max IOPS: Millions (NVMe) | Max Throughput: Up to 10+ GB/s | Latency: Microseconds | Availability: Ephemeral (data lost on stop/terminate) | Multi-Attach Support: No — physically attached to a single host; cannot share across instances | Boot Volume Support: No | Pricing Model: Included in instance cost | Best Use Cases: Temporary storage, caches, buffers, high-IOPS needs | Storage Type: NVMe SSD or HDD — physically attached to host; type depends on instance family | Access Pattern: Random and sequential — very high IOPS for temporary data; buffer, cache, scratch space | Scaling: Fixed — cannot resize; determined by instance type; must change instance type | Performance: Highest raw IOPS — millions of IOPS possible on NVMe; lowest latency; ephemeral
Amazon EBS
Volume Type: Multiple types: gp3/gp2 (General Purpose SSD), io2/io1 (Provisioned IOPS SSD), st1 (Throughput HDD), sc1 (Cold HDD) | Max Volume Size: 16 TiB for most types; io2 Block Express supports up to 64 TiB | Max IOPS: Up to 256,000 IOPS (io2 Block Express); gp3 up to 16,000; io2 up to 64,000 | Max Throughput: Up to 4,000 MiB/s (io2 Block Express); gp3 up to 1,000 MiB/s; st1 up to 500 MiB/s | Latency: Sub-millisecond for io2 Block Express; 1-4ms for gp3; varies by volume type | Availability: Single AZ (99.999% within AZ) | Multi-Attach Support: Yes — io1/io2 volumes support Multi-Attach to up to 16 Nitro instances in same AZ | Boot Volume Support: Yes — gp2, gp3, io1, io2 can be used as boot volumes; HDD types (st1, sc1) cannot | Pricing Model: Pay per usage based on file system type | Best Use Cases: Web serving, content management, home directories | Storage Type: Block storage (like virtual hard drives) | Access Pattern: Single EC2 instance (except Multi-Attach) | Scaling: Manual - attach/detach volumes | Performance: Highest - consistent low latency IOPS
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#83 Complete AWS Storage Comparison
Storage complete_aws_storage_comparison 4 cols 9 rows
▼

Comprehensive comparison of all major AWS storage services

Feature Amazon S3 Amazon EBS Amazon EFS Amazon FSx
Protocol REST API (HTTPS) Block device (iSCSI) NFSv4.1 SMB, NFS depending on type
Data Access Anywhere (web, apps, CLI) Single EC2 instance in same AZ Thousands of EC2 instances, multi-AZ Multiple instances based on type
Max Object/File Size 5 TB per object 16 TiB per volume 47.9 TiB per file Varies by file system
Storage Capacity Virtually unlimited Up to 16 TiB per volume Elastic, unlimited Scalable per file system
Durability 99.999999999% (11 9's) 99.999% within single AZ 99.999999999% (multi-AZ) 99.999% (varies by type)
Latency Low (milliseconds) Lowest (consistent) Low (consistent) Low to very low (optimized)
Cost per GB Lowest for object storage Medium (provisioned IOPS is higher) Medium-High Higher (performance optimized)
Data from Outside VPC Yes (with proper auth) No Via VPN or Direct Connect Via VPN or Direct Connect
Use for Databases No (use backup tier) Yes (primary storage) No (file storage not suitable) Yes for FSx for ONTAP/NetApp
💡 Mnemonics

SE3F = S3 is for Everything (object), EBS for EC2 boot, EFS for File sharing, FSx for Fancy specialized file systems

⚠️ Exam Tips

S3 is the default choice for object storage - it's the most versatile

If question mentions 'multiple EC2 instances accessing shared storage' → EFS

If question mentions 'Windows file shares' → FSx for Windows

If question mentions 'high-performance computing or ML training' → FSx for Lustre

EBS does NOT replicate across AZs - use snapshots to S3 for cross-AZ backup

Amazon S3
Object storage accessible globally via HTTP/HTTPS API, designed for unlimited scalability and maximum durability with extensive management features.
Amazon EBS
Block storage volumes for EC2 instances with persistent storage and high IOPS capabilities, limited to a single availability zone.
Amazon EFS
Scalable file storage for EC2 instances using NFSv4 protocol, automatically managing capacity and accessible across multiple AZs.
Amazon FSx
Fully managed third-party file systems optimized for specific enterprise workloads including Windows, Lustre, NetApp ONTAP, and OpenZFS.
Amazon S3
Protocol: REST API (HTTPS) | Data Access: Anywhere (web, apps, CLI) | Max Object/File Size: 5 TB per object | Storage Capacity: Virtually unlimited | Durability: 99.999999999% (11 9's) | Latency: Low (milliseconds) | Cost per GB: Lowest for object storage | Data from Outside VPC: Yes (with proper auth) | Use for Databases: No (use backup tier)
Amazon EBS
Protocol: Block device (iSCSI) | Data Access: Single EC2 instance in same AZ | Max Object/File Size: 16 TiB per volume | Storage Capacity: Up to 16 TiB per volume | Durability: 99.999% within single AZ | Latency: Lowest (consistent) | Cost per GB: Medium (provisioned IOPS is higher) | Data from Outside VPC: No | Use for Databases: Yes (primary storage)
Amazon EFS
Protocol: NFSv4.1 | Data Access: Thousands of EC2 instances, multi-AZ | Max Object/File Size: 47.9 TiB per file | Storage Capacity: Elastic, unlimited | Durability: 99.999999999% (multi-AZ) | Latency: Low (consistent) | Cost per GB: Medium-High | Data from Outside VPC: Via VPN or Direct Connect | Use for Databases: No (file storage not suitable)
Amazon FSx
Protocol: SMB, NFS depending on type | Data Access: Multiple instances based on type | Max Object/File Size: Varies by file system | Storage Capacity: Scalable per file system | Durability: 99.999% (varies by type) | Latency: Low to very low (optimized) | Cost per GB: Higher (performance optimized) | Data from Outside VPC: Via VPN or Direct Connect | Use for Databases: Yes for FSx for ONTAP/NetApp
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#84 File Storage Comparison
Storage file_storage_comparison 5 cols 11 rows
▼

AWS file storage services provide managed, scalable file systems accessible via standard protocols (NFS, SMB). This comparison covers Amazon EFS for Linux workloads and Amazon FSx family for Windows, high-performance computing, and NetApp workloads. Understanding the differences between these services is crucial for selecting the right file storage solution based on protocol requirements, performance needs, and integration with existing infrastructure.

Feature EFS Standard EFS Infrequent Access (IA) FSx for Windows File Server FSx for Lustre FSx for NetApp ONTAP
Protocol Support NFS v4.0, v4.1, v4.2 NFS v4.0, v4.1, v4.2 SMB 2.0-3.1.1, NTFS, Active Directory Lustre client, HDFS (via S3 integration) NFS v3/v4, SMB, iSCSI
Operating System Linux-based Linux-based Windows Linux (Lustre client required) Linux, Windows, mixed environments
Performance Mode General Purpose, Max I/O General Purpose, Max I/O Standard, Multi-AZ Scratch (temp), Persistent Single-AZ, Multi-AZ
