Throughput Scaling Bursting (default), Provisioned Bursting (default), Provisioned Up to 2+ GB/s Up to 1,000+ GB/s Up to 4+ GB/s
Latency Low (single-digit ms) Low (single-digit ms, retrieval time) Sub-millisecond (SSD) Sub-millisecond to microseconds Sub-millisecond
Durability 99.999999999% (11 nines) 99.999999999% (11 nines) 99.9999% (Multi-AZ) 99.9% (Persistent), None (Scratch) 99.999%
Availability 99.9% (Regional) 99.9% (Regional) 99.99% (Multi-AZ) 99.99% (Persistent) 99.99% (Multi-AZ)
Multi-AZ Support Yes (Regional service) Yes (Regional service) Yes (optional) No (Single-AZ only) Yes (optional)
Storage Efficiency Lifecycle management to IA Automatic tiering Deduplication, Compression None Deduplication, Compression, Thin provisioning
Data Replication Cross-Region Replication Cross-Region Replication Multi-AZ, Backups S3 integration, Backups SnapMirror replication, Multi-AZ
Best Use Cases Content management, web serving, container storage, home directories Long-lived, infrequently accessed file data Windows applications, SQL Server, .NET apps, AD integration HPC, ML training, video rendering, financial modeling Lift-and-shift ONTAP workloads, hybrid cloud, mixed OS environments
⚠️ Exam Tips

EFS = NFS for Linux; FSx for Windows = SMB for Windows; FSx for Lustre = HPC performance

EFS IA (Infrequent Access) lifecycle policy moves files automatically for up to 92% savings

FSx for Lustre integrates seamlessly with S3 — presents an S3 bucket as a high-performance file system

FSx for NetApp ONTAP supports NFS, SMB, and iSCSI simultaneously from the same file system

For the exam: Linux shared storage = EFS; Windows shared storage = FSx for Windows; ML training = FSx for Lustre

EFS Standard
Fully managed NFS file system with automatic scaling up to petabytes. Supports thousands of concurrent EC2 connections across AZs. Ideal for shared file storage, CMS, and development tools.
EFS Infrequent Access (IA)
Lower-cost EFS storage class for files not accessed every day. Uses lifecycle policies to automatically move files between Standard and IA. Up to 92% lower cost than Standard.
FSx for Windows File Server
Fully managed Windows-native file system built on Windows Server with full SMB protocol, NTFS, and Active Directory integration. Ideal for Windows workloads, .NET applications, and SQL Server.
FSx for Lustre
High-performance file system designed for compute-intensive workloads. Provides sub-millisecond latencies and hundreds of GB/s throughput. Integrates seamlessly with S3 for ML training and HPC.
FSx for NetApp ONTAP
Managed NetApp ONTAP file system supporting NFS, SMB, and iSCSI protocols simultaneously. Provides advanced data management features like snapshots, clones, and data tiering.
EFS Standard
Protocol Support: NFS v4.0, v4.1, v4.2 | Operating System: Linux-based | Performance Mode: General Purpose, Max I/O | Throughput Scaling: Bursting (default), Provisioned | Latency: Low (single-digit ms) | Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Availability: 99.9% (Regional) | Multi-AZ Support: Yes (Regional service) | Storage Efficiency: Lifecycle management to IA | Data Replication: Cross-Region Replication | Best Use Cases: Content management, web serving, container storage, home directories
EFS Infrequent Access (IA)
Protocol Support: NFS v4.0, v4.1, v4.2 | Operating System: Linux-based | Performance Mode: General Purpose, Max I/O | Throughput Scaling: Bursting (default), Provisioned | Latency: Low (single-digit ms, retrieval time) | Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Availability: 99.9% (Regional) | Multi-AZ Support: Yes (Regional service) | Storage Efficiency: Automatic tiering | Data Replication: Cross-Region Replication | Best Use Cases: Long-lived, infrequently accessed file data
FSx for Windows File Server
Protocol Support: SMB 2.0-3.1.1, NTFS, Active Directory | Operating System: Windows | Performance Mode: Standard, Multi-AZ | Throughput Scaling: Up to 2+ GB/s | Latency: Sub-millisecond (SSD) | Durability: 99.9999% (Multi-AZ) | Availability: 99.99% (Multi-AZ) | Multi-AZ Support: Yes (optional) | Storage Efficiency: Deduplication, Compression | Data Replication: Multi-AZ, Backups | Best Use Cases: Windows applications, SQL Server, .NET apps, AD integration
FSx for Lustre
Protocol Support: Lustre client, HDFS (via S3 integration) | Operating System: Linux (Lustre client required) | Performance Mode: Scratch (temp), Persistent | Throughput Scaling: Up to 1,000+ GB/s | Latency: Sub-millisecond to microseconds | Durability: 99.9% (Persistent), None (Scratch) | Availability: 99.99% (Persistent) | Multi-AZ Support: No (Single-AZ only) | Storage Efficiency: None | Data Replication: S3 integration, Backups | Best Use Cases: HPC, ML training, video rendering, financial modeling
FSx for NetApp ONTAP
Protocol Support: NFS v3/v4, SMB, iSCSI | Operating System: Linux, Windows, mixed environments | Performance Mode: Single-AZ, Multi-AZ | Throughput Scaling: Up to 4+ GB/s | Latency: Sub-millisecond | Durability: 99.999% | Availability: 99.99% (Multi-AZ) | Multi-AZ Support: Yes (optional) | Storage Efficiency: Deduplication, Compression, Thin provisioning | Data Replication: SnapMirror replication, Multi-AZ | Best Use Cases: Lift-and-shift ONTAP workloads, hybrid cloud, mixed OS environments
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#85 S3 Storage Classes Comparison
Storage s3_storage_classes_comparison 8 cols 13 rows
▼

Comparison of S3 storage classes for cost optimization based on access patterns

Amazon S3 offers multiple storage classes designed for different use cases, access patterns, and cost requirements. Understanding the differences between these classes is critical for the SAA-C03 exam, as questions frequently test your ability to select the most cost-effective storage class based on data access frequency, retrieval time requirements, and durability needs. Each storage class offers the same 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability but differs in availability, retrieval times, minimum storage duration, and pricing.

Feature S3 Standard S3 Intelligent-Tiering S3 Standard-IA S3 One Zone-IA S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval S3 Glacier Deep Archive S3 Express One Zone
Durability 99.999999999% (11 nines) 99.999999999% (11 nines) 99.999999999% (11 nines) 99.999999999% (11 nines) 99.999999999% (11 nines) 99.999999999% (11 nines) 99.999999999% (11 nines) 99.999999999% (11 nines)
Availability Zones 99.99% 99.9% 99.9% 99.5% 99.9% 99.99% (after retrieval) 99.99% (after retrieval) 99.95%
Retrieval Time Milliseconds Milliseconds Milliseconds Milliseconds Milliseconds (similar to Standard) 1-5 minutes (Expedited), 3-5 hours (Standard), 5-12 hours (Bulk) 12 hours (Standard), 48 hours (Bulk) Single-digit milliseconds
Minimum Object Size None None 128 KB 128 KB 128 KB 40 KB (after compression) 40 KB (after compression) None
Minimum Storage Duration 30 days None 30 days 30 days 90 days 90 days 180 days None
Retrieval Fee Per GB retrieved None Per GB retrieved Per GB retrieved Per GB retrieved (higher than Standard-IA) Per GB retrieved; expedited costs more Per GB retrieved Per GB retrieved
Storage Cost (Relative) Highest ($$$$) $$ (automatic optimization) $ (lower than Standard) $ (20% less than Standard-IA) ¢ (lowest for instant access) ¢¢ (lower cost, longer retrieval) ¢¢¢ (lowest cost) High ($$$)
AZ Redundancy 3+ AZs 3+ AZs 3+ AZs 1 AZ only 3+ AZs 3+ AZs 3+ AZs 1 AZ only
Primary Use Case Frequently accessed data, dynamic websites, content distribution Unknown/variable access patterns, data lakes, analytics Long-lived, infrequently accessed backups, disaster recovery Secondary backups, easily re-creatable data, on-premises copies Long-term storage, quarterly access, archive repositories Backups, media archives, compliance data with flexible retrieval Long-term retention, compliance archives, tape replacement High-performance, single-AZ applications, latency-sensitive workloads
Monitoring/Automation None Auto-tiering (frequent/infrequent/archive access tiers) Lifecycle policies Lifecycle policies Lifecycle policies Lifecycle policies Lifecycle policies None
Designed For Frequently accessed data Unknown or changing access patterns Infrequently accessed, immediate access needed Infrequent access, reproducible data Rarely accessed, instant retrieval needed Archives, 1-2 accesses per year Long-term retention, compliance Performance-critical applications needing single-digit millisecond latency — ML training, analytics, real-time data processing
Cost Ranking 60% lower than Standard Variable — automatically moves data between tiers; no retrieval fees; small monthly monitoring fee per object Low storage cost (~40% less than Standard) but charges per-GB retrieval fee; minimum 30-day and 128KB billing ~20% cheaper than Standard-IA; single AZ reduces durability; retrieval fees apply 68% lower than Standard-IA Lowest for infrequent access Lowest storage cost (~$1/TB/month); highest retrieval cost and time (12-48 hours) Highest storage and request cost; optimized for speed (10x faster than Standard); single AZ
S3 Intelligent-Tiering N/A (it's a tier within Intelligent-Tiering) Automatic — built-in tiering between Frequent, Infrequent, and Archive tiers based on access patterns Not automatic — must use Lifecycle policies to move data to/from Standard-IA Not automatic — must use Lifecycle policies to move data to/from One Zone-IA N/A (it's a tier within Intelligent-Tiering) N/A (it's a tier within Intelligent-Tiering) Not automatic — must use Lifecycle policies to transition to Deep Archive Not applicable — S3 Express One Zone is a separate directory bucket type, not part of Intelligent-Tiering
💡 Mnemonics

S.S.I.G.F - Standard for Speed, Standard-IA for Infrequent, Glacier for archive, Flexible for backup options

⚠️ Exam Tips

Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering when access patterns are unpredictable - AWS auto-tiers objects

Minimum storage durations mean deleting early incurs penalties (pro-rated charge)

Glacier Instant Retrieval offers Standard-like access speeds at much lower cost

Glacier Flexible Retrieval has expedited (1-5 min), standard (3-12 hr), and bulk (5-12 hr) options

S3 Lifecycle policies automate transitions between storage classes

For SAA-C03, know when to recommend Standard vs IA vs Glacier based on access frequency

S3 Standard
Default storage class designed for frequently accessed data with high throughput, low latency, and high availability across multiple Availability Zones.
S3 Intelligent-Tiering
Automatically optimizes storage costs by moving data between access tiers based on changing access patterns, with no retrieval fees or operational overhead.
S3 One Zone-IA
Lower-cost option for infrequently accessed, non-critical data stored in a single Availability Zone, offering 20% lower cost than Standard-IA.
S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval
Archive storage for rarely accessed data that requires millisecond retrieval, offering the lowest-cost storage with instant access for archive workloads.
S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval
Low-cost archive storage for data accessed 1-2 times per year with flexible retrieval options from minutes to hours, ideal for backup and media archives.
S3 Glacier Deep Archive
Lowest-cost storage class for long-term retention of data accessed less than once per year, with retrieval times of 12-48 hours for regulatory compliance.
S3 Standard
Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Availability Zones: 99.99% | Retrieval Time: Milliseconds | Minimum Object Size: None | Minimum Storage Duration: 30 days | Retrieval Fee: Per GB retrieved | Storage Cost (Relative): Highest ($$$$) | AZ Redundancy: 3+ AZs | Primary Use Case: Frequently accessed data, dynamic websites, content distribution | Monitoring/Automation: None | Designed For: Frequently accessed data | Cost Ranking: 60% lower than Standard | S3 Intelligent-Tiering: N/A (it's a tier within Intelligent-Tiering)
S3 Intelligent-Tiering
Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Availability Zones: 99.9% | Retrieval Time: Milliseconds | Minimum Object Size: None | Minimum Storage Duration: None | Retrieval Fee: None | Storage Cost (Relative): $$ (automatic optimization) | AZ Redundancy: 3+ AZs | Primary Use Case: Unknown/variable access patterns, data lakes, analytics | Monitoring/Automation: Auto-tiering (frequent/infrequent/archive access tiers) | Designed For: Unknown or changing access patterns | Cost Ranking: Variable — automatically moves data between tiers; no retrieval fees; small monthly monitoring fee per object | S3 Intelligent-Tiering: Automatic — built-in tiering between Frequent, Infrequent, and Archive tiers based on access patterns
S3 Standard-IA
Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Availability Zones: 99.9% | Retrieval Time: Milliseconds | Minimum Object Size: 128 KB | Minimum Storage Duration: 30 days | Retrieval Fee: Per GB retrieved | Storage Cost (Relative): $ (lower than Standard) | AZ Redundancy: 3+ AZs | Primary Use Case: Long-lived, infrequently accessed backups, disaster recovery | Monitoring/Automation: Lifecycle policies | Designed For: Infrequently accessed, immediate access needed | Cost Ranking: Low storage cost (~40% less than Standard) but charges per-GB retrieval fee; minimum 30-day and 128KB billing | S3 Intelligent-Tiering: Not automatic — must use Lifecycle policies to move data to/from Standard-IA
S3 One Zone-IA
Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Availability Zones: 99.5% | Retrieval Time: Milliseconds | Minimum Object Size: 128 KB | Minimum Storage Duration: 30 days | Retrieval Fee: Per GB retrieved | Storage Cost (Relative): $ (20% less than Standard-IA) | AZ Redundancy: 1 AZ only | Primary Use Case: Secondary backups, easily re-creatable data, on-premises copies | Monitoring/Automation: Lifecycle policies | Designed For: Infrequent access, reproducible data | Cost Ranking: ~20% cheaper than Standard-IA; single AZ reduces durability; retrieval fees apply | S3 Intelligent-Tiering: Not automatic — must use Lifecycle policies to move data to/from One Zone-IA
S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval
Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Availability Zones: 99.9% | Retrieval Time: Milliseconds (similar to Standard) | Minimum Object Size: 128 KB | Minimum Storage Duration: 90 days | Retrieval Fee: Per GB retrieved (higher than Standard-IA) | Storage Cost (Relative): ¢ (lowest for instant access) | AZ Redundancy: 3+ AZs | Primary Use Case: Long-term storage, quarterly access, archive repositories | Monitoring/Automation: Lifecycle policies | Designed For: Rarely accessed, instant retrieval needed | Cost Ranking: 68% lower than Standard-IA | S3 Intelligent-Tiering: N/A (it's a tier within Intelligent-Tiering)
S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval
Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Availability Zones: 99.99% (after retrieval) | Retrieval Time: 1-5 minutes (Expedited), 3-5 hours (Standard), 5-12 hours (Bulk) | Minimum Object Size: 40 KB (after compression) | Minimum Storage Duration: 90 days | Retrieval Fee: Per GB retrieved; expedited costs more | Storage Cost (Relative): ¢¢ (lower cost, longer retrieval) | AZ Redundancy: 3+ AZs | Primary Use Case: Backups, media archives, compliance data with flexible retrieval | Monitoring/Automation: Lifecycle policies | Designed For: Archives, 1-2 accesses per year | Cost Ranking: Lowest for infrequent access | S3 Intelligent-Tiering: N/A (it's a tier within Intelligent-Tiering)
S3 Glacier Deep Archive
Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Availability Zones: 99.99% (after retrieval) | Retrieval Time: 12 hours (Standard), 48 hours (Bulk) | Minimum Object Size: 40 KB (after compression) | Minimum Storage Duration: 180 days | Retrieval Fee: Per GB retrieved | Storage Cost (Relative): ¢¢¢ (lowest cost) | AZ Redundancy: 3+ AZs | Primary Use Case: Long-term retention, compliance archives, tape replacement | Monitoring/Automation: Lifecycle policies | Designed For: Long-term retention, compliance | Cost Ranking: Lowest storage cost (~$1/TB/month); highest retrieval cost and time (12-48 hours) | S3 Intelligent-Tiering: Not automatic — must use Lifecycle policies to transition to Deep Archive
S3 Express One Zone
Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) | Availability Zones: 99.95% | Retrieval Time: Single-digit milliseconds | Minimum Object Size: None | Minimum Storage Duration: None | Retrieval Fee: Per GB retrieved | Storage Cost (Relative): High ($$$) | AZ Redundancy: 1 AZ only | Primary Use Case: High-performance, single-AZ applications, latency-sensitive workloads | Monitoring/Automation: None | Designed For: Performance-critical applications needing single-digit millisecond latency — ML training, analytics, real-time data processing | Cost Ranking: Highest storage and request cost; optimized for speed (10x faster than Standard); single AZ | S3 Intelligent-Tiering: Not applicable — S3 Express One Zone is a separate directory bucket type, not part of Intelligent-Tiering
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#86 S3 Storage Classes — Cost-Optimized Storage
Storage SAA-012 5 cols 6 rows
▼

S3 offers multiple storage classes with very different price points based on access frequency and retrieval times. The exam presents a scenario describing how OFTEN data is accessed and asks you to pick the most cost-effective storage class. The core rule: the less frequently you access data, the cheaper the storage but the more expensive (or slower) the retrieval.

Feature S3 Standard S3 Standard-IA S3 One Zone-IA S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval S3 Glacier Deep Archive
Storage Cost (relative) Highest Lower (~50% less than Standard) Lower (~20% less than Standard-IA) Much Lower (~68% less than Standard) LOWEST — up to 95% less than Standard
Retrieval Time Milliseconds — instant Milliseconds — instant Milliseconds — instant Milliseconds — instant 12-48 hours
Availability Zones 3+ AZs 3+ AZs 1 AZ ONLY 3+ AZs 3+ AZs
Retrieval Fee NONE Yes — per GB retrieved Yes — per GB retrieved Yes — higher retrieval fee Yes — but retrieval is very infrequent
Minimum Storage Duration None 30 days 30 days 90 days 180 days
Best For Websites, ML training data, frequently accessed files Backups, DR copies, data accessed monthly Easily reproducible backups, secondary redundant data Medical image archives, media archives — quarterly access 7-10 year regulatory archives, compliance backups
⚠️ Exam Tips

S3 Standard = frequently accessed data (hot); S3 Standard-IA = infrequent but immediate access

S3 One Zone-IA = lower cost but single AZ — use for reproducible data like thumbnails

S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval = archive with millisecond access for quarterly accessed data

S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval = minutes to hours retrieval; Deep Archive = 12-48 hour retrieval

S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves data between tiers based on access patterns — no retrieval fees

Lifecycle policies automate transitions between storage classes based on object age

S3 Standard
S3 Standard is the default general-purpose storage for frequently accessed data — high durability (11 nines), high availability (99.99%), and no retrieval fees. It is the most expensive per GB but has no hidden retrieval costs, making it ideal for active data accessed multiple times per month.
S3 Standard-IA
Standard-IA (Infrequent Access) stores data in 3+ AZs (same durability as Standard) but at a lower storage cost. There IS a retrieval fee per GB, making it cost-effective only for data accessed less than once a month — like backups and DR copies.
S3 One Zone-IA
One Zone-IA is like Standard-IA but stores data in only ONE Availability Zone — reducing cost by ~20% more but introducing the risk of data loss if that AZ fails. Only use for data you can regenerate (thumbnails, transcoded files) or that already has a copy elsewhere.
S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval
Glacier Instant Retrieval offers archival pricing (much cheaper storage) but with the access pattern of IA — millisecond retrieval. Use for archive data accessed quarterly (e.g., medical images, news media archives) where instant access is occasionally needed.
S3 Glacier Deep Archive
Deep Archive is the CHEAPEST storage in all of AWS — designed for data retained for 7-10 years to meet regulatory requirements. Retrieval takes 12-48 hours. Think: compliance archives, long-term backups, tape replacement.
S3 Standard
Storage Cost (relative): Highest | Retrieval Time: Milliseconds — instant | Availability Zones: 3+ AZs | Retrieval Fee: NONE | Minimum Storage Duration: None | Best For: Websites, ML training data, frequently accessed files
S3 Standard-IA
Storage Cost (relative): Lower (~50% less than Standard) | Retrieval Time: Milliseconds — instant | Availability Zones: 3+ AZs | Retrieval Fee: Yes — per GB retrieved | Minimum Storage Duration: 30 days | Best For: Backups, DR copies, data accessed monthly
S3 One Zone-IA
Storage Cost (relative): Lower (~20% less than Standard-IA) | Retrieval Time: Milliseconds — instant | Availability Zones: 1 AZ ONLY | Retrieval Fee: Yes — per GB retrieved | Minimum Storage Duration: 30 days | Best For: Easily reproducible backups, secondary redundant data
S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval
Storage Cost (relative): Much Lower (~68% less than Standard) | Retrieval Time: Milliseconds — instant | Availability Zones: 3+ AZs | Retrieval Fee: Yes — higher retrieval fee | Minimum Storage Duration: 90 days | Best For: Medical image archives, media archives — quarterly access
S3 Glacier Deep Archive
Storage Cost (relative): LOWEST — up to 95% less than Standard | Retrieval Time: 12-48 hours | Availability Zones: 3+ AZs | Retrieval Fee: Yes — but retrieval is very infrequent | Minimum Storage Duration: 180 days | Best For: 7-10 year regulatory archives, compliance backups
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#87 S3 Storage Tiering Choices
Storage saa_d4_s3_cost_tiering 5 cols 6 rows
▼
Feature S3 Standard S3 Standard-IA S3 One Zone-IA S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval S3 Glacier Deep Archive
Access pattern Frequent Infrequent Infrequent, lower-cost secondary Archive Deep archive
Retrieval expectation Immediate and common Immediate but rare Immediate but rare Occasional archive retrieval Very rare retrieval
Best exam clue Hot objects Rarely accessed but quickly needed Re-creatable infrequent data Archive with periodic retrieval needs Compliance or long-retention cold storage
Cost tendency Highest storage cost of this set Lower than hot tier Lower with more tradeoff Low for archive Lowest for very cold data
Typical lifecycle role Start here for active objects Warm-to-cold transition Secondary cost-optimized transition Cold archive stage Final deepest archive
Common trap Keeping cold data here forever Using it for constantly accessed data Using it for irreplaceable primary data without thinking hard Expecting hot-storage access pattern Choosing it when retrieval urgency is nontrivial
⚠️ Exam Tips

Intelligent-Tiering is the best default when access patterns are unknown or change over time

Lifecycle rules are better than Intelligent-Tiering when access patterns are well-understood

Glacier Instant Retrieval replaced the old 'Reduced Redundancy' use case for infrequent millisecond access

Deep Archive is the cheapest storage option on AWS at ~$1/TB/month

Minimum storage duration charges apply: Standard-IA (30 days), Glacier (90 days), Deep Archive (180 days)

S3 Standard
S3 Standard is the hot tier for frequently accessed objects where low-latency retrieval matters. It is the default when access is regular and you do not want retrieval friction.
S3 Standard-IA
S3 Standard-IA is for infrequently accessed data that still needs rapid retrieval when requested. It is the exam middle ground between hot storage and archive.
S3 One Zone-IA
S3 One Zone-IA is like infrequent access with a lower resilience pattern than multi-AZ class choices. It fits reproducible or secondary data where lower cost outweighs stronger placement resilience.
S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval
Glacier Flexible Retrieval is archival storage when retrieval is needed sometimes but not instantly like hot storage. It is the common archive answer when Deep Archive is too cold.
S3 Glacier Deep Archive
Glacier Deep Archive is the coldest low-cost archival option for data that is very rarely retrieved. It is the 'cheapest but slowest practical archive' exam clue.
S3 Standard
Access pattern: Frequent | Retrieval expectation: Immediate and common | Best exam clue: Hot objects | Cost tendency: Highest storage cost of this set | Typical lifecycle role: Start here for active objects | Common trap: Keeping cold data here forever
S3 Standard-IA
Access pattern: Infrequent | Retrieval expectation: Immediate but rare | Best exam clue: Rarely accessed but quickly needed | Cost tendency: Lower than hot tier | Typical lifecycle role: Warm-to-cold transition | Common trap: Using it for constantly accessed data
S3 One Zone-IA
Access pattern: Infrequent, lower-cost secondary | Retrieval expectation: Immediate but rare | Best exam clue: Re-creatable infrequent data | Cost tendency: Lower with more tradeoff | Typical lifecycle role: Secondary cost-optimized transition | Common trap: Using it for irreplaceable primary data without thinking hard
S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval
Access pattern: Archive | Retrieval expectation: Occasional archive retrieval | Best exam clue: Archive with periodic retrieval needs | Cost tendency: Low for archive | Typical lifecycle role: Cold archive stage | Common trap: Expecting hot-storage access pattern
S3 Glacier Deep Archive
Access pattern: Deep archive | Retrieval expectation: Very rare retrieval | Best exam clue: Compliance or long-retention cold storage | Cost tendency: Lowest for very cold data | Typical lifecycle role: Final deepest archive | Common trap: Choosing it when retrieval urgency is nontrivial
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 4 expects you to align storage class with access frequency, retrieval urgency, and durability needs. This table keeps hot, infrequent, and archival choices cleanly separated.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

#88 Storage Services: Object vs Block vs File
Storage saa_d3_storage_types 5 cols 18 rows
▼

Comparison of S3, EBS, EFS, and FSx storage types and their use cases

Understanding the fundamental differences between AWS storage types is essential for the SAA-C03 exam. This comparison covers the three primary storage paradigms: Object (S3), Block (EBS/Instance Store), and File (EFS/FSx). Each serves different use cases, access patterns, and architectural requirements. Questions often test your ability to select the appropriate storage type based on data structure, access method, and integration needs.

Feature Amazon S3 Amazon EBS Amazon EFS Amazon FSx Instance Store (Block)
Max Storage Size Object storage - files stored with metadata and unique ID Block storage - raw storage volumes with file systems File storage (NFS) - shared file system accessed via network File storage - multiple file system options (Windows, Lustre, etc.) Up to ~60 TiB (instance dependent)
Typical attachment model Access via API Attach to EC2 Mount shared filesystem Mount specialized managed filesystem Physically attached — directly connected to the host server; cannot be detached or reattached to other instances
Best exam clue Static content, backups, lakes, archives Boot volume, database volume, low-latency attached disk Shared POSIX-like file storage Need managed Windows or specialized file system behavior 'Temporary storage', 'highest IOPS', 'buffer/cache/scratch data', 'data lost on stop/terminate'
Multi-instance sharing No (single instance, io1/io2 Multi-Attach exception) Single EC2 instance only (except io2 Block Express) Multiple EC2 instances simultaneously Multiple compute instances simultaneously No (single instance only)
Elasticity style Massively scalable objects Volume-centric Elastic shared file storage Managed capacity/performance tuned by workload Fixed — size determined by instance type; cannot be resized; must change instance type for different storage
Common mistake Treating it like a block disk Using it when many instances need shared files Using it where block storage latency pattern is needed Ignoring it when Windows file semantics are required Assuming data persists after stop/terminate — instance store is ephemeral; only survives reboots, not stops
Access Method HTTP/HTTPS REST API — GET, PUT, DELETE objects via URLs; SDKs, CLI, and console Block device — mounted as a filesystem on EC2; accessed like a local disk via OS file operations NFS v4.1 protocol — mounted as network filesystem on multiple Linux EC2 instances simultaneously SMB protocol (Windows) or Lustre protocol (HPC) — mounted as network filesystem Directly attached to EC2 instance
Geographic Scope Global — accessible from anywhere via internet; data stored in selected region with cross-region replication option Single AZ — volume exists in one Availability Zone; must snapshot + restore to move to another AZ Regional — accessible across all AZs in a region; cross-region replication available Regional — single AZ or multi-AZ deployment depending on filesystem type Single AZ only
Durability 99.999999999% (11 nines) — data automatically replicated across minimum 3 AZs 99.999% — replicated within a single AZ; use snapshots to S3 for cross-AZ protection 99.999999999% (11 nines) — data replicated across multiple AZs within a region High — varies by type; FSx for Windows has automatic backups; Lustre can be linked to S3 for durability Ephemeral (data lost on stop/terminate)
Latency Milliseconds (first-byte) — typically 100-200ms for Standard; single-digit ms for S3 Express One Zone Sub-millisecond — io2 Block Express offers <1ms; gp3 typically 1-4ms Low single-digit milliseconds — slightly higher than EBS due to network protocol overhead Sub-millisecond — FSx for Lustre optimized for HPC; FSx for Windows ~1-2ms Microseconds (lowest)
Throughput Very high — 5,500 GET and 3,500 PUT requests per second per prefix; scales with prefixes Up to 4,000 MiB/s (io2 Block Express) or 1,000 MiB/s (gp3); depends on volume type Bursting or provisioned — up to 10+ GiB/s in Elastic throughput mode; scales with filesystem size Very high — Lustre delivers 100s of GiB/s for HPC; Windows up to 2 GiB/s Up to 10+ GB/s (highest)
Pricing Model Per GB stored per month + per request + data transfer out; varies by storage class Per GB provisioned per month + IOPS provisioned (io2); gp3 has free baseline IOPS Per GB stored per month (pay for what you use); Standard ~$0.30/GB, IA ~$0.025/GB Per GB provisioned per month + throughput capacity; varies by filesystem type Included in EC2 instance cost
Data Structure Flat object store — key-value pairs with metadata; no hierarchy (prefixes simulate folders) Block storage — raw blocks formatted with filesystem (ext4, NTFS); OS manages file structure Hierarchical filesystem — POSIX-compliant directory structure with files and folders Hierarchical filesystem — Windows NTFS (FSx for Windows) or Lustre parallel filesystem Raw blocks (requires file system)
Backup/Snapshot Versioning + Cross-Region Replication; S3 Object Lock for immutability; AWS Backup integration EBS Snapshots to S3; incremental snapshots; automated via Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager or AWS Backup AWS Backup for scheduled backups; EFS-to-EFS replication for cross-region protection Automatic daily backups; manual backups; AWS Backup integration for all FSx types Must backup to persistent storage
Best Use Cases Data lakes, static website hosting, backup/archive, big data analytics, ML training data Boot volumes, databases (RDS on EBS), transactional workloads needing low-latency block storage Shared file storage for Linux — content management, web serving, container storage, ML shared data Windows file shares (FSx for Windows), HPC/ML workloads (Lustre), NetApp migrations (FSx for ONTAP) Temporary cache, buffers, high-IOPS scratch space
Scaling Virtually unlimited (PETABYTES+ supported) Single volume up to 64 TiB (io2 Block Express) Elastic - grows and shrinks automatically (PETABYTES) Fixed capacity with manual scaling (100s of TiB) Not scalable — fixed to instance type; must launch new instance type for different storage capacity
Persistence Data persists until explicitly deleted Data persists independently of EC2 instance lifecycle Data persists; independent of EC2 lifecycle Data persists; independent of compute lifecycle Ephemeral — data survives reboots but lost on stop, terminate, or hardware failure
Typical Exam Scenario Static website hosting, S3 Glacier archiving, data lake with Athena Root volume for EC2, RDS storage, high-performance databases Shared file storage for containers, ML training data sharing High-performance ML training (FSx for Lustre), Windows file shares 'High-performance temporary storage for cache/buffer', 'application needs highest possible IOPS with no persistence requirement'
💡 Mnemonics

S.E.E.F - S3 is Simple (object), EBS is Essential (block), EFS is Excellent (file sharing), FSx is Fabulous (specialized file systems)

⚠️ Exam Tips

S3 is NOT a file system - you cannot mount it as a drive; use EFS or FSx for that

EBS volumes are AZ-specific; use Snapshot + another AZ or use EFS/FSx for multi-AZ access

EFS is more expensive than S3 but provides lower latency for shared file access

FSx for Lustre integrates with S3 and provides high-throughput for ML training workloads

Storage Gateway is for hybrid scenarios connecting on-premises to S3-backed storage

Amazon S3
Amazon S3 is scalable object storage and is the default answer for durable object storage, static assets, backups, and data lakes. It is not mounted as block storage for a single instance like EBS.
Amazon EBS
Amazon EBS is block storage for EC2 and is a strong fit for boot volumes and low-latency attached storage. Think single-instance attachment pattern first, not shared file system.
Amazon EFS
Amazon EFS is a managed elastic file system that can be shared across many Linux-based compute resources. It fits when many instances or containers need shared file storage.
Amazon FSx
Amazon FSx is a family of managed file systems optimized for specific workloads such as Windows or high-performance file use cases. In exam questions, it appears when generic EFS is not the best semantic fit.
Amazon S3
Max Storage Size: Object storage - files stored with metadata and unique ID | Typical attachment model: Access via API | Best exam clue: Static content, backups, lakes, archives | Multi-instance sharing: No (single instance, io1/io2 Multi-Attach exception) | Elasticity style: Massively scalable objects | Common mistake: Treating it like a block disk | Access Method: HTTP/HTTPS REST API — GET, PUT, DELETE objects via URLs; SDKs, CLI, and console | Geographic Scope: Global — accessible from anywhere via internet; data stored in selected region with cross-region replication option | Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) — data automatically replicated across minimum 3 AZs | Latency: Milliseconds (first-byte) — typically 100-200ms for Standard; single-digit ms for S3 Express One Zone | Throughput: Very high — 5,500 GET and 3,500 PUT requests per second per prefix; scales with prefixes | Pricing Model: Per GB stored per month + per request + data transfer out; varies by storage class | Data Structure: Flat object store — key-value pairs with metadata; no hierarchy (prefixes simulate folders) | Backup/Snapshot: Versioning + Cross-Region Replication; S3 Object Lock for immutability; AWS Backup integration | Best Use Cases: Data lakes, static website hosting, backup/archive, big data analytics, ML training data | Scaling: Virtually unlimited (PETABYTES+ supported) | Persistence: Data persists until explicitly deleted | Typical Exam Scenario: Static website hosting, S3 Glacier archiving, data lake with Athena
Amazon EBS
Max Storage Size: Block storage - raw storage volumes with file systems | Typical attachment model: Attach to EC2 | Best exam clue: Boot volume, database volume, low-latency attached disk | Multi-instance sharing: Single EC2 instance only (except io2 Block Express) | Elasticity style: Volume-centric | Common mistake: Using it when many instances need shared files | Access Method: Block device — mounted as a filesystem on EC2; accessed like a local disk via OS file operations | Geographic Scope: Single AZ — volume exists in one Availability Zone; must snapshot + restore to move to another AZ | Durability: 99.999% — replicated within a single AZ; use snapshots to S3 for cross-AZ protection | Latency: Sub-millisecond — io2 Block Express offers <1ms; gp3 typically 1-4ms | Throughput: Up to 4,000 MiB/s (io2 Block Express) or 1,000 MiB/s (gp3); depends on volume type | Pricing Model: Per GB provisioned per month + IOPS provisioned (io2); gp3 has free baseline IOPS | Data Structure: Block storage — raw blocks formatted with filesystem (ext4, NTFS); OS manages file structure | Backup/Snapshot: EBS Snapshots to S3; incremental snapshots; automated via Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager or AWS Backup | Best Use Cases: Boot volumes, databases (RDS on EBS), transactional workloads needing low-latency block storage | Scaling: Single volume up to 64 TiB (io2 Block Express) | Persistence: Data persists independently of EC2 instance lifecycle | Typical Exam Scenario: Root volume for EC2, RDS storage, high-performance databases
Amazon EFS
Max Storage Size: File storage (NFS) - shared file system accessed via network | Typical attachment model: Mount shared filesystem | Best exam clue: Shared POSIX-like file storage | Multi-instance sharing: Multiple EC2 instances simultaneously | Elasticity style: Elastic shared file storage | Common mistake: Using it where block storage latency pattern is needed | Access Method: NFS v4.1 protocol — mounted as network filesystem on multiple Linux EC2 instances simultaneously | Geographic Scope: Regional — accessible across all AZs in a region; cross-region replication available | Durability: 99.999999999% (11 nines) — data replicated across multiple AZs within a region | Latency: Low single-digit milliseconds — slightly higher than EBS due to network protocol overhead | Throughput: Bursting or provisioned — up to 10+ GiB/s in Elastic throughput mode; scales with filesystem size | Pricing Model: Per GB stored per month (pay for what you use); Standard ~$0.30/GB, IA ~$0.025/GB | Data Structure: Hierarchical filesystem — POSIX-compliant directory structure with files and folders | Backup/Snapshot: AWS Backup for scheduled backups; EFS-to-EFS replication for cross-region protection | Best Use Cases: Shared file storage for Linux — content management, web serving, container storage, ML shared data | Scaling: Elastic - grows and shrinks automatically (PETABYTES) | Persistence: Data persists; independent of EC2 lifecycle | Typical Exam Scenario: Shared file storage for containers, ML training data sharing
Amazon FSx
Max Storage Size: File storage - multiple file system options (Windows, Lustre, etc.) | Typical attachment model: Mount specialized managed filesystem | Best exam clue: Need managed Windows or specialized file system behavior | Multi-instance sharing: Multiple compute instances simultaneously | Elasticity style: Managed capacity/performance tuned by workload | Common mistake: Ignoring it when Windows file semantics are required | Access Method: SMB protocol (Windows) or Lustre protocol (HPC) — mounted as network filesystem | Geographic Scope: Regional — single AZ or multi-AZ deployment depending on filesystem type | Durability: High — varies by type; FSx for Windows has automatic backups; Lustre can be linked to S3 for durability | Latency: Sub-millisecond — FSx for Lustre optimized for HPC; FSx for Windows ~1-2ms | Throughput: Very high — Lustre delivers 100s of GiB/s for HPC; Windows up to 2 GiB/s | Pricing Model: Per GB provisioned per month + throughput capacity; varies by filesystem type | Data Structure: Hierarchical filesystem — Windows NTFS (FSx for Windows) or Lustre parallel filesystem | Backup/Snapshot: Automatic daily backups; manual backups; AWS Backup integration for all FSx types | Best Use Cases: Windows file shares (FSx for Windows), HPC/ML workloads (Lustre), NetApp migrations (FSx for ONTAP) | Scaling: Fixed capacity with manual scaling (100s of TiB) | Persistence: Data persists; independent of compute lifecycle | Typical Exam Scenario: High-performance ML training (FSx for Lustre), Windows file shares
Instance Store (Block)
Max Storage Size: Up to ~60 TiB (instance dependent) | Typical attachment model: Physically attached — directly connected to the host server; cannot be detached or reattached to other instances | Best exam clue: 'Temporary storage', 'highest IOPS', 'buffer/cache/scratch data', 'data lost on stop/terminate' | Multi-instance sharing: No (single instance only) | Elasticity style: Fixed — size determined by instance type; cannot be resized; must change instance type for different storage | Common mistake: Assuming data persists after stop/terminate — instance store is ephemeral; only survives reboots, not stops | Access Method: Directly attached to EC2 instance | Geographic Scope: Single AZ only | Durability: Ephemeral (data lost on stop/terminate) | Latency: Microseconds (lowest) | Throughput: Up to 10+ GB/s (highest) | Pricing Model: Included in EC2 instance cost | Data Structure: Raw blocks (requires file system) | Backup/Snapshot: Must backup to persistent storage | Best Use Cases: Temporary cache, buffers, high-IOPS scratch space | Scaling: Not scalable — fixed to instance type; must launch new instance type for different storage capacity | Persistence: Ephemeral — data survives reboots but lost on stop, terminate, or hardware failure | Typical Exam Scenario: 'High-performance temporary storage for cache/buffer', 'application needs highest possible IOPS with no persistence requirement'
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 3 and Domain 4 expect strong intuition about object, block, and file storage. This table maps the classic exam question: which storage service matches the access pattern.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

Databases 10 tables
#89 AWS Caching Services Comparison
Databases cache_comparison 4 cols 8 rows
▼

This table compares AWS caching services designed to improve application performance by storing frequently accessed data closer to compute resources. ElastiCache provides managed Redis and Memcached, DAX offers in-memory acceleration for DynamoDB, and CloudFront provides global edge caching.

Feature ElastiCache for Redis ElastiCache for Memcached DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) Amazon CloudFront
Cache Type In-memory data structure store In-memory object cache Purpose-built DynamoDB cache CDN / Edge cache
Data Persistence Optional (RDB, AOF) No (pure cache) No (write-through cache) Configurable TTL
Latency Sub-millisecond Sub-millisecond Microsecond Edge locations (10s of ms)
Primary Use Case Caching, sessions, pub/sub, leaderboards Simple object caching DynamoDB read acceleration Static/dynamic content delivery
Data Structure Support Strings, lists, sets, hashes, streams, etc. Key-value only DynamoDB items HTTP objects
High Availability Cluster mode, Multi-AZ, replicas No native HA (manual sharding) Multi-AZ clusters Global edge network
Integration Any application (Redis protocol) Any application (Memcached protocol) DynamoDB API compatible S3, ELB, EC2, custom origins
Scaling Vertical and horizontal (sharding) Horizontal (add nodes) Add nodes to cluster Automatic (global)
⚠️ Exam Tips

ElastiCache Redis = multi-AZ, persistence, complex data types; Memcached = simple, multi-threaded

DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) is ONLY for DynamoDB — provides microsecond response times

CloudFront caches content at edge locations — it’s a CDN, not an in-memory database cache

MemoryDB for Redis provides durability — use it as a primary database, not just a cache

For the exam: 'DynamoDB performance' = DAX; 'session store' = ElastiCache Redis; 'static content' = CloudFront

ElastiCache for Redis
Fully managed in-memory data store compatible with Redis that provides sub-millisecond latency for caching, session management, real-time analytics, and message brokering.
ElastiCache for Memcached
Fully managed implementation of Memcached providing a simple, high-performance caching layer for use cases requiring object caching with no persistence requirements.
DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX)
Fully managed, in-memory cache purpose-built for DynamoDB that delivers microsecond latency for eventually consistent reads with full API compatibility.
Amazon CloudFront
Global content delivery network service that securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs to customers globally with low latency and high transfer speeds through edge locations.
ElastiCache for Redis
Cache Type: In-memory data structure store | Data Persistence: Optional (RDB, AOF) | Latency: Sub-millisecond | Primary Use Case: Caching, sessions, pub/sub, leaderboards | Data Structure Support: Strings, lists, sets, hashes, streams, etc. | High Availability: Cluster mode, Multi-AZ, replicas | Integration: Any application (Redis protocol) | Scaling: Vertical and horizontal (sharding)
ElastiCache for Memcached
Cache Type: In-memory object cache | Data Persistence: No (pure cache) | Latency: Sub-millisecond | Primary Use Case: Simple object caching | Data Structure Support: Key-value only | High Availability: No native HA (manual sharding) | Integration: Any application (Memcached protocol) | Scaling: Horizontal (add nodes)
DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX)
Cache Type: Purpose-built DynamoDB cache | Data Persistence: No (write-through cache) | Latency: Microsecond | Primary Use Case: DynamoDB read acceleration | Data Structure Support: DynamoDB items | High Availability: Multi-AZ clusters | Integration: DynamoDB API compatible | Scaling: Add nodes to cluster
Amazon CloudFront
Cache Type: CDN / Edge cache | Data Persistence: Configurable TTL | Latency: Edge locations (10s of ms) | Primary Use Case: Static/dynamic content delivery | Data Structure Support: HTTP objects | High Availability: Global edge network | Integration: S3, ELB, EC2, custom origins | Scaling: Automatic (global)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#90 Caching and In-Memory Database Services
Databases caching_and_inmemory_database_services 3 cols 9 rows
▼

Comparison of ElastiCache, MemoryDB, and DynamoDB DAX for caching and performance optimization

Feature Amazon ElastiCache Amazon MemoryDB DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX)
Primary Purpose Caching layer - stores session data, query results, computed values Primary database - ultra-fast reads with durability guarantees In-memory caching layer specifically for DynamoDB — microsecond read latency for eventually consistent reads
Data Persistence Optional (RDB snapshots, AOF); data loss possible Yes - multi-AZ transaction log with durable storage Cache only — DAX is a read-through/write-through cache; DynamoDB is the persistent store
Data Operations Strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams (Redis); simple key-value (Memcached) Redis-compatible data structures GetItem, BatchGetItem, Query, Scan — transparently caches DynamoDB reads; writes pass through to DynamoDB
Integration Works with any database; commonly used with RDS, Aurora Can replace Redis + traditional DB for ultra-fast workloads DynamoDB only — drop-in replacement using DAX SDK; same API as DynamoDB client
Use Case Session management, leaderboards, real-time analytics, pub/sub Real-time bidding, gaming, caching with persistence requirements Read-heavy DynamoDB workloads needing microsecond latency — gaming leaderboards, session stores, ad-tech bidding
Consistency Eventually consistent by default; strong consistency optional Strong consistency with eventual secondary reads Eventually consistent reads from cache; strongly consistent reads bypass cache and go to DynamoDB
Cluster Support Redis Cluster (sharding) and Redis Replication (HA) Multi-AZ with automatic failover, up to 15 read replicas Yes — multi-node cluster with primary + read replicas; automatic failover within cluster
Type In-memory cache (Redis/Memcached) In-memory database with durability Managed in-memory cache — purpose-built for DynamoDB; not general-purpose like ElastiCache
Protocol Redis or Memcached protocol Redis protocol DynamoDB-compatible API — uses DAX SDK (drop-in for DynamoDB SDK); not Memcached/Redis protocol
💡 Mnemonics

E.M.D - ElastiCache caches, MemoryDB stores in memory durably, DAX accelerates DynamoDB

EEM = ElastiCache for Caching, ElastiCache for Memory, DAX = DynamoDB Accelerator

⚠️ Exam Tips

ElastiCache is the go-to for caching layer in front of RDS/Aurora

DAX provides up to 10x performance improvement for DynamoDB reads

For microservices needing both cache and primary store, consider MemoryDB

ElastiCache Redis supports more data structures than Memcached (which is simpler)

Global data stores with ElastiCache require custom replication; MemoryDB has built-in replication

ElastiCache is for caching ANY database - not just RDS

Use ElastiCache for sessions when scaling horizontally (stateless instances)

MemoryDB is a primary database; ElastiCache is typically a cache layer

DAX is specifically for DynamoDB - don't use for other databases

ElastiCache Memcached does NOT support persistence or replication

Amazon ElastiCache
A fully managed in-memory caching service supporting Redis and Memcached, enabling real-time performance for applications requiring sub-millisecond latency responses.
Amazon MemoryDB
A Redis-compatible in-memory database service that offers ultra-fast performance with durability, designed for modern application architectures requiring microsecond latency.
DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX)
A fully managed, highly available in-memory cache for DynamoDB that enables dramatic performance improvement from milliseconds to microseconds for read-intensive workloads.
Amazon ElastiCache
Primary Purpose: Caching layer - stores session data, query results, computed values | Data Persistence: Optional (RDB snapshots, AOF); data loss possible | Data Operations: Strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams (Redis); simple key-value (Memcached) | Integration: Works with any database; commonly used with RDS, Aurora | Use Case: Session management, leaderboards, real-time analytics, pub/sub | Consistency: Eventually consistent by default; strong consistency optional | Cluster Support: Redis Cluster (sharding) and Redis Replication (HA) | Type: In-memory cache (Redis/Memcached) | Protocol: Redis or Memcached protocol
Amazon MemoryDB
Primary Purpose: Primary database - ultra-fast reads with durability guarantees | Data Persistence: Yes - multi-AZ transaction log with durable storage | Data Operations: Redis-compatible data structures | Integration: Can replace Redis + traditional DB for ultra-fast workloads | Use Case: Real-time bidding, gaming, caching with persistence requirements | Consistency: Strong consistency with eventual secondary reads | Cluster Support: Multi-AZ with automatic failover, up to 15 read replicas | Type: In-memory database with durability | Protocol: Redis protocol
DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX)
Primary Purpose: In-memory caching layer specifically for DynamoDB — microsecond read latency for eventually consistent reads | Data Persistence: Cache only — DAX is a read-through/write-through cache; DynamoDB is the persistent store | Data Operations: GetItem, BatchGetItem, Query, Scan — transparently caches DynamoDB reads; writes pass through to DynamoDB | Integration: DynamoDB only — drop-in replacement using DAX SDK; same API as DynamoDB client | Use Case: Read-heavy DynamoDB workloads needing microsecond latency — gaming leaderboards, session stores, ad-tech bidding | Consistency: Eventually consistent reads from cache; strongly consistent reads bypass cache and go to DynamoDB | Cluster Support: Yes — multi-node cluster with primary + read replicas; automatic failover within cluster | Type: Managed in-memory cache — purpose-built for DynamoDB; not general-purpose like ElastiCache | Protocol: DynamoDB-compatible API — uses DAX SDK (drop-in for DynamoDB SDK); not Memcached/Redis protocol
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#91 Database Selection Decision Matrix
Databases database_selection_decision_matrix 4 cols 6 rows
▼

Guide for selecting the appropriate AWS database service based on requirements

Feature Traditional OLTP (Relational) High-Performance OLTP (Aurora) Internet-Scale NoSQL (DynamoDB) Analytics/Data Warehouse (Redshift)
Workload Type Transactional — structured data with complex joins, ACID compliance, SQL queries High-throughput transactional — 5x MySQL / 3x PostgreSQL performance; cloud-native architecture Key-value and document — single-digit millisecond latency at any scale; flexible schema OLAP — complex analytical queries across massive datasets; columnar storage optimized for aggregations
Data Volume GBs to low TBs — single-instance or Multi-AZ; max 64 TiB per RDS instance Up to 128 TiB — auto-scales storage in 10GB increments; distributed storage across 3 AZs Unlimited — horizontal scaling; no practical storage limit per table PBs — petabyte-scale data warehouse; RA3 nodes with managed storage up to 8 PB
Query Flexibility High — full SQL support with complex joins, subqueries, stored procedures, triggers High — full MySQL/PostgreSQL SQL compatibility; parallel query for analytics on transactional data Limited — primary key + sort key queries; GSI/LSI for additional access patterns; no joins Very high — full SQL with window functions, CTEs, materialized views, Redshift Spectrum for S3 queries
Scaling Approach Vertical — scale up instance size; read replicas for read scaling; limited horizontal scaling Horizontal reads — up to 15 read replicas; Aurora Serverless v2 for auto-scaling compute; storage auto-scales Automatic horizontal — on-demand or provisioned capacity; auto-scaling partitions transparently Cluster resize — add/remove nodes; concurrency scaling for burst read queries; Serverless option available
Cost Model Per instance-hour + storage + I/O; Reserved Instances save up to 60%; Multi-AZ doubles compute cost Per ACU (Serverless) or instance-hour + I/O + storage; I/O-Optimized option for heavy workloads On-demand: per read/write unit; Provisioned: per RCU/WCU hour; storage per GB/month Per node-hour (RA3/DC2/DS2) + managed storage; Serverless per RPU-hour + storage
Serverless Option No — RDS requires provisioned instance sizes; cannot scale to zero Yes — Aurora Serverless v2 auto-scales compute in fine-grained increments (0.5 ACU steps); can scale to zero (v1 only) Yes — On-Demand mode is effectively serverless; no capacity planning; scales instantly to zero Yes — Redshift Serverless auto-provisions and scales compute based on query complexity
💡 Mnemonics

RRDRA: RDS and Aurora for traditional/enterprise, DynamoDB for scale, Redshift for analytics

⚠️ Exam Tips

Can't decide? Consider: Transactional SQL → RDS; High performance → Aurora; Scale → DynamoDB; Analytics → Redshift

DynamoDB has no schema - perfect for evolving data models

Aurora auto-shrinks storage unlike RDS - you only pay for what you use

Redshift uses columnar storage optimized for analytical queries, not OLTP

For multi-model needs, consider Amazon Neptune for graphs or DocumentDB for JSON

Traditional OLTP (Relational)
Standard transactional database workloads requiring ACID compliance, complex joins, and SQL support.
High-Performance OLTP (Aurora)
Enterprise-grade transactional workloads requiring high throughput, auto-scaling storage, and global distribution.
Internet-Scale NoSQL (DynamoDB)
Massively scalable workloads with unpredictable traffic, requiring single-digit millisecond latency at any scale.
Analytics/Data Warehouse (Redshift)
Petabyte-scale analytics workloads requiring complex queries, BI integration, and columnar storage.
Traditional OLTP (Relational)
Workload Type: Transactional — structured data with complex joins, ACID compliance, SQL queries | Data Volume: GBs to low TBs — single-instance or Multi-AZ; max 64 TiB per RDS instance | Query Flexibility: High — full SQL support with complex joins, subqueries, stored procedures, triggers | Scaling Approach: Vertical — scale up instance size; read replicas for read scaling; limited horizontal scaling | Cost Model: Per instance-hour + storage + I/O; Reserved Instances save up to 60%; Multi-AZ doubles compute cost | Serverless Option: No — RDS requires provisioned instance sizes; cannot scale to zero
High-Performance OLTP (Aurora)
Workload Type: High-throughput transactional — 5x MySQL / 3x PostgreSQL performance; cloud-native architecture | Data Volume: Up to 128 TiB — auto-scales storage in 10GB increments; distributed storage across 3 AZs | Query Flexibility: High — full MySQL/PostgreSQL SQL compatibility; parallel query for analytics on transactional data | Scaling Approach: Horizontal reads — up to 15 read replicas; Aurora Serverless v2 for auto-scaling compute; storage auto-scales | Cost Model: Per ACU (Serverless) or instance-hour + I/O + storage; I/O-Optimized option for heavy workloads | Serverless Option: Yes — Aurora Serverless v2 auto-scales compute in fine-grained increments (0.5 ACU steps); can scale to zero (v1 only)
Internet-Scale NoSQL (DynamoDB)
Workload Type: Key-value and document — single-digit millisecond latency at any scale; flexible schema | Data Volume: Unlimited — horizontal scaling; no practical storage limit per table | Query Flexibility: Limited — primary key + sort key queries; GSI/LSI for additional access patterns; no joins | Scaling Approach: Automatic horizontal — on-demand or provisioned capacity; auto-scaling partitions transparently | Cost Model: On-demand: per read/write unit; Provisioned: per RCU/WCU hour; storage per GB/month | Serverless Option: Yes — On-Demand mode is effectively serverless; no capacity planning; scales instantly to zero
Analytics/Data Warehouse (Redshift)
Workload Type: OLAP — complex analytical queries across massive datasets; columnar storage optimized for aggregations | Data Volume: PBs — petabyte-scale data warehouse; RA3 nodes with managed storage up to 8 PB | Query Flexibility: Very high — full SQL with window functions, CTEs, materialized views, Redshift Spectrum for S3 queries | Scaling Approach: Cluster resize — add/remove nodes; concurrency scaling for burst read queries; Serverless option available | Cost Model: Per node-hour (RA3/DC2/DS2) + managed storage; Serverless per RPU-hour + storage | Serverless Option: Yes — Redshift Serverless auto-provisions and scales compute based on query complexity
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#92 Database Service Comparison
Databases saa_d3_database_choices 3 cols 14 rows
▼

This table compares the primary database services on AWS. RDS provides managed relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, Oracle), Aurora is AWS's cloud-native relational database with enhanced performance, DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL database for applications requiring single-digit millisecond latency at any scale, DocumentDB offers MongoDB compatibility, and Neptune is a fully managed graph database.

Feature Amazon RDS Amazon Aurora Amazon DynamoDB
Pricing Model Instance-based + storage + I/O Instance-based + I/O operations On-demand (pay per request) or provisioned
Best exam clue Managed SQL for traditional apps Higher-performance managed relational needs Massive scale, low-latency NoSQL
Scaling style Vertical (scale up instance), Read Replicas Auto-scaling storage (up to 128TB), up to 15 Read Replicas Serverless, virtually unlimited, on-demand or provisioned
Query style SQL with joins and transactions SQL with joins and transactions Access-pattern-driven NoSQL queries
When it shines Conventional app migrations and SQL workloads High-throughput relational workloads Burst-heavy or internet-scale simple access workloads
Common mistake Choosing it when extreme NoSQL scale is needed Treating it as identical to any generic RDS engine Using it when relational joins are central
Database Type NoSQL (Document - MongoDB compatible) Relational (Cloud-Native) NoSQL (Key-Value/Document)
Engines Supported MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MariaDB MySQL-compatible, PostgreSQL-compatible Proprietary (AWS-managed)
Performance High performance for document queries 5x faster than MySQL, 3x faster than PostgreSQL Single-digit millisecond latency
Read Replicas Up to 15 Up to 15 Global Tables (multi-region)
Multi-AZ Support Yes (synchronous standby) Yes (6 copies across 3 AZs) Yes (Global Tables)
Serverless Option No (Aurora Serverless is separate) Aurora Serverless v2 Yes (on-demand mode)
Best Use Case Social networks, fraud detection, recommendation engines High-performance relational workloads, enterprise apps Serverless apps, high throughput, unpredictable traffic
Availability Multi-AZ deployment for HA 6-way replication across 3 AZs, self-healing Global Tables (multi-region, active-active)
💡 Mnemonics

RDS = Reliable Data SQL, Aurora = Accelerated Relational, DynamoDB = Dynamic NoSQL, Redshift = Reporting/Analytics

Need JOINs and SQL? RDS or Aurora. Need massive scale and simple queries? DynamoDB. Need analytics? Redshift. Need performance? Aurora.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Aurora offers the best performance for relational databases - 5x MySQL

DynamoDB is the only truly serverless database with unlimited scale

RDS Multi-AZ provides HA; Read Replicas improve read performance

Aurora replicates 6 copies across 3 AZs automatically

Global Tables make DynamoDB multi-region with active-active replication

Redshift is for OLAP; RDS/Aurora/DynamoDB for OLTP

Amazon RDS
A managed relational database service supporting multiple database engines (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MariaDB). Automates administrative tasks like backups, patching, and replication, with Multi-AZ for high availability and read replicas for scaling read workloads.
Amazon Aurora
A cloud-native relational database engine offering MySQL and PostgreSQL compatibility with superior performance (5x MySQL, 3x PostgreSQL). Features automatic six-copy replication across three AZs, up to 15 read replicas, and storage auto-scaling to 128TB.
Amazon DynamoDB
A fully managed NoSQL database delivering single-digit millisecond latency at any scale. Offers serverless capacity mode, global tables for multi-region replication, and DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) for microsecond latency caching.
Amazon RDS
Pricing Model: Instance-based + storage + I/O | Best exam clue: Managed SQL for traditional apps | Scaling style: Vertical (scale up instance), Read Replicas | Query style: SQL with joins and transactions | When it shines: Conventional app migrations and SQL workloads | Common mistake: Choosing it when extreme NoSQL scale is needed | Database Type: NoSQL (Document - MongoDB compatible) | Engines Supported: MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MariaDB | Performance: High performance for document queries | Read Replicas: Up to 15 | Multi-AZ Support: Yes (synchronous standby) | Serverless Option: No (Aurora Serverless is separate) | Best Use Case: Social networks, fraud detection, recommendation engines | Availability: Multi-AZ deployment for HA
Amazon Aurora
Pricing Model: Instance-based + I/O operations | Best exam clue: Higher-performance managed relational needs | Scaling style: Auto-scaling storage (up to 128TB), up to 15 Read Replicas | Query style: SQL with joins and transactions | When it shines: High-throughput relational workloads | Common mistake: Treating it as identical to any generic RDS engine | Database Type: Relational (Cloud-Native) | Engines Supported: MySQL-compatible, PostgreSQL-compatible | Performance: 5x faster than MySQL, 3x faster than PostgreSQL | Read Replicas: Up to 15 | Multi-AZ Support: Yes (6 copies across 3 AZs) | Serverless Option: Aurora Serverless v2 | Best Use Case: High-performance relational workloads, enterprise apps | Availability: 6-way replication across 3 AZs, self-healing
Amazon DynamoDB
Pricing Model: On-demand (pay per request) or provisioned | Best exam clue: Massive scale, low-latency NoSQL | Scaling style: Serverless, virtually unlimited, on-demand or provisioned | Query style: Access-pattern-driven NoSQL queries | When it shines: Burst-heavy or internet-scale simple access workloads | Common mistake: Using it when relational joins are central | Database Type: NoSQL (Key-Value/Document) | Engines Supported: Proprietary (AWS-managed) | Performance: Single-digit millisecond latency | Read Replicas: Global Tables (multi-region) | Multi-AZ Support: Yes (Global Tables) | Serverless Option: Yes (on-demand mode) | Best Use Case: Serverless apps, high throughput, unpredictable traffic | Availability: Global Tables (multi-region, active-active)
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 3 and 4 repeatedly test when to choose relational, cloud-native relational, or NoSQL. This table mirrors one of the most common exam comparison patterns.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#93 In-Memory and Cache Comparison
Databases inmemory_and_cache_comparison 3 cols 11 rows
▼

AWS offers multiple in-memory data store solutions for caching and real-time data processing. ElastiCache provides managed Redis and Memcached for caching use cases, while MemoryDB for Redis offers a durable, Redis-compatible database for primary data storage. Understanding their persistence models, data structures, and use cases is critical for exam success.

Feature ElastiCache Redis ElastiCache Memcached MemoryDB for Redis
Service Type Managed in-memory cache/data store Managed in-memory cache only Managed in-memory database (durable)
Data Persistence Optional (RDB snapshots, AOF) None (pure cache) Yes (durability is primary feature)
Multi-AZ Support Yes (cluster mode disabled) No Yes (with automatic failover)
Data Structures Strings, Lists, Sets, Hashes, Sorted Sets, Streams, Geospatial Simple key-value only Same as Redis (full Redis compatibility)
Replication Async replication to read replicas None (client-side sharding) Multi-AZ with sync replication to replicas
Clustering Yes (cluster mode enabled) Auto-discovery supported Yes (sharding built-in)
Failover Automatic (cluster mode disabled) Manual rebuild required Automatic with Multi-AZ
Use as Primary Database Not recommended (cache use case) Never (cache only) Yes (designed for primary data)
Maximum Data Size Up to 500 GB per node (cluster mode) Up to 128 GB per node Up to 500 GB per cluster
Backup & Restore Manual/automated snapshots None (data rebuilt from source) Point-in-time recovery (up to 35 days)
Best Use Cases Caching, session store, real-time analytics, leaderboards, geospatial Simple caching, object caching, database query result caching Primary database for microservices, gaming leaderboards, session stores requiring durability
⚠️ Exam Tips

Redis supports complex data structures, persistence, and replication — it’s more than just a cache

Memcached is simpler and multi-threaded but has NO persistence or replication

MemoryDB for Redis is durable (Multi-AZ) — use when Redis must be the primary data store

Choose ElastiCache Redis for: session stores, leaderboards, real-time analytics, pub/sub

Choose Memcached only for simple caching where data can be regenerated from the source

ElastiCache Redis
Managed Redis with support for complex data structures (lists, sets, sorted sets, hashes), persistence, replication, automatic failover, and pub/sub messaging. Ideal for session stores, leaderboards, and real-time analytics.
ElastiCache Memcached
Managed Memcached for simple key-value caching with multi-threaded architecture. No persistence or replication — designed for pure caching use cases where data can be regenerated from the database.
MemoryDB for Redis
A Redis-compatible, durable in-memory database that provides microsecond read latency and single-digit millisecond write latency with Multi-AZ durability. Use as a primary database, not just a cache.
ElastiCache Redis
Service Type: Managed in-memory cache/data store | Data Persistence: Optional (RDB snapshots, AOF) | Multi-AZ Support: Yes (cluster mode disabled) | Data Structures: Strings, Lists, Sets, Hashes, Sorted Sets, Streams, Geospatial | Replication: Async replication to read replicas | Clustering: Yes (cluster mode enabled) | Failover: Automatic (cluster mode disabled) | Use as Primary Database: Not recommended (cache use case) | Maximum Data Size: Up to 500 GB per node (cluster mode) | Backup & Restore: Manual/automated snapshots | Best Use Cases: Caching, session store, real-time analytics, leaderboards, geospatial
ElastiCache Memcached
Service Type: Managed in-memory cache only | Data Persistence: None (pure cache) | Multi-AZ Support: No | Data Structures: Simple key-value only | Replication: None (client-side sharding) | Clustering: Auto-discovery supported | Failover: Manual rebuild required | Use as Primary Database: Never (cache only) | Maximum Data Size: Up to 128 GB per node | Backup & Restore: None (data rebuilt from source) | Best Use Cases: Simple caching, object caching, database query result caching
MemoryDB for Redis
Service Type: Managed in-memory database (durable) | Data Persistence: Yes (durability is primary feature) | Multi-AZ Support: Yes (with automatic failover) | Data Structures: Same as Redis (full Redis compatibility) | Replication: Multi-AZ with sync replication to replicas | Clustering: Yes (sharding built-in) | Failover: Automatic with Multi-AZ | Use as Primary Database: Yes (designed for primary data) | Maximum Data Size: Up to 500 GB per cluster | Backup & Restore: Point-in-time recovery (up to 35 days) | Best Use Cases: Primary database for microservices, gaming leaderboards, session stores requiring durability
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#94 Multi-AZ vs Read Replicas vs Multi-Region
Databases SAA-007 3 cols 6 rows
▼

Database resilience in AWS comes in three flavors that serve different purposes: Multi-AZ for HA (high availability) and failover, Read Replicas for performance scaling, and Multi-Region for DR and global low-latency access. The exam frequently confuses candidates by presenting scenarios where these overlap — know the PRIMARY PURPOSE of each.

Feature Multi-AZ Deployment Read Replicas Multi-Region
Primary Purpose HIGH AVAILABILITY — automatic failover on primary failure PERFORMANCE — offload read traffic from primary DR + GLOBAL PERFORMANCE — regional resilience and low-latency reads
Replication Type SYNCHRONOUS — zero data loss, always consistent ASYNCHRONOUS — slight replication lag possible ASYNCHRONOUS (Aurora Global: < 1 second)
Can Serve Reads? NO — standby is passive, no read traffic YES — primary purpose is serving reads YES — each region can serve local reads
Automatic Failover? YES — automatic in ~1-2 minutes NO — manual promotion required Varies — Aurora Global has automated failover
Max Replicas (RDS) 1 standby (or 2 with Multi-AZ Cluster) Up to 5 (RDS), Up to 15 (Aurora) Up to 5 secondary regions (Aurora Global)
Cost ~2x the primary cost (standby instance running 24/7) Additional instance cost per replica Highest — cross-region data transfer + multiple instances
⚠️ Exam Tips

Multi-AZ = high availability (automatic failover within a region, synchronous replication)

Read Replicas = read scalability (asynchronous replication, can serve read traffic)

Multi-Region = disaster recovery + global performance (cross-region replication)

Multi-AZ failover is automatic; promoting a Read Replica to standalone requires manual action

Aurora supports both Multi-AZ and up to 15 Read Replicas with automatic failover

Multi-AZ Deployment
Multi-AZ creates a SYNCHRONOUS standby replica in a DIFFERENT Availability Zone within the same region. If the primary fails, AWS automatically failovers to the standby in 1-2 minutes with no data loss. It is purely for high availability and disaster recovery — the standby cannot serve read traffic.
Read Replicas
Read Replicas are ASYNCHRONOUS copies of your database that can serve SELECT queries, relieving read pressure from the primary. They can be in the same AZ, different AZ, or even different regions. They do NOT provide failover — if the primary fails, you must manually promote a read replica.
Multi-Region
Multi-Region deployments replicate your database across AWS regions for global low-latency access and disaster recovery at the regional level. Aurora Global Database and DynamoDB Global Tables are the key AWS services for this pattern.
Multi-AZ Deployment
Primary Purpose: HIGH AVAILABILITY — automatic failover on primary failure | Replication Type: SYNCHRONOUS — zero data loss, always consistent | Can Serve Reads?: NO — standby is passive, no read traffic | Automatic Failover?: YES — automatic in ~1-2 minutes | Max Replicas (RDS): 1 standby (or 2 with Multi-AZ Cluster) | Cost: ~2x the primary cost (standby instance running 24/7)
Read Replicas
Primary Purpose: PERFORMANCE — offload read traffic from primary | Replication Type: ASYNCHRONOUS — slight replication lag possible | Can Serve Reads?: YES — primary purpose is serving reads | Automatic Failover?: NO — manual promotion required | Max Replicas (RDS): Up to 5 (RDS), Up to 15 (Aurora) | Cost: Additional instance cost per replica
Multi-Region
Primary Purpose: DR + GLOBAL PERFORMANCE — regional resilience and low-latency reads | Replication Type: ASYNCHRONOUS (Aurora Global: < 1 second) | Can Serve Reads?: YES — each region can serve local reads | Automatic Failover?: Varies — Aurora Global has automated failover | Max Replicas (RDS): Up to 5 secondary regions (Aurora Global) | Cost: Highest — cross-region data transfer + multiple instances
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#95 NoSQL Databases Comparison
Databases nosql_databases_comparison 6 cols 14 rows
▼

Comparing AWS managed NoSQL and document database services

AWS provides multiple NoSQL database options optimized for different data models and access patterns. DynamoDB is the flagship key-value and document store with various deployment options. DocumentDB offers MongoDB compatibility, while Keyspaces provides managed Cassandra. Understanding these services' consistency models, scaling characteristics, and pricing is essential for the exam.

Feature DynamoDB Standard DynamoDB DAX DynamoDB Global Tables DocumentDB Keyspaces (Cassandra) Amazon Neptune
Data Model Key-value and Document Key-value and Document (cached) Key-value and Document (multi-region) Document (JSON, MongoDB-compatible) Wide-column (Cassandra-compatible) Nodes, edges, and properties
Consistency Model Eventually consistent (default) or Strongly consistent Eventually consistent (cache), Strongly consistent (bypass) Eventually consistent (multi-master) Session, eventual, strong consistency Tunable consistency (ONE, QUORUM, ALL, etc.) Read replicas with eventual consistency
Read Latency Single-digit milliseconds Microseconds (10x faster) Single-digit milliseconds (local region) Milliseconds Milliseconds Single-digit milliseconds — optimized for graph traversals and relationship queries
Scaling Model On-demand or Provisioned (auto-scaling) On-demand or auto-scaling, virtually unlimited Same as Standard, multi-region Instance-based (up to 15 replicas), storage auto-scales Serverless (auto-scales), or Provisioned Read replicas up to 15
Replication Multi-AZ synchronous N/A (in-memory cache) Multi-region, multi-master (last-writer-wins) Up to 15 read replicas, async Multi-AZ by default, tunable replication factor Up to 15 read replicas across 3 AZs; storage replicated 6 ways across 3 AZs (Aurora-based)
Availability SLA 99.999% 99.9% 99.999% 99.99% 99.99% 99.99% with Multi-AZ; automatic failover to read replica in <30 seconds
Maximum Item Size 400 KB Same as DynamoDB 400 KB 16 MB 1 MB per row Not item-based — graph database stores vertices and edges; no per-item size limit like DynamoDB
Query Language DynamoDB API (GetItem, Query, Scan) Same as DynamoDB Same as DynamoDB MongoDB API (find, aggregate, etc.) CQL (Cassandra Query Language) Gremlin (property graphs) and SPARQL (RDF graphs); openCypher support added
Pricing Model On-demand (per request) or Provisioned (RCU/WCU) Node instance hours (cache.r5, cache.t3) Replicated write units + storage per region Instance hours + Storage + IOPS On-demand (per million CQL requests) or Provisioned Per instance-hour + I/O requests + storage (per GB/month); Serverless option available
Best Use Cases High-scale apps, gaming, IoT, session management Read-heavy workloads requiring microsecond latency Global apps requiring low-latency writes in multiple regions MongoDB workloads, content management, catalogs Cassandra migrations, time-series data, IoT Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs, recommendation engines, network topology, identity graphs
Database Type Key-value and document NoSQL — single-digit ms latency; fully managed; on-demand or provisioned capacity In-memory cache for DynamoDB — microsecond read latency; transparent caching layer Multi-region active-active — DynamoDB tables replicated across multiple AWS regions Document database — MongoDB-compatible; stores JSON documents; managed with automatic scaling Wide-column store — Apache Cassandra-compatible; serverless; CQL query language Graph database (Property Graph/RDF)
Primary Use High-scale transactional — session stores, gaming profiles, IoT data, e-commerce carts, serverless backends Accelerate DynamoDB reads — reduce latency from ms to microseconds for read-heavy workloads Multi-region applications — globally distributed apps needing local read/write latency in each region MongoDB-compatible workloads — content management, user profiles, catalogs needing flexible document schemas Cassandra migration to AWS — time-series, IoT, fleet management; CQL-compatible serverless Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs
Query Pattern Primary key (partition + sort key) lookups; Global/Local Secondary Indexes for alternative access patterns Same as DynamoDB — GetItem, Query, Scan cached transparently; write-through for PutItem/UpdateItem Same as DynamoDB Standard — local region queries with eventual cross-region replication MongoDB Query API — flexible querying on any field; aggregation pipeline; full-text search with indexes CQL (Cassandra Query Language) — partition key lookups; no joins or subqueries; denormalized data model Graph traversal (Gremlin, SPARQL)
ACID Transactions Yes — TransactWriteItems and TransactGetItems for multi-item, cross-table ACID transactions (up to 100 items) Not cached — transactions bypass DAX and go directly to DynamoDB; DAX caches individual reads only Per-region — transactions are local to one region; cross-region replication is eventually consistent Yes — multi-document ACID transactions with snapshot isolation; MongoDB 4.0+ transaction API Lightweight transactions (LWT) — compare-and-set operations; not full multi-row ACID like DynamoDB Yes (serializable isolation)
💡 Mnemonics

DDN = DynamoDB for key-value, DocumentDB for Mongo-style, Neptune for Networks (graphs)

⚠️ Exam Tips

DynamoDB is serverless by default with on-demand capacity mode

DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) provides in-memory caching for microsecond latency

Use DocumentDB if migrating from MongoDB - it's MongoDB-compatible
