Neptune is specifically for highly connected data - social, fraud, recommendation engines

DynamoDB Global Tables provide multi-region active-active replication

Amazon DynamoDB
A fully managed NoSQL database service providing single-digit millisecond latency at any scale, with built-in security, caching, and backup capabilities for document and key-value workloads.
Amazon DocumentDB
A fully managed document database service that is MongoDB-compatible, enabling you to store, query, and index JSON data with the scalability and reliability of AWS.
Amazon Neptune
A fully managed graph database service supporting both Property Graph and RDF standards, ideal for building applications that require highly connected data relationships.
DynamoDB Standard
Data Model: Key-value and Document | Consistency Model: Eventually consistent (default) or Strongly consistent | Read Latency: Single-digit milliseconds | Scaling Model: On-demand or Provisioned (auto-scaling) | Replication: Multi-AZ synchronous | Availability SLA: 99.999% | Maximum Item Size: 400 KB | Query Language: DynamoDB API (GetItem, Query, Scan) | Pricing Model: On-demand (per request) or Provisioned (RCU/WCU) | Best Use Cases: High-scale apps, gaming, IoT, session management | Database Type: Key-value and document NoSQL — single-digit ms latency; fully managed; on-demand or provisioned capacity | Primary Use: High-scale transactional — session stores, gaming profiles, IoT data, e-commerce carts, serverless backends | Query Pattern: Primary key (partition + sort key) lookups; Global/Local Secondary Indexes for alternative access patterns | ACID Transactions: Yes — TransactWriteItems and TransactGetItems for multi-item, cross-table ACID transactions (up to 100 items)
DynamoDB DAX
Data Model: Key-value and Document (cached) | Consistency Model: Eventually consistent (cache), Strongly consistent (bypass) | Read Latency: Microseconds (10x faster) | Scaling Model: On-demand or auto-scaling, virtually unlimited | Replication: N/A (in-memory cache) | Availability SLA: 99.9% | Maximum Item Size: Same as DynamoDB | Query Language: Same as DynamoDB | Pricing Model: Node instance hours (cache.r5, cache.t3) | Best Use Cases: Read-heavy workloads requiring microsecond latency | Database Type: In-memory cache for DynamoDB — microsecond read latency; transparent caching layer | Primary Use: Accelerate DynamoDB reads — reduce latency from ms to microseconds for read-heavy workloads | Query Pattern: Same as DynamoDB — GetItem, Query, Scan cached transparently; write-through for PutItem/UpdateItem | ACID Transactions: Not cached — transactions bypass DAX and go directly to DynamoDB; DAX caches individual reads only
DynamoDB Global Tables
Data Model: Key-value and Document (multi-region) | Consistency Model: Eventually consistent (multi-master) | Read Latency: Single-digit milliseconds (local region) | Scaling Model: Same as Standard, multi-region | Replication: Multi-region, multi-master (last-writer-wins) | Availability SLA: 99.999% | Maximum Item Size: 400 KB | Query Language: Same as DynamoDB | Pricing Model: Replicated write units + storage per region | Best Use Cases: Global apps requiring low-latency writes in multiple regions | Database Type: Multi-region active-active — DynamoDB tables replicated across multiple AWS regions | Primary Use: Multi-region applications — globally distributed apps needing local read/write latency in each region | Query Pattern: Same as DynamoDB Standard — local region queries with eventual cross-region replication | ACID Transactions: Per-region — transactions are local to one region; cross-region replication is eventually consistent
DocumentDB
Data Model: Document (JSON, MongoDB-compatible) | Consistency Model: Session, eventual, strong consistency | Read Latency: Milliseconds | Scaling Model: Instance-based (up to 15 replicas), storage auto-scales | Replication: Up to 15 read replicas, async | Availability SLA: 99.99% | Maximum Item Size: 16 MB | Query Language: MongoDB API (find, aggregate, etc.) | Pricing Model: Instance hours + Storage + IOPS | Best Use Cases: MongoDB workloads, content management, catalogs | Database Type: Document database — MongoDB-compatible; stores JSON documents; managed with automatic scaling | Primary Use: MongoDB-compatible workloads — content management, user profiles, catalogs needing flexible document schemas | Query Pattern: MongoDB Query API — flexible querying on any field; aggregation pipeline; full-text search with indexes | ACID Transactions: Yes — multi-document ACID transactions with snapshot isolation; MongoDB 4.0+ transaction API
Keyspaces (Cassandra)
Data Model: Wide-column (Cassandra-compatible) | Consistency Model: Tunable consistency (ONE, QUORUM, ALL, etc.) | Read Latency: Milliseconds | Scaling Model: Serverless (auto-scales), or Provisioned | Replication: Multi-AZ by default, tunable replication factor | Availability SLA: 99.99% | Maximum Item Size: 1 MB per row | Query Language: CQL (Cassandra Query Language) | Pricing Model: On-demand (per million CQL requests) or Provisioned | Best Use Cases: Cassandra migrations, time-series data, IoT | Database Type: Wide-column store — Apache Cassandra-compatible; serverless; CQL query language | Primary Use: Cassandra migration to AWS — time-series, IoT, fleet management; CQL-compatible serverless | Query Pattern: CQL (Cassandra Query Language) — partition key lookups; no joins or subqueries; denormalized data model | ACID Transactions: Lightweight transactions (LWT) — compare-and-set operations; not full multi-row ACID like DynamoDB
Amazon Neptune
Data Model: Nodes, edges, and properties | Consistency Model: Read replicas with eventual consistency | Read Latency: Single-digit milliseconds — optimized for graph traversals and relationship queries | Scaling Model: Read replicas up to 15 | Replication: Up to 15 read replicas across 3 AZs; storage replicated 6 ways across 3 AZs (Aurora-based) | Availability SLA: 99.99% with Multi-AZ; automatic failover to read replica in <30 seconds | Maximum Item Size: Not item-based — graph database stores vertices and edges; no per-item size limit like DynamoDB | Query Language: Gremlin (property graphs) and SPARQL (RDF graphs); openCypher support added | Pricing Model: Per instance-hour + I/O requests + storage (per GB/month); Serverless option available | Best Use Cases: Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs, recommendation engines, network topology, identity graphs | Database Type: Graph database (Property Graph/RDF) | Primary Use: Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs | Query Pattern: Graph traversal (Gremlin, SPARQL) | ACID Transactions: Yes (serializable isolation)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#96 RDS vs DynamoDB vs Aurora
Databases rds_vs_dynamodb_vs_aurora 3 cols 16 rows
▼

Comparison of managed database services for relational and NoSQL workloads

When designing database architectures on AWS, choosing between RDS (traditional relational), DynamoDB (NoSQL), and Aurora (cloud-native relational) is a fundamental decision. This comparison focuses on the high-level characteristics that drive this choice including consistency requirements, scaling patterns, query flexibility, and operational overhead.

Feature Amazon RDS Amazon DynamoDB Amazon Aurora
Database Category Traditional applications, lift-and-shift workloads, specific engine requirements Internet-scale applications, serverless architectures, unpredictable workloads Enterprise applications needing high performance and availability, microservices
Schema Flexibility Fixed schema with defined tables, columns, relationships Flexible schema - items can have different attributes Fixed schema with defined tables, columns, relationships
Query Pattern Flexible SQL queries, JOINs, complex aggregations Key-based access, limited query flexibility Flexible SQL queries, JOINs, complex aggregations
Scaling Model Vertical scaling (instance size) + Read Replicas (horizontal for reads) Horizontal scaling (virtually unlimited); On-Demand or Provisioned capacity Auto-scaling storage (up to 128 TB) + up to 15 Read Replicas
Consistency Options Strong consistency (ACID transactions) Eventually or Strongly consistent (configurable) Strong consistency (ACID transactions)
Transaction Support Full ACID support with JOINs and complex queries Single-digit ms latency; On-Demand and Standard transaction modes Full ACID support with JOINs and complex queries
Operational Overhead Low (managed patches, backups) None (fully serverless) Low (managed patches, backups)
Performance at Scale Good performance; limited by instance size and storage type Single-digit millisecond latency at any scale 5x faster than MySQL, 3x faster than PostgreSQL (standard configurations)
Cost Model Instance + Storage + IOPS On-demand (per request) or Provisioned (RCU/WCU) Instance + Storage (I/O included)
Maximum Storage 64 TB (16 TB for SQL Server) Virtually unlimited 128 TB
Read Replicas Up to 5 replicas Global Tables (multi-region), no read replicas Up to 15 replicas
Choose When... You need specific DB engine, have existing SQL apps, need complex queries You need massive scale, predictable performance, simple key-based access You need high performance, many read replicas, MySQL/PostgreSQL compatibility
High Availability Multi-AZ deployments with automatic failover Multi-AZ replication by default, automatic failover, built-in high availability 6-way replication across 3 AZs, automatic failover, self-healing storage
Serverless Option No native serverless (Aurora Serverless available) Yes - DynamoDB On-Demand and DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) Yes - Aurora Serverless v2 with auto-scaling
Global Tables Cross-region read replicas (manual setup) DynamoDB Global Tables - multi-region, multi-active Aurora Global Database - up to 5 secondary regions
Pricing Instance hours + storage + backups + I/O Pay per request unit or provisioned capacity Similar to RDS but includes storage auto-scaling
💡 Mnemonics

R.A.D - RDS for traditional, Aurora for advanced/high-performance, DynamoDB for NoSQL scale

⚠️ Exam Tips

Aurora is 5x faster than MySQL and 3x faster than PostgreSQL - memorize these numbers

Aurora can have up to 15 Read Replicas vs RDS MySQL's 5

DynamoDB is serverless-first design with virtually unlimited scalability

For SAA-C03: Choose based on data model needs (relational vs NoSQL) and scaling requirements

RDS and Aurora support Multi-AZ for HA; single-AZ is for dev/test only

Amazon RDS
A cloud-native relational database compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL, offering up to 5x the throughput of MySQL and enterprise-grade availability with auto-scaling storage.
Amazon RDS
Database Category: Traditional applications, lift-and-shift workloads, specific engine requirements | Schema Flexibility: Fixed schema with defined tables, columns, relationships | Query Pattern: Flexible SQL queries, JOINs, complex aggregations | Scaling Model: Vertical scaling (instance size) + Read Replicas (horizontal for reads) | Consistency Options: Strong consistency (ACID transactions) | Transaction Support: Full ACID support with JOINs and complex queries | Operational Overhead: Low (managed patches, backups) | Performance at Scale: Good performance; limited by instance size and storage type | Cost Model: Instance + Storage + IOPS | Maximum Storage: 64 TB (16 TB for SQL Server) | Read Replicas: Up to 5 replicas | Choose When...: You need specific DB engine, have existing SQL apps, need complex queries | High Availability: Multi-AZ deployments with automatic failover | Serverless Option: No native serverless (Aurora Serverless available) | Global Tables: Cross-region read replicas (manual setup) | Pricing: Instance hours + storage + backups + I/O
Amazon DynamoDB
Database Category: Internet-scale applications, serverless architectures, unpredictable workloads | Schema Flexibility: Flexible schema - items can have different attributes | Query Pattern: Key-based access, limited query flexibility | Scaling Model: Horizontal scaling (virtually unlimited); On-Demand or Provisioned capacity | Consistency Options: Eventually or Strongly consistent (configurable) | Transaction Support: Single-digit ms latency; On-Demand and Standard transaction modes | Operational Overhead: None (fully serverless) | Performance at Scale: Single-digit millisecond latency at any scale | Cost Model: On-demand (per request) or Provisioned (RCU/WCU) | Maximum Storage: Virtually unlimited | Read Replicas: Global Tables (multi-region), no read replicas | Choose When...: You need massive scale, predictable performance, simple key-based access | High Availability: Multi-AZ replication by default, automatic failover, built-in high availability | Serverless Option: Yes - DynamoDB On-Demand and DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) | Global Tables: DynamoDB Global Tables - multi-region, multi-active | Pricing: Pay per request unit or provisioned capacity
Amazon Aurora
Database Category: Enterprise applications needing high performance and availability, microservices | Schema Flexibility: Fixed schema with defined tables, columns, relationships | Query Pattern: Flexible SQL queries, JOINs, complex aggregations | Scaling Model: Auto-scaling storage (up to 128 TB) + up to 15 Read Replicas | Consistency Options: Strong consistency (ACID transactions) | Transaction Support: Full ACID support with JOINs and complex queries | Operational Overhead: Low (managed patches, backups) | Performance at Scale: 5x faster than MySQL, 3x faster than PostgreSQL (standard configurations) | Cost Model: Instance + Storage (I/O included) | Maximum Storage: 128 TB | Read Replicas: Up to 15 replicas | Choose When...: You need high performance, many read replicas, MySQL/PostgreSQL compatibility | High Availability: 6-way replication across 3 AZs, automatic failover, self-healing storage | Serverless Option: Yes - Aurora Serverless v2 with auto-scaling | Global Tables: Aurora Global Database - up to 5 secondary regions | Pricing: Similar to RDS but includes storage auto-scaling
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#97 Relational Databases Comparison
Databases relational_databases_comparison 7 cols 14 rows
▼

Comparing AWS managed relational database services

AWS offers multiple relational database solutions ranging from traditional managed databases (RDS) to cloud-native Aurora variants. RDS supports popular open-source and commercial engines, while Aurora provides enhanced performance and scalability. Aurora Serverless offers automatic scaling for variable workloads. Understanding the differences between these services is critical for selecting the right database architecture for your application.

Feature RDS MySQL/PostgreSQL/MariaDB RDS SQL Server/Oracle Aurora MySQL Aurora PostgreSQL Aurora Serverless v1 Aurora Serverless v2 Amazon RDS
Engine Type Open-source engines Commercial licensed engines MySQL-compatible (cloud-native) PostgreSQL-compatible (cloud-native) MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible Multi-engine managed service — MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server
Performance vs Open Source Standard (1x) Standard (1x) Up to 5x faster than MySQL Up to 3x faster than PostgreSQL Same as Aurora provisioned Same as Aurora provisioned Columnar storage, massive parallelism
Storage Scaling Up to 64 TB, auto-scaling Up to 16 TB Up to 128 TB, auto-scaling in 10GB increments Up to 128 TB, auto-scaling in 10GB increments Up to 128 TB, auto-scaling Up to 128 TB, auto-scaling Auto-scaling storage + up to 15 read replicas
Compute Scaling Manual instance scaling Manual instance scaling Manual (up to 15 read replicas) Manual (up to 15 read replicas) Automatic (2-256 ACUs) Automatic (0.5-128 ACUs), instant scaling Vertical — change instance class (brief downtime); read replicas for read scaling; no auto-scaling compute
Read Replicas Up to 5 replicas, async replication Up to 5 replicas (SQL Server), 0-5 (Oracle) Up to 15 replicas, <10ms lag, shared storage Up to 15 replicas, <10ms lag, shared storage Not supported Supported (up to 15 replicas) Up to 5 (MySQL/MariaDB/PostgreSQL)
Multi-AZ Deployment Yes (synchronous standby) Yes (SQL Server Mirroring/Always On) Yes (6 copies across 3 AZs) Yes (6 copies across 3 AZs) Yes (high availability built-in) Yes (high availability built-in) Single-AZ (use cluster for HA)
Failover Time 60-120 seconds 60-120 seconds <30 seconds (typically <10s) <30 seconds (typically <10s) Automatic, no downtime Automatic, no downtime 60-120 seconds for Multi-AZ failover — DNS-based automatic switchover to standby instance
Backup & Recovery Automated backups, point-in-time recovery (35 days) Automated backups, point-in-time recovery (35 days) Continuous backup to S3, 35-day retention, instant restore Continuous backup to S3, 35-day retention, instant restore Same as Aurora provisioned Same as Aurora provisioned Automated daily snapshots + transaction logs; point-in-time restore; manual snapshots retained indefinitely
Licensing Model Open-source (no license fees) License Included or BYOL No license fees No license fees No license fees No license fees License-included for MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB; BYOL or license-included for Oracle/SQL Server
Cost Model Instance + Storage + IOPS Instance + Storage + IOPS + License Instance + Storage (I/O included) Instance + Storage (I/O included) Per ACU-hour + Storage Per ACU-second + Storage Per instance-hour + storage (gp3/io2) + I/O; Reserved Instances save up to 60%; Multi-AZ doubles compute
Best Use Cases Lift-and-shift, existing apps, specific engine needs Enterprise apps requiring specific commercial DB features High throughput, scalability needs, MySQL compatibility High throughput, scalability needs, PostgreSQL compatibility Infrequent/intermittent workloads, dev/test Variable workloads, microservices, multi-tenant apps Traditional OLTP apps, web backends, CMS, ERP systems requiring standard SQL with managed infrastructure
Database Type Open-source relational — community editions managed by AWS; standard SQL functionality Commercial relational — enterprise databases with BYOL or license-included options Cloud-native MySQL-compatible — 5x performance; distributed storage; up to 128 TiB auto-scaling Cloud-native PostgreSQL-compatible — 3x performance; distributed storage; Aurora-specific features Auto-scaling Aurora — scales to zero; on-demand capacity; 5-min scale pause; legacy version Fine-grained auto-scaling — 0.5 ACU increments; instant scaling; production-ready; no scale-to-zero OLTP relational (6 engines)
Primary Use Standard web apps, WordPress, content management, small-medium databases with open-source compatibility Enterprise applications requiring SQL Server or Oracle — .NET apps, SAP, PeopleSoft, custom ERP High-throughput MySQL workloads — SaaS platforms, e-commerce, gaming backends needing 5x MySQL throughput Advanced PostgreSQL features — GIS (PostGIS), JSON processing, complex analytics alongside OLTP Infrequent/unpredictable workloads — dev/test environments; apps that can tolerate scale-to-zero pause Variable workloads — production apps with spiky traffic; seamless scaling without connection drops Standard applications, ERP, CRM, e-commerce
Serverless No — must provision and pay for a fixed instance size; no auto-scaling compute No — requires provisioned instances; commercial license terms add complexity Yes — Aurora Serverless v2; scales from 0.5 to 256 ACUs in fine-grained increments Yes — Aurora Serverless v2; same auto-scaling capabilities as Aurora MySQL Serverless Yes — original serverless; scales to zero (5-min pause); limited features; being deprecated Yes — current production serverless; instant scaling; does NOT scale to zero; full Aurora feature set Aurora Serverless, RDS Proxy
💡 Mnemonics

RRA = RDS for Regular apps, Aurora for high-performance, Redshift for Reports (analytics)

⚠️ Exam Tips

Aurora is NOT just storage - it's a cluster with writer and readers across 3 AZs

Aurora automatically scales storage in 10GB increments up to 128TB

Use Aurora Global Database for cross-region disaster recovery

Redshift is for analytics/BI - NOT for transactional applications

Multi-AZ in RDS uses synchronous replication; Aurora uses quorum-based approach

Amazon RDS
A MySQL and PostgreSQL-compatible relational database with the speed and availability of high-end commercial databases, offering up to 5x throughput of standard MySQL and 3x of standard PostgreSQL.
RDS MySQL/PostgreSQL/MariaDB
Engine Type: Open-source engines | Performance vs Open Source: Standard (1x) | Storage Scaling: Up to 64 TB, auto-scaling | Compute Scaling: Manual instance scaling | Read Replicas: Up to 5 replicas, async replication | Multi-AZ Deployment: Yes (synchronous standby) | Failover Time: 60-120 seconds | Backup & Recovery: Automated backups, point-in-time recovery (35 days) | Licensing Model: Open-source (no license fees) | Cost Model: Instance + Storage + IOPS | Best Use Cases: Lift-and-shift, existing apps, specific engine needs | Database Type: Open-source relational — community editions managed by AWS; standard SQL functionality | Primary Use: Standard web apps, WordPress, content management, small-medium databases with open-source compatibility | Serverless: No — must provision and pay for a fixed instance size; no auto-scaling compute
RDS SQL Server/Oracle
Engine Type: Commercial licensed engines | Performance vs Open Source: Standard (1x) | Storage Scaling: Up to 16 TB | Compute Scaling: Manual instance scaling | Read Replicas: Up to 5 replicas (SQL Server), 0-5 (Oracle) | Multi-AZ Deployment: Yes (SQL Server Mirroring/Always On) | Failover Time: 60-120 seconds | Backup & Recovery: Automated backups, point-in-time recovery (35 days) | Licensing Model: License Included or BYOL | Cost Model: Instance + Storage + IOPS + License | Best Use Cases: Enterprise apps requiring specific commercial DB features | Database Type: Commercial relational — enterprise databases with BYOL or license-included options | Primary Use: Enterprise applications requiring SQL Server or Oracle — .NET apps, SAP, PeopleSoft, custom ERP | Serverless: No — requires provisioned instances; commercial license terms add complexity
Aurora MySQL
Engine Type: MySQL-compatible (cloud-native) | Performance vs Open Source: Up to 5x faster than MySQL | Storage Scaling: Up to 128 TB, auto-scaling in 10GB increments | Compute Scaling: Manual (up to 15 read replicas) | Read Replicas: Up to 15 replicas, <10ms lag, shared storage | Multi-AZ Deployment: Yes (6 copies across 3 AZs) | Failover Time: <30 seconds (typically <10s) | Backup & Recovery: Continuous backup to S3, 35-day retention, instant restore | Licensing Model: No license fees | Cost Model: Instance + Storage (I/O included) | Best Use Cases: High throughput, scalability needs, MySQL compatibility | Database Type: Cloud-native MySQL-compatible — 5x performance; distributed storage; up to 128 TiB auto-scaling | Primary Use: High-throughput MySQL workloads — SaaS platforms, e-commerce, gaming backends needing 5x MySQL throughput | Serverless: Yes — Aurora Serverless v2; scales from 0.5 to 256 ACUs in fine-grained increments
Aurora PostgreSQL
Engine Type: PostgreSQL-compatible (cloud-native) | Performance vs Open Source: Up to 3x faster than PostgreSQL | Storage Scaling: Up to 128 TB, auto-scaling in 10GB increments | Compute Scaling: Manual (up to 15 read replicas) | Read Replicas: Up to 15 replicas, <10ms lag, shared storage | Multi-AZ Deployment: Yes (6 copies across 3 AZs) | Failover Time: <30 seconds (typically <10s) | Backup & Recovery: Continuous backup to S3, 35-day retention, instant restore | Licensing Model: No license fees | Cost Model: Instance + Storage (I/O included) | Best Use Cases: High throughput, scalability needs, PostgreSQL compatibility | Database Type: Cloud-native PostgreSQL-compatible — 3x performance; distributed storage; Aurora-specific features | Primary Use: Advanced PostgreSQL features — GIS (PostGIS), JSON processing, complex analytics alongside OLTP | Serverless: Yes — Aurora Serverless v2; same auto-scaling capabilities as Aurora MySQL Serverless
Aurora Serverless v1
Engine Type: MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible | Performance vs Open Source: Same as Aurora provisioned | Storage Scaling: Up to 128 TB, auto-scaling | Compute Scaling: Automatic (2-256 ACUs) | Read Replicas: Not supported | Multi-AZ Deployment: Yes (high availability built-in) | Failover Time: Automatic, no downtime | Backup & Recovery: Same as Aurora provisioned | Licensing Model: No license fees | Cost Model: Per ACU-hour + Storage | Best Use Cases: Infrequent/intermittent workloads, dev/test | Database Type: Auto-scaling Aurora — scales to zero; on-demand capacity; 5-min scale pause; legacy version | Primary Use: Infrequent/unpredictable workloads — dev/test environments; apps that can tolerate scale-to-zero pause | Serverless: Yes — original serverless; scales to zero (5-min pause); limited features; being deprecated
Aurora Serverless v2
Engine Type: MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible | Performance vs Open Source: Same as Aurora provisioned | Storage Scaling: Up to 128 TB, auto-scaling | Compute Scaling: Automatic (0.5-128 ACUs), instant scaling | Read Replicas: Supported (up to 15 replicas) | Multi-AZ Deployment: Yes (high availability built-in) | Failover Time: Automatic, no downtime | Backup & Recovery: Same as Aurora provisioned | Licensing Model: No license fees | Cost Model: Per ACU-second + Storage | Best Use Cases: Variable workloads, microservices, multi-tenant apps | Database Type: Fine-grained auto-scaling — 0.5 ACU increments; instant scaling; production-ready; no scale-to-zero | Primary Use: Variable workloads — production apps with spiky traffic; seamless scaling without connection drops | Serverless: Yes — current production serverless; instant scaling; does NOT scale to zero; full Aurora feature set
Amazon RDS
Engine Type: Multi-engine managed service — MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server | Performance vs Open Source: Columnar storage, massive parallelism | Storage Scaling: Auto-scaling storage + up to 15 read replicas | Compute Scaling: Vertical — change instance class (brief downtime); read replicas for read scaling; no auto-scaling compute | Read Replicas: Up to 5 (MySQL/MariaDB/PostgreSQL) | Multi-AZ Deployment: Single-AZ (use cluster for HA) | Failover Time: 60-120 seconds for Multi-AZ failover — DNS-based automatic switchover to standby instance | Backup & Recovery: Automated daily snapshots + transaction logs; point-in-time restore; manual snapshots retained indefinitely | Licensing Model: License-included for MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB; BYOL or license-included for Oracle/SQL Server | Cost Model: Per instance-hour + storage (gp3/io2) + I/O; Reserved Instances save up to 60%; Multi-AZ doubles compute | Best Use Cases: Traditional OLTP apps, web backends, CMS, ERP systems requiring standard SQL with managed infrastructure | Database Type: OLTP relational (6 engines) | Primary Use: Standard applications, ERP, CRM, e-commerce | Serverless: Aurora Serverless, RDS Proxy
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#98 Specialty Databases Comparison
Databases specialty_databases_comparison 4 cols 11 rows
▼

AWS offers purpose-built databases for specialized workloads including graph data (Neptune), time-series data (Timestream), immutable ledgers (QLDB), and data warehousing (Redshift). Each service is optimized for specific data models and query patterns. Understanding their unique features, scaling models, and ideal use cases is essential for the Solutions Architect exam.

Feature Neptune Timestream QLDB Redshift
Database Type Graph database Time-series database Ledger database Data warehouse (columnar)
Data Model Property graph and RDF (triples) Time-series (timestamp, measure, dimensions) Document (Amazon Ion format) Relational (columnar storage)
Query Languages Gremlin, SPARQL, openCypher SQL with time-series extensions PartiQL (SQL-compatible) Standard SQL (PostgreSQL-compatible)
Consistency Model Read-after-write consistency Eventual consistency Strong consistency (immutable history) Strong consistency within cluster
Scaling Model Instance-based (up to 15 read replicas) Serverless (auto-scaling) Serverless (auto-scaling) Cluster-based (ra3 nodes), Concurrency Scaling, Serverless
Storage Up to 64 TB per cluster, auto-scaling Unlimited, magnetic store (S3-backed) Unlimited, journal-backed Managed storage (RA3 nodes up to 64 TB per node)
Data Immutability No (mutable graph) No (mutable with updates) Yes (append-only, cryptographically verifiable) No (standard CRUD operations)
Encryption KMS encryption at rest, TLS in transit KMS encryption at rest, TLS in transit KMS encryption at rest, TLS in transit KMS/AES-256 encryption at rest, SSL/TLS in transit
Availability Multi-AZ (1 primary + replicas) Multi-AZ built-in Multi-AZ built-in (3 AZs) Multi-AZ (RA3), Single-AZ (other node types)
Integration S3, Lambda, OpenSearch, SageMaker Grafana, Telegraf, Kinesis, IoT Core, SageMaker Kinesis, Lambda, S3 (export) S3, DynamoDB, RDS, Kinesis, EMR, QuickSight, SageMaker
Best Use Cases Social networks, fraud detection, recommendation engines, knowledge graphs IoT sensor data, application monitoring, operational metrics, DevOps telemetry Financial transactions, supply chain, healthcare records (audit trails, compliance) Data warehousing, BI/analytics, complex queries on large datasets
⚠️ Exam Tips

Neptune = graph database for social networks and knowledge graphs (Gremlin/SPARQL)

Timestream = time-series for IoT and operational metrics with automatic tiered storage

QLDB = immutable ledger for audit trails and financial records with cryptographic verification

Redshift = data warehouse for complex analytics and BI queries (not OLTP)

For the exam: 'relationships between entities' = Neptune; 'time-series data' = Timestream; 'immutable records' = QLDB

Neptune
A fully managed graph database supporting Apache TinkerPop (Gremlin) and RDF (SPARQL) query languages. Ideal for social networks, knowledge graphs, fraud detection, and recommendation engines.
Timestream
A serverless time-series database optimized for IoT and operational applications. Automatically moves data between memory and magnetic storage tiers. Processes trillions of time-series events per day.
QLDB
Quantum Ledger Database — a fully managed ledger database providing a transparent, immutable, and cryptographically verifiable transaction log. Ideal for audit trails, supply chain, and financial record systems.
Redshift
A fully managed petabyte-scale data warehouse using columnar storage and massively parallel processing (MPP). Optimized for complex analytical queries across large datasets with RA3 nodes separating compute from storage.
Neptune
Database Type: Graph database | Data Model: Property graph and RDF (triples) | Query Languages: Gremlin, SPARQL, openCypher | Consistency Model: Read-after-write consistency | Scaling Model: Instance-based (up to 15 read replicas) | Storage: Up to 64 TB per cluster, auto-scaling | Data Immutability: No (mutable graph) | Encryption: KMS encryption at rest, TLS in transit | Availability: Multi-AZ (1 primary + replicas) | Integration: S3, Lambda, OpenSearch, SageMaker | Best Use Cases: Social networks, fraud detection, recommendation engines, knowledge graphs
Timestream
Database Type: Time-series database | Data Model: Time-series (timestamp, measure, dimensions) | Query Languages: SQL with time-series extensions | Consistency Model: Eventual consistency | Scaling Model: Serverless (auto-scaling) | Storage: Unlimited, magnetic store (S3-backed) | Data Immutability: No (mutable with updates) | Encryption: KMS encryption at rest, TLS in transit | Availability: Multi-AZ built-in | Integration: Grafana, Telegraf, Kinesis, IoT Core, SageMaker | Best Use Cases: IoT sensor data, application monitoring, operational metrics, DevOps telemetry
QLDB
Database Type: Ledger database | Data Model: Document (Amazon Ion format) | Query Languages: PartiQL (SQL-compatible) | Consistency Model: Strong consistency (immutable history) | Scaling Model: Serverless (auto-scaling) | Storage: Unlimited, journal-backed | Data Immutability: Yes (append-only, cryptographically verifiable) | Encryption: KMS encryption at rest, TLS in transit | Availability: Multi-AZ built-in (3 AZs) | Integration: Kinesis, Lambda, S3 (export) | Best Use Cases: Financial transactions, supply chain, healthcare records (audit trails, compliance)
Redshift
Database Type: Data warehouse (columnar) | Data Model: Relational (columnar storage) | Query Languages: Standard SQL (PostgreSQL-compatible) | Consistency Model: Strong consistency within cluster | Scaling Model: Cluster-based (ra3 nodes), Concurrency Scaling, Serverless | Storage: Managed storage (RA3 nodes up to 64 TB per node) | Data Immutability: No (standard CRUD operations) | Encryption: KMS/AES-256 encryption at rest, SSL/TLS in transit | Availability: Multi-AZ (RA3), Single-AZ (other node types) | Integration: S3, DynamoDB, RDS, Kinesis, EMR, QuickSight, SageMaker | Best Use Cases: Data warehousing, BI/analytics, complex queries on large datasets
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

Networking 9 tables
#99 API Management Comparison: API Gateway (REST vs HTTP vs WebSocket) vs AppSync
Networking api_management_comparison_api_gateway_rest_vs_http_vs_websoc 4 cols 14 rows
▼

AWS offers multiple API management services for exposing backend functionality. API Gateway provides RESTful, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs for different communication patterns. AppSync offers GraphQL APIs with real-time and offline capabilities. Understanding their capabilities helps design appropriate API architectures for different client requirements.

Feature API Gateway REST API API Gateway HTTP API API Gateway WebSocket API AWS AppSync
Protocol REST (HTTP/HTTPS) HTTP/HTTPS WebSocket (persistent connection) GraphQL over HTTP/WebSocket
API Type Request/Response (synchronous) Request/Response (synchronous) Bidirectional, persistent Query/Mutation/Subscription
Latency Higher (feature-rich) Lower (up to 71% faster than REST) Low (persistent connection) Low with caching
Pricing $3.50/million requests + data $1.00/million requests (cheaper) $1.00/million messages + connection time $4.00/million queries/mutations + $2.00/million subscription minutes
Authorization IAM, Cognito, Lambda authorizer, API keys IAM, Cognito, Lambda authorizer, JWT (OpenID) Lambda authorizer, IAM IAM, Cognito, API key, Lambda
Request Validation JSON Schema validation Basic validation Manual validation GraphQL schema validation
Response Caching Built-in cache (optional) Not supported Not applicable Built-in caching
Request/Response Transformation Full transformation (mapping templates) Limited transformation Manual transformation Resolver-based transformation
Usage Plans & API Keys Full support (throttling, quotas) Not supported Not supported API keys supported
AWS WAF Integration Full WAF support Full WAF support Limited WAF support WAF supported
Real-time Updates Not supported Not supported Native support Native subscriptions support
Offline Sync Not supported Not supported Not supported Built-in offline synchronization
Data Sources Lambda, HTTP, AWS services Lambda, HTTP Lambda, HTTP, DynamoDB DynamoDB, Lambda, HTTP, RDS, Elasticsearch, None
Best Use Case Full-featured REST APIs with caching, throttling Simple, low-latency, cost-effective APIs Real-time chat, gaming, live dashboards Mobile/web apps with real-time, offline, complex data needs
⚠️ Exam Tips

HTTP API is cheaper and faster than REST API — use it unless you need REST-specific features

REST API features not in HTTP API: caching, request transformation, usage plans, API keys

WebSocket API enables real-time bidirectional communication — chat apps, live dashboards

AppSync = GraphQL with real-time subscriptions and offline sync — ideal for mobile apps

For the exam: 'real-time pub/sub to clients' = AppSync or WebSocket API; 'simple Lambda proxy' = HTTP API

API Gateway REST API
Full-featured REST API management with request/response transformation, API keys, usage plans, caching, WAF integration, and resource policies. Higher latency but richest feature set.
API Gateway HTTP API
Lightweight, low-latency HTTP API optimized for serverless workloads. Up to 71% cheaper than REST API with simpler configuration. Supports JWT authorizers and Lambda proxy integration.
API Gateway WebSocket API
Enables real-time two-way communication between clients and backends. Ideal for chat applications, live dashboards, and real-time notifications. Manages persistent connections automatically.
AWS AppSync
Managed GraphQL and Pub/Sub API service with built-in real-time subscriptions, offline data synchronization, and direct integrations with DynamoDB, Lambda, and other AWS data sources.
API Gateway REST API
Protocol: REST (HTTP/HTTPS) | API Type: Request/Response (synchronous) | Latency: Higher (feature-rich) | Pricing: $3.50/million requests + data | Authorization: IAM, Cognito, Lambda authorizer, API keys | Request Validation: JSON Schema validation | Response Caching: Built-in cache (optional) | Request/Response Transformation: Full transformation (mapping templates) | Usage Plans & API Keys: Full support (throttling, quotas) | AWS WAF Integration: Full WAF support | Real-time Updates: Not supported | Offline Sync: Not supported | Data Sources: Lambda, HTTP, AWS services | Best Use Case: Full-featured REST APIs with caching, throttling
API Gateway HTTP API
Protocol: HTTP/HTTPS | API Type: Request/Response (synchronous) | Latency: Lower (up to 71% faster than REST) | Pricing: $1.00/million requests (cheaper) | Authorization: IAM, Cognito, Lambda authorizer, JWT (OpenID) | Request Validation: Basic validation | Response Caching: Not supported | Request/Response Transformation: Limited transformation | Usage Plans & API Keys: Not supported | AWS WAF Integration: Full WAF support | Real-time Updates: Not supported | Offline Sync: Not supported | Data Sources: Lambda, HTTP | Best Use Case: Simple, low-latency, cost-effective APIs
API Gateway WebSocket API
Protocol: WebSocket (persistent connection) | API Type: Bidirectional, persistent | Latency: Low (persistent connection) | Pricing: $1.00/million messages + connection time | Authorization: Lambda authorizer, IAM | Request Validation: Manual validation | Response Caching: Not applicable | Request/Response Transformation: Manual transformation | Usage Plans & API Keys: Not supported | AWS WAF Integration: Limited WAF support | Real-time Updates: Native support | Offline Sync: Not supported | Data Sources: Lambda, HTTP, DynamoDB | Best Use Case: Real-time chat, gaming, live dashboards
AWS AppSync
Protocol: GraphQL over HTTP/WebSocket | API Type: Query/Mutation/Subscription | Latency: Low with caching | Pricing: $4.00/million queries/mutations + $2.00/million subscription minutes | Authorization: IAM, Cognito, API key, Lambda | Request Validation: GraphQL schema validation | Response Caching: Built-in caching | Request/Response Transformation: Resolver-based transformation | Usage Plans & API Keys: API keys supported | AWS WAF Integration: WAF supported | Real-time Updates: Native subscriptions support | Offline Sync: Built-in offline synchronization | Data Sources: DynamoDB, Lambda, HTTP, RDS, Elasticsearch, None | Best Use Case: Mobile/web apps with real-time, offline, complex data needs
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#100 AWS Load Balancer Types Comparison
Networking SAA-004_2 4 cols 22 rows
▼

Comparison of Application Load Balancer, Network Load Balancer, and Gateway Load Balancer

This table compares the three types of AWS Elastic Load Balancers. Application Load Balancer (ALB) operates at Layer 7 for HTTP/HTTPS traffic with advanced routing capabilities, Network Load Balancer (NLB) operates at Layer 4 for extreme performance with TCP/UDP traffic, and Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB) is designed for deploying third-party virtual appliances like firewalls and intrusion detection systems.

Feature Application Load Balancer (ALB) Network Load Balancer (NLB) Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB) Classic Load Balancer (CLB)
OSI Layer Layer 7 (Application) - HTTP/HTTPS Layer 4 (Transport) - TCP/UDP Layer 3 (Network) + Layer 4 Layer 4 & Layer 7 (legacy)
Protocols Supported HTTP, HTTPS, WebSocket, gRPC TCP, UDP, TLS IP Protocol (all traffic) HTTP, HTTPS, TCP, SSL
Routing Features Path-based, host-based, header routing IP-based routing, static IP Flow-based, transparent Basic routing only
Target Types EC2, ECS, Lambda, IP addresses EC2, IP addresses, ALB Third-party virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS) EC2 instances only
Performance / Latency Handles millions of requests per second Handles tens of millions of requests per second; ultra-low latency High (through appliance fleet) Higher latency (deprecated)
SSL/TLS Termination Yes - built-in certificate management and HTTPS listener Yes - can terminate TLS but less feature-rich than ALB N/A (passes through to appliances) Yes - single SSL cert only
WebSockets Supported Supported Not applicable — GWLB operates at Layer 3 for network appliances; does not handle WebSocket connections Limited support
Best For Web applications, microservices High-performance TCP/UDP Network appliances, security Legacy applications (deprecated)
Health Check Granularity HTTP/HTTPS health checks with custom response codes TCP health checks, HTTP/HTTPS optional Health checks to virtual appliances Basic TCP/HTTP/HTTPS
Routing Capabilities Path-based, Host-based, Query string, HTTP headers Static IP per AZ, Port-based Transparent traffic inspection Basic request routing only
Static IP / Elastic IP No - uses DNS names; can use regional IP addresses Yes - can assign Elastic IPs (one per AZ) No (uses Gateway Load Balancer endpoints) No (DNS name only)
Throughput High throughput Millions of requests/sec, handles sudden spikes High throughput for traffic inspection Limited (not recommended)
Preserve Client IP No (X-Forwarded-For header) Yes (preserves source IP) Yes (transparent) No (X-Forwarded-For header)
Pricing Model LCU-based (more complex but often cheaper for HTTP traffic) Per hour + NLCU (Network Load Balancer Capacity Units) Per hour + GLCU (Gateway Load Balancer Capacity Units) Per hour + data processed
Primary Use Cases Web applications, microservices, containers, Lambda targets High-throughput, low-latency apps, SSL termination, static IPs, gaming Traffic inspection, firewalls, intrusion detection Legacy applications (being deprecated)
Cross-Zone Load Balancing Always enabled (no charge) Disabled by default (charges apply if enabled) Disabled by default — can be enabled; routes traffic to appliances within the same AZ for efficiency Enabled by default
Connection Draining Deregistration delay (configurable) Connection termination on deregistration Configurable deregistration delay Connection draining (configurable)
Routing Based On URL path, host header, query string, HTTP headers, IP address IP address, port, protocol, source/destination ports Transparent traffic forwarding Basic request-level (HTTP) or connection-level (TCP)
Static IP Support No (use NLB in front if needed) Yes (Elastic IP per AZ) No (transparent insertion) No
Source IP Preservation No (X-Forwarded-For header) Yes (client IP preserved) Yes (transparent) X-Forwarded-For (HTTP mode)
Container Support Yes — native ECS/EKS integration; dynamic port mapping; path-based routing to different containers Yes — supports ECS/EKS; use for TCP/UDP container workloads requiring ultra-low latency Not applicable — GWLB targets are network appliances, not application containers Limited — basic EC2 targets only; no dynamic port mapping; not recommended for containers
Lambda Target Yes — ALB can invoke Lambda functions as targets; used for serverless web applications No — NLB does not support Lambda targets directly; use ALB or API Gateway instead No — GWLB targets are network appliance instances/IPs only No — CLB only supports EC2 instances as targets
💡 Mnemonics

ALB = Application Layer 7, NLB = Network Layer 4, GWLB = Gateway for appliances, CLB = Classic (deprecated)

Routing web traffic? ALB. Need extreme performance? NLB. Need third-party appliances? GWLB. CLB is legacy - avoid for new designs.

A.N.G - ALB for Apps (Layer 7), NLB for Networks (Layer 4), GLB for Gateways (appliances)

ANG = ALB for web, NLB for Network/performance, GLB for Gateway/security appliances

⚠️ Exam Tips

ALB is the default choice for HTTP/HTTPS traffic with routing needs

NLB can handle millions of requests per second with ultra-low latency

ALB supports Lambda as a target for serverless architectures

GWLB is for deploying third-party virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS)

CLB is being deprecated - migrate to ALB/NLB

ALB supports path-based routing: /api → one target group, /web → another

ALB is the most common choice for web applications and microservices

Use NLB when you need static IPs, UDP traffic, or ultra-low latency

ALB can route based on path (/api/* goes to service A, /web/* goes to service B)

For container workloads, ALB integrates with ECS/EKS service discovery

GLB is specifically for inserting third-party virtual appliances into traffic flow

ALB is best for web applications with URL-based routing

NLB is best for high-performance TCP/UDP applications

ALB can route based on path (/api/*, /images/*)

NLB preserves source IP - important for logging and geo-blocking

GLB is specifically for inserting third-party security appliances in the traffic path

Application Load Balancer (ALB)
A Layer 7 load balancer optimized for HTTP and HTTPS traffic. Provides advanced routing capabilities including path-based, host-based, and query string routing, with support for Lambda targets, WebSockets, and gRPC protocols.
Network Load Balancer (NLB)
A Layer 4 load balancer capable of handling millions of requests per second with ultra-low latency. Operates at the connection level for TCP and UDP traffic, ideal for high-performance workloads, gaming, and IoT applications.
Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)
A Layer 3 load balancer that deploys, scales, and manages third-party virtual appliances like firewalls, intrusion detection systems, and deep packet inspection systems. Provides transparent network gateway and load balancing in a single service.
Classic Load Balancer (CLB)
A legacy load balancer providing basic Layer 4 and Layer 7 capabilities. Being deprecated in favor of ALB and NLB. Should only be used for legacy applications that cannot migrate to newer load balancer types.
Application Load Balancer (ALB)
OSI Layer: Layer 7 (Application) - HTTP/HTTPS | Protocols Supported: HTTP, HTTPS, WebSocket, gRPC | Routing Features: Path-based, host-based, header routing | Target Types: EC2, ECS, Lambda, IP addresses | Performance / Latency: Handles millions of requests per second | SSL/TLS Termination: Yes - built-in certificate management and HTTPS listener | WebSockets: Supported | Best For: Web applications, microservices | Health Check Granularity: HTTP/HTTPS health checks with custom response codes | Routing Capabilities: Path-based, Host-based, Query string, HTTP headers | Static IP / Elastic IP: No - uses DNS names; can use regional IP addresses | Throughput: High throughput | Preserve Client IP: No (X-Forwarded-For header) | Pricing Model: LCU-based (more complex but often cheaper for HTTP traffic) | Primary Use Cases: Web applications, microservices, containers, Lambda targets | Cross-Zone Load Balancing: Always enabled (no charge) | Connection Draining: Deregistration delay (configurable) | Routing Based On: URL path, host header, query string, HTTP headers, IP address | Static IP Support: No (use NLB in front if needed) | Source IP Preservation: No (X-Forwarded-For header) | Container Support: Yes — native ECS/EKS integration; dynamic port mapping; path-based routing to different containers | Lambda Target: Yes — ALB can invoke Lambda functions as targets; used for serverless web applications
Network Load Balancer (NLB)
OSI Layer: Layer 4 (Transport) - TCP/UDP | Protocols Supported: TCP, UDP, TLS | Routing Features: IP-based routing, static IP | Target Types: EC2, IP addresses, ALB | Performance / Latency: Handles tens of millions of requests per second; ultra-low latency | SSL/TLS Termination: Yes - can terminate TLS but less feature-rich than ALB | WebSockets: Supported | Best For: High-performance TCP/UDP | Health Check Granularity: TCP health checks, HTTP/HTTPS optional | Routing Capabilities: Static IP per AZ, Port-based | Static IP / Elastic IP: Yes - can assign Elastic IPs (one per AZ) | Throughput: Millions of requests/sec, handles sudden spikes | Preserve Client IP: Yes (preserves source IP) | Pricing Model: Per hour + NLCU (Network Load Balancer Capacity Units) | Primary Use Cases: High-throughput, low-latency apps, SSL termination, static IPs, gaming | Cross-Zone Load Balancing: Disabled by default (charges apply if enabled) | Connection Draining: Connection termination on deregistration | Routing Based On: IP address, port, protocol, source/destination ports | Static IP Support: Yes (Elastic IP per AZ) | Source IP Preservation: Yes (client IP preserved) | Container Support: Yes — supports ECS/EKS; use for TCP/UDP container workloads requiring ultra-low latency | Lambda Target: No — NLB does not support Lambda targets directly; use ALB or API Gateway instead
Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)
OSI Layer: Layer 3 (Network) + Layer 4 | Protocols Supported: IP Protocol (all traffic) | Routing Features: Flow-based, transparent | Target Types: Third-party virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS) | Performance / Latency: High (through appliance fleet) | SSL/TLS Termination: N/A (passes through to appliances) | WebSockets: Not applicable — GWLB operates at Layer 3 for network appliances; does not handle WebSocket connections | Best For: Network appliances, security | Health Check Granularity: Health checks to virtual appliances | Routing Capabilities: Transparent traffic inspection | Static IP / Elastic IP: No (uses Gateway Load Balancer endpoints) | Throughput: High throughput for traffic inspection | Preserve Client IP: Yes (transparent) | Pricing Model: Per hour + GLCU (Gateway Load Balancer Capacity Units) | Primary Use Cases: Traffic inspection, firewalls, intrusion detection | Cross-Zone Load Balancing: Disabled by default — can be enabled; routes traffic to appliances within the same AZ for efficiency | Connection Draining: Configurable deregistration delay | Routing Based On: Transparent traffic forwarding | Static IP Support: No (transparent insertion) | Source IP Preservation: Yes (transparent) | Container Support: Not applicable — GWLB targets are network appliances, not application containers | Lambda Target: No — GWLB targets are network appliance instances/IPs only
Classic Load Balancer (CLB)
OSI Layer: Layer 4 & Layer 7 (legacy) | Protocols Supported: HTTP, HTTPS, TCP, SSL | Routing Features: Basic routing only | Target Types: EC2 instances only | Performance / Latency: Higher latency (deprecated) | SSL/TLS Termination: Yes - single SSL cert only | WebSockets: Limited support | Best For: Legacy applications (deprecated) | Health Check Granularity: Basic TCP/HTTP/HTTPS | Routing Capabilities: Basic request routing only | Static IP / Elastic IP: No (DNS name only) | Throughput: Limited (not recommended) | Preserve Client IP: No (X-Forwarded-For header) | Pricing Model: Per hour + data processed | Primary Use Cases: Legacy applications (being deprecated) | Cross-Zone Load Balancing: Enabled by default | Connection Draining: Connection draining (configurable) | Routing Based On: Basic request-level (HTTP) or connection-level (TCP) | Static IP Support: No | Source IP Preservation: X-Forwarded-For (HTTP mode) | Container Support: Limited — basic EC2 targets only; no dynamic port mapping; not recommended for containers | Lambda Target: No — CLB only supports EC2 instances as targets
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#101 AWS VPC Networking Components
Networking SAA-006 6 cols 14 rows
▼

This table compares key VPC networking components for connecting AWS resources to each other and to the internet or on-premises networks. Understanding these components is critical for designing secure and scalable network architectures.

Feature Security Groups Network ACLs (NACLs) Route Tables NAT Gateway VPC Peering PrivateLink
Layer Instance-level (Layer 4-7) Subnet-level (Layer 3-4) Subnet-level routing Subnet-level NAT Network layer (L3) — routes traffic between VPC CIDRs at the IP level Application layer (L4/L7) — exposes specific services via ENIs, not full network
State Stateful (return traffic auto-allowed) Stateless (explicit allow) N/A - routing only Stateful — tracks outbound connections and allows return traffic automatically Stateless at peering level — relies on security groups and NACLs for state tracking Stateful — ENI-based connections tracked by underlying security groups
Rule Evaluation All rules evaluated Process in order (lowest to highest) Longest prefix match Not applicable — NAT Gateway does not evaluate rules; relies on route tables and NACLs Not applicable — peering itself has no rules; traffic controlled by route tables, security groups, and NACLs Not applicable — traffic controlled by endpoint policies, security groups on ENIs
Allow/Deny Allow rules only Allow and deny rules N/A - routing only Not applicable — NAT translates addresses; does not allow/deny traffic directly Not applicable — peering enables connectivity; allow/deny handled by security groups and NACLs Allow only — endpoint policies control which principals can access the service
Default Behavior Deny all inbound, allow all outbound Allow all traffic (modifiable) Local + main route table Allows outbound internet access for private subnets; blocks unsolicited inbound No traffic flows until route table entries are added in both VPCs No access until endpoint is created and security groups allow traffic
Connectivity Scope ENI/instance Subnet Subnet or VPC Multiple VPCs, VPN, Direct Connect VPC to VPC (same or different account/region) Private access to services across VPCs
Associations Multiple SGs per instance One NACL per subnet One route table per subnet One NAT per AZ Route table entries in both VPCs pointing to the peering connection Subnet ENIs in consumer VPC; endpoint policy; security groups
Use Cases Instance-level firewall Subnet-level firewall Traffic routing Private subnet instances needing internet access Two VPCs needing direct connection Private SaaS access, service exposure
Cost Model Free Free Free Per-hour attachment + data processing Data transfer charges only Per-hour interface endpoint + data processing
Traffic Direction Inbound and outbound rules evaluated independently; stateful return traffic auto-allowed Inbound and outbound rules evaluated separately; stateless so return traffic must be explicitly allowed Destination-based — routes traffic to targets (IGW, NAT, peering, etc.) based on CIDR matching Outbound only (from private subnet) Bidirectional (between peered VPCs) Service provider to consumer (unidirectional)
Transitive Routing Not applicable — security groups filter traffic, not route it Not applicable — NACLs filter traffic at subnet boundary, not route it Not natively — VPC peering is non-transitive; use Transit Gateway for transitive routing No — NAT Gateway provides internet access, not inter-VPC transitive routing No (non-transitive) No — PrivateLink provides point-to-point service access, not transitive routing
IP Addressing Reference security group IDs or CIDR blocks in rules; support IPv4 and IPv6 CIDR-based rules only; support IPv4 and IPv6 address ranges CIDR destination matching with longest-prefix match; IPv4 and IPv6 routes Uses Elastic IP; instances use private IPs Private IP communication Private IP within consumer VPC
Bandwidth Not applicable — security groups do not limit bandwidth Not applicable — NACLs do not limit bandwidth Not applicable — route tables direct traffic; bandwidth depends on the target resource Up to 100 Gbps No bandwidth limit (AWS backbone) High (AWS backbone)
High Availability Automatic — security groups are VPC-level constructs, always available Automatic — NACLs are VPC-level constructs, always available Automatic — route tables are VPC-level constructs, always available AZ-specific (deploy in multiple AZs for HA) AWS backbone redundancy Built-in redundancy
💡 Mnemonics

SG = Stateful Guard (instance), NACL = Network Access Control List (subnet), RT = Route Table, NAT = Network Address Translation

Need instance-level security? Security Groups. Need subnet-level protection? NACLs. Need to route traffic? Route tables. Need internet from private subnet? NAT Gateway.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Security Groups are stateful - you only need to allow inbound, outbound is automatic

NACLs are stateless - you must explicitly allow both directions

Security Groups can only have ALLOW rules (no DENY)

NACLs have numbered rules processed in order

NAT Gateway is required for private subnet instances to access internet

Security Groups reference other security groups for inter-service communication

Security Groups
A virtual firewall at the instance level that controls inbound and outbound traffic. Stateful - return traffic is automatically allowed regardless of rules. Can only specify allow rules and can reference other security groups as sources/destinations.
Network ACLs (NACLs)
An optional layer of security at the subnet level that acts as a firewall for controlling traffic in and out of subnets. Stateless - requires explicit allow rules for both directions. Processed in order from lowest to highest rule number.
Route Tables
A set of rules (routes) that determine where network traffic from a subnet or gateway is directed. Each route specifies a destination CIDR and a target (gateway, instance, VPC peering connection, etc.). Uses longest prefix match for route selection.
NAT Gateway
A managed Network Address Translation service that enables instances in private subnets to connect to the internet while preventing the internet from initiating connections with those instances. Highly available within an Availability Zone.
VPC Peering
Networking connection between two VPCs that enables routing traffic using private IP addresses, but is non-transitive (no transitive routing through hub).
PrivateLink
Technology that provides private connectivity between VPCs, AWS services, and on-premises networks without traversing the public internet.
Security Groups
Layer: Instance-level (Layer 4-7) | State: Stateful (return traffic auto-allowed) | Rule Evaluation: All rules evaluated | Allow/Deny: Allow rules only | Default Behavior: Deny all inbound, allow all outbound | Connectivity Scope: ENI/instance | Associations: Multiple SGs per instance | Use Cases: Instance-level firewall | Cost Model: Free | Traffic Direction: Inbound and outbound rules evaluated independently; stateful return traffic auto-allowed | Transitive Routing: Not applicable — security groups filter traffic, not route it | IP Addressing: Reference security group IDs or CIDR blocks in rules; support IPv4 and IPv6 | Bandwidth: Not applicable — security groups do not limit bandwidth | High Availability: Automatic — security groups are VPC-level constructs, always available
Network ACLs (NACLs)
Layer: Subnet-level (Layer 3-4) | State: Stateless (explicit allow) | Rule Evaluation: Process in order (lowest to highest) | Allow/Deny: Allow and deny rules | Default Behavior: Allow all traffic (modifiable) | Connectivity Scope: Subnet | Associations: One NACL per subnet | Use Cases: Subnet-level firewall | Cost Model: Free | Traffic Direction: Inbound and outbound rules evaluated separately; stateless so return traffic must be explicitly allowed | Transitive Routing: Not applicable — NACLs filter traffic at subnet boundary, not route it | IP Addressing: CIDR-based rules only; support IPv4 and IPv6 address ranges | Bandwidth: Not applicable — NACLs do not limit bandwidth | High Availability: Automatic — NACLs are VPC-level constructs, always available
Route Tables
Layer: Subnet-level routing | State: N/A - routing only | Rule Evaluation: Longest prefix match | Allow/Deny: N/A - routing only | Default Behavior: Local + main route table | Connectivity Scope: Subnet or VPC | Associations: One route table per subnet | Use Cases: Traffic routing | Cost Model: Free | Traffic Direction: Destination-based — routes traffic to targets (IGW, NAT, peering, etc.) based on CIDR matching | Transitive Routing: Not natively — VPC peering is non-transitive; use Transit Gateway for transitive routing | IP Addressing: CIDR destination matching with longest-prefix match; IPv4 and IPv6 routes | Bandwidth: Not applicable — route tables direct traffic; bandwidth depends on the target resource | High Availability: Automatic — route tables are VPC-level constructs, always available
NAT Gateway
Layer: Subnet-level NAT | State: Stateful — tracks outbound connections and allows return traffic automatically | Rule Evaluation: Not applicable — NAT Gateway does not evaluate rules; relies on route tables and NACLs | Allow/Deny: Not applicable — NAT translates addresses; does not allow/deny traffic directly | Default Behavior: Allows outbound internet access for private subnets; blocks unsolicited inbound | Connectivity Scope: Multiple VPCs, VPN, Direct Connect | Associations: One NAT per AZ | Use Cases: Private subnet instances needing internet access | Cost Model: Per-hour attachment + data processing | Traffic Direction: Outbound only (from private subnet) | Transitive Routing: No — NAT Gateway provides internet access, not inter-VPC transitive routing | IP Addressing: Uses Elastic IP; instances use private IPs | Bandwidth: Up to 100 Gbps | High Availability: AZ-specific (deploy in multiple AZs for HA)
VPC Peering
Layer: Network layer (L3) — routes traffic between VPC CIDRs at the IP level | State: Stateless at peering level — relies on security groups and NACLs for state tracking | Rule Evaluation: Not applicable — peering itself has no rules; traffic controlled by route tables, security groups, and NACLs | Allow/Deny: Not applicable — peering enables connectivity; allow/deny handled by security groups and NACLs | Default Behavior: No traffic flows until route table entries are added in both VPCs | Connectivity Scope: VPC to VPC (same or different account/region) | Associations: Route table entries in both VPCs pointing to the peering connection | Use Cases: Two VPCs needing direct connection | Cost Model: Data transfer charges only | Traffic Direction: Bidirectional (between peered VPCs) | Transitive Routing: No (non-transitive) | IP Addressing: Private IP communication | Bandwidth: No bandwidth limit (AWS backbone) | High Availability: AWS backbone redundancy
PrivateLink
Layer: Application layer (L4/L7) — exposes specific services via ENIs, not full network | State: Stateful — ENI-based connections tracked by underlying security groups | Rule Evaluation: Not applicable — traffic controlled by endpoint policies, security groups on ENIs | Allow/Deny: Allow only — endpoint policies control which principals can access the service | Default Behavior: No access until endpoint is created and security groups allow traffic | Connectivity Scope: Private access to services across VPCs | Associations: Subnet ENIs in consumer VPC; endpoint policy; security groups | Use Cases: Private SaaS access, service exposure | Cost Model: Per-hour interface endpoint + data processing | Traffic Direction: Service provider to consumer (unidirectional) | Transitive Routing: No — PrivateLink provides point-to-point service access, not transitive routing | IP Addressing: Private IP within consumer VPC | Bandwidth: High (AWS backbone) | High Availability: Built-in redundancy
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#102 Application vs Network vs Gateway Load Balancer
Networking SAA-009 3 cols 7 rows
▼

AWS offers three types of load balancers under the Elastic Load Balancing (ELB) family. Each operates at a different network layer and solves a different problem. The exam will describe traffic characteristics and ask you to pick the right load balancer. Layer is the primary differentiator.

Feature Application Load Balancer (ALB) Network Load Balancer (NLB) Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)
OSI Layer Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) Layer 4 (TCP/UDP/TLS) Layer 3 (IP) + Layer 4
Routing Intelligence Path-based, host-based, header-based, query string routing IP + Port only — no content inspection Routes through virtual appliances transparently
Static IP? No — DNS name only YES — one static IP per AZ No
Performance High — but slightly higher latency (HTTP processing) Extreme — millions of req/sec, sub-millisecond latency Variable — depends on appliance throughput
Best Use Case Web apps, REST APIs, microservices, containers, WebSocket TCP/UDP traffic, gaming, VoIP, financial trading, static IP required Inline network security appliances (firewall, IDS/IPS)
Protocol Support HTTP, HTTPS, WebSocket TCP, UDP, TLS All IP protocols (GENEVE encapsulation)
Target Types EC2, ECS tasks, Lambda, IP addresses EC2, IP addresses, ALB Virtual network appliance instances
⚠️ Exam Tips

ALB = Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS routing); NLB = Layer 4 (TCP/UDP ultra-low latency); GLB = security appliances

ALB supports path-based and host-based routing, sticky sessions, and gRPC

NLB provides static IP addresses and handles millions of requests with ultra-low latency

GLB transparently inserts third-party firewalls/IDS into the traffic flow

For the exam: 'content-based routing' = ALB; 'extreme performance/static IP' = NLB; 'inline security appliance' = GLB

Application Load Balancer (ALB)
ALB operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) and makes intelligent routing decisions based on the content of requests — URL path, host header, HTTP method, query strings. It is the standard choice for web applications, microservices, and containerized apps requiring content-based routing.
Network Load Balancer (NLB)
NLB operates at Layer 4 (TCP/UDP/TLS) and forwards traffic based purely on IP address and port — it does not inspect HTTP content. It handles millions of requests per second with ultra-low latency and is the right choice for non-HTTP traffic or applications requiring static IP addresses.
Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)
GWLB is specialized for deploying, scaling, and managing third-party virtual network appliances (firewalls, intrusion detection systems, deep packet inspection tools). Traffic flows THROUGH the appliance transparently — the appliance inspects it and sends it back. Used for advanced network security architectures.
Application Load Balancer (ALB)
OSI Layer: Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) | Routing Intelligence: Path-based, host-based, header-based, query string routing | Static IP?: No — DNS name only | Performance: High — but slightly higher latency (HTTP processing) | Best Use Case: Web apps, REST APIs, microservices, containers, WebSocket | Protocol Support: HTTP, HTTPS, WebSocket | Target Types: EC2, ECS tasks, Lambda, IP addresses
Network Load Balancer (NLB)
OSI Layer: Layer 4 (TCP/UDP/TLS) | Routing Intelligence: IP + Port only — no content inspection | Static IP?: YES — one static IP per AZ | Performance: Extreme — millions of req/sec, sub-millisecond latency | Best Use Case: TCP/UDP traffic, gaming, VoIP, financial trading, static IP required | Protocol Support: TCP, UDP, TLS | Target Types: EC2, IP addresses, ALB
Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)
OSI Layer: Layer 3 (IP) + Layer 4 | Routing Intelligence: Routes through virtual appliances transparently | Static IP?: No | Performance: Variable — depends on appliance throughput | Best Use Case: Inline network security appliances (firewall, IDS/IPS) | Protocol Support: All IP protocols (GENEVE encapsulation) | Target Types: Virtual network appliance instances
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#103 Network Connectivity Services Comparison
Networking network_connectivity_services_comparison 3 cols 5 rows
▼

Comparing AWS services for network connectivity and VPN

Feature AWS Direct Connect AWS VPN AWS PrivateLink
Connection Type Dedicated physical connection (1 Gbps to 100 Gbps) Encrypted VPN tunnel over internet Private connectivity to services via VPC endpoint
Bandwidth Up to 100 Gbps dedicated Limited by internet bandwidth Full network speed within VPC
Latency Low, consistent Variable (internet-dependent) Low (within AWS network)
Use Case Large data transfers, hybrid cloud Remote access, site-to-site connectivity Access AWS services privately from VPC
Public/Private Both public and private VIFs Encrypted tunnel (private) Private only (no internet)
💡 Mnemonics

DVP = Direct Connect for Data transfers, VPN for remote access, PrivateLink for private service access

⚠️ Exam Tips

Direct Connect provides consistent network performance for hybrid environments

VPN is quick to set up but performance varies with internet

PrivateLink is essential for securely accessing AWS services without internet

Use VPC endpoints (powered by PrivateLink) for S3, DynamoDB, and other AWS services

Direct Connect + VPN provides both performance and security

AWS Direct Connect
A cloud service that establishes a dedicated network connection from your premises to AWS, providing consistent network performance and private connectivity.
AWS VPN
A managed VPN service that creates an encrypted tunnel between your network or device and the AWS global network, including Site-to-Site and Client VPN options.
AWS PrivateLink
A technology that enables private connectivity between VPCs and AWS services, keeping traffic within the AWS network without exposing it to the internet.
AWS Direct Connect
Connection Type: Dedicated physical connection (1 Gbps to 100 Gbps) | Bandwidth: Up to 100 Gbps dedicated | Latency: Low, consistent | Use Case: Large data transfers, hybrid cloud | Public/Private: Both public and private VIFs
AWS VPN
Connection Type: Encrypted VPN tunnel over internet | Bandwidth: Limited by internet bandwidth | Latency: Variable (internet-dependent) | Use Case: Remote access, site-to-site connectivity | Public/Private: Encrypted tunnel (private)
AWS PrivateLink
Connection Type: Private connectivity to services via VPC endpoint | Bandwidth: Full network speed within VPC | Latency: Low (within AWS network) | Use Case: Access AWS services privately from VPC | Public/Private: Private only (no internet)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#104 Private Connectivity Options
Networking saa_d1_private_connectivity 5 cols 18 rows
▼

AWS provides multiple options for connecting on-premises networks to AWS or connecting AWS VPCs privately. Understanding Site-to-Site VPN, Client VPN, Direct Connect, PrivateLink, and Transit Gateway is critical for designing secure, scalable hybrid cloud architectures.

Feature Site-to-Site VPN AWS Direct Connect AWS PrivateLink Client VPN Transit Gateway
Connection style Encrypted tunnel over public internet Shared physical connection via partner Private connectivity to AWS services or VPCs Encrypted tunnel for individual remote users VPC and on-premises network hub
Use Case: Site-to-AWS Quick, encrypted connection over internet High-bandwidth without dedicated infrastructure Consume a specific service privately N/A (user access, not site) Hub for multiple VPCs and on-premises
Typical exam clue Need secure connection quickly and cheaply Need consistent private hybrid connection Need private access to service endpoints 'Remote workers need secure access to VPC', 'employees working from home', 'laptop to VPC connectivity' 'Connect multiple VPCs', 'hub-and-spoke topology', 'transitive routing between VPCs and on-premises'
Scope Network-to-network Network-to-network Service-to-consumer Remote user to VPC — individual user connections from any internet location to VPC resources Regional hub — connects multiple VPCs, VPNs, and Direct Connect gateways in a hub-and-spoke model
Internet dependency Yes, underlying path uses internet No, dedicated connection No public internet exposure for service access Yes — client connects over the public internet using OpenVPN protocol with TLS encryption No for VPC-to-VPC; Site-to-Site VPN attachments traverse internet; Direct Connect attachments are private
Common trap Assuming it behaves like a dedicated circuit Assuming it automatically encrypts like VPN Assuming it replaces all VPC-to-VPC networking Confusing with Site-to-Site VPN (network-to-network) — Client VPN is user-to-network for individual remote access Thinking VPC Peering is transitive — it's not; Transit Gateway is needed for transitive routing between VPCs
Encryption IPsec encryption (AES-256) Not encrypted by default — data travels over private connection but in cleartext; add Site-to-Site VPN over DX for encryption Encrypted in transit via TLS — traffic stays on AWS network; no internet exposure TLS 1.2+ encryption Not encrypted (private AWS backbone)
Bandwidth Up to 1.25 Gbps per tunnel (2 tunnels = 2.5 Gbps) 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, or 100 Gbps dedicated connections; sub-1Gbps via partner connections Scales with the underlying ENI — up to 100 Gbps per AZ with multiple endpoints; no fixed bandwidth cap Up to 1 Gbps per client Up to 50 Gbps per attachment
Latency Variable (internet-dependent) Consistent low latency — dedicated private connection bypasses internet routing variability Very low — traffic stays within AWS network; same-AZ latency is sub-millisecond Variable (internet-dependent) Low latency (AWS backbone)
Setup Time Minutes Weeks to months — requires physical cross-connect at a DX location or partner provisioning Minutes — create VPC endpoint via console/CLI; no physical infrastructure needed Hours Hours
Cost Model Hourly per VPN connection + data transfer Port-hour fee + data transfer out; no data transfer fee for data coming into AWS over DX Per VPC endpoint per AZ per hour + per GB data processed through the endpoint Hourly per VPN connection + association fee Hourly per attachment + data processing
High Availability Two tunnels per connection (automatic) Single connection is single point of failure; use redundant connections at different DX locations for HA Deploy endpoints in multiple AZs; service provider must also be multi-AZ for full HA Multi-AZ redundancy Multi-AZ by default
Use Case: Remote Workers N/A (site-to-site only) Not suitable — DX is site-to-site; remote workers should use Client VPN or VPN through DX Not directly — PrivateLink accesses services from within VPC; remote workers need VPN first to reach VPC Purpose-built for remote user access N/A (infrastructure, not user access)
Use Case: Access AWS Services Through VPC Yes — public VIF accesses all AWS public endpoints; transit VIF via Transit Gateway for broader access Primary use case — private access to AWS services (S3, SageMaker, etc.) and third-party services from within VPC Through VPC Through attached VPCs
Use Case: VPC-to-VPC Possible but not recommended Yes — via Direct Connect Gateway + Transit Gateway for multi-VPC connectivity Yes — expose services in one VPC to consumers in another VPC via endpoint services Not designed for VPC-to-VPC — Client VPN is for individual user access to a single VPC Preferred method - central hub
Requires Public IP Yes (customer gateway needs public IP) No — uses private IP addressing; public VIF allows access to AWS public IPs over private connection No — entirely private IP-based; endpoints get private IPs within your VPC subnets No (uses endpoint in VPC) No
Supports BGP Optional (dynamic routing) Yes — BGP is required for route advertisements on all Direct Connect virtual interfaces No — PrivateLink uses DNS-based resolution, not BGP routing No Yes (with Direct Connect Gateway)
Cross-Region Support Single region per connection Yes — via Direct Connect Gateway for multi-region VPC access from a single DX connection Limited — endpoints are regional; cross-region requires peering or Transit Gateway + endpoint in each region Single region per endpoint Cross-region peering available
⚠️ Exam Tips

VPC Endpoints keep traffic within AWS network — Gateway endpoints (S3/DynamoDB) are free

Interface Endpoints (PrivateLink) cost money but support most AWS services

Direct Connect provides dedicated private network connection from on-premises to AWS

Site-to-Site VPN provides encrypted tunnel over public internet to AWS VPC

For the exam: 'private access to S3' = Gateway Endpoint; 'dedicated connection' = Direct Connect

Site-to-Site VPN
Site-to-Site VPN is the quicker, internet-based way to securely connect on-premises networks to AWS. It is good when you need encryption fast and can tolerate internet-based variability.
AWS Direct Connect
Direct Connect provides a dedicated private network connection into AWS for more consistent connectivity characteristics. On the exam, it is the premium answer for predictable private hybrid connectivity.
AWS PrivateLink
PrivateLink exposes services privately between VPCs and accounts without opening broad network paths. It is the exam clue for private service consumption without full mesh peering or internet exposure.
Site-to-Site VPN
Connection style: Encrypted tunnel over public internet | Use Case: Site-to-AWS: Quick, encrypted connection over internet | Typical exam clue: Need secure connection quickly and cheaply | Scope: Network-to-network | Internet dependency: Yes, underlying path uses internet | Common trap: Assuming it behaves like a dedicated circuit | Encryption: IPsec encryption (AES-256) | Bandwidth: Up to 1.25 Gbps per tunnel (2 tunnels = 2.5 Gbps) | Latency: Variable (internet-dependent) | Setup Time: Minutes | Cost Model: Hourly per VPN connection + data transfer | High Availability: Two tunnels per connection (automatic) | Use Case: Remote Workers: N/A (site-to-site only) | Use Case: Access AWS Services: Through VPC | Use Case: VPC-to-VPC: Possible but not recommended | Requires Public IP: Yes (customer gateway needs public IP) | Supports BGP: Optional (dynamic routing) | Cross-Region Support: Single region per connection
AWS Direct Connect
