Connection style: Shared physical connection via partner | Use Case: Site-to-AWS: High-bandwidth without dedicated infrastructure | Typical exam clue: Need consistent private hybrid connection | Scope: Network-to-network | Internet dependency: No, dedicated connection | Common trap: Assuming it automatically encrypts like VPN | Encryption: Not encrypted by default — data travels over private connection but in cleartext; add Site-to-Site VPN over DX for encryption | Bandwidth: 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, or 100 Gbps dedicated connections; sub-1Gbps via partner connections | Latency: Consistent low latency — dedicated private connection bypasses internet routing variability | Setup Time: Weeks to months — requires physical cross-connect at a DX location or partner provisioning | Cost Model: Port-hour fee + data transfer out; no data transfer fee for data coming into AWS over DX | High Availability: Single connection is single point of failure; use redundant connections at different DX locations for HA | Use Case: Remote Workers: Not suitable — DX is site-to-site; remote workers should use Client VPN or VPN through DX | Use Case: Access AWS Services: Yes — public VIF accesses all AWS public endpoints; transit VIF via Transit Gateway for broader access | Use Case: VPC-to-VPC: Yes — via Direct Connect Gateway + Transit Gateway for multi-VPC connectivity | Requires Public IP: No — uses private IP addressing; public VIF allows access to AWS public IPs over private connection | Supports BGP: Yes — BGP is required for route advertisements on all Direct Connect virtual interfaces | Cross-Region Support: Yes — via Direct Connect Gateway for multi-region VPC access from a single DX connection
AWS PrivateLink
Connection style: Private connectivity to AWS services or VPCs | Use Case: Site-to-AWS: Consume a specific service privately | Typical exam clue: Need private access to service endpoints | Scope: Service-to-consumer | Internet dependency: No public internet exposure for service access | Common trap: Assuming it replaces all VPC-to-VPC networking | Encryption: Encrypted in transit via TLS — traffic stays on AWS network; no internet exposure | Bandwidth: Scales with the underlying ENI — up to 100 Gbps per AZ with multiple endpoints; no fixed bandwidth cap | Latency: Very low — traffic stays within AWS network; same-AZ latency is sub-millisecond | Setup Time: Minutes — create VPC endpoint via console/CLI; no physical infrastructure needed | Cost Model: Per VPC endpoint per AZ per hour + per GB data processed through the endpoint | High Availability: Deploy endpoints in multiple AZs; service provider must also be multi-AZ for full HA | Use Case: Remote Workers: Not directly — PrivateLink accesses services from within VPC; remote workers need VPN first to reach VPC | Use Case: Access AWS Services: Primary use case — private access to AWS services (S3, SageMaker, etc.) and third-party services from within VPC | Use Case: VPC-to-VPC: Yes — expose services in one VPC to consumers in another VPC via endpoint services | Requires Public IP: No — entirely private IP-based; endpoints get private IPs within your VPC subnets | Supports BGP: No — PrivateLink uses DNS-based resolution, not BGP routing | Cross-Region Support: Limited — endpoints are regional; cross-region requires peering or Transit Gateway + endpoint in each region
Client VPN
Connection style: Encrypted tunnel for individual remote users | Use Case: Site-to-AWS: N/A (user access, not site) | Typical exam clue: 'Remote workers need secure access to VPC', 'employees working from home', 'laptop to VPC connectivity' | Scope: Remote user to VPC — individual user connections from any internet location to VPC resources | Internet dependency: Yes — client connects over the public internet using OpenVPN protocol with TLS encryption | Common trap: Confusing with Site-to-Site VPN (network-to-network) — Client VPN is user-to-network for individual remote access | Encryption: TLS 1.2+ encryption | Bandwidth: Up to 1 Gbps per client | Latency: Variable (internet-dependent) | Setup Time: Hours | Cost Model: Hourly per VPN connection + association fee | High Availability: Multi-AZ redundancy | Use Case: Remote Workers: Purpose-built for remote user access | Use Case: Access AWS Services: Through VPC | Use Case: VPC-to-VPC: Not designed for VPC-to-VPC — Client VPN is for individual user access to a single VPC | Requires Public IP: No (uses endpoint in VPC) | Supports BGP: No | Cross-Region Support: Single region per endpoint
Transit Gateway
Connection style: VPC and on-premises network hub | Use Case: Site-to-AWS: Hub for multiple VPCs and on-premises | Typical exam clue: 'Connect multiple VPCs', 'hub-and-spoke topology', 'transitive routing between VPCs and on-premises' | Scope: Regional hub — connects multiple VPCs, VPNs, and Direct Connect gateways in a hub-and-spoke model | Internet dependency: No for VPC-to-VPC; Site-to-Site VPN attachments traverse internet; Direct Connect attachments are private | Common trap: Thinking VPC Peering is transitive — it's not; Transit Gateway is needed for transitive routing between VPCs | Encryption: Not encrypted (private AWS backbone) | Bandwidth: Up to 50 Gbps per attachment | Latency: Low latency (AWS backbone) | Setup Time: Hours | Cost Model: Hourly per attachment + data processing | High Availability: Multi-AZ by default | Use Case: Remote Workers: N/A (infrastructure, not user access) | Use Case: Access AWS Services: Through attached VPCs | Use Case: VPC-to-VPC: Preferred method - central hub | Requires Public IP: No | Supports BGP: Yes (with Direct Connect Gateway) | Cross-Region Support: Cross-region peering available
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 1 and Domain 3 both touch private and secure connectivity. This table separates encrypted internet tunnels, dedicated private circuits, and private service exposure without traversing the public internet.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#105 Public Subnet vs Private Subnet Comparison
Networking subnet_types_comparison 2 cols 8 rows
▼

This table compares public and private subnets in AWS VPC. Public subnets have direct internet access via an Internet Gateway, making them suitable for web-facing resources. Private subnets do not have direct internet access, providing enhanced security for backend resources like databases and application servers.

Feature Public Subnet Private Subnet
Route to Internet Yes (via Internet Gateway) No (no IGW route)
Instance Types Web servers, load balancers, bastion hosts Database servers, backend servers, application servers
Security Groups More open to allow inbound internet traffic More restrictive, limited inbound access
IP Addressing Instances can have public IPs Instances use private IPs only
Outbound Internet Direct via IGW Via NAT Gateway (optional)
Accessibility Accessible from internet (with security group rules) Not directly accessible from internet
Use Cases Hosting websites, public APIs, bastion hosts Secure databases, backend services, internal apps
Security Level Lower (exposed to internet) Higher (isolated from internet)
⚠️ Exam Tips

Public subnet = route table has route to Internet Gateway; resources CAN have public IPs

Private subnet = no route to Internet Gateway; resources need NAT Gateway for outbound internet

Best practice: put web servers in public subnet, databases and application servers in private subnets

NAT Gateway allows private subnet resources to reach the internet for updates, not receive inbound

Bastion hosts or Session Manager provide secure access to private subnet instances

Public Subnet
Subnet with a route to an Internet Gateway allowing direct inbound and outbound internet connectivity for resources like web servers and load balancers.
Private Subnet
Subnet without a route to an Internet Gateway, isolating resources from direct internet access while allowing outbound connectivity via NAT Gateway if needed.
Public Subnet
Route to Internet: Yes (via Internet Gateway) | Instance Types: Web servers, load balancers, bastion hosts | Security Groups: More open to allow inbound internet traffic | IP Addressing: Instances can have public IPs | Outbound Internet: Direct via IGW | Accessibility: Accessible from internet (with security group rules) | Use Cases: Hosting websites, public APIs, bastion hosts | Security Level: Lower (exposed to internet)
Private Subnet
Route to Internet: No (no IGW route) | Instance Types: Database servers, backend servers, application servers | Security Groups: More restrictive, limited inbound access | IP Addressing: Instances use private IPs only | Outbound Internet: Via NAT Gateway (optional) | Accessibility: Not directly accessible from internet | Use Cases: Secure databases, backend services, internal apps | Security Level: Higher (isolated from internet)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#106 VPC Components Comparison
Networking vpc_components_comparison 7 cols 13 rows
▼

VPC components enable private connectivity, internet access, and network segmentation within AWS. Understanding the differences between VPC endpoints, NAT options, and VPC connectivity methods is essential for designing secure and cost-effective network architectures.

Feature VPC Gateway Endpoint VPC Interface Endpoint NAT Gateway NAT Instance VPC Peering Transit Gateway VPC Sharing
Purpose Private access to S3 and DynamoDB Private access to most AWS services + third-party Outbound internet access from private subnets Outbound internet access from private subnets (legacy) Direct VPC-to-VPC connectivity Hub-and-spoke VPC and network connectivity Share VPC across multiple AWS accounts
OSI Layer Layer 3 (uses route table) Layer 3/4 (uses ENI with private IP) Layer 3/4 Layer 3/4 Layer 3 Layer 3 N/A (resource sharing)
Availability Zones Highly available across all AZs automatically One ENI per AZ you specify One per AZ (not highly available across AZs) One per AZ (requires HA setup) N/A (connection-level) Highly available across all AZs N/A (resource sharing)
Bandwidth No bandwidth limit (scales automatically) Up to 10 Gbps per AZ (burstable) Up to 45 Gbps (burstable) Depends on EC2 instance size No bandwidth limit Up to 50 Gbps per attachment Shared VPC resources use host account bandwidth limits
Pricing Model Free (no hourly charge, data transfer only) Hourly per ENI + data processed Hourly per NAT GW + data processing charge EC2 instance cost only Data transfer charge only (no hourly fee) Hourly per attachment + data processed No additional charge
Highly Available Yes (automatic) Yes (with multiple AZs) No (per AZ, need multiple for HA) No (requires manual HA setup) Yes (uses AWS backbone) Yes (multi-AZ by default) Depends on host account VPC design; no built-in HA mechanism
Setup Complexity Simple (add route to route table) Moderate (ENI per AZ + security groups) Simple (replace route in route table) Complex (EC2 setup, scripts for HA) Simple (accept connection, update routes) Moderate (create attachments, update routes) Moderate (RAM setup, IAM permissions)
Cross-Region Support No (single region only) No (single region only) No (single region only) No (single region only) Yes (inter-region peering available) Yes (inter-region peering) No (same region only)
Transitive Routing No — routes only within the VPC to S3/DynamoDB No — endpoint is local to the VPC No — NAT Gateway does not support transitive routing No — NAT Instance does not support transitive routing No (not transitive) Yes (fully transitive) No — participant accounts cannot route transitively through host VPC
Security Groups Supported No (uses endpoint policies only) Yes (security groups on ENIs) No — uses NACL only; cannot attach security groups Yes (security groups on EC2) N/A (separate SGs per VPC) N/A (separate SGs per VPC) Yes — participant accounts manage their own security groups in shared subnets
Overlapping CIDRs Not applicable — operates within a single VPC Not applicable — operates within a single VPC Not applicable — operates within a single VPC Not applicable — operates within a single VPC No (CIDRs must not overlap) No (CIDRs must not overlap) Not supported — shared VPC subnets must not have overlapping CIDRs
Services Supported S3 and DynamoDB only Most AWS services + AWS Marketplace services All internet services (outbound only) All internet services (outbound only) All services in peered VPCs All services in attached VPCs All services in shared VPC
AWS Recommendation Recommended for S3/DynamoDB (free) Recommended for other services Recommended over NAT Instances Not recommended (use NAT Gateway) Use for simple 2-VPC connections Recommended for 3+ VPCs Use for centralized network management
⚠️ Exam Tips

Gateway Endpoints are FREE for S3 and DynamoDB — always use them instead of Interface Endpoints for these services

Interface Endpoints (PrivateLink) support 100+ AWS services and third-party SaaS

NAT Gateway is managed and highly available; NAT Instance is self-managed and single point of failure

Transit Gateway enables transitive routing — connect many VPCs without individual peering connections

VPC Peering does NOT support transitive routing — A<>B and B<>C does not mean A<>C

VPC Gateway Endpoint
Free endpoint for accessing S3 and DynamoDB privately from within your VPC. Traffic stays on the AWS network, never touching the internet. No additional charges beyond standard data transfer.
VPC Interface Endpoint
Powered by AWS PrivateLink, creates an ENI in your subnet for private access to AWS services and third-party SaaS. Charges per hour and per GB processed.
NAT Gateway
Managed, highly available network address translation service enabling private subnet resources to access the internet for updates and patches while preventing inbound internet connections.
NAT Instance
Self-managed EC2 instance performing NAT. Less recommended than NAT Gateway but may be cheaper for low-traffic scenarios. Requires manual HA configuration and maintenance.
VPC Peering
One-to-one private network connection between two VPCs. Traffic stays on AWS backbone. Does not support transitive routing — each VPC pair needs its own peering connection.
Transit Gateway
A hub-and-spoke network transit hub that connects VPCs and on-premises networks through a central gateway. Supports transitive routing, simplifying complex multi-VPC architectures.
VPC Sharing
Allows multiple AWS accounts to create resources in shared VPCs using AWS Resource Access Manager. Reduces VPC count and simplifies network management in multi-account environments.
VPC Gateway Endpoint
Purpose: Private access to S3 and DynamoDB | OSI Layer: Layer 3 (uses route table) | Availability Zones: Highly available across all AZs automatically | Bandwidth: No bandwidth limit (scales automatically) | Pricing Model: Free (no hourly charge, data transfer only) | Highly Available: Yes (automatic) | Setup Complexity: Simple (add route to route table) | Cross-Region Support: No (single region only) | Transitive Routing: No — routes only within the VPC to S3/DynamoDB | Security Groups Supported: No (uses endpoint policies only) | Overlapping CIDRs: Not applicable — operates within a single VPC | Services Supported: S3 and DynamoDB only | AWS Recommendation: Recommended for S3/DynamoDB (free)
VPC Interface Endpoint
Purpose: Private access to most AWS services + third-party | OSI Layer: Layer 3/4 (uses ENI with private IP) | Availability Zones: One ENI per AZ you specify | Bandwidth: Up to 10 Gbps per AZ (burstable) | Pricing Model: Hourly per ENI + data processed | Highly Available: Yes (with multiple AZs) | Setup Complexity: Moderate (ENI per AZ + security groups) | Cross-Region Support: No (single region only) | Transitive Routing: No — endpoint is local to the VPC | Security Groups Supported: Yes (security groups on ENIs) | Overlapping CIDRs: Not applicable — operates within a single VPC | Services Supported: Most AWS services + AWS Marketplace services | AWS Recommendation: Recommended for other services
NAT Gateway
Purpose: Outbound internet access from private subnets | OSI Layer: Layer 3/4 | Availability Zones: One per AZ (not highly available across AZs) | Bandwidth: Up to 45 Gbps (burstable) | Pricing Model: Hourly per NAT GW + data processing charge | Highly Available: No (per AZ, need multiple for HA) | Setup Complexity: Simple (replace route in route table) | Cross-Region Support: No (single region only) | Transitive Routing: No — NAT Gateway does not support transitive routing | Security Groups Supported: No — uses NACL only; cannot attach security groups | Overlapping CIDRs: Not applicable — operates within a single VPC | Services Supported: All internet services (outbound only) | AWS Recommendation: Recommended over NAT Instances
NAT Instance
Purpose: Outbound internet access from private subnets (legacy) | OSI Layer: Layer 3/4 | Availability Zones: One per AZ (requires HA setup) | Bandwidth: Depends on EC2 instance size | Pricing Model: EC2 instance cost only | Highly Available: No (requires manual HA setup) | Setup Complexity: Complex (EC2 setup, scripts for HA) | Cross-Region Support: No (single region only) | Transitive Routing: No — NAT Instance does not support transitive routing | Security Groups Supported: Yes (security groups on EC2) | Overlapping CIDRs: Not applicable — operates within a single VPC | Services Supported: All internet services (outbound only) | AWS Recommendation: Not recommended (use NAT Gateway)
VPC Peering
Purpose: Direct VPC-to-VPC connectivity | OSI Layer: Layer 3 | Availability Zones: N/A (connection-level) | Bandwidth: No bandwidth limit | Pricing Model: Data transfer charge only (no hourly fee) | Highly Available: Yes (uses AWS backbone) | Setup Complexity: Simple (accept connection, update routes) | Cross-Region Support: Yes (inter-region peering available) | Transitive Routing: No (not transitive) | Security Groups Supported: N/A (separate SGs per VPC) | Overlapping CIDRs: No (CIDRs must not overlap) | Services Supported: All services in peered VPCs | AWS Recommendation: Use for simple 2-VPC connections
Transit Gateway
Purpose: Hub-and-spoke VPC and network connectivity | OSI Layer: Layer 3 | Availability Zones: Highly available across all AZs | Bandwidth: Up to 50 Gbps per attachment | Pricing Model: Hourly per attachment + data processed | Highly Available: Yes (multi-AZ by default) | Setup Complexity: Moderate (create attachments, update routes) | Cross-Region Support: Yes (inter-region peering) | Transitive Routing: Yes (fully transitive) | Security Groups Supported: N/A (separate SGs per VPC) | Overlapping CIDRs: No (CIDRs must not overlap) | Services Supported: All services in attached VPCs | AWS Recommendation: Recommended for 3+ VPCs
VPC Sharing
Purpose: Share VPC across multiple AWS accounts | OSI Layer: N/A (resource sharing) | Availability Zones: N/A (resource sharing) | Bandwidth: Shared VPC resources use host account bandwidth limits | Pricing Model: No additional charge | Highly Available: Depends on host account VPC design; no built-in HA mechanism | Setup Complexity: Moderate (RAM setup, IAM permissions) | Cross-Region Support: No (same region only) | Transitive Routing: No — participant accounts cannot route transitively through host VPC | Security Groups Supported: Yes — participant accounts manage their own security groups in shared subnets | Overlapping CIDRs: Not supported — shared VPC subnets must not have overlapping CIDRs | Services Supported: All services in shared VPC | AWS Recommendation: Use for centralized network management
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#107 VPC Endpoints Comparison
Networking vpc_endpoints_comparison 2 cols 9 rows
▼

Comparison of VPC Gateway Endpoints vs Interface Endpoints for private access to AWS services

Feature Gateway Endpoint Interface Endpoint
Services Supported S3 and DynamoDB only 70+ AWS services including S3, DynamoDB, Kinesis, SNS, SQS, and many others
Connection Method Route table entries - no network interface created Elastic Network Interface (ENI) with private IP in subnet
Traffic Routing Via gateway prefix list in route table Via ENI with private IP address
Access Control IAM policies + bucket/volume policies Security groups attached to ENI + IAM policies
Pricing No additional charge Per hour + per GB data processed
Availability Regional - access from any AZ without additional cost AZ-specific (one per AZ recommended for HA) + cross-AZ charges
DNS Configuration Uses public DNS with endpoint URL Can use private DNS to override public endpoints
Use Case Simple, cost-effective private access to S3 or DynamoDB Private access to services requiring ENI, or when gateway endpoint unavailable
VPC Endpoint Services Uses prefix lists (target for routes) Powered by AWS PrivateLink; enables custom endpoint services
💡 Mnemonics

G.I - Gateway for Internal (S3/DynamoDB) free, Interface for more services

⚠️ Exam Tips

Gateway endpoints are FREE - always prefer them for S3 and DynamoDB when possible

Interface endpoints are required for services other than S3 and DynamoDB

Both endpoints keep traffic within AWS network, avoiding internet traversal

Interface endpoints support private DNS which can override public service endpoints

For high availability with interface endpoints, create one per AZ

Gateway endpoints use prefix lists in route tables - not security groups

Gateway Endpoint
A VPC endpoint that routes traffic to S3 or DynamoDB through AWS's internal network without traversing the internet, using route table entries.
Interface Endpoint
An VPC endpoint powered by AWS PrivateLink that creates an elastic network interface with a private IP address for accessing AWS services.
Gateway Endpoint
Services Supported: S3 and DynamoDB only | Connection Method: Route table entries - no network interface created | Traffic Routing: Via gateway prefix list in route table | Access Control: IAM policies + bucket/volume policies | Pricing: No additional charge | Availability: Regional - access from any AZ without additional cost | DNS Configuration: Uses public DNS with endpoint URL | Use Case: Simple, cost-effective private access to S3 or DynamoDB | VPC Endpoint Services: Uses prefix lists (target for routes)
Interface Endpoint
Services Supported: 70+ AWS services including S3, DynamoDB, Kinesis, SNS, SQS, and many others | Connection Method: Elastic Network Interface (ENI) with private IP in subnet | Traffic Routing: Via ENI with private IP address | Access Control: Security groups attached to ENI + IAM policies | Pricing: Per hour + per GB data processed | Availability: AZ-specific (one per AZ recommended for HA) + cross-AZ charges | DNS Configuration: Can use private DNS to override public endpoints | Use Case: Private access to services requiring ENI, or when gateway endpoint unavailable | VPC Endpoint Services: Powered by AWS PrivateLink; enables custom endpoint services
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

DNS & Routing 3 tables
#108 AWS Route 53 Routing Policies
DNS & Routing SAA-009_2 7 cols 17 rows
▼

Comparison of Amazon Route 53 DNS routing policies for different use cases

Route 53 offers multiple routing policies to control how DNS queries are answered. Each policy serves different use cases from simple single-resource routing to complex latency-based or geographic routing. Understanding when to use each policy is essential for both exams.

Feature Simple Weighted Latency-based Failover Geolocation Geoproximity Multivalue Answer
Purpose Single resource routing Traffic distribution by weight Route to lowest latency Active-passive failover Route by user location Route traffic based on geographic distance between users and resources, with optional bias to shift traffic between regions Return multiple healthy IP addresses (up to 8) for DNS queries; provides simple DNS-level load balancing with health checks
Route Based On Single resource, no routing logic Configured weight values (0-255) AWS region with lowest latency to user Health check status (primary vs secondary) Route based on user location (country/continent) Route based on resource location + optional bias Return multiple healthy records (basic load balancing)
Health Checks Required Optional - can associate health check but not required Optional - traffic only sent to healthy endpoints Optional - only sends to healthy endpoints with lowest latency Required - triggers failover when primary becomes unhealthy Optional (excludes unhealthy only if enabled) Optional (excludes unhealthy only if enabled) Required for health-based responses
DNS Record Type Multiple A records in single record set Separate record sets with weights Records tagged with AWS region Primary (with health check) + secondary record sets A, AAAA, CNAME, etc. Any record type — requires Route 53 Traffic Flow (traffic policies) A or AAAA records — returns up to 8 healthy records per query
Region Awareness No No Yes (AWS regions) Yes (health check) Yes (continents/countries) Yes — routes based on geographic proximity to AWS regions or custom coordinates; supports bias values No — region-agnostic; returns all healthy endpoints regardless of location
Multiple Endpoints Returns all IP addresses (client picks one randomly) Multiple resources with different weights Multiple resources with latency evaluation One primary + one secondary (can have multiple secondaries) Yes (location-based) Yes — routes to nearest endpoint; Traffic Flow policy defines resources and bias Yes — returns up to 8 healthy endpoints per DNS query
Traffic Splitting No Yes (percentage-based) No (automatic) No (all or nothing) No (location-based) Geographic proximity with bias — adjust bias values to shift more or less traffic to a region Even split — DNS returns multiple IPs and client picks one (roughly random)
Best For Simple websites Testing, canary deployments Multi-region apps HA, DR scenarios Regulatory compliance, CDN routing Multi-region deployments where you want traffic routed to the nearest region with ability to fine-tune traffic distribution via bias Simple DNS-level load balancing across multiple endpoints with health checking; alternative to simple routing with health checks
Multiple Records Returned No (single record only) No (one selected by weight) No — returns only the record with lowest latency to the user No (primary or secondary) No (one based on location) No (one based on proximity) Yes (up to 8 healthy records)
Default Record Support Not applicable — Simple routing returns all values in a single record No default needed No — only latency-based selection; combine with Failover for default behavior N/A (primary/secondary model) Default record for unmatched locations No default needed No default needed
Traffic Flow Required No No No — can be configured without Traffic Flow using standard latency alias records No No Yes (requires Traffic Flow) No
Use with Alias Records Yes Yes Yes — commonly used with Alias records pointing to regional resources (ALB, CloudFront, etc.) Yes Yes Yes No (not supported)
Typical Scenario Development environment, single server answer A/B testing, blue-green deployments, gradual migrations Global application with resources in multiple AWS regions; route each user to the region with lowest network latency Primary in us-east-1 fails over to us-west-2 Content localization, compliance requirements Multi-region deployment where you need fine-grained control over traffic distribution using geographic bias Basic load balancing across multiple web servers
Record Set Configuration One record per name Multiple records with assigned weights One record per region with same name; Route 53 selects based on latency measurements Primary + Secondary record pair Multiple records with location specified Multiple records with coordinates/bias Multiple records, all returned if healthy
Cost Consideration Standard query pricing Standard query pricing Standard Route 53 pricing; no additional cost over other routing policies Standard query pricing + health check costs Standard query pricing Requires Traffic Flow (additional cost) Standard query pricing + health check costs
Primary Use Single resource routing — basic DNS resolution without health checks or complex logic Distribute traffic by percentage — A/B testing, blue/green deployments, gradual migrations Route to lowest-latency region — optimize performance for global users Active-passive high availability — automatic failover to secondary when primary is unhealthy Compliance and localization — route users to region-specific endpoints based on their country/continent Proximity-based routing with traffic bias — route to nearest region with ability to tune traffic distribution Simple DNS load balancing — return multiple healthy IPs for client-side selection
Geographic Control None — no geographic awareness None — traffic split by weight regardless of user location Indirect — routes by network latency which correlates with geography None — failover is health-based, not geography-based Strict — routes based on exact user continent/country/state; requires default record for unmatched locations Flexible — routes by geographic proximity with adjustable bias (-99 to +99) to shift traffic between regions None — returns all healthy endpoints regardless of user location
💡 Mnemonics

SIMPLE-W-LATENCY-FAILOVER-GEO: Simple = Single, Weighted = distribution, Latency = performance, Failover = backup, Geo = location

One endpoint? Simple. Need to split traffic? Weighted. Need lowest latency? Latency-based. Need DR? Failover. Need location routing? Geolocation.

S.W.L.F - Simple is straightforward, Weighted splits traffic, Latency optimizes speed, Failover handles disaster

⚠️ Exam Tips

Simple routing doesn't support health checks (optional)

Weighted routing is for controlled traffic distribution (10%/90%)

Latency-based routes to the region with lowest network latency

Failover routing requires health checks for automatic switching

Geolocation routes based on user location, not latency

Geoproximity routing uses bias to shift traffic toward resources

Simple routing doesn't support health checks - use with caution for production

Weighted routing weights must sum to 100 for predictable distribution

Latency-based routing can improve performance but doesn't guarantee geographic routing

Failover routing requires health checks - understand Health Check configurations

For geo-restrictions, use Route 53 Geolocation routing (different from latency)

Simple Routing
The default routing policy that routes traffic to a single resource. Best for single-server websites or when Route 53 responds to DNS queries using only the records you've created without any special routing logic.
Weighted Routing
Routes traffic to multiple resources in proportions that you specify. Ideal for A/B testing, gradual migrations, or load balancing across resources in the same or different regions.
Latency-based Routing
Routes traffic to the AWS region that provides the lowest latency for the user. Best for global applications where performance is critical and resources are deployed in multiple regions.
Failover Routing
Routes traffic to a primary resource when it's healthy and to a secondary resource when the primary is unhealthy. Essential for active-passive disaster recovery configurations.
Geolocation Routing
Routes traffic based on the geographic location of the user (continent, country, or US state). Ideal for compliance requirements, content localization, or restricting content distribution.
Simple
Purpose: Single resource routing | Route Based On: Single resource, no routing logic | Health Checks Required: Optional - can associate health check but not required | DNS Record Type: Multiple A records in single record set | Region Awareness: No | Multiple Endpoints: Returns all IP addresses (client picks one randomly) | Traffic Splitting: No | Best For: Simple websites | Multiple Records Returned: No (single record only) | Default Record Support: Not applicable — Simple routing returns all values in a single record | Traffic Flow Required: No | Use with Alias Records: Yes | Typical Scenario: Development environment, single server answer | Record Set Configuration: One record per name | Cost Consideration: Standard query pricing | Primary Use: Single resource routing — basic DNS resolution without health checks or complex logic | Geographic Control: None — no geographic awareness
Weighted
Purpose: Traffic distribution by weight | Route Based On: Configured weight values (0-255) | Health Checks Required: Optional - traffic only sent to healthy endpoints | DNS Record Type: Separate record sets with weights | Region Awareness: No | Multiple Endpoints: Multiple resources with different weights | Traffic Splitting: Yes (percentage-based) | Best For: Testing, canary deployments | Multiple Records Returned: No (one selected by weight) | Default Record Support: No default needed | Traffic Flow Required: No | Use with Alias Records: Yes | Typical Scenario: A/B testing, blue-green deployments, gradual migrations | Record Set Configuration: Multiple records with assigned weights | Cost Consideration: Standard query pricing | Primary Use: Distribute traffic by percentage — A/B testing, blue/green deployments, gradual migrations | Geographic Control: None — traffic split by weight regardless of user location
Latency-based
Purpose: Route to lowest latency | Route Based On: AWS region with lowest latency to user | Health Checks Required: Optional - only sends to healthy endpoints with lowest latency | DNS Record Type: Records tagged with AWS region | Region Awareness: Yes (AWS regions) | Multiple Endpoints: Multiple resources with latency evaluation | Traffic Splitting: No (automatic) | Best For: Multi-region apps | Multiple Records Returned: No — returns only the record with lowest latency to the user | Default Record Support: No — only latency-based selection; combine with Failover for default behavior | Traffic Flow Required: No — can be configured without Traffic Flow using standard latency alias records | Use with Alias Records: Yes — commonly used with Alias records pointing to regional resources (ALB, CloudFront, etc.) | Typical Scenario: Global application with resources in multiple AWS regions; route each user to the region with lowest network latency | Record Set Configuration: One record per region with same name; Route 53 selects based on latency measurements | Cost Consideration: Standard Route 53 pricing; no additional cost over other routing policies | Primary Use: Route to lowest-latency region — optimize performance for global users | Geographic Control: Indirect — routes by network latency which correlates with geography
Failover
Purpose: Active-passive failover | Route Based On: Health check status (primary vs secondary) | Health Checks Required: Required - triggers failover when primary becomes unhealthy | DNS Record Type: Primary (with health check) + secondary record sets | Region Awareness: Yes (health check) | Multiple Endpoints: One primary + one secondary (can have multiple secondaries) | Traffic Splitting: No (all or nothing) | Best For: HA, DR scenarios | Multiple Records Returned: No (primary or secondary) | Default Record Support: N/A (primary/secondary model) | Traffic Flow Required: No | Use with Alias Records: Yes | Typical Scenario: Primary in us-east-1 fails over to us-west-2 | Record Set Configuration: Primary + Secondary record pair | Cost Consideration: Standard query pricing + health check costs | Primary Use: Active-passive high availability — automatic failover to secondary when primary is unhealthy | Geographic Control: None — failover is health-based, not geography-based
Geolocation
Purpose: Route by user location | Route Based On: Route based on user location (country/continent) | Health Checks Required: Optional (excludes unhealthy only if enabled) | DNS Record Type: A, AAAA, CNAME, etc. | Region Awareness: Yes (continents/countries) | Multiple Endpoints: Yes (location-based) | Traffic Splitting: No (location-based) | Best For: Regulatory compliance, CDN routing | Multiple Records Returned: No (one based on location) | Default Record Support: Default record for unmatched locations | Traffic Flow Required: No | Use with Alias Records: Yes | Typical Scenario: Content localization, compliance requirements | Record Set Configuration: Multiple records with location specified | Cost Consideration: Standard query pricing | Primary Use: Compliance and localization — route users to region-specific endpoints based on their country/continent | Geographic Control: Strict — routes based on exact user continent/country/state; requires default record for unmatched locations
Geoproximity
Purpose: Route traffic based on geographic distance between users and resources, with optional bias to shift traffic between regions | Route Based On: Route based on resource location + optional bias | Health Checks Required: Optional (excludes unhealthy only if enabled) | DNS Record Type: Any record type — requires Route 53 Traffic Flow (traffic policies) | Region Awareness: Yes — routes based on geographic proximity to AWS regions or custom coordinates; supports bias values | Multiple Endpoints: Yes — routes to nearest endpoint; Traffic Flow policy defines resources and bias | Traffic Splitting: Geographic proximity with bias — adjust bias values to shift more or less traffic to a region | Best For: Multi-region deployments where you want traffic routed to the nearest region with ability to fine-tune traffic distribution via bias | Multiple Records Returned: No (one based on proximity) | Default Record Support: No default needed | Traffic Flow Required: Yes (requires Traffic Flow) | Use with Alias Records: Yes | Typical Scenario: Multi-region deployment where you need fine-grained control over traffic distribution using geographic bias | Record Set Configuration: Multiple records with coordinates/bias | Cost Consideration: Requires Traffic Flow (additional cost) | Primary Use: Proximity-based routing with traffic bias — route to nearest region with ability to tune traffic distribution | Geographic Control: Flexible — routes by geographic proximity with adjustable bias (-99 to +99) to shift traffic between regions
Multivalue Answer
Purpose: Return multiple healthy IP addresses (up to 8) for DNS queries; provides simple DNS-level load balancing with health checks | Route Based On: Return multiple healthy records (basic load balancing) | Health Checks Required: Required for health-based responses | DNS Record Type: A or AAAA records — returns up to 8 healthy records per query | Region Awareness: No — region-agnostic; returns all healthy endpoints regardless of location | Multiple Endpoints: Yes — returns up to 8 healthy endpoints per DNS query | Traffic Splitting: Even split — DNS returns multiple IPs and client picks one (roughly random) | Best For: Simple DNS-level load balancing across multiple endpoints with health checking; alternative to simple routing with health checks | Multiple Records Returned: Yes (up to 8 healthy records) | Default Record Support: No default needed | Traffic Flow Required: No | Use with Alias Records: No (not supported) | Typical Scenario: Basic load balancing across multiple web servers | Record Set Configuration: Multiple records, all returned if healthy | Cost Consideration: Standard query pricing + health check costs | Primary Use: Simple DNS load balancing — return multiple healthy IPs for client-side selection | Geographic Control: None — returns all healthy endpoints regardless of user location
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#109 CloudFront vs Global Accelerator vs Route 53
DNS & Routing SAA-010_2 3 cols 6 rows
▼

These three services all help users around the world access your application faster, but they work in completely different ways. CloudFront caches content at the edge. Global Accelerator routes TCP/UDP traffic over AWS's private network for speed. Route 53 is DNS with intelligent routing policies. The exam frequently tests CloudFront vs Global Accelerator — know the caching distinction.

Feature Amazon CloudFront AWS Global Accelerator Amazon Route 53
Core Technology CDN — content caching at edge locations Anycast IP + AWS private backbone routing DNS — domain-to-IP resolution with routing intelligence
Does It Cache? YES — caches content at 400+ edge locations worldwide NO — purely network acceleration, no caching No — DNS only, no content caching
Static IP? No — DNS name (d1234.cloudfront.net) YES — 2 static Anycast IPs provided No — DNS names
DDoS Protection Shield Standard included Shield Standard included Shield Standard included
Best For Static websites, video streaming, API caching, S3 content distribution TCP/UDP applications, gaming, VoIP, fast regional failover DNS management, health checks, latency/geo/weighted/failover routing
Failover Capability Via origin failover groups FAST — reroutes in < 30 seconds using health checks Via health checks + failover routing policies
⚠️ Exam Tips

CloudFront = content delivery (caching at edge); Global Accelerator = network acceleration (no caching)

CloudFront is best for HTTP/HTTPS content delivery; Global Accelerator works for TCP/UDP traffic

Route 53 is DNS — it routes users to the right endpoint but doesn’t accelerate content delivery

Global Accelerator provides static anycast IPs that route to the nearest healthy endpoint

For the exam: 'static content caching' = CloudFront; 'non-HTTP acceleration' = Global Accelerator

Amazon CloudFront
CloudFront is AWS's CDN (Content Delivery Network) — it CACHES content (images, videos, HTML, JavaScript) at edge locations close to users, so repeat requests are served locally without going back to the origin. It is the best choice for improving performance of cacheable, web-based content.
AWS Global Accelerator
Global Accelerator routes your traffic through AWS's private global network from the nearest edge location to your backend, bypassing the public internet. Unlike CloudFront, it does NOT cache — it just makes the network path faster for TCP/UDP traffic. Ideal for non-HTTP use cases requiring consistent low latency.
Amazon Route 53
Route 53 is AWS's highly available DNS service — it translates domain names to IP addresses and supports intelligent routing policies (latency-based, geolocation, weighted, failover). It is the entry point for global traffic distribution and disaster recovery failover.
Amazon CloudFront
Core Technology: CDN — content caching at edge locations | Does It Cache?: YES — caches content at 400+ edge locations worldwide | Static IP?: No — DNS name (d1234.cloudfront.net) | DDoS Protection: Shield Standard included | Best For: Static websites, video streaming, API caching, S3 content distribution | Failover Capability: Via origin failover groups
AWS Global Accelerator
Core Technology: Anycast IP + AWS private backbone routing | Does It Cache?: NO — purely network acceleration, no caching | Static IP?: YES — 2 static Anycast IPs provided | DDoS Protection: Shield Standard included | Best For: TCP/UDP applications, gaming, VoIP, fast regional failover | Failover Capability: FAST — reroutes in < 30 seconds using health checks
Amazon Route 53
Core Technology: DNS — domain-to-IP resolution with routing intelligence | Does It Cache?: No — DNS only, no content caching | Static IP?: No — DNS names | DDoS Protection: Shield Standard included | Best For: DNS management, health checks, latency/geo/weighted/failover routing | Failover Capability: Via health checks + failover routing policies
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#110 DNS and Routing Services Comparison
DNS & Routing dns_and_routing_services_comparison 3 cols 6 rows
▼

Comparing AWS DNS and traffic routing services

Feature Amazon Route 53 Amazon CloudFront AWS Global Accelerator
Primary Function DNS service - domain registration and resolution CDN - cache and deliver content globally Anycast routing - optimize traffic path
Caching No caching (DNS resolution only) Yes - edge locations cache content No caching (direct routing)
Use Case Domain registration, DNS routing, health checks Static content, APIs, media streaming Global application availability, gaming, VoIP
Latency Benefit Route to nearest region (latency routing) Deliver from nearest edge (cache hit) Faster path through AWS network
Static IP No (uses Route 53 name servers) Provides static IP via custom origins Yes - two static anycast IPs
DDoS Protection Basic DDoS protection AWS Shield Standard + WAF integration AWS Shield Standard (always on)
💡 Mnemonics

RCG = Route53 for DNS, CloudFront for CDN, Global Accelerator for performance

⚠️ Exam Tips

Route 53 supports multiple routing policies: latency, geolocation, weighted, failover

CloudFront caches content at edge locations - great for static assets

Global Accelerator uses AWS global network for faster routing

Use Route 53 health checks for DNS failover to backup resources

CloudFront integrates with AWS Shield and WAF for DDoS protection

Amazon Route 53
A highly available and scalable cloud DNS web service providing reliable and cost-effective routing of traffic to applications using various routing policies.
Amazon CloudFront
A content delivery network (CDN) that securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs to customers globally with low latency.
AWS Global Accelerator
A networking service that improves application availability and performance by optimizing the path from users to application endpoints.
Amazon Route 53
Primary Function: DNS service - domain registration and resolution | Caching: No caching (DNS resolution only) | Use Case: Domain registration, DNS routing, health checks | Latency Benefit: Route to nearest region (latency routing) | Static IP: No (uses Route 53 name servers) | DDoS Protection: Basic DDoS protection
Amazon CloudFront
Primary Function: CDN - cache and deliver content globally | Caching: Yes - edge locations cache content | Use Case: Static content, APIs, media streaming | Latency Benefit: Deliver from nearest edge (cache hit) | Static IP: Provides static IP via custom origins | DDoS Protection: AWS Shield Standard + WAF integration
AWS Global Accelerator
Primary Function: Anycast routing - optimize traffic path | Caching: No caching (direct routing) | Use Case: Global application availability, gaming, VoIP | Latency Benefit: Faster path through AWS network | Static IP: Yes - two static anycast IPs | DDoS Protection: AWS Shield Standard (always on)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

Edge & CDN 2 tables
#111 AWS Content Delivery and Edge Services
Edge & CDN SAA-015 4 cols 16 rows
▼

AWS offers multiple services for accelerating content delivery and improving application performance globally. Understanding the differences between CloudFront, Global Accelerator, and S3 Transfer Acceleration helps you choose the right service for caching, static content delivery, and dynamic application acceleration.

Feature Amazon CloudFront AWS Global Accelerator Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration AWS Edge Locations
Primary Purpose Content Delivery Network (CDN) with caching Network layer acceleration for any application Fast uploads to S3 buckets over long distances Edge compute and caching
Protocol Optimization HTTP/HTTPS, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, WebSocket TCP, UDP (any protocol) HTTP/HTTPS for S3 uploads Various
Global Infrastructure 450+ Points of Presence AWS global network backbone Edge locations Regional Edge Caches + PoPs
Use Case: Dynamic Content Good with caching disabled or short TTL Global application acceleration N/A - upload acceleration only Edge compute (Lambda@Edge)
Caching Capability Yes - edge caching with TTL control No - passes through to application No - upload acceleration Yes (CloudFront caching)
Origin Types Supported S3, EC2, ELB, API Gateway, MediaStore, custom HTTP servers EC2, ELB, Elastic IPs, Application Load Balancers S3 buckets only N/A - infrastructure concept
DDoS Protection AWS Shield Standard (free), Shield Advanced (paid) AWS Shield Standard (free), Shield Advanced (paid) Inherits S3 and CloudFront protection Shield Standard included
Pricing Model Data transfer out + HTTP requests + invalidation requests Fixed hourly fee + data transfer accelerator charge Data transfer acceleration charge (no hourly fee) Part of service pricing
Performance Gain 50-70% latency reduction Up to 60% latency improvement 50-500% faster uploads Location-dependent
OSI Layer Layer 7 (Application) — HTTP/HTTPS content delivery with caching Layer 4 (Transport) — TCP/UDP acceleration at the network level Layer 7 (Application) — HTTP-based accelerated S3 uploads Infrastructure — physical PoP locations used by CloudFront, Global Accelerator, Route 53, and other edge services
Static IP Addresses No — uses dynamic IP addresses (DNS-based); use Global Accelerator if static IPs needed Yes — provides two static anycast IPv4 addresses that serve as fixed entry points No — uses accelerated DNS endpoint (bucketname.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com) Not directly — edge locations have IPs but not customer-assignable static IPs
Edge Locations Yes — caches content at 450+ edge locations worldwide Yes — routes traffic through edge locations to AWS backbone network Yes — uses CloudFront edge locations to accelerate uploads to S3 This IS the infrastructure — 450+ PoPs globally used by other AWS edge services
Use Case: Gaming/Real-time Limited — optimized for HTTP/HTTPS; use WebSocket support for some real-time cases Excellent — TCP/UDP support ideal for gaming, VoIP, IoT with consistent low latency Not applicable — designed for S3 uploads only, not real-time communication Infrastructure supporting both CloudFront and Global Accelerator use cases
Use Case: Upload Acceleration Limited — primarily for downloads/delivery; use S3 Transfer Acceleration for uploads Possible — accelerates any TCP/UDP traffic including uploads to AWS endpoints Primary use case — accelerates S3 PUT operations over long distances via edge locations Infrastructure supporting S3 Transfer Acceleration and Global Accelerator uploads
SSL/TLS Termination Yes — terminates TLS at edge locations; supports custom SSL certificates via ACM No — passes through TCP/UDP connections; TLS termination happens at the origin Yes — uses HTTPS for accelerated uploads; TLS handled by S3 endpoint Depends on service — CloudFront terminates at edge; Global Accelerator passes through
Integration with WAF Yes — AWS WAF can be directly attached to CloudFront distributions for web application protection No — WAF cannot be attached directly; attach WAF to the ALB origin instead No — WAF integration not applicable for S3 upload acceleration Infrastructure level — WAF rules execute at edge locations when attached to CloudFront or ALB
💡 Mnemonics

CloudFront = CDN for content, Global Accelerator = Network speedup, Transfer Acceleration = S3 fast uploads, Edge = Global locations

Need content caching? CloudFront. Need network acceleration? Global Accelerator. Need faster S3 uploads? Transfer Acceleration.

⚠️ Exam Tips

CloudFront caches content at edge locations close to users

Global Accelerator uses AWS backbone for consistent performance

Transfer Acceleration is for uploading to S3, not downloading

CloudFront supports Lambda@Edge for edge compute

Global Accelerator provides static anycast IP addresses

Use CloudFront for HTTP content, Global Accelerator for non-HTTP traffic

Amazon CloudFront
A fast content delivery network (CDN) service that securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs to viewers globally with low latency and high transfer speeds. Caches content at edge locations worldwide and integrates with Lambda@Edge for edge computing.
AWS Global Accelerator
A networking service that improves availability and performance of applications by routing traffic through AWS's global network infrastructure. Provides static anycast IP addresses and uses the AWS global network to optimize the path to your applications.
Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration
A bucket-level feature that enables fast, easy, and secure transfers of files over long distances between your client and S3. Uses Amazon CloudFront's globally distributed edge locations to accelerate uploads by routing data over the AWS network.
AWS Edge Locations
Data centers deployed in major cities worldwide that host CloudFront and other edge services. Points of Presence (PoPs) serve cached content directly to users, while Regional Edge Caches provide additional caching capacity for less frequently accessed content.
Amazon CloudFront
Primary Purpose: Content Delivery Network (CDN) with caching | Protocol Optimization: HTTP/HTTPS, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, WebSocket | Global Infrastructure: 450+ Points of Presence | Use Case: Dynamic Content: Good with caching disabled or short TTL | Caching Capability: Yes - edge caching with TTL control | Origin Types Supported: S3, EC2, ELB, API Gateway, MediaStore, custom HTTP servers | DDoS Protection: AWS Shield Standard (free), Shield Advanced (paid) | Pricing Model: Data transfer out + HTTP requests + invalidation requests | Performance Gain: 50-70% latency reduction | OSI Layer: Layer 7 (Application) — HTTP/HTTPS content delivery with caching | Static IP Addresses: No — uses dynamic IP addresses (DNS-based); use Global Accelerator if static IPs needed | Edge Locations: Yes — caches content at 450+ edge locations worldwide | Use Case: Gaming/Real-time: Limited — optimized for HTTP/HTTPS; use WebSocket support for some real-time cases | Use Case: Upload Acceleration: Limited — primarily for downloads/delivery; use S3 Transfer Acceleration for uploads | SSL/TLS Termination: Yes — terminates TLS at edge locations; supports custom SSL certificates via ACM | Integration with WAF: Yes — AWS WAF can be directly attached to CloudFront distributions for web application protection
AWS Global Accelerator
Primary Purpose: Network layer acceleration for any application | Protocol Optimization: TCP, UDP (any protocol) | Global Infrastructure: AWS global network backbone | Use Case: Dynamic Content: Global application acceleration | Caching Capability: No - passes through to application | Origin Types Supported: EC2, ELB, Elastic IPs, Application Load Balancers | DDoS Protection: AWS Shield Standard (free), Shield Advanced (paid) | Pricing Model: Fixed hourly fee + data transfer accelerator charge | Performance Gain: Up to 60% latency improvement | OSI Layer: Layer 4 (Transport) — TCP/UDP acceleration at the network level | Static IP Addresses: Yes — provides two static anycast IPv4 addresses that serve as fixed entry points | Edge Locations: Yes — routes traffic through edge locations to AWS backbone network | Use Case: Gaming/Real-time: Excellent — TCP/UDP support ideal for gaming, VoIP, IoT with consistent low latency | Use Case: Upload Acceleration: Possible — accelerates any TCP/UDP traffic including uploads to AWS endpoints | SSL/TLS Termination: No — passes through TCP/UDP connections; TLS termination happens at the origin | Integration with WAF: No — WAF cannot be attached directly; attach WAF to the ALB origin instead
Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration
Primary Purpose: Fast uploads to S3 buckets over long distances | Protocol Optimization: HTTP/HTTPS for S3 uploads | Global Infrastructure: Edge locations | Use Case: Dynamic Content: N/A - upload acceleration only | Caching Capability: No - upload acceleration | Origin Types Supported: S3 buckets only | DDoS Protection: Inherits S3 and CloudFront protection | Pricing Model: Data transfer acceleration charge (no hourly fee) | Performance Gain: 50-500% faster uploads | OSI Layer: Layer 7 (Application) — HTTP-based accelerated S3 uploads | Static IP Addresses: No — uses accelerated DNS endpoint (bucketname.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com) | Edge Locations: Yes — uses CloudFront edge locations to accelerate uploads to S3 | Use Case: Gaming/Real-time: Not applicable — designed for S3 uploads only, not real-time communication | Use Case: Upload Acceleration: Primary use case — accelerates S3 PUT operations over long distances via edge locations | SSL/TLS Termination: Yes — uses HTTPS for accelerated uploads; TLS handled by S3 endpoint | Integration with WAF: No — WAF integration not applicable for S3 upload acceleration
AWS Edge Locations
Primary Purpose: Edge compute and caching | Protocol Optimization: Various | Global Infrastructure: Regional Edge Caches + PoPs | Use Case: Dynamic Content: Edge compute (Lambda@Edge) | Caching Capability: Yes (CloudFront caching) | Origin Types Supported: N/A - infrastructure concept | DDoS Protection: Shield Standard included | Pricing Model: Part of service pricing | Performance Gain: Location-dependent | OSI Layer: Infrastructure — physical PoP locations used by CloudFront, Global Accelerator, Route 53, and other edge services | Static IP Addresses: Not directly — edge locations have IPs but not customer-assignable static IPs | Edge Locations: This IS the infrastructure — 450+ PoPs globally used by other AWS edge services | Use Case: Gaming/Real-time: Infrastructure supporting both CloudFront and Global Accelerator use cases | Use Case: Upload Acceleration: Infrastructure supporting S3 Transfer Acceleration and Global Accelerator uploads | SSL/TLS Termination: Depends on service — CloudFront terminates at edge; Global Accelerator passes through | Integration with WAF: Infrastructure level — WAF rules execute at edge locations when attached to CloudFront or ALB
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#112 Edge and Traffic Distribution Services
Edge & CDN saa_d3_edge_networking 4 cols 6 rows
▼
Feature Amazon CloudFront AWS Global Accelerator Amazon Route 53 Application Load Balancer
Core role Edge caching and content delivery Global traffic acceleration DNS and routing policies Layer 7 load balancing
Best exam clue Static assets, CDN, cache at edge Improve global app pathing to regional endpoints DNS routing, health checks, failover policies HTTP routing across app targets
Caches content Yes No, not its core job No No
Works at Edge delivery layer Global network entry layer DNS control plane Application request distribution
Typical use Website acceleration and edge delivery Global applications needing better routing performance Traffic steering and DNS failover Distribute web requests to targets
Common trap Using it for all routing questions Confusing it with CDN caching Confusing DNS with request-level balancing Using it when the issue is really global routing or CDN
⚠️ Exam Tips

CloudFront caches content at 450+ edge locations globally for low-latency delivery

Global Accelerator uses the AWS global network backbone to reduce internet hops

Route 53 latency-based routing sends users to the lowest-latency AWS Region

S3 Transfer Acceleration uses CloudFront edge locations to speed up S3 uploads

For the exam: consider whether the requirement is about caching (CloudFront) or routing (Route 53/GA)

Amazon CloudFront
CloudFront is a CDN focused on caching and edge delivery for content and supported origin patterns. When latency is improved by serving cached content closer to users, CloudFront is the clue.
AWS Global Accelerator
Global Accelerator improves pathing for non-cache-centric global applications by routing users over the AWS global network to healthy endpoints. Think traffic acceleration, not content caching.
Amazon Route 53
Route 53 is DNS plus routing policies and health-aware traffic steering. It decides where users should go, but it is not itself a CDN or application-layer load balancer.
Application Load Balancer
ALB distributes HTTP/HTTPS traffic across targets and understands Layer 7 constructs. It is the go-to exam answer for path-based or host-based routing inside an application stack.
Amazon CloudFront
Core role: Edge caching and content delivery | Best exam clue: Static assets, CDN, cache at edge | Caches content: Yes | Works at: Edge delivery layer | Typical use: Website acceleration and edge delivery | Common trap: Using it for all routing questions
AWS Global Accelerator
Core role: Global traffic acceleration | Best exam clue: Improve global app pathing to regional endpoints | Caches content: No, not its core job | Works at: Global network entry layer | Typical use: Global applications needing better routing performance | Common trap: Confusing it with CDN caching
Amazon Route 53
Core role: DNS and routing policies | Best exam clue: DNS routing, health checks, failover policies | Caches content: No | Works at: DNS control plane | Typical use: Traffic steering and DNS failover | Common trap: Confusing DNS with request-level balancing
Application Load Balancer
Core role: Layer 7 load balancing | Best exam clue: HTTP routing across app targets | Caches content: No | Works at: Application request distribution | Typical use: Distribute web requests to targets | Common trap: Using it when the issue is really global routing or CDN
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 3 asks you to separate CDN caching, traffic acceleration, DNS routing, and load balancing. The exam loves mixing these together in one scenario to see whether you can isolate the real bottleneck.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

Analytics & Data 7 tables
#113 AWS Data Analytics Services
Analytics & Data SAA-011_2 4 cols 9 rows
▼

Comparing AWS analytics services - Domain 3 (High-Performing Architectures)

Feature Amazon Athena Amazon QuickSight AWS Glue Amazon EMR
Service Type Serverless query service Business intelligence (BI) ETL and data catalog Big data platform
Query Engine Presto (SQL) SPICE + SQL Spark-based ETL Spark, Hadoop, Presto
Data Source S3 data (schema-on-read) Multiple sources S3, databases, streams S3, HDFS, databases
Serverless Yes - fully serverless Yes - managed BI Yes - serverless ETL No - cluster management
Use Case Ad-hoc SQL queries on S3 Dashboards, visualizations Data transformation, catalog Big data processing, ML
Pricing Per TB of data scanned Per user + SPICE capacity Per DPU-hour Per instance-hour
Performance Optimization Columnar formats, partitioning SPICE for fast queries Job bookmarks, autoscaling Cluster scaling, spot instances
Best For Log analysis, S3 data exploration Executive dashboards, reporting Data pipelines, catalog Large-scale data processing
Integration Glue Data Catalog Athena, Redshift, RDS All AWS data services S3, Hadoop ecosystem
💡 Mnemonics

Athena = Ad-hoc queries, QuickSight = BI dashboards, Glue = ETL glue, EMR = Elastic MapReduce (big data)

Need SQL on S3? Athena. Need dashboards? QuickSight. Need ETL? Glue. Need big data processing? EMR.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Athena is serverless - no infrastructure to manage

QuickSight SPICE provides fast in-memory queries

Glue Data Catalog is central metadata repository for Athena and Redshift

EMR is best for complex big data processing (Spark, Hadoop)

Athena cost depends on data scanned - use columnar formats (Parquet)

Glue crawlers automatically discover and catalog data

Amazon Athena
A serverless interactive query service that makes it easy to analyze data in S3 using standard SQL. No infrastructure to set up or manage, paying only for queries run. Ideal for ad-hoc analysis, log querying, and data exploration without data movement.
Amazon QuickSight
A scalable, serverless business intelligence service for creating dashboards and visualizations. Uses SPICE (Super-fast, Parallel, In-memory Calculation Engine) for fast queries, connecting to multiple data sources including Athena, Redshift, and RDS.
AWS Glue
A fully managed ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) service that makes it easy to prepare and load data for analytics. Includes a central Data Catalog, automated crawlers to discover data, and serverless Spark-based job execution.
Amazon EMR
A managed big data platform for processing vast amounts of data using open-source frameworks like Apache Spark, Hadoop, and Presto. Ideal for large-scale data processing, machine learning, and log analysis workloads that require cluster computing.
Amazon Athena
Service Type: Serverless query service | Query Engine: Presto (SQL) | Data Source: S3 data (schema-on-read) | Serverless: Yes - fully serverless | Use Case: Ad-hoc SQL queries on S3 | Pricing: Per TB of data scanned | Performance Optimization: Columnar formats, partitioning | Best For: Log analysis, S3 data exploration | Integration: Glue Data Catalog
Amazon QuickSight
Service Type: Business intelligence (BI) | Query Engine: SPICE + SQL | Data Source: Multiple sources | Serverless: Yes - managed BI | Use Case: Dashboards, visualizations | Pricing: Per user + SPICE capacity | Performance Optimization: SPICE for fast queries | Best For: Executive dashboards, reporting | Integration: Athena, Redshift, RDS
AWS Glue
Service Type: ETL and data catalog | Query Engine: Spark-based ETL | Data Source: S3, databases, streams | Serverless: Yes - serverless ETL | Use Case: Data transformation, catalog | Pricing: Per DPU-hour | Performance Optimization: Job bookmarks, autoscaling | Best For: Data pipelines, catalog | Integration: All AWS data services
Amazon EMR
Service Type: Big data platform | Query Engine: Spark, Hadoop, Presto | Data Source: S3, HDFS, databases | Serverless: No - cluster management | Use Case: Big data processing, ML | Pricing: Per instance-hour | Performance Optimization: Cluster scaling, spot instances | Best For: Large-scale data processing | Integration: S3, Hadoop ecosystem
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json

#114 Analytics Architecture Decision Matrix
Analytics & Data analytics_architecture_decision_matrix 6 cols 8 rows
▼

When designing analytics solutions, architects must choose between multiple AWS services based on data volume, query patterns, performance requirements, and team expertise. This decision matrix helps identify the best service for common analytics scenarios encountered in the exam.

Feature Athena Redshift EMR OpenSearch QuickSight S3 + Glue
Best For Data Size Any S3 data (scans on demand) Up to petabytes (optimized storage) Any size (Spark/Hadoop distributed) GBs to TBs (inverted index) SPICE capacity (GBs to TBs) Data lake (unlimited)
Query Complexity Medium (SQL, limited JOINs) High (complex SQL, many JOINs) Very High (Spark, ML, custom code) Low (search queries, aggregations) Low-Medium (dashboard queries) Depends on query service used
Query Latency Seconds to minutes Sub-second to seconds Minutes to hours (batch) Milliseconds Seconds (SPICE) or minutes Varies by service
Infrastructure Management None (serverless) Low (managed, optional serverless) Medium (cluster management) Low (managed, some tuning) None (fully managed) Low (serverless ETL)
Team Skill Required SQL knowledge SQL + data warehousing Spark/Hadoop expertise Search/Log analytics skills Business user friendly ETL and data engineering
Cost Model Pay per TB scanned Provisioned or RPU-hours Per-second cluster pricing Instance-based hourly Per-user monthly + SPICE Storage + DPU-hours
When to Choose Ad-hoc queries, log analysis, data exploration on S3 BI workloads, complex analytics, predictable queries Big data processing, ML, ETL at scale, custom frameworks Search, log analytics, real-time monitoring, text search Dashboards, self-service BI, sharing visualizations Building data lakes, ETL pipelines, unified analytics
Integration Pattern Glue Catalog → Athena → Results Redshift Spectrum can query S3 directly Can read/write to Glue Catalog, S3 Kinesis → OpenSearch for logs Connects to multiple sources including Athena S3 as lake, Glue for ETL and catalog
⚠️ Exam Tips

Athena = ad-hoc SQL queries on S3; Redshift = complex analytics on large datasets

EMR = big data processing with Spark/Hadoop; OpenSearch = log analytics and full-text search

QuickSight = business dashboards and visualization; Glue = ETL and data cataloging

For serverless analytics: Athena + QuickSight + Glue is the go-to stack

For high-performance analytics: Redshift with RA3 nodes + QuickSight

Athena
Serverless interactive SQL query service that analyzes data directly in S3 using standard SQL. Pay per query ($5/TB scanned). Best for ad-hoc queries and data exploration without infrastructure management.
Redshift
Managed petabyte-scale data warehouse for complex analytical workloads. RA3 nodes separate compute from storage. Best for repeated complex queries, BI dashboards, and large-scale aggregations.
EMR
Managed big data platform running Apache Spark, Hadoop, Hive, and Presto. Best for large-scale data processing, ML training on big data, and workloads requiring open-source framework control.
OpenSearch
Managed search and analytics engine for log analytics, real-time application monitoring, and full-text search. Includes built-in Kibana dashboards for visualization.
QuickSight
Serverless BI service for creating interactive dashboards and visualizations. Features SPICE in-memory engine, ML insights, and embedded analytics. Pay per user per session.
S3 + Glue
Data lake pattern combining S3 for storage with Glue for ETL and cataloging. Glue crawlers automatically discover schemas. Best for building centralized data lakes with automated data preparation.
Athena
Best For Data Size: Any S3 data (scans on demand) | Query Complexity: Medium (SQL, limited JOINs) | Query Latency: Seconds to minutes | Infrastructure Management: None (serverless) | Team Skill Required: SQL knowledge | Cost Model: Pay per TB scanned | When to Choose: Ad-hoc queries, log analysis, data exploration on S3 | Integration Pattern: Glue Catalog → Athena → Results
Redshift
Best For Data Size: Up to petabytes (optimized storage) | Query Complexity: High (complex SQL, many JOINs) | Query Latency: Sub-second to seconds | Infrastructure Management: Low (managed, optional serverless) | Team Skill Required: SQL + data warehousing | Cost Model: Provisioned or RPU-hours | When to Choose: BI workloads, complex analytics, predictable queries | Integration Pattern: Redshift Spectrum can query S3 directly
EMR
Best For Data Size: Any size (Spark/Hadoop distributed) | Query Complexity: Very High (Spark, ML, custom code) | Query Latency: Minutes to hours (batch) | Infrastructure Management: Medium (cluster management) | Team Skill Required: Spark/Hadoop expertise | Cost Model: Per-second cluster pricing | When to Choose: Big data processing, ML, ETL at scale, custom frameworks | Integration Pattern: Can read/write to Glue Catalog, S3
OpenSearch
Best For Data Size: GBs to TBs (inverted index) | Query Complexity: Low (search queries, aggregations) | Query Latency: Milliseconds | Infrastructure Management: Low (managed, some tuning) | Team Skill Required: Search/Log analytics skills | Cost Model: Instance-based hourly | When to Choose: Search, log analytics, real-time monitoring, text search | Integration Pattern: Kinesis → OpenSearch for logs
QuickSight
Best For Data Size: SPICE capacity (GBs to TBs) | Query Complexity: Low-Medium (dashboard queries) | Query Latency: Seconds (SPICE) or minutes | Infrastructure Management: None (fully managed) | Team Skill Required: Business user friendly | Cost Model: Per-user monthly + SPICE | When to Choose: Dashboards, self-service BI, sharing visualizations | Integration Pattern: Connects to multiple sources including Athena
S3 + Glue
Best For Data Size: Data lake (unlimited) | Query Complexity: Depends on query service used | Query Latency: Varies by service | Infrastructure Management: Low (serverless ETL) | Team Skill Required: ETL and data engineering | Cost Model: Storage + DPU-hours | When to Choose: Building data lakes, ETL pipelines, unified analytics | Integration Pattern: S3 as lake, Glue for ETL and catalog
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#115 Business Intelligence Comparison
Analytics & Data business_intelligence_comparison 4 cols 8 rows
▼

AWS QuickSight is the primary BI service, offering two editions with different capabilities. Understanding when to use QuickSight versus querying directly with Athena is important for exam scenarios involving dashboards, visualizations, and self-service analytics.

Feature QuickSight Standard QuickSight Enterprise Athena (for comparison) QuickSight Q
Target Users Individual users, small teams Organizations, enterprise BI Data analysts, developers Business users (natural language)
User Pricing $9/user/month $18/user/month $5/TB scanned (no user fee) $250/month for 500 queries (add-on)
Data Sources AWS sources, files, databases All Standard + additional connectors S3 via Glue Catalog only Works with Enterprise datasets
SPICE Capacity 10GB per user 25GB per user (scales higher) None (direct query) Uses Enterprise SPICE
Key Features Dashboards, visualizations, sharing ML Insights, encryption, VPC, Row-level security SQL queries, results to S3 Natural language Q&A, auto-visualizations
Row-Level Security Not available Yes (user-based rules) Must implement in queries Inherits from Enterprise
ML Capabilities Limited ML Insights, anomaly detection, forecasting None (query only) Auto-suggests visualizations
Best For Basic dashboards, ad-hoc analysis Enterprise BI, embedded analytics Technical users, data exploration Self-service BI for non-technical users
⚠️ Exam Tips

QuickSight Enterprise adds ML Insights, row-level security, and email reports over Standard

QuickSight Q enables natural language querying for business users — Enterprise only

SPICE is QuickSight’s in-memory engine for fast dashboard rendering

Athena is a query engine, not a BI tool — but commonly used as a data source for QuickSight

QuickSight supports embedding dashboards in custom applications

QuickSight Standard
Self-service BI for individual analysts with dashboards, charts, and basic data analysis. Limited collaboration features. Author pricing per month.
QuickSight Enterprise
Full-featured BI with row-level security, hourly data refresh, ML Insights (anomaly detection, forecasting), email reports, and embedded analytics. Supports Active Directory integration.
Athena (for comparison)
Serverless SQL engine for querying S3 data directly. Not a BI tool itself but frequently used as a data source for QuickSight. Best for ad-hoc analysis by technically skilled users.
QuickSight Q
Natural language query feature (Enterprise only) that lets business users ask questions in plain English and get visualized answers automatically. Uses ML to understand business terms.
QuickSight Standard
Target Users: Individual users, small teams | User Pricing: $9/user/month | Data Sources: AWS sources, files, databases | SPICE Capacity: 10GB per user | Key Features: Dashboards, visualizations, sharing | Row-Level Security: Not available | ML Capabilities: Limited | Best For: Basic dashboards, ad-hoc analysis
QuickSight Enterprise
Target Users: Organizations, enterprise BI | User Pricing: $18/user/month | Data Sources: All Standard + additional connectors | SPICE Capacity: 25GB per user (scales higher) | Key Features: ML Insights, encryption, VPC, Row-level security | Row-Level Security: Yes (user-based rules) | ML Capabilities: ML Insights, anomaly detection, forecasting | Best For: Enterprise BI, embedded analytics
Athena (for comparison)
Target Users: Data analysts, developers | User Pricing: $5/TB scanned (no user fee) | Data Sources: S3 via Glue Catalog only | SPICE Capacity: None (direct query) | Key Features: SQL queries, results to S3 | Row-Level Security: Must implement in queries | ML Capabilities: None (query only) | Best For: Technical users, data exploration
QuickSight Q
Target Users: Business users (natural language) | User Pricing: $250/month for 500 queries (add-on) | Data Sources: Works with Enterprise datasets | SPICE Capacity: Uses Enterprise SPICE | Key Features: Natural language Q&A, auto-visualizations | Row-Level Security: Inherits from Enterprise | ML Capabilities: Auto-suggests visualizations | Best For: Self-service BI for non-technical users
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#116 Data Ingestion and Analytics Services
Analytics & Data AIF-C01 ordering saa_d3_data_ingestion_analytics 5 cols 12 rows
▼

Comparing AWS services for data analytics and business intelligence

Feature Amazon Kinesis AWS Glue Amazon Athena Amazon EMR Amazon QuickSight
Pipeline role Stream ingest Transform / catalog Query Heavy processing Visualize
Best exam clue Continuous event stream ETL and schema work Run SQL on S3 data Managed big data clusters/jobs Dashboards and BI
Infrastructure overhead Managed stream service Managed ETL Very low Higher than Athena/Glue Low, BI-focused
Great for Streaming architecture Data transformation and prep Interactive ad hoc analytics Large-scale data processing Business reporting
Not primarily for Dashboards End-user visualization Complex ETL orchestration Simple quick ad hoc SQL only Stream ingestion
Common trap Using it as a BI tool Using it when you only need to query data Using it when you need deep ETL pipelines Choosing it when Athena is enough Thinking it stores or transforms data
Purpose Real-time data streaming — collect, process, and analyze streaming data at scale (logs, IoT, clickstream) Serverless ETL — extract, transform, and load data for analytics; includes Data Catalog for metadata Ad-hoc SQL queries on S3 data Big data processing — managed Hadoop/Spark clusters for large-scale data transformation and analytics Business intelligence dashboards
Data Source Streaming sources — IoT devices, application logs, clickstreams, social media feeds, financial transactions Batch sources — S3 data lakes, RDS/Redshift databases, DynamoDB, JDBC-compatible data stores Data in S3 (query-in-place) Large datasets — S3 data lakes, HDFS, HBase; any data accessible via Hadoop ecosystem connectors Various AWS and external sources
Use Case Real-time dashboards, log analytics, IoT telemetry processing, fraud detection, live video streaming Data lake ETL, data cataloging, schema discovery, preparing data for Athena/Redshift analytics One-time analysis, data exploration Petabyte-scale analytics, ML feature engineering, genomics processing, log analysis with Spark/Hive Executive dashboards, reports
Interface AWS SDK, Kinesis Agent, Kinesis Producer Library (KPL), Kinesis Client Library (KCL), Kinesis Data Firehose AWS Console (visual ETL), Glue Studio, PySpark/Scala scripts, Glue DataBrew (visual data prep) SQL queries (Presto engine) SSH to cluster, EMR Studio (notebooks), Step Functions orchestration, Spark Submit, Hive/Presto CLI Visual dashboards, Q (NL queries)
Cost Model Per shard-hour (Data Streams) or per GB processed (Firehose); pay for provisioned capacity or on-demand Per DPU-hour for ETL jobs; Data Catalog free for first million objects; crawlers charged per DPU-hour Per query (data scanned) Per instance-hour for EC2 nodes + EMR service fee; Spot instances can reduce costs significantly Per user per month
Scalability Horizontal — add shards (Data Streams) or automatic scaling (Firehose); scales to GB/s throughput Automatic — serverless scaling of DPUs based on workload; no capacity planning needed Serverless auto-scaling Manual or auto-scaling — add/remove nodes; EMR Managed Scaling adjusts cluster size based on workload Enterprise SPICE capacity
💡 Mnemonics

AQO = Athena for Queries, QuickSight for BI, OpenSearch for search

⚠️ Exam Tips

Athena is serverless - just point to S3 and query with SQL

Use Athena with Glue Data Catalog for unified metadata access

QuickSight uses SPICE engine for fast in-memory calculations

OpenSearch is commonly used for CloudWatch Logs analysis

Athena pricing is based on data scanned - use columnar formats (Parquet) to reduce cost

Amazon Kinesis
