Amazon Kinesis is for streaming data ingestion and near-real-time pipelines. In exam terms, it is the answer when data arrives continuously and must be processed as a stream.
AWS Glue
AWS Glue is the managed data integration and transformation layer, commonly used for ETL and cataloging. Think schema discovery, transformation, and data preparation.
Amazon Athena
Athena is serverless SQL querying over data in Amazon S3. It is usually the fastest exam answer when you need ad hoc analysis without managing database servers.
Amazon EMR
Amazon EMR is managed big data processing for larger-scale distributed analytics jobs. It is appropriate when the workload needs more customizable or heavier data processing frameworks.
Amazon QuickSight
A fully managed business intelligence (BI) service that enables everyone in an organization to understand data through visual dashboards and insights.
Amazon Kinesis
Pipeline role: Stream ingest | Best exam clue: Continuous event stream | Infrastructure overhead: Managed stream service | Great for: Streaming architecture | Not primarily for: Dashboards | Common trap: Using it as a BI tool | Purpose: Real-time data streaming — collect, process, and analyze streaming data at scale (logs, IoT, clickstream) | Data Source: Streaming sources — IoT devices, application logs, clickstreams, social media feeds, financial transactions | Use Case: Real-time dashboards, log analytics, IoT telemetry processing, fraud detection, live video streaming | Interface: AWS SDK, Kinesis Agent, Kinesis Producer Library (KPL), Kinesis Client Library (KCL), Kinesis Data Firehose | Cost Model: Per shard-hour (Data Streams) or per GB processed (Firehose); pay for provisioned capacity or on-demand | Scalability: Horizontal — add shards (Data Streams) or automatic scaling (Firehose); scales to GB/s throughput
AWS Glue
Pipeline role: Transform / catalog | Best exam clue: ETL and schema work | Infrastructure overhead: Managed ETL | Great for: Data transformation and prep | Not primarily for: End-user visualization | Common trap: Using it when you only need to query data | Purpose: Serverless ETL — extract, transform, and load data for analytics; includes Data Catalog for metadata | Data Source: Batch sources — S3 data lakes, RDS/Redshift databases, DynamoDB, JDBC-compatible data stores | Use Case: Data lake ETL, data cataloging, schema discovery, preparing data for Athena/Redshift analytics | Interface: AWS Console (visual ETL), Glue Studio, PySpark/Scala scripts, Glue DataBrew (visual data prep) | Cost Model: Per DPU-hour for ETL jobs; Data Catalog free for first million objects; crawlers charged per DPU-hour | Scalability: Automatic — serverless scaling of DPUs based on workload; no capacity planning needed
Amazon Athena
Pipeline role: Query | Best exam clue: Run SQL on S3 data | Infrastructure overhead: Very low | Great for: Interactive ad hoc analytics | Not primarily for: Complex ETL orchestration | Common trap: Using it when you need deep ETL pipelines | Purpose: Ad-hoc SQL queries on S3 data | Data Source: Data in S3 (query-in-place) | Use Case: One-time analysis, data exploration | Interface: SQL queries (Presto engine) | Cost Model: Per query (data scanned) | Scalability: Serverless auto-scaling
Amazon EMR
Pipeline role: Heavy processing | Best exam clue: Managed big data clusters/jobs | Infrastructure overhead: Higher than Athena/Glue | Great for: Large-scale data processing | Not primarily for: Simple quick ad hoc SQL only | Common trap: Choosing it when Athena is enough | Purpose: Big data processing — managed Hadoop/Spark clusters for large-scale data transformation and analytics | Data Source: Large datasets — S3 data lakes, HDFS, HBase; any data accessible via Hadoop ecosystem connectors | Use Case: Petabyte-scale analytics, ML feature engineering, genomics processing, log analysis with Spark/Hive | Interface: SSH to cluster, EMR Studio (notebooks), Step Functions orchestration, Spark Submit, Hive/Presto CLI | Cost Model: Per instance-hour for EC2 nodes + EMR service fee; Spot instances can reduce costs significantly | Scalability: Manual or auto-scaling — add/remove nodes; EMR Managed Scaling adjusts cluster size based on workload
Amazon QuickSight
Pipeline role: Visualize | Best exam clue: Dashboards and BI | Infrastructure overhead: Low, BI-focused | Great for: Business reporting | Not primarily for: Stream ingestion | Common trap: Thinking it stores or transforms data | Purpose: Business intelligence dashboards | Data Source: Various AWS and external sources | Use Case: Executive dashboards, reports | Interface: Visual dashboards, Q (NL queries) | Cost Model: Per user per month | Scalability: Enterprise SPICE capacity
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 3 includes ingestion, transformation, querying, and visualization. This table keeps the data pipeline pieces from blurring together under exam pressure.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#117 Data Movement Comparison
Analytics & Data AIF-C01 ordering data_movement_comparison 6 cols 8 rows
▼

AWS provides multiple services for moving data between on-premises and cloud, or between cloud storage services. DataSync is for online data transfer, Storage Gateway provides hybrid cloud storage, Transfer Family offers managed file transfers, and Snow Family handles offline data migration for massive datasets or low-bandwidth scenarios.

Feature DataSync Storage Gateway Transfer Family Snowcone Snowball Edge Snowmobile
Transfer Method Online (network-based) Online (hybrid cloud) Online (managed SFTP/FTPS/FTP) Offline (physical device) Offline (physical device) Offline (truck-mounted container)
Data Volume Any size (network dependent) Any size (cached or stored) Any size (protocol-based) Up to 8 TB Up to 80 TB (Storage) or 28 TB (Compute) Up to 100 PB
Use Case Migrate to S3, EFS, FSx Hybrid cloud, on-prem cache Third-party file transfers Edge computing, small migrations Large migrations, edge computing Exabyte-scale migration
Network Required Yes (AWS Direct Connect supported) Yes (internet or DX) Yes (internet accessible) No (offline transport) No (offline transport) No (offline transport)
Encryption TLS 1.2 in transit, AES-256 at rest AES-256 (all data encrypted) TLS 1.2+ for transfers 256-bit encryption, TPM 256-bit encryption, TPM 256-bit encryption, dedicated security
Pricing Model $0.0125 per GB transferred Per-hour gateway fee + storage Per-hour endpoint fee + data Per-device fee + shipping Per-device fee + shipping Custom pricing (contact AWS)
Bandwidth Consideration Uses available bandwidth (throttle available) Ongoing bandwidth for sync Depends on client connections None required None required None required
Typical Migration Time Hours to days (network dependent) Ongoing synchronization Ongoing file transfers Days (shipping + transfer) 1-2 weeks (shipping + transfer) Weeks to months
⚠️ Exam Tips

DataSync = automated online transfer (up to 10 Gbps); Storage Gateway = hybrid cloud storage

Transfer Family = managed SFTP/FTP for file exchange workflows

Snow Family for offline: Snowcone (8 TB), Snowball Edge (80 TB), Snowmobile (100 PB)

Choose Snow devices when network transfer would take weeks or more

DataSync handles scheduling, verification, and encryption automatically

DataSync
Automated data transfer service for moving data between on-premises storage and AWS (S3, EFS, FSx) at speeds up to 10 Gbps. Handles scheduling, integrity verification, and encryption automatically.
Storage Gateway
Hybrid cloud storage service providing on-premises applications with virtually unlimited cloud-backed storage. Three modes: File Gateway (NFS/SMB to S3), Volume Gateway (iSCSI), Tape Gateway (virtual tape library).
Transfer Family
Managed SFTP, FTPS, FTP, and AS2 file transfer service with data stored directly in S3 or EFS. Ideal for B2B file exchanges and migrating existing file transfer workflows.
Snowcone
Smallest Snow device (8 TB usable) for edge computing and data transfer in austere environments. Rugged, portable, and can run EC2 instances locally. Ships data to AWS or uses DataSync over network.
Snowball Edge
Petabyte-scale data transport device (80 TB) with on-board compute capabilities. Available in Storage Optimized and Compute Optimized variants. Ideal for large data migrations and edge computing.
Snowmobile
Exabyte-scale data transfer service using a 45-foot shipping container truck. Moves up to 100 PB per Snowmobile. Used for datacenter migrations and massive one-time transfers.
DataSync
Transfer Method: Online (network-based) | Data Volume: Any size (network dependent) | Use Case: Migrate to S3, EFS, FSx | Network Required: Yes (AWS Direct Connect supported) | Encryption: TLS 1.2 in transit, AES-256 at rest | Pricing Model: $0.0125 per GB transferred | Bandwidth Consideration: Uses available bandwidth (throttle available) | Typical Migration Time: Hours to days (network dependent)
Storage Gateway
Transfer Method: Online (hybrid cloud) | Data Volume: Any size (cached or stored) | Use Case: Hybrid cloud, on-prem cache | Network Required: Yes (internet or DX) | Encryption: AES-256 (all data encrypted) | Pricing Model: Per-hour gateway fee + storage | Bandwidth Consideration: Ongoing bandwidth for sync | Typical Migration Time: Ongoing synchronization
Transfer Family
Transfer Method: Online (managed SFTP/FTPS/FTP) | Data Volume: Any size (protocol-based) | Use Case: Third-party file transfers | Network Required: Yes (internet accessible) | Encryption: TLS 1.2+ for transfers | Pricing Model: Per-hour endpoint fee + data | Bandwidth Consideration: Depends on client connections | Typical Migration Time: Ongoing file transfers
Snowcone
Transfer Method: Offline (physical device) | Data Volume: Up to 8 TB | Use Case: Edge computing, small migrations | Network Required: No (offline transport) | Encryption: 256-bit encryption, TPM | Pricing Model: Per-device fee + shipping | Bandwidth Consideration: None required | Typical Migration Time: Days (shipping + transfer)
Snowball Edge
Transfer Method: Offline (physical device) | Data Volume: Up to 80 TB (Storage) or 28 TB (Compute) | Use Case: Large migrations, edge computing | Network Required: No (offline transport) | Encryption: 256-bit encryption, TPM | Pricing Model: Per-device fee + shipping | Bandwidth Consideration: None required | Typical Migration Time: 1-2 weeks (shipping + transfer)
Snowmobile
Transfer Method: Offline (truck-mounted container) | Data Volume: Up to 100 PB | Use Case: Exabyte-scale migration | Network Required: No (offline transport) | Encryption: 256-bit encryption, dedicated security | Pricing Model: Custom pricing (contact AWS) | Bandwidth Consideration: None required | Typical Migration Time: Weeks to months
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#118 ETL and Data Processing Comparison
Analytics & Data AIF-C01 ordering etl_and_data_processing_comparison 9 cols 12 rows
▼

Comparing AWS services for data processing, ETL, and analytics

AWS provides multiple services for ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) and data processing workloads. Glue offers serverless ETL with a data catalog, EMR provides managed big data frameworks like Spark and Hadoop, Lake Formation simplifies data lake security and governance, and Data Pipeline is a legacy service for data movement. Understanding the capabilities and trade-offs is essential for exam success.

Feature Glue ETL Glue DataBrew Glue Data Catalog EMR (Spark/Hadoop) Lake Formation Data Pipeline AWS Glue Amazon EMR AWS Lambda
Service Category Serverless ETL service Visual data preparation Central metadata repository Managed big data platform Data lake governance Legacy data movement service Serverless ETL and data integration — managed Apache Spark environment with visual job authoring Managed big data platform — Hadoop, Spark, Hive, Presto, HBase clusters on EC2 or EKS Serverless compute — event-driven functions for lightweight data transformations and triggers
Primary Use Case ETL jobs, data transformation No-code data cleaning Metadata management, schema registry Big data processing, ML Data lake security, access control Scheduled data workflows (deprecated) ETL pipelines for data lakes — extract from RDS/S3, transform with Spark, load to Redshift/S3/Athena Large-scale data processing — petabyte-scale Spark/Hadoop jobs, complex ML feature engineering, genomics Lightweight event-driven processing — S3 event triggers, DynamoDB Streams, API Gateway backends, simple ETL
Code Requirement Python or Scala (Spark) No code (visual interface) No code (automatic) Spark, Hadoop, or other frameworks Minimal code (configuration) JSON definition Optional — Glue Studio visual editor or PySpark/Scala scripts; DataBrew is fully no-code Required — Spark (PySpark/Scala), Hive SQL, Presto SQL, or custom MapReduce jobs Required — function code in Python, Node.js, Java, Go, .NET, Ruby, or custom runtime
Compute Model Serverless (DPU-based) Serverless Metadata only (no compute) EC2 instances or EKS Metadata layer (uses other services) EC2 or EMR resources DPUs (Data Processing Units) per hour Per-instance-hour + storage Per invocation + duration + memory
Pricing $0.44 per DPU-hour $0.48 per session-hour $1 per 100K objects/month EC2 costs + EMR markup Free (pay for underlying services) Pipeline + resource costs Per DPU-hour for ETL jobs (~$0.44/DPU-hr); crawlers per DPU-hr; Data Catalog free for 1M objects Per instance-hour (EC2 + EMR surcharge ~15-25%); Spot Instances save up to 90% Per request ($0.20/1M) + per GB-second of compute; generous free tier (1M requests/month)
Data Sources S3, RDS, JDBC, DynamoDB, Redshift S3, RDS, Redshift, Glue Catalog All AWS data sources Virtually any data source S3 primary (with other services) S3, RDS, DynamoDB, Redshift S3, RDS, Redshift, DynamoDB, JDBC databases, Kafka, Kinesis; Glue Crawlers auto-discover schemas S3, HDFS, HBase, DynamoDB, JDBC, Kafka, Kinesis; any Hadoop-ecosystem connector S3 events, DynamoDB Streams, Kinesis, SQS, SNS, API Gateway, EventBridge, custom triggers
Transformation Complexity High (full Spark capabilities) Low-Medium (250+ built-in transforms) None (metadata only) Very High (any Spark/Hadoop job) None (governance layer) Medium (shell commands, EMR) Medium to high — full Spark capabilities; visual ETL for simple transforms; custom code for complex logic Highest — full Spark/Hadoop ecosystem; custom UDFs, ML pipelines, graph processing Low — simple transformations; limited by 15-min timeout and 10GB memory; not for large datasets
Integration with Athena Writes to catalog for Athena Outputs to catalog for Athena Required for Athena Can write to Glue Catalog Enforces access for Athena Indirect Glue Data Catalog, Lake Formation S3, DynamoDB, Glacier, various stores Any AWS service via SDK
Type Serverless Spark ETL — PySpark/Scala jobs with managed infrastructure; visual and code authoring No-code visual data prep — 250+ built-in transformations; profiling, cleaning, normalizing Metadata repository — centralized schema and table definitions; Hive Metastore compatible Managed cluster — full Apache Spark, Hadoop, Hive, Presto, HBase on EC2 or EKS Data lake management — fine-grained access control, data sharing, governed tables on S3 Orchestration — schedule and run data-driven workflows across AWS services (legacy; prefer Step Functions) Serverless ETL/integration Managed big data processing cluster Serverless function execution
Best For Serverless ETL pipelines for data lakes; auto-schema discovery; integration with Athena and Redshift Non-technical users needing visual data cleaning; quick data profiling and preparation Centralized metadata for all data assets; making data discoverable for Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR Petabyte-scale processing; teams with Spark/Hadoop expertise; cost optimization with Spot Governed data lakes with fine-grained column/row-level permissions; cross-account data sharing Legacy scheduled ETL orchestration; migrating to Step Functions recommended for new workloads Data cataloging, ETL pipelines, data prep Petabyte-scale batch processing, ML Small data transformations, real-time processing
Scale Auto-scaling DPUs — from 2 to 100 DPUs; handles GBs to TBs of data per job Auto-scaling — processes datasets up to TBs; samples large datasets for profiling Unlimited — millions of tables and partitions; regional service with no storage limits Massive — hundreds of nodes; petabyte-scale; EMR Managed Scaling auto-adjusts cluster size Unlimited — operates on top of S3 data lake; governance layer scales with S3 Moderate — orchestrates jobs but processing depends on underlying compute (EC2, EMR) Automatic scaling serverless Manually configured cluster scaling Automatic per-invocation scaling
Data Format Support Parquet, ORC, Avro, JSON, CSV, XML; custom classifiers for other formats CSV, JSON, Parquet, ORC, Avro, Excel; 250+ built-in transformations All formats — metadata only; stores schema definitions for any format the crawler detects All Hadoop-supported formats — Parquet, ORC, Avro, CSV, JSON, sequence files, custom SerDe Inherits from underlying services — Parquet, ORC, CSV, JSON; governed tables use Parquet Depends on activity — shell commands, Hive, Pig, SQL; any format the underlying tool supports CSV, JSON, Parquet, ORC, JDBC Many formats including sequence files, custom Any format (you write the code)
💡 Mnemonics

GEL = Glue for ETL/Integration, EMR for Massive data, Lambda for Light transformations

⚠️ Exam Tips

Glue is fully serverless - no cluster management

EMR is for BIG data - Spark, Hive, HBase on large clusters

Glue Data Catalog provides unified metadata repository across AWS

Use Lambda for simple, short-duration transformations

Glue supports running Scala or Python code as jobs

AWS Glue
A serverless data integration service that makes it easy to discover, prepare, and combine data for analytics, ML, and application development.
Amazon EMR
A managed Hadoop framework service for processing large-scale data using tools like Spark, Hive, and Presto in the cloud.
AWS Lambda
A serverless compute service for processing data in real-time or in response to events, suitable for lightweight ETL transformations.
Glue ETL
Service Category: Serverless ETL service | Primary Use Case: ETL jobs, data transformation | Code Requirement: Python or Scala (Spark) | Compute Model: Serverless (DPU-based) | Pricing: $0.44 per DPU-hour | Data Sources: S3, RDS, JDBC, DynamoDB, Redshift | Transformation Complexity: High (full Spark capabilities) | Integration with Athena: Writes to catalog for Athena | Type: Serverless Spark ETL — PySpark/Scala jobs with managed infrastructure; visual and code authoring | Best For: Serverless ETL pipelines for data lakes; auto-schema discovery; integration with Athena and Redshift | Scale: Auto-scaling DPUs — from 2 to 100 DPUs; handles GBs to TBs of data per job | Data Format Support: Parquet, ORC, Avro, JSON, CSV, XML; custom classifiers for other formats
Glue DataBrew
Service Category: Visual data preparation | Primary Use Case: No-code data cleaning | Code Requirement: No code (visual interface) | Compute Model: Serverless | Pricing: $0.48 per session-hour | Data Sources: S3, RDS, Redshift, Glue Catalog | Transformation Complexity: Low-Medium (250+ built-in transforms) | Integration with Athena: Outputs to catalog for Athena | Type: No-code visual data prep — 250+ built-in transformations; profiling, cleaning, normalizing | Best For: Non-technical users needing visual data cleaning; quick data profiling and preparation | Scale: Auto-scaling — processes datasets up to TBs; samples large datasets for profiling | Data Format Support: CSV, JSON, Parquet, ORC, Avro, Excel; 250+ built-in transformations
Glue Data Catalog
Service Category: Central metadata repository | Primary Use Case: Metadata management, schema registry | Code Requirement: No code (automatic) | Compute Model: Metadata only (no compute) | Pricing: $1 per 100K objects/month | Data Sources: All AWS data sources | Transformation Complexity: None (metadata only) | Integration with Athena: Required for Athena | Type: Metadata repository — centralized schema and table definitions; Hive Metastore compatible | Best For: Centralized metadata for all data assets; making data discoverable for Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR | Scale: Unlimited — millions of tables and partitions; regional service with no storage limits | Data Format Support: All formats — metadata only; stores schema definitions for any format the crawler detects
EMR (Spark/Hadoop)
Service Category: Managed big data platform | Primary Use Case: Big data processing, ML | Code Requirement: Spark, Hadoop, or other frameworks | Compute Model: EC2 instances or EKS | Pricing: EC2 costs + EMR markup | Data Sources: Virtually any data source | Transformation Complexity: Very High (any Spark/Hadoop job) | Integration with Athena: Can write to Glue Catalog | Type: Managed cluster — full Apache Spark, Hadoop, Hive, Presto, HBase on EC2 or EKS | Best For: Petabyte-scale processing; teams with Spark/Hadoop expertise; cost optimization with Spot | Scale: Massive — hundreds of nodes; petabyte-scale; EMR Managed Scaling auto-adjusts cluster size | Data Format Support: All Hadoop-supported formats — Parquet, ORC, Avro, CSV, JSON, sequence files, custom SerDe
Lake Formation
Service Category: Data lake governance | Primary Use Case: Data lake security, access control | Code Requirement: Minimal code (configuration) | Compute Model: Metadata layer (uses other services) | Pricing: Free (pay for underlying services) | Data Sources: S3 primary (with other services) | Transformation Complexity: None (governance layer) | Integration with Athena: Enforces access for Athena | Type: Data lake management — fine-grained access control, data sharing, governed tables on S3 | Best For: Governed data lakes with fine-grained column/row-level permissions; cross-account data sharing | Scale: Unlimited — operates on top of S3 data lake; governance layer scales with S3 | Data Format Support: Inherits from underlying services — Parquet, ORC, CSV, JSON; governed tables use Parquet
Data Pipeline
Service Category: Legacy data movement service | Primary Use Case: Scheduled data workflows (deprecated) | Code Requirement: JSON definition | Compute Model: EC2 or EMR resources | Pricing: Pipeline + resource costs | Data Sources: S3, RDS, DynamoDB, Redshift | Transformation Complexity: Medium (shell commands, EMR) | Integration with Athena: Indirect | Type: Orchestration — schedule and run data-driven workflows across AWS services (legacy; prefer Step Functions) | Best For: Legacy scheduled ETL orchestration; migrating to Step Functions recommended for new workloads | Scale: Moderate — orchestrates jobs but processing depends on underlying compute (EC2, EMR) | Data Format Support: Depends on activity — shell commands, Hive, Pig, SQL; any format the underlying tool supports
AWS Glue
Service Category: Serverless ETL and data integration — managed Apache Spark environment with visual job authoring | Primary Use Case: ETL pipelines for data lakes — extract from RDS/S3, transform with Spark, load to Redshift/S3/Athena | Code Requirement: Optional — Glue Studio visual editor or PySpark/Scala scripts; DataBrew is fully no-code | Compute Model: DPUs (Data Processing Units) per hour | Pricing: Per DPU-hour for ETL jobs (~$0.44/DPU-hr); crawlers per DPU-hr; Data Catalog free for 1M objects | Data Sources: S3, RDS, Redshift, DynamoDB, JDBC databases, Kafka, Kinesis; Glue Crawlers auto-discover schemas | Transformation Complexity: Medium to high — full Spark capabilities; visual ETL for simple transforms; custom code for complex logic | Integration with Athena: Glue Data Catalog, Lake Formation | Type: Serverless ETL/integration | Best For: Data cataloging, ETL pipelines, data prep | Scale: Automatic scaling serverless | Data Format Support: CSV, JSON, Parquet, ORC, JDBC
Amazon EMR
Service Category: Managed big data platform — Hadoop, Spark, Hive, Presto, HBase clusters on EC2 or EKS | Primary Use Case: Large-scale data processing — petabyte-scale Spark/Hadoop jobs, complex ML feature engineering, genomics | Code Requirement: Required — Spark (PySpark/Scala), Hive SQL, Presto SQL, or custom MapReduce jobs | Compute Model: Per-instance-hour + storage | Pricing: Per instance-hour (EC2 + EMR surcharge ~15-25%); Spot Instances save up to 90% | Data Sources: S3, HDFS, HBase, DynamoDB, JDBC, Kafka, Kinesis; any Hadoop-ecosystem connector | Transformation Complexity: Highest — full Spark/Hadoop ecosystem; custom UDFs, ML pipelines, graph processing | Integration with Athena: S3, DynamoDB, Glacier, various stores | Type: Managed big data processing cluster | Best For: Petabyte-scale batch processing, ML | Scale: Manually configured cluster scaling | Data Format Support: Many formats including sequence files, custom
AWS Lambda
Service Category: Serverless compute — event-driven functions for lightweight data transformations and triggers | Primary Use Case: Lightweight event-driven processing — S3 event triggers, DynamoDB Streams, API Gateway backends, simple ETL | Code Requirement: Required — function code in Python, Node.js, Java, Go, .NET, Ruby, or custom runtime | Compute Model: Per invocation + duration + memory | Pricing: Per request ($0.20/1M) + per GB-second of compute; generous free tier (1M requests/month) | Data Sources: S3 events, DynamoDB Streams, Kinesis, SQS, SNS, API Gateway, EventBridge, custom triggers | Transformation Complexity: Low — simple transformations; limited by 15-min timeout and 10GB memory; not for large datasets | Integration with Athena: Any AWS service via SDK | Type: Serverless function execution | Best For: Small data transformations, real-time processing | Scale: Automatic per-invocation scaling | Data Format Support: Any format (you write the code)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#119 Query Services Comparison
Analytics & Data query_services_comparison 5 cols 9 rows
▼

AWS offers multiple query services for analyzing data at different scales and performance requirements. Athena provides serverless SQL queries on S3 data, Redshift offers high-performance data warehousing with various node types and serverless options, Redshift Spectrum extends Redshift queries to S3 without loading data, and OpenSearch Service provides full-text search and log analytics capabilities. Understanding when to use each service is critical for the exam.

Feature Athena Redshift (RA3) Redshift Serverless Redshift Spectrum OpenSearch Service
Service Type Serverless SQL query service Managed data warehouse (provisioned) Serverless data warehouse (auto-scaling) Query extension for external S3 data Managed search and analytics engine
Data Location S3 only (external tables) Managed storage + S3 (RA3 nodes) Managed storage + S3 S3 data queried from Redshift Managed cluster storage
Query Language ANSI SQL (Presto/Trino-based) PostgreSQL-compatible SQL PostgreSQL-compatible SQL PostgreSQL-compatible SQL OpenSearch DSL, SQL (limited)
Performance Good for ad-hoc queries (seconds to minutes) Excellent (sub-second to seconds) Excellent with auto-scaling Good (adds latency for S3) Millisecond response for search
Pricing Model $5 per TB scanned Per-hour per node + storage RPU-hours (compute) + storage $5 per TB scanned (like Athena) Per-hour per instance + storage
Data Formats CSV, JSON, Parquet, ORC, Avro, Ion Native columnar format Native columnar format Same as Athena (external formats) JSON documents
Best Use Case Ad-hoc analysis, log analysis on S3 High-performance BI, complex analytics Variable workloads, dev/test Query cold data without ETL Full-text search, log analytics, monitoring
Scalability Automatic (serverless) Manual (resize cluster) Automatic (serverless) Automatic (serverless queries) Manual (add nodes) or auto-scaling
Concurrency 20 concurrent queries (soft limit) Up to 50 concurrent queries Higher concurrency limits Shares Redshift concurrency Depends on cluster size
⚠️ Exam Tips

Athena = serverless pay-per-query on S3; Redshift = managed data warehouse for complex analytics

Redshift Serverless = on-demand Redshift without cluster management

Redshift Spectrum extends Redshift queries to S3 data without loading it

OpenSearch = real-time search and log analytics with Kibana/dashboards

For the exam: 'interactive queries on S3' = Athena; 'complex joins on large datasets' = Redshift

Athena
Serverless SQL queries directly against S3 data. Uses Presto/Trino engine. Pay per TB scanned — optimize costs by partitioning and using columnar formats (Parquet, ORC).
Redshift (RA3)
Managed data warehouse with RA3 nodes that separate compute and storage. Data stored in S3-backed managed storage. Best for predictable, complex analytical query workloads.
Redshift Serverless
On-demand Redshift without managing clusters. Automatically scales compute based on query complexity. Pay only for compute used. Best for variable analytical workloads.
Redshift Spectrum
Extends Redshift queries to exabytes of data in S3 without loading it. Uses Redshift SQL and optimizers but processes S3 data using independent compute resources.
OpenSearch Service
Managed Elasticsearch/OpenSearch for log analytics, full-text search, real-time application monitoring, and clickstream analytics. Includes OpenSearch Dashboards (Kibana) for visualization.
Athena
Service Type: Serverless SQL query service | Data Location: S3 only (external tables) | Query Language: ANSI SQL (Presto/Trino-based) | Performance: Good for ad-hoc queries (seconds to minutes) | Pricing Model: $5 per TB scanned | Data Formats: CSV, JSON, Parquet, ORC, Avro, Ion | Best Use Case: Ad-hoc analysis, log analysis on S3 | Scalability: Automatic (serverless) | Concurrency: 20 concurrent queries (soft limit)
Redshift (RA3)
Service Type: Managed data warehouse (provisioned) | Data Location: Managed storage + S3 (RA3 nodes) | Query Language: PostgreSQL-compatible SQL | Performance: Excellent (sub-second to seconds) | Pricing Model: Per-hour per node + storage | Data Formats: Native columnar format | Best Use Case: High-performance BI, complex analytics | Scalability: Manual (resize cluster) | Concurrency: Up to 50 concurrent queries
Redshift Serverless
Service Type: Serverless data warehouse (auto-scaling) | Data Location: Managed storage + S3 | Query Language: PostgreSQL-compatible SQL | Performance: Excellent with auto-scaling | Pricing Model: RPU-hours (compute) + storage | Data Formats: Native columnar format | Best Use Case: Variable workloads, dev/test | Scalability: Automatic (serverless) | Concurrency: Higher concurrency limits
Redshift Spectrum
Service Type: Query extension for external S3 data | Data Location: S3 data queried from Redshift | Query Language: PostgreSQL-compatible SQL | Performance: Good (adds latency for S3) | Pricing Model: $5 per TB scanned (like Athena) | Data Formats: Same as Athena (external formats) | Best Use Case: Query cold data without ETL | Scalability: Automatic (serverless queries) | Concurrency: Shares Redshift concurrency
OpenSearch Service
Service Type: Managed search and analytics engine | Data Location: Managed cluster storage | Query Language: OpenSearch DSL, SQL (limited) | Performance: Millisecond response for search | Pricing Model: Per-hour per instance + storage | Data Formats: JSON documents | Best Use Case: Full-text search, log analytics, monitoring | Scalability: Manual (add nodes) or auto-scaling | Concurrency: Depends on cluster size
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

Messaging & Events 11 tables
#120 AWS Messaging and Decoupling Services
Messaging & Events SAA-005 4 cols 16 rows
▼

This table compares AWS messaging services for decoupling and scaling distributed systems. SQS provides managed message queues for asynchronous communication, SNS enables pub/sub messaging with fan-out patterns, and EventBridge offers serverless event bus for connecting applications with event-driven architecture.

Feature Amazon SQS Amazon SNS Amazon Kinesis Amazon MQ
Service Type Message queue Pub/sub notification Data streaming Message broker
Communication Pattern Event bus (rules-based routing) Publish-subscribe (fan-out) Stream processing (1:many) Message broker (JMS, AMQP)
Message Retention 1-14 days No retention (push) 1-365 days Configurable
Consumer Model Pull-based (polling) Push-based (subscriptions) Pull-based (shards) Pull-based (JMS/AMQP)
Ordering Guarantee Standard: best-effort; FIFO: guaranteed Standard: best-effort; FIFO: guaranteed Per-shard ordering Guaranteed (per queue/topic)
Throughput Unlimited (standard queue) Unlimited topics Limited by shard count Broker capacity
Message Persistence 4 days default, up to 14 days No persistence (immediate delivery) Up to 1MB per record Configurable (broker persistence)
Best For Decoupling microservices Fan-out notifications Real-time analytics, streaming Migration from on-premises JMS
Dead Letter Queue Yes (configurable) Yes (DLQ for subscriptions) No (enhanced fan-out) Configurable
Delivery Model Pull-based (consumers poll) Push-based (pushes to subscribers) Pull-based — consumers (KCL, Lambda) pull records from shards using iterators; not push-based Pull-based (JMS/AMQP consumers)
Exactly-Once Processing FIFO queues only FIFO topics only At-least-once — duplicates possible on retries; application must handle deduplication; no built-in exactly-once Supported (configurable)
Subscribers/Targets Single consumer per message Multiple subscribers (SQS, Lambda, HTTP, email, SMS) Kinesis Client Library (KCL) consumers, Lambda, Kinesis Data Firehose, Kinesis Data Analytics Multiple consumers per queue/topic
Event Filtering No (all messages in queue) Limited (attribute-based) Application-level — no built-in server-side filtering; consumers process all records and filter in code No (application-level)
Schema Registry No No AWS Glue Schema Registry integration for Avro/JSON schema validation No
SaaS Integration No Limited Limited — primarily AWS-native; use Kinesis Agent or API for custom integrations No
Use Cases Work queues, buffer requests, decouple components Fan-out, notifications, mobile push Real-time analytics, log aggregation, IoT telemetry, clickstream analysis, video streaming (Video Streams) Lift-and-shift existing messaging (ActiveMQ/RabbitMQ)
💡 Mnemonics

SQS = Simple Queue Service, SNS = Simple Notification Service, Kinesis = Stream processing, MQ = Message Queue (enterprise)

Need point-to-point decoupling? SQS. Need fan-out to many? SNS. Need real-time streaming? Kinesis. Migrating from JMS? Amazon MQ.

⚠️ Exam Tips

SQS decouples components - producers and consumers don't need to know each other

SNS + SQS pattern provides fan-out with durability

SQS FIFO guarantees exactly-once processing and ordering

Kinesis is for streaming data - multiple consumers can read same data

Kinesis Data Streams vs SQS: Kinesis keeps data for replay; SQS deletes after read

Amazon MQ is for migrating existing JMS/AMQP applications

Amazon SQS
A fully managed message queuing service that enables decoupling of distributed software systems. Offers standard queues for maximum throughput and FIFO queues for exactly-once processing, with automatic scaling and dead-letter queue support.
Amazon SNS
A fully managed publish/subscribe messaging service that enables message delivery to multiple subscribers. Supports multiple protocols (HTTP, email, Lambda, SQS) for fan-out scenarios where one message needs to reach multiple destinations.
Amazon Kinesis
A platform for streaming data on AWS, making it easy to load and analyze streaming data. Kinesis Data Streams enables real-time processing of streaming data at massive scale with multiple consumers reading from the same stream.
Amazon MQ
A managed message broker service for Apache ActiveMQ and RabbitMQ that makes it easy to migrate existing messaging applications to the cloud. Supports JMS, AMQP, and other standard protocols for enterprise messaging scenarios.
Amazon SQS
Service Type: Message queue | Communication Pattern: Event bus (rules-based routing) | Message Retention: 1-14 days | Consumer Model: Pull-based (polling) | Ordering Guarantee: Standard: best-effort; FIFO: guaranteed | Throughput: Unlimited (standard queue) | Message Persistence: 4 days default, up to 14 days | Best For: Decoupling microservices | Dead Letter Queue: Yes (configurable) | Delivery Model: Pull-based (consumers poll) | Exactly-Once Processing: FIFO queues only | Subscribers/Targets: Single consumer per message | Event Filtering: No (all messages in queue) | Schema Registry: No | SaaS Integration: No | Use Cases: Work queues, buffer requests, decouple components
Amazon SNS
Service Type: Pub/sub notification | Communication Pattern: Publish-subscribe (fan-out) | Message Retention: No retention (push) | Consumer Model: Push-based (subscriptions) | Ordering Guarantee: Standard: best-effort; FIFO: guaranteed | Throughput: Unlimited topics | Message Persistence: No persistence (immediate delivery) | Best For: Fan-out notifications | Dead Letter Queue: Yes (DLQ for subscriptions) | Delivery Model: Push-based (pushes to subscribers) | Exactly-Once Processing: FIFO topics only | Subscribers/Targets: Multiple subscribers (SQS, Lambda, HTTP, email, SMS) | Event Filtering: Limited (attribute-based) | Schema Registry: No | SaaS Integration: Limited | Use Cases: Fan-out, notifications, mobile push
Amazon Kinesis
Service Type: Data streaming | Communication Pattern: Stream processing (1:many) | Message Retention: 1-365 days | Consumer Model: Pull-based (shards) | Ordering Guarantee: Per-shard ordering | Throughput: Limited by shard count | Message Persistence: Up to 1MB per record | Best For: Real-time analytics, streaming | Dead Letter Queue: No (enhanced fan-out) | Delivery Model: Pull-based — consumers (KCL, Lambda) pull records from shards using iterators; not push-based | Exactly-Once Processing: At-least-once — duplicates possible on retries; application must handle deduplication; no built-in exactly-once | Subscribers/Targets: Kinesis Client Library (KCL) consumers, Lambda, Kinesis Data Firehose, Kinesis Data Analytics | Event Filtering: Application-level — no built-in server-side filtering; consumers process all records and filter in code | Schema Registry: AWS Glue Schema Registry integration for Avro/JSON schema validation | SaaS Integration: Limited — primarily AWS-native; use Kinesis Agent or API for custom integrations | Use Cases: Real-time analytics, log aggregation, IoT telemetry, clickstream analysis, video streaming (Video Streams)
Amazon MQ
Service Type: Message broker | Communication Pattern: Message broker (JMS, AMQP) | Message Retention: Configurable | Consumer Model: Pull-based (JMS/AMQP) | Ordering Guarantee: Guaranteed (per queue/topic) | Throughput: Broker capacity | Message Persistence: Configurable (broker persistence) | Best For: Migration from on-premises JMS | Dead Letter Queue: Configurable | Delivery Model: Pull-based (JMS/AMQP consumers) | Exactly-Once Processing: Supported (configurable) | Subscribers/Targets: Multiple consumers per queue/topic | Event Filtering: No (application-level) | Schema Registry: No | SaaS Integration: No | Use Cases: Lift-and-shift existing messaging (ActiveMQ/RabbitMQ)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#121 Application Integration Comparison: MQ, AppFlow, DataSync, Transfer Family
Messaging & Events application_integration_comparison_mq_appflow_datasync_trans 4 cols 13 rows
▼

AWS provides several services for integrating applications and transferring data between on-premises and cloud environments. Each service is optimized for specific integration patterns including message brokering, SaaS data transfer, large-scale data migration, and secure file transfers. Understanding their capabilities helps design appropriate integration architectures.

Feature Amazon MQ Amazon AppFlow AWS DataSync AWS Transfer Family
Service Type Managed message broker SaaS integration service Data transfer service Managed file transfer
Protocols Supported JMS, AMQP, MQTT, OpenWire, STOMP HTTPS/API (SaaS connectors) NFS, SMB, HDFS, S3 API SFTP, FTPS, FTP
Source/Origin Applications (on-premises or cloud) SaaS applications (Salesforce, SAP, etc.) On-premises storage (NFS/SMB) On-premises file transfer clients
Destination/Target Applications (consumers) AWS services (S3, Redshift, etc.) S3, EFS, FSx S3, EFS
Data Volume Message-based (individual messages) Up to 100 GB per flow run Up to 10 Gbps (high volume) Unlimited (file-based)
Transfer Speed Real-time messaging Scheduled or event-driven High-speed parallel transfer Line speed dependent
Encryption TLS in-transit, KMS at-rest TLS in-transit, KMS at-rest TLS in-transit, no at-rest (data in motion) SFTP/FTPS encryption in-transit
Network Requirements VPC, public or private endpoints Public internet (SaaS APIs) Agent on-premises, VPC endpoint optional Public endpoint or VPC
Pricing Model Per instance-hour + storage Per flow run + data volume Per GB transferred Per endpoint-hour + data transferred
Scheduling Real-time (no scheduling) Scheduled, event-driven, or on-demand Scheduled or on-demand Real-time (client-initiated)
Data Transformation None (message broker) Filtering, validation, basic mapping None (direct transfer) None (direct transfer)
Common Use Cases Migrate existing JMS/ActiveMQ apps to cloud Salesforce to S3, SAP integration, marketing data Migrate NFS to S3, backup to cloud, data lake ingestion Replace on-premises SFTP servers, partner file exchange
Exam Key Point Use when application already uses JMS/ActiveMQ Use for SaaS integrations without coding Use for large-scale data migration Use to eliminate on-premises SFTP servers
⚠️ Exam Tips

Amazon MQ is for migrating existing JMS/AMQP message brokers to AWS

AppFlow integrates SaaS apps (Salesforce, SAP, Slack) with AWS services bidirectionally

DataSync automates large-scale data migration between on-premises and AWS storage

Transfer Family preserves existing SFTP/FTP workflows during migration to AWS

For the exam: 'migrate existing messaging' = MQ; 'SaaS integration' = AppFlow

Amazon MQ
Managed message broker for Apache ActiveMQ and RabbitMQ. Supports JMS, AMQP, MQTT, STOMP, and OpenWire protocols. Best for migrating existing on-premises message broker applications.
Amazon AppFlow
Managed integration service for SaaS applications (Salesforce, SAP, Slack, etc.) and AWS services. Bidirectional data transfer with built-in transformations and encryption.
AWS DataSync
Automated data transfer between on-premises storage systems and AWS storage services. Handles scheduling, integrity checks, and throttling. Best for ongoing data migration and replication.
AWS Transfer Family
Managed SFTP/FTPS/FTP/AS2 service with data stored in S3 or EFS. Preserves existing file transfer workflows and client configurations during cloud migration.
Amazon MQ
Service Type: Managed message broker | Protocols Supported: JMS, AMQP, MQTT, OpenWire, STOMP | Source/Origin: Applications (on-premises or cloud) | Destination/Target: Applications (consumers) | Data Volume: Message-based (individual messages) | Transfer Speed: Real-time messaging | Encryption: TLS in-transit, KMS at-rest | Network Requirements: VPC, public or private endpoints | Pricing Model: Per instance-hour + storage | Scheduling: Real-time (no scheduling) | Data Transformation: None (message broker) | Common Use Cases: Migrate existing JMS/ActiveMQ apps to cloud | Exam Key Point: Use when application already uses JMS/ActiveMQ
Amazon AppFlow
Service Type: SaaS integration service | Protocols Supported: HTTPS/API (SaaS connectors) | Source/Origin: SaaS applications (Salesforce, SAP, etc.) | Destination/Target: AWS services (S3, Redshift, etc.) | Data Volume: Up to 100 GB per flow run | Transfer Speed: Scheduled or event-driven | Encryption: TLS in-transit, KMS at-rest | Network Requirements: Public internet (SaaS APIs) | Pricing Model: Per flow run + data volume | Scheduling: Scheduled, event-driven, or on-demand | Data Transformation: Filtering, validation, basic mapping | Common Use Cases: Salesforce to S3, SAP integration, marketing data | Exam Key Point: Use for SaaS integrations without coding
AWS DataSync
Service Type: Data transfer service | Protocols Supported: NFS, SMB, HDFS, S3 API | Source/Origin: On-premises storage (NFS/SMB) | Destination/Target: S3, EFS, FSx | Data Volume: Up to 10 Gbps (high volume) | Transfer Speed: High-speed parallel transfer | Encryption: TLS in-transit, no at-rest (data in motion) | Network Requirements: Agent on-premises, VPC endpoint optional | Pricing Model: Per GB transferred | Scheduling: Scheduled or on-demand | Data Transformation: None (direct transfer) | Common Use Cases: Migrate NFS to S3, backup to cloud, data lake ingestion | Exam Key Point: Use for large-scale data migration
AWS Transfer Family
Service Type: Managed file transfer | Protocols Supported: SFTP, FTPS, FTP | Source/Origin: On-premises file transfer clients | Destination/Target: S3, EFS | Data Volume: Unlimited (file-based) | Transfer Speed: Line speed dependent | Encryption: SFTP/FTPS encryption in-transit | Network Requirements: Public endpoint or VPC | Pricing Model: Per endpoint-hour + data transferred | Scheduling: Real-time (client-initiated) | Data Transformation: None (direct transfer) | Common Use Cases: Replace on-premises SFTP servers, partner file exchange | Exam Key Point: Use to eliminate on-premises SFTP servers
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#122 Batch Processing Comparison
Messaging & Events batch_processing_comparison 3 cols 10 rows
▼

AWS provides multiple services for batch processing and big data workloads. Understanding the differences between AWS Batch, EMR, and Glue helps select the right tool for data processing requirements.

Feature AWS Batch Amazon EMR AWS Glue
Service Type Batch job scheduling and orchestration Managed big data platform (Hadoop/Spark) Serverless data integration (ETL)
Primary Use Case General batch computing, HPC workloads Big data processing, analytics, ML Data preparation, ETL, data cataloging
Infrastructure Model Managed compute environments (EC2/Fargate) Managed clusters (EC2 instances) Fully serverless (no cluster management)
Job Types Supported Any containerized job or script Spark, Hadoop, Hive, Presto, Flink PySpark, Scala ETL jobs, Python shell
Scaling Dynamic scaling based on job queue Auto-scaling cluster nodes Automatic (DPU-based scaling)
Pricing Model No additional charge (pay for EC2/Fargate) Per-second cluster pricing + EC2 costs Per DPU-hour (data processing unit)
Job Scheduling Advanced scheduling with priorities, dependencies Step-based execution or external schedulers Time-based triggers or event-driven
Data Catalog Integration N/A (bring your own) Integrates with AWS Glue Data Catalog Built-in AWS Glue Data Catalog
Development Interface API, CLI, SDK - job definitions Notebooks (EMR Studio), SSH, APIs Visual ETL designer, notebooks, scripts
Best For HPC, rendering, financial modeling, genomics Petabyte-scale analytics, ML training Data lakes, ETL pipelines, data discovery
⚠️ Exam Tips

AWS Batch = managed batch computing with automatic resource provisioning and scheduling

EMR = managed Spark/Hadoop for big data processing requiring open-source frameworks

Glue = serverless ETL with built-in data catalog and visual job editor

Batch uses Docker containers; EMR uses Spark/Hadoop clusters; Glue uses PySpark scripts

For the exam: 'containerized batch jobs' = AWS Batch; 'Spark processing' = EMR; 'ETL pipeline' = Glue

AWS Batch
Fully managed batch computing service that plans, schedules, and executes containerized batch jobs across EC2 and Fargate. Automatically provisions optimal compute resources based on job requirements.
Amazon EMR
Managed big data platform for running Apache Spark, Hadoop, and Presto clusters. Best for large-scale data processing, ML training, and analytics requiring open-source framework ecosystems.
AWS Glue
Serverless ETL service with a built-in data catalog. Features visual ETL editor, automatic schema discovery via crawlers, and PySpark/Python Shell job execution. Best for data lake preparation.
AWS Batch
Service Type: Batch job scheduling and orchestration | Primary Use Case: General batch computing, HPC workloads | Infrastructure Model: Managed compute environments (EC2/Fargate) | Job Types Supported: Any containerized job or script | Scaling: Dynamic scaling based on job queue | Pricing Model: No additional charge (pay for EC2/Fargate) | Job Scheduling: Advanced scheduling with priorities, dependencies | Data Catalog Integration: N/A (bring your own) | Development Interface: API, CLI, SDK - job definitions | Best For: HPC, rendering, financial modeling, genomics
Amazon EMR
Service Type: Managed big data platform (Hadoop/Spark) | Primary Use Case: Big data processing, analytics, ML | Infrastructure Model: Managed clusters (EC2 instances) | Job Types Supported: Spark, Hadoop, Hive, Presto, Flink | Scaling: Auto-scaling cluster nodes | Pricing Model: Per-second cluster pricing + EC2 costs | Job Scheduling: Step-based execution or external schedulers | Data Catalog Integration: Integrates with AWS Glue Data Catalog | Development Interface: Notebooks (EMR Studio), SSH, APIs | Best For: Petabyte-scale analytics, ML training
AWS Glue
Service Type: Serverless data integration (ETL) | Primary Use Case: Data preparation, ETL, data cataloging | Infrastructure Model: Fully serverless (no cluster management) | Job Types Supported: PySpark, Scala ETL jobs, Python shell | Scaling: Automatic (DPU-based scaling) | Pricing Model: Per DPU-hour (data processing unit) | Job Scheduling: Time-based triggers or event-driven | Data Catalog Integration: Built-in AWS Glue Data Catalog | Development Interface: Visual ETL designer, notebooks, scripts | Best For: Data lakes, ETL pipelines, data discovery
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#123 Decoupling and Orchestration Patterns
Messaging & Events saa_d2_decoupling_orchestration 4 cols 6 rows
▼
Feature Amazon SQS Amazon SNS Amazon API Gateway AWS Step Functions
Primary pattern Queue / buffering Publish / subscribe fan-out Managed API entry point Workflow orchestration
Best when Consumers can process later One event must reach many destinations Clients need an API endpoint Many coordinated steps need retries and state
Typical exam clue Decouple producer and consumer Fan-out notifications Expose serverless or microservice API Coordinate Lambda or service workflow
Persistence of work Message waits for consumer Event broadcast, not queue-first Request handling, not durable queueing Stateful execution tracking
Great for retry logic Yes, at queue-consumer level Not the main strength Not its main job Yes, built into workflow design
Common mistake Using it for broadcast fan-out Using it as durable work queue Treating it as orchestration engine Using it where a simple queue would do
⚠️ Exam Tips

SQS decouples producers from consumers with queued messages

SNS delivers messages to multiple subscribers simultaneously (fan-out)

EventBridge routes events based on content matching rules to targets

Step Functions orchestrates multi-step workflows with visual state machines

For the exam: 'fan-out' = SNS; 'decouple with queue' = SQS; 'event routing' = EventBridge; 'workflow' = Step Functions

Amazon SQS
Amazon SQS is a queue for decoupling producers and consumers so components can work asynchronously. On the exam, it is the clean answer for buffering, smoothing bursts, and preventing tight coupling.
Amazon SNS
Amazon SNS is for pub/sub fan-out where one message needs to reach multiple subscribers. If the question says 'notify many targets' or fan-out events, SNS is the clue.
Amazon API Gateway
API Gateway is the managed front door for APIs and is common for exposing REST or HTTP endpoints. It is not a queue, but it often fronts serverless and microservice patterns.
AWS Step Functions
Step Functions orchestrates multi-step workflows with branching, retries, and state transitions. When business logic spans several services and needs explicit workflow control, this is your answer.
Amazon SQS
Primary pattern: Queue / buffering | Best when: Consumers can process later | Typical exam clue: Decouple producer and consumer | Persistence of work: Message waits for consumer | Great for retry logic: Yes, at queue-consumer level | Common mistake: Using it for broadcast fan-out
Amazon SNS
Primary pattern: Publish / subscribe fan-out | Best when: One event must reach many destinations | Typical exam clue: Fan-out notifications | Persistence of work: Event broadcast, not queue-first | Great for retry logic: Not the main strength | Common mistake: Using it as durable work queue
Amazon API Gateway
Primary pattern: Managed API entry point | Best when: Clients need an API endpoint | Typical exam clue: Expose serverless or microservice API | Persistence of work: Request handling, not durable queueing | Great for retry logic: Not its main job | Common mistake: Treating it as orchestration engine
AWS Step Functions
Primary pattern: Workflow orchestration | Best when: Many coordinated steps need retries and state | Typical exam clue: Coordinate Lambda or service workflow | Persistence of work: Stateful execution tracking | Great for retry logic: Yes, built into workflow design | Common mistake: Using it where a simple queue would do
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 2 pushes loosely coupled and event-driven design. This table helps distinguish queueing, pub/sub fan-out, API front doors, and workflow orchestration.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

#124 Message Queue and Integration Services Comparison
Messaging & Events message_queue_and_integration_services_comparison 4 cols 6 rows
▼

Comparing AWS messaging and event-driven integration services

Feature Amazon SQS Amazon SNS Amazon EventBridge AWS Step Functions
Pattern Point-to-point queue (one consumer) Pub-sub (multiple subscribers) Event routing (rules-based) Workflow orchestration (state machines)
Message Delivery Pull-based (consumer polls) Push-based to subscribers Event-driven to targets Orchestrated task execution
Use Case Decouple producers and consumers, task queues Fanout notifications, mobile push Event-driven architectures, SaaS integration Multi-step workflows, business processes
Message Retention Up to 14 days No retention (immediate delivery) Up to 24 hours Execution history up to 90 days
Dead Letter Queue Yes (built-in DLQ support) Yes (via subscription filter) Yes (archival to S3) Yes (try-catch-error handling)
Pricing Per request (very low cost) Per notification Per event ingestion Per state transition
💡 Mnemonics

SNSE = SQS for Queue, SNS for Notifications, EventBridge for Events, Step for Steps/workflows

⚠️ Exam Tips

SQS queues are for DECOUPLING - producer and consumer don't need to be running simultaneously

SNS pushes to MULTIPLE subscribers - good for fanout patterns

EventBridge can receive events from SaaS applications (not just AWS)

Step Functions coordinate workflows - don't write complex state machines in Lambda

Use FIFO queues when message ordering matters

Amazon SQS
A fully managed message queuing service that enables decoupling and scaling of microservices, distributed systems, and serverless applications.
Amazon SNS
A fully managed pub/sub messaging and mobile notifications service enabling fanout messaging to multiple subscribers simultaneously.
Amazon EventBridge
A serverless event bus service that connects application data from your own apps, SaaS apps, and AWS services to targets like Lambda.
AWS Step Functions
A serverless workflow orchestration service that coordinates multiple AWS services into visual workflows for business processes.
Amazon SQS
Pattern: Point-to-point queue (one consumer) | Message Delivery: Pull-based (consumer polls) | Use Case: Decouple producers and consumers, task queues | Message Retention: Up to 14 days | Dead Letter Queue: Yes (built-in DLQ support) | Pricing: Per request (very low cost)
Amazon SNS
Pattern: Pub-sub (multiple subscribers) | Message Delivery: Push-based to subscribers | Use Case: Fanout notifications, mobile push | Message Retention: No retention (immediate delivery) | Dead Letter Queue: Yes (via subscription filter) | Pricing: Per notification
Amazon EventBridge
Pattern: Event routing (rules-based) | Message Delivery: Event-driven to targets | Use Case: Event-driven architectures, SaaS integration | Message Retention: Up to 24 hours | Dead Letter Queue: Yes (archival to S3) | Pricing: Per event ingestion
AWS Step Functions
Pattern: Workflow orchestration (state machines) | Message Delivery: Orchestrated task execution | Use Case: Multi-step workflows, business processes | Message Retention: Execution history up to 90 days | Dead Letter Queue: Yes (try-catch-error handling) | Pricing: Per state transition
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#125 Messaging Patterns Comparison: SQS vs SNS vs EventBridge vs Kinesis
Messaging & Events messaging_patterns_comparison_sqs_vs_sns_vs_eventbridge_vs_k 4 cols 12 rows
▼

AWS provides multiple messaging services optimized for different architectural patterns. Understanding when to use each service is essential for designing scalable, decoupled systems. This comparison focuses on the key differentiators that drive service selection in exam scenarios and real-world architectures.

Feature SQS SNS EventBridge Kinesis Data Streams
Pattern Type Point-to-point (queue) Pub/Sub (fan-out) Event bus with routing Real-time streaming
Message Consumption Pull-based (consumers poll) Push-based (pushes to subscribers) Push-based (pushes to targets) Pull-based (consumers read shards)
Ordering Guarantee FIFO only (Standard = best-effort) FIFO topics only Best-effort only Within a shard (partition key)
Message Persistence 1 min to 14 days No persistence (immediate delivery) No persistence (can archive) 1 to 365 days (default 24h)
Throughput Standard = unlimited, FIFO = 3K/sec Standard = unlimited, FIFO = 3K/sec 10K events/sec (scalable) 1 MB/sec or 1K records/sec per shard
Fan-out Capability Single consumer (unless using multiple queues) Native fan-out to unlimited subscribers Multiple targets per rule Multiple consumers per shard
Filtering None (consumer filters) Attribute-based filtering Content-based JSON filtering None (consumer filters)
Replay Capability No No Yes (with archive enabled) Yes (within retention period)
Real-time Processing Near real-time (polling delay) Real-time push Real-time push Real-time streaming (sub-second)
Scale Out Add more consumers Automatic Automatic Add more shards
Use Case Decoupling, work queues, job processing Alerts, notifications, simple fan-out Event routing, SaaS integration, scheduling Real-time analytics, log processing, metrics
Pricing Model Per request + data transfer Per request + data transfer + delivery Per event published Per shard-hour + PUT payload unit
⚠️ Exam Tips

SQS = point-to-point (one message, one consumer); SNS = fan-out (one message, many subscribers)

EventBridge = event-driven routing with content-based filtering and schema registry

Kinesis = real-time data streaming for continuous data ingestion at scale

SQS + SNS together (fan-out pattern) = publish to SNS topic which fans out to multiple SQS queues

For the exam: 'real-time streaming' = Kinesis; 'event filtering' = EventBridge; 'simple decoupling' = SQS

SQS
Fully managed message queue for point-to-point asynchronous communication. Messages are consumed by a single consumer, enabling decoupled architectures. Supports Standard (at-least-once) and FIFO (exactly-once) delivery.
SNS
Fully managed pub/sub messaging service for one-to-many (fan-out) communication. A single message published to a topic is delivered to multiple subscribers (SQS, Lambda, HTTP, email, SMS).
EventBridge
Serverless event bus that connects applications using events from AWS services, SaaS apps, and custom applications. Features content-based filtering, schema registry, and archive/replay capabilities.
Kinesis Data Streams
Real-time data streaming service for collecting, processing, and analyzing streaming data at any scale. Provides ordering within shards and supports multiple consumers reading the same data.
SQS
Pattern Type: Point-to-point (queue) | Message Consumption: Pull-based (consumers poll) | Ordering Guarantee: FIFO only (Standard = best-effort) | Message Persistence: 1 min to 14 days | Throughput: Standard = unlimited, FIFO = 3K/sec | Fan-out Capability: Single consumer (unless using multiple queues) | Filtering: None (consumer filters) | Replay Capability: No | Real-time Processing: Near real-time (polling delay) | Scale Out: Add more consumers | Use Case: Decoupling, work queues, job processing | Pricing Model: Per request + data transfer
SNS
Pattern Type: Pub/Sub (fan-out) | Message Consumption: Push-based (pushes to subscribers) | Ordering Guarantee: FIFO topics only | Message Persistence: No persistence (immediate delivery) | Throughput: Standard = unlimited, FIFO = 3K/sec | Fan-out Capability: Native fan-out to unlimited subscribers | Filtering: Attribute-based filtering | Replay Capability: No | Real-time Processing: Real-time push | Scale Out: Automatic | Use Case: Alerts, notifications, simple fan-out | Pricing Model: Per request + data transfer + delivery
EventBridge
Pattern Type: Event bus with routing | Message Consumption: Push-based (pushes to targets) | Ordering Guarantee: Best-effort only | Message Persistence: No persistence (can archive) | Throughput: 10K events/sec (scalable) | Fan-out Capability: Multiple targets per rule | Filtering: Content-based JSON filtering | Replay Capability: Yes (with archive enabled) | Real-time Processing: Real-time push | Scale Out: Automatic | Use Case: Event routing, SaaS integration, scheduling | Pricing Model: Per event published
Kinesis Data Streams
Pattern Type: Real-time streaming | Message Consumption: Pull-based (consumers read shards) | Ordering Guarantee: Within a shard (partition key) | Message Persistence: 1 to 365 days (default 24h) | Throughput: 1 MB/sec or 1K records/sec per shard | Fan-out Capability: Multiple consumers per shard | Filtering: None (consumer filters) | Replay Capability: Yes (within retention period) | Real-time Processing: Real-time streaming (sub-second) | Scale Out: Add more shards | Use Case: Real-time analytics, log processing, metrics | Pricing Model: Per shard-hour + PUT payload unit
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#126 Pub/Sub Services Comparison: SNS Standard, SNS FIFO, EventBridge
Messaging & Events pubsub_services_comparison_sns_standard_sns_fifo_eventbridge 4 cols 12 rows
▼

AWS offers multiple publish-subscribe messaging services for event-driven architectures. SNS provides simple topic-based pub/sub with both standard and FIFO variants, while EventBridge offers sophisticated event routing with content-based filtering and integration with SaaS applications. Understanding their capabilities helps select the right service for fan-out patterns, event routing, and application integration.

Feature SNS Standard Topics SNS FIFO Topics EventBridge (Default Bus) EventBridge (Custom Bus)
Message Ordering Best-effort (no ordering guarantee) Strict ordering within message groups Best-effort ordering Best-effort ordering
Delivery Guarantee At-least-once delivery Exactly-once delivery At-least-once delivery At-least-once delivery
Throughput Nearly unlimited 300 msg/sec (3,000 with batching) 10,000 events/second (can increase) 10,000 events/second (can increase)
Message Filtering Message attributes filtering (basic) Message attributes filtering (basic) Content-based filtering with JSON patterns Content-based filtering with JSON patterns
Event Sources AWS services, SDK, direct publish AWS services, SDK, direct publish AWS services only (CloudWatch, CodePipeline, etc.) AWS services, SDK, SaaS integrations, scheduled events
SaaS Integration Not supported Not supported Not supported Native integration (Datadog, Zendesk, etc.)
Scheduled Events Not supported (use EventBridge) Not supported Not supported Cron expressions and rate expressions supported
Archive & Replay Not supported Not supported Not supported Event archive and replay capability
Schema Registry Not supported Not supported Supported Supported
Cross-Region Within region (cross-region via replication) Within region Within region Cross-region event bus targets supported
DLQ Support SQS DLQ for failed deliveries SQS FIFO DLQ for failed deliveries SQS DLQ for failed rule invocations SQS DLQ for failed rule invocations
Primary Use Case Simple fan-out, SMS/email alerts, mobile push Ordered fan-out to multiple subscribers Reacting to AWS service events Complex event routing, SaaS integration, scheduled tasks
⚠️ Exam Tips

SNS Standard = high throughput, at-least-once delivery, best for notifications and fan-out

SNS FIFO = ordered, exactly-once delivery but limited throughput (300 msg/sec)

EventBridge Default Bus = AWS service events; Custom Bus = your application events

EventBridge supports cross-account event delivery and archive/replay

For the exam: 'ordered messaging' = FIFO topics; 'event-driven architecture' = EventBridge

SNS Standard Topics
High-throughput pub/sub with at-least-once delivery and best-effort ordering. Supports millions of messages per second. Ideal for notifications, fan-out patterns, and alert systems.
SNS FIFO Topics
Ordered, exactly-once pub/sub messaging with message deduplication. Limited to 300 messages/second (3000 with batching). Ideal when message ordering and deduplication are critical.
EventBridge (Default Bus)
Receives events from AWS services automatically. Rules match events by pattern and route to over 20 target types. Built-in schema discovery for AWS service events.
EventBridge (Custom Bus)
Dedicated event bus for your application events and third-party SaaS integrations. Supports cross-account event delivery and custom event schemas.
SNS Standard Topics
Message Ordering: Best-effort (no ordering guarantee) | Delivery Guarantee: At-least-once delivery | Throughput: Nearly unlimited | Message Filtering: Message attributes filtering (basic) | Event Sources: AWS services, SDK, direct publish | SaaS Integration: Not supported | Scheduled Events: Not supported (use EventBridge) | Archive & Replay: Not supported | Schema Registry: Not supported | Cross-Region: Within region (cross-region via replication) | DLQ Support: SQS DLQ for failed deliveries | Primary Use Case: Simple fan-out, SMS/email alerts, mobile push
SNS FIFO Topics
Message Ordering: Strict ordering within message groups | Delivery Guarantee: Exactly-once delivery | Throughput: 300 msg/sec (3,000 with batching) | Message Filtering: Message attributes filtering (basic) | Event Sources: AWS services, SDK, direct publish | SaaS Integration: Not supported | Scheduled Events: Not supported | Archive & Replay: Not supported | Schema Registry: Not supported | Cross-Region: Within region | DLQ Support: SQS FIFO DLQ for failed deliveries | Primary Use Case: Ordered fan-out to multiple subscribers
EventBridge (Default Bus)
Message Ordering: Best-effort ordering | Delivery Guarantee: At-least-once delivery | Throughput: 10,000 events/second (can increase) | Message Filtering: Content-based filtering with JSON patterns | Event Sources: AWS services only (CloudWatch, CodePipeline, etc.) | SaaS Integration: Not supported | Scheduled Events: Not supported | Archive & Replay: Not supported | Schema Registry: Supported | Cross-Region: Within region | DLQ Support: SQS DLQ for failed rule invocations | Primary Use Case: Reacting to AWS service events
EventBridge (Custom Bus)
Message Ordering: Best-effort ordering | Delivery Guarantee: At-least-once delivery | Throughput: 10,000 events/second (can increase) | Message Filtering: Content-based filtering with JSON patterns | Event Sources: AWS services, SDK, SaaS integrations, scheduled events | SaaS Integration: Native integration (Datadog, Zendesk, etc.) | Scheduled Events: Cron expressions and rate expressions supported | Archive & Replay: Event archive and replay capability | Schema Registry: Supported | Cross-Region: Cross-region event bus targets supported | DLQ Support: SQS DLQ for failed rule invocations | Primary Use Case: Complex event routing, SaaS integration, scheduled tasks
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#127 Queue Services Comparison: SQS Standard vs FIFO Queue
Messaging & Events queue_services_comparison_sqs_standard_vs_fifo_queue 2 cols 12 rows
▼

Amazon SQS (Simple Queue Service) offers two queue types designed for different messaging requirements. Standard queues provide maximum throughput with at-least-once delivery, while FIFO queues guarantee message ordering and exactly-once processing. Understanding when to use each is critical for exam scenarios involving decoupling, load balancing, and message processing requirements.

Feature SQS Standard Queue SQS FIFO Queue
Ordering Guarantee Best-effort ordering (messages may arrive out of order) Strict first-in-first-out ordering guaranteed
Delivery Guarantee At-least-once delivery (messages may be delivered multiple times) Exactly-once processing (no duplicate delivery)
Throughput Nearly unlimited throughput 300 messages/second (without batching), 3,000 msg/sec (with batching)
Message Deduplication Not supported 5-minute deduplication window using MessageDeduplicationId
Message Grouping Not supported MessageGroupId for parallel processing of different groups
Pricing Pay per API request Higher cost per API request
Visibility Timeout 30 sec default, up to 12 hours 30 sec default, up to 12 hours
Message Retention 1 minute to 14 days (default 4 days) 1 minute to 14 days (default 4 days)
Maximum Message Size 256 KB (use S3 for larger payloads) 256 KB (use S3 for larger payloads)
Long Polling Supported (1-20 seconds) Supported (1-20 seconds)
Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) Supported Supported (DLQ must also be FIFO)
Use Cases Decoupling, load balancing, background jobs, scalable processing Bank transactions, order processing, stock trades, price updates
⚠️ Exam Tips

Standard = unlimited throughput, at-least-once delivery, best-effort ordering

FIFO = exactly-once processing, strict ordering, 300 msg/sec (3000 with batching)

FIFO queues require message group IDs for ordering within groups

Standard queues may deliver duplicate messages — your application must handle deduplication

For the exam: 'financial transactions' or 'order processing' = FIFO; 'high throughput' = Standard

SQS Standard Queue
Maximum throughput with at-least-once delivery and best-effort ordering. Supports nearly unlimited transactions per second. Ideal for high-volume workloads where occasional duplicates are acceptable.
SQS FIFO Queue
Exactly-once processing with strict message ordering within message groups. Limited to 300 messages/second (3000 with batching). Ideal for financial transactions and ordered event processing.
SQS Standard Queue
Ordering Guarantee: Best-effort ordering (messages may arrive out of order) | Delivery Guarantee: At-least-once delivery (messages may be delivered multiple times) | Throughput: Nearly unlimited throughput | Message Deduplication: Not supported | Message Grouping: Not supported | Pricing: Pay per API request | Visibility Timeout: 30 sec default, up to 12 hours | Message Retention: 1 minute to 14 days (default 4 days) | Maximum Message Size: 256 KB (use S3 for larger payloads) | Long Polling: Supported (1-20 seconds) | Dead Letter Queue (DLQ): Supported | Use Cases: Decoupling, load balancing, background jobs, scalable processing
SQS FIFO Queue
Ordering Guarantee: Strict first-in-first-out ordering guaranteed | Delivery Guarantee: Exactly-once processing (no duplicate delivery) | Throughput: 300 messages/second (without batching), 3,000 msg/sec (with batching) | Message Deduplication: 5-minute deduplication window using MessageDeduplicationId | Message Grouping: MessageGroupId for parallel processing of different groups | Pricing: Higher cost per API request | Visibility Timeout: 30 sec default, up to 12 hours | Message Retention: 1 minute to 14 days (default 4 days) | Maximum Message Size: 256 KB (use S3 for larger payloads) | Long Polling: Supported (1-20 seconds) | Dead Letter Queue (DLQ): Supported (DLQ must also be FIFO) | Use Cases: Bank transactions, order processing, stock trades, price updates
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#128 SNS vs SQS vs EventBridge vs Amazon Kinesis
Messaging & Events SAA-005_2 4 cols 11 rows
▼

Comparison of AWS messaging services for building decoupled architectures

Decoupling architecture components with messaging services is a core SAA-C03 skill. These four services all handle asynchronous communication but have very different patterns. Getting confused between them is one of the most common exam mistakes. The differentiators are: push vs pull, one-to-one vs one-to-many, and batch vs streaming.

Feature Amazon SQS Amazon SNS Amazon EventBridge Amazon Kinesis
Communication Pattern Queue — point-to-point (one producer, one consumer group) Publish-subscribe (fan-out) - multiple subscribers per message Event-driven (event bus) - rules route events to targets Data Stream — ordered, real-time streaming with replay
Message Persistence Yes — messages stored up to 14 days No — push-and-forget (no storage) Limited — 24-hour replay via archive Yes — up to 365 days (configurable retention)
Message Retention Messages stored until consumed (1 min to 14 days) No ordering guarantee (except with FIFO topics) No ordering guarantee (events are independent) YES — multiple consumers can re-read the same stream
Real-Time Processing Near real-time with polling Real-time push Near real-time Real-time — millisecond latency streaming
Typical Use Cases Decouple microservices, buffer tasks, retry failed jobs Mobile push, SMS, email notifications, fan-out to multiple systems Event-driven architecture, SaaS integration, rule-based routing Real-time log analytics, IoT telemetry, clickstream analysis
Consumer Model Pull-based - consumers poll the queue Push-based - SNS pushes to subscribers Event-driven - rules trigger target services PULL — consumers read from shards (or push via Lambda trigger)
Delivery Guarantees Standard: no guarantee. FIFO: strict order, exactly-once At-least-once (retries with exponential backoff) At-least-once with retry and dead-letter queue Ordered within each shard
Filtering No - consumers receive all messages Message filtering - subscribers can filter by attributes Content filtering in rules with event patterns Application-level — consumers filter records in code; no built-in server-side filtering
Schema Registry No No Yes - Schema Registry for event validation AWS Glue Schema Registry integration for Avro/JSON schema validation
SaaS Integration No No Yes - 200+ SaaS partner event sources Limited — primarily AWS-native; use Kinesis Agent or API for custom integrations
Cross-Account Via resource-based policies Via topic policies Yes - native cross-account event routing Yes — via resource-based policies on Kinesis streams for cross-account access
💡 Mnemonics

S.S.E - SQS queues for sequential, SNS notifies multiple, EventBridge routes events

⚠️ Exam Tips

Use SQS for decoupling - producers and consumers work independently

Use SNS for broadcasting - one message to many subscribers

EventBridge is ideal for event-driven architectures with AWS SaaS integration

SQS FIFO ensures ordered processing - important for financial transactions

EventBridge rules can filter events before routing - reduce unnecessary processing

Amazon SQS
SQS (Simple Queue Service) is a message queue — messages are stored until a consumer pulls them out for processing. It is perfect for decoupling producers and consumers and handling traffic spikes by buffering work, ensuring no messages are lost even if the consumer is down.
Amazon SNS
SNS (Simple Notification Service) is a pub/sub messaging service that pushes a message to MULTIPLE subscribers simultaneously — email, Lambda, SQS, HTTP endpoints. One publisher sends, many subscribers receive. It is push-based with no message persistence.
Amazon EventBridge
EventBridge is a serverless event bus that routes events from AWS services, SaaS applications, and your own apps to targets (Lambda, SQS, Step Functions). It supports content-based routing with sophisticated filtering rules — the most powerful event router in AWS.
Amazon Kinesis
Kinesis is a real-time DATA STREAMING service for ingesting, processing, and analyzing high-volume streaming data (logs, clickstreams, IoT telemetry) at scale. Unlike SQS/SNS, messages can be replayed and multiple consumers can read the same stream independently.
Amazon SQS
Communication Pattern: Queue — point-to-point (one producer, one consumer group) | Message Persistence: Yes — messages stored up to 14 days | Message Retention: Messages stored until consumed (1 min to 14 days) | Real-Time Processing: Near real-time with polling | Typical Use Cases: Decouple microservices, buffer tasks, retry failed jobs | Consumer Model: Pull-based - consumers poll the queue | Delivery Guarantees: Standard: no guarantee. FIFO: strict order, exactly-once | Filtering: No - consumers receive all messages | Schema Registry: No | SaaS Integration: No | Cross-Account: Via resource-based policies
Amazon SNS
Communication Pattern: Publish-subscribe (fan-out) - multiple subscribers per message | Message Persistence: No — push-and-forget (no storage) | Message Retention: No ordering guarantee (except with FIFO topics) | Real-Time Processing: Real-time push | Typical Use Cases: Mobile push, SMS, email notifications, fan-out to multiple systems | Consumer Model: Push-based - SNS pushes to subscribers | Delivery Guarantees: At-least-once (retries with exponential backoff) | Filtering: Message filtering - subscribers can filter by attributes | Schema Registry: No | SaaS Integration: No | Cross-Account: Via topic policies
Amazon EventBridge
Communication Pattern: Event-driven (event bus) - rules route events to targets | Message Persistence: Limited — 24-hour replay via archive | Message Retention: No ordering guarantee (events are independent) | Real-Time Processing: Near real-time | Typical Use Cases: Event-driven architecture, SaaS integration, rule-based routing | Consumer Model: Event-driven - rules trigger target services | Delivery Guarantees: At-least-once with retry and dead-letter queue | Filtering: Content filtering in rules with event patterns | Schema Registry: Yes - Schema Registry for event validation | SaaS Integration: Yes - 200+ SaaS partner event sources | Cross-Account: Yes - native cross-account event routing
Amazon Kinesis
