Communication Pattern: Data Stream — ordered, real-time streaming with replay | Message Persistence: Yes — up to 365 days (configurable retention) | Message Retention: YES — multiple consumers can re-read the same stream | Real-Time Processing: Real-time — millisecond latency streaming | Typical Use Cases: Real-time log analytics, IoT telemetry, clickstream analysis | Consumer Model: PULL — consumers read from shards (or push via Lambda trigger) | Delivery Guarantees: Ordered within each shard | Filtering: Application-level — consumers filter records in code; no built-in server-side filtering | Schema Registry: AWS Glue Schema Registry integration for Avro/JSON schema validation | SaaS Integration: Limited — primarily AWS-native; use Kinesis Agent or API for custom integrations | Cross-Account: Yes — via resource-based policies on Kinesis streams for cross-account access
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#129 Streaming Services Comparison
Messaging & Events streaming_services_comparison 6 cols 9 rows
▼

Comparing AWS services for real-time data streaming and processing

AWS offers multiple services for real-time data streaming and processing. Kinesis Data Streams provides low-latency data ingestion, Firehose handles data delivery to destinations, Data Analytics enables real-time SQL/Flink processing, Video Streams is for video ingestion, and MSK offers managed Kafka. Understanding latency, scalability, and use cases is critical for the exam.

Feature Kinesis Data Streams Kinesis Data Firehose Kinesis Data Analytics Kinesis Video Streams MSK (Managed Kafka) Amazon MSK
Primary Purpose Real-time data ingestion Data delivery to destinations Real-time stream processing Video stream ingestion Managed Apache Kafka service Managed Apache Kafka — fully managed Kafka clusters for high-throughput event streaming with Kafka API compatibility
Latency ~200ms (real-time) 60 seconds minimum (near real-time) Sub-second processing Low latency for video Millisecond to seconds Low single-digit milliseconds — producer-to-consumer latency; comparable to self-managed Kafka
Data Retention No retention (immediate delivery) None (transient buffer) None (processing only) 1 hour to 10 years Configurable (Kafka retention) Configurable (hours to unlimited)
Scaling Model Manual shard splitting/merging Automatic scaling KPU (Kinesis Processing Units) Automatic scaling Broker nodes (manual) Automatic broker scaling (via partitions)
Destinations Consumer applications (Lambda, EC2, etc.) S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, Splunk, HTTP Kinesis Streams, Firehose, Lambda S3, SageMaker, Rekognition Any Kafka consumer Kafka consumers, S3, Redshift, OpenSearch (via Kafka Connect or MSK Connect), Lambda, custom apps
Processing Capability No custom processing (Lambda transforms) Lightweight (Lambda transform) Full SQL and Apache Flink None (ingestion only) Via Kafka Streams, Flink, etc. Use Kafka ecosystem tools
Pricing Model Per shard-hour + PUT payload units Per GB ingested + format conversion Per KPU-hour used Per GB ingested + storage Per broker-hour + storage + data transfer Per broker-hour + per GB storage; MSK Serverless per cluster-hour + partition-hour + data in/out
Best Use Case Load data to S3, Redshift, Elasticsearch Load streaming data to data stores Real-time analytics, aggregations Video analytics, live streaming Kafka-compatible workloads Kafka-based streaming applications
Management Level Semi-managed — provision shards (or on-demand); manage consumer applications; AWS handles infrastructure Fully managed — no shards or capacity planning; auto-scales; zero administration delivery pipeline Fully managed — serverless Apache Flink; auto-scales; no infrastructure management needed Fully managed — ingest, store, and process video/audio streams; no infrastructure to manage Semi-managed — AWS manages Kafka brokers/ZooKeeper; customer manages topics, partitions, consumer groups Managed Kafka (you manage topics)
💡 Mnemonics

KFD = Kinesis for custom, Firehose for loading, MSK for Kafka lovers

⚠️ Exam Tips

Kinesis Data Streams requires managing shards - more control but more complexity

Firehose is simpler - it automatically delivers to destinations (S3, Redshift, ES)

MSK is for teams already familiar with Apache Kafka

Kinesis Data Analytics can process streaming data with SQL

Kinesis is NOT for message queuing - use SQS for that

Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
A massively scalable and durable real-time data streaming service that can continuously capture gigabytes of data per second from hundreds of thousands of sources.
Kinesis Data Streams
Primary Purpose: Real-time data ingestion | Latency: ~200ms (real-time) | Data Retention: No retention (immediate delivery) | Scaling Model: Manual shard splitting/merging | Destinations: Consumer applications (Lambda, EC2, etc.) | Processing Capability: No custom processing (Lambda transforms) | Pricing Model: Per shard-hour + PUT payload units | Best Use Case: Load data to S3, Redshift, Elasticsearch | Management Level: Semi-managed — provision shards (or on-demand); manage consumer applications; AWS handles infrastructure
Kinesis Data Firehose
Primary Purpose: Data delivery to destinations | Latency: 60 seconds minimum (near real-time) | Data Retention: None (transient buffer) | Scaling Model: Automatic scaling | Destinations: S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, Splunk, HTTP | Processing Capability: Lightweight (Lambda transform) | Pricing Model: Per GB ingested + format conversion | Best Use Case: Load streaming data to data stores | Management Level: Fully managed — no shards or capacity planning; auto-scales; zero administration delivery pipeline
Kinesis Data Analytics
Primary Purpose: Real-time stream processing | Latency: Sub-second processing | Data Retention: None (processing only) | Scaling Model: KPU (Kinesis Processing Units) | Destinations: Kinesis Streams, Firehose, Lambda | Processing Capability: Full SQL and Apache Flink | Pricing Model: Per KPU-hour used | Best Use Case: Real-time analytics, aggregations | Management Level: Fully managed — serverless Apache Flink; auto-scales; no infrastructure management needed
Kinesis Video Streams
Primary Purpose: Video stream ingestion | Latency: Low latency for video | Data Retention: 1 hour to 10 years | Scaling Model: Automatic scaling | Destinations: S3, SageMaker, Rekognition | Processing Capability: None (ingestion only) | Pricing Model: Per GB ingested + storage | Best Use Case: Video analytics, live streaming | Management Level: Fully managed — ingest, store, and process video/audio streams; no infrastructure to manage
MSK (Managed Kafka)
Primary Purpose: Managed Apache Kafka service | Latency: Millisecond to seconds | Data Retention: Configurable (Kafka retention) | Scaling Model: Broker nodes (manual) | Destinations: Any Kafka consumer | Processing Capability: Via Kafka Streams, Flink, etc. | Pricing Model: Per broker-hour + storage + data transfer | Best Use Case: Kafka-compatible workloads | Management Level: Semi-managed — AWS manages Kafka brokers/ZooKeeper; customer manages topics, partitions, consumer groups
Amazon MSK
Primary Purpose: Managed Apache Kafka — fully managed Kafka clusters for high-throughput event streaming with Kafka API compatibility | Latency: Low single-digit milliseconds — producer-to-consumer latency; comparable to self-managed Kafka | Data Retention: Configurable (hours to unlimited) | Scaling Model: Automatic broker scaling (via partitions) | Destinations: Kafka consumers, S3, Redshift, OpenSearch (via Kafka Connect or MSK Connect), Lambda, custom apps | Processing Capability: Use Kafka ecosystem tools | Pricing Model: Per broker-hour + per GB storage; MSK Serverless per cluster-hour + partition-hour + data in/out | Best Use Case: Kafka-based streaming applications | Management Level: Managed Kafka (you manage topics)
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#130 Workflow Orchestration Comparison: Step Functions (Standard vs Express) vs SWF
Messaging & Events workflow_orchestration_comparison_step_functions_standard_vs 3 cols 12 rows
▼

AWS Step Functions provides workflow orchestration for distributed applications and microservices. It offers two workflow types optimized for different use cases. Amazon SWF is a legacy service that may appear on exams for migration scenarios. Understanding the differences in execution model, duration limits, and pricing helps select the appropriate service for business processes.

Feature Step Functions Standard Step Functions Express Amazon SWF (Legacy)
Max Execution Duration Up to 1 year Up to 5 minutes Up to 1 year
Execution Model Durable, exactly-once execution At-least-once execution (idempotent design needed) Durable execution with decision tasks
State Transitions Unlimited within duration Unlimited within duration Unlimited within duration
Pricing Model Per state transition ($0.025 per 1,000 transitions) Per execution + duration ($1.00 per million + GB-seconds) Per workflow execution + decision tasks
Execution Rate Up to 2,000 executions/second Up to 100,000 executions/second Lower throughput limits
Execution History Full history retained (90 days) CloudWatch Logs only (configurable) Full history available
Error Handling Retry logic, catchers, Dead Letter Queues Retry logic, catchers, CloudWatch Logs Manual error handling in deciders
Wait States Free (no charge during wait) Billed for duration (including wait time) Billed for active workflows
Service Integration AWS SDK integration (200+ services), Lambda, nested workflows AWS SDK integration, Lambda, nested workflows Activity workers and deciders (manual integration)
Visual Workflow Built-in visual editor and execution graph Built-in visual editor and execution graph No native visual workflow
Use Cases Order processing, data pipelines, human approval workflows IoT data ingestion, streaming processing, microservice orchestration Legacy applications (migrate to Step Functions)
Exam Relevance Primary choice for durable workflows High-volume, short-duration processing Know it exists for migration questions only
⚠️ Exam Tips

Step Functions Standard = long-running (up to 1 year), exactly-once, for business workflows

Step Functions Express = high-volume (up to 5 min), at-least-once, for event processing

SWF is legacy — only use if migrating existing SWF workflows, otherwise always use Step Functions

Step Functions supports 200+ AWS service integrations directly in workflow steps

For the exam: 'orchestrate microservices' = Step Functions Standard; 'high-volume short tasks' = Express

Step Functions Standard
Durable workflow orchestration with up to 1-year execution. Supports exactly-once execution with full execution history. Priced per state transition. Best for long-running business processes.
Step Functions Express
High-volume, short-duration workflow execution (up to 5 minutes). At-least-once execution model. Priced per execution and duration. Best for high-volume event processing and IoT data ingestion.
Amazon SWF (Legacy)
Simple Workflow Service — the original AWS workflow orchestration service. Supports external worker processes (deciders and activity workers). Superseded by Step Functions for most use cases.
Step Functions Standard
Max Execution Duration: Up to 1 year | Execution Model: Durable, exactly-once execution | State Transitions: Unlimited within duration | Pricing Model: Per state transition ($0.025 per 1,000 transitions) | Execution Rate: Up to 2,000 executions/second | Execution History: Full history retained (90 days) | Error Handling: Retry logic, catchers, Dead Letter Queues | Wait States: Free (no charge during wait) | Service Integration: AWS SDK integration (200+ services), Lambda, nested workflows | Visual Workflow: Built-in visual editor and execution graph | Use Cases: Order processing, data pipelines, human approval workflows | Exam Relevance: Primary choice for durable workflows
Step Functions Express
Max Execution Duration: Up to 5 minutes | Execution Model: At-least-once execution (idempotent design needed) | State Transitions: Unlimited within duration | Pricing Model: Per execution + duration ($1.00 per million + GB-seconds) | Execution Rate: Up to 100,000 executions/second | Execution History: CloudWatch Logs only (configurable) | Error Handling: Retry logic, catchers, CloudWatch Logs | Wait States: Billed for duration (including wait time) | Service Integration: AWS SDK integration, Lambda, nested workflows | Visual Workflow: Built-in visual editor and execution graph | Use Cases: IoT data ingestion, streaming processing, microservice orchestration | Exam Relevance: High-volume, short-duration processing
Amazon SWF (Legacy)
Max Execution Duration: Up to 1 year | Execution Model: Durable execution with decision tasks | State Transitions: Unlimited within duration | Pricing Model: Per workflow execution + decision tasks | Execution Rate: Lower throughput limits | Execution History: Full history available | Error Handling: Manual error handling in deciders | Wait States: Billed for active workflows | Service Integration: Activity workers and deciders (manual integration) | Visual Workflow: No native visual workflow | Use Cases: Legacy applications (migrate to Step Functions) | Exam Relevance: Know it exists for migration questions only
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

Security 10 tables
#131 AI Security Threats and Mitigations
Security AIF-006_2 4 cols 9 rows
▼

Comparison of AI security threats and AWS mitigation strategies

Comparing AI security threats and their mitigations - critical for Domain 5 (Security, Compliance, and Governance)

Feature Prompt Injection Jailbreak Attacks Data Poisoning Model Extraction
Definition Malicious prompts to manipulate output Bypassing safety controls Corrupting training data Stealing model parameters
Attack Vector Override system prompts, extract data, execute unauthorized actions Specially crafted prompts exploiting model reasoning Compromise model behavior, create backdoors API query exploitation
Impact Unauthorized outputs, data leaks Bypassed safety filters Degraded model accuracy IP theft, competitive advantage loss
Mitigation (AWS) Input validation, context isolation, Bedrock guardrails Bedrock content filters, denied topics, output validation Data validation, provenance tracking, AWS Glue Data Quality API rate limiting, access controls
Detection Difficulty Moderate - can be embedded in seemingly normal text Moderate - sophisticated prompt patterns High - subtle data changes hard to detect API usage monitoring
Primary Service Amazon Bedrock Guardrails Amazon Bedrock Guardrails SageMaker, Macie IAM, CloudTrail
Compliance Impact High - data breach risk High - safety bypass Medium - model quality High - IP risk
Prevention Best Practices Separate user input from system instructions, validate inputs Use Guardrails for Bedrock, implement prompt templates with strict system instructions, apply content filters, and monitor for pattern-based attacks Data lineage, vendor assessment, dataset auditing Implement API rate limiting, enforce authentication and access controls, monitor for unusual query patterns, use AWS CloudTrail for API auditing
Relevance to Exams High for AIF-C01 - Domain 5 coverage AIF-C01 Domain 5 — understand how Guardrails for Bedrock prevents prompt-based attacks and content filter bypasses Medium for AIF-C01 - training pipeline security AIF-C01 Domain 5 — know API security best practices, rate limiting, and monitoring with CloudWatch/CloudTrail
💡 Mnemonics

PI-JB-DP-ME: Prompt Injection manipulates, JailBreak bypasses, Data Poison corrupts, Model Extract steals

Prompt injection and jailbreaks are mitigated by Guardrails. Data poisoning needs data governance. Model extraction needs access controls.

P.I.J.D - Prompt Injection jams, Jailbreaking jumps rules, Data Poisoning poisons

⚠️ Exam Tips

Prompt injection is the most common AI security threat for GenAI applications

Guardrails for Bedrock is the primary defense against prompt-based attacks

Jailbreak attacks try to bypass safety filters with creative prompts

Data poisoning attacks target the training phase, not inference

Always use least privilege IAM for model API access

Enable CloudTrail for auditing all AI service API calls

Always separate system instructions from user input in prompts

Bedrock agents should validate tool outputs before using them

Guardrails can help prevent but cannot eliminate all jailbreak attempts

Data provenance is critical for preventing poisoning in RAG systems

Regular monitoring and model evaluation helps detect compromised behavior

Prompt Injection
A security attack where malicious actors craft inputs designed to manipulate an AI model into producing unintended outputs, potentially exposing sensitive information or bypassing content filters. The primary mitigation is Guardrails for Bedrock with input validation.
Jailbreak Attacks
Sophisticated prompt engineering attacks designed to bypass an AI model's safety controls and content filters. These attacks exploit the model's training to circumvent restrictions and produce prohibited content. Mitigated through Guardrails and prompt templates.
Data Poisoning
An attack that corrupts the training data used to build ML models, causing the model to learn incorrect patterns and produce unreliable or malicious outputs. Requires robust data governance, validation pipelines, and versioning.
Model Extraction
An attack where adversaries query a model API repeatedly to reverse-engineer and steal the model's parameters or training data. Mitigated through API rate limiting, access controls, and monitoring for suspicious query patterns.
Prompt Injection
Definition: Malicious prompts to manipulate output | Attack Vector: Override system prompts, extract data, execute unauthorized actions | Impact: Unauthorized outputs, data leaks | Mitigation (AWS): Input validation, context isolation, Bedrock guardrails | Detection Difficulty: Moderate - can be embedded in seemingly normal text | Primary Service: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails | Compliance Impact: High - data breach risk | Prevention Best Practices: Separate user input from system instructions, validate inputs | Relevance to Exams: High for AIF-C01 - Domain 5 coverage
Jailbreak Attacks
Definition: Bypassing safety controls | Attack Vector: Specially crafted prompts exploiting model reasoning | Impact: Bypassed safety filters | Mitigation (AWS): Bedrock content filters, denied topics, output validation | Detection Difficulty: Moderate - sophisticated prompt patterns | Primary Service: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails | Compliance Impact: High - safety bypass | Prevention Best Practices: Use Guardrails for Bedrock, implement prompt templates with strict system instructions, apply content filters, and monitor for pattern-based attacks | Relevance to Exams: AIF-C01 Domain 5 — understand how Guardrails for Bedrock prevents prompt-based attacks and content filter bypasses
Data Poisoning
Definition: Corrupting training data | Attack Vector: Compromise model behavior, create backdoors | Impact: Degraded model accuracy | Mitigation (AWS): Data validation, provenance tracking, AWS Glue Data Quality | Detection Difficulty: High - subtle data changes hard to detect | Primary Service: SageMaker, Macie | Compliance Impact: Medium - model quality | Prevention Best Practices: Data lineage, vendor assessment, dataset auditing | Relevance to Exams: Medium for AIF-C01 - training pipeline security
Model Extraction
Definition: Stealing model parameters | Attack Vector: API query exploitation | Impact: IP theft, competitive advantage loss | Mitigation (AWS): API rate limiting, access controls | Detection Difficulty: API usage monitoring | Primary Service: IAM, CloudTrail | Compliance Impact: High - IP risk | Prevention Best Practices: Implement API rate limiting, enforce authentication and access controls, monitor for unusual query patterns, use AWS CloudTrail for API auditing | Relevance to Exams: AIF-C01 Domain 5 — know API security best practices, rate limiting, and monitoring with CloudWatch/CloudTrail
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

#132 AI Security Tools — IAM vs KMS vs Macie vs PrivateLink
Security AIF-010 4 cols 4 rows
▼

Securing AI systems on AWS requires the same foundational security services used everywhere else in AWS. The exam tests whether you know which service addresses which type of security concern in the context of AI/ML workloads. The Shared Responsibility Model applies equally here — AWS secures the infrastructure, you secure your data and access.

Feature AWS IAM AWS KMS Amazon Macie AWS PrivateLink
Purpose Access control — who can do what Encryption key management — protect data confidentiality Sensitive data discovery and classification in S3 Private network connectivity — no public internet exposure
Security Layer Identity & Access Data Encryption (at rest + in transit) Data Classification / Compliance Network Security
AI/ML Use Case Control which data scientists can access SageMaker, which models are available via Bedrock Encrypt S3 training data, encrypt model artifacts, encrypt Bedrock logs Find PII in training datasets that shouldn't be there Keep SageMaker training traffic private from internet
Principle Least Privilege — grant only the minimum permissions required Encryption at rest and in transit Data governance — know what sensitive data you have Network isolation — minimize attack surface
⚠️ Exam Tips

IAM controls WHO can access AI resources — use fine-grained policies for SageMaker and Bedrock

KMS encrypts data at rest and in transit — model artifacts, training data, and inference data

Macie discovers sensitive data (PII, financial data) in S3 — critical before feeding data to ML models

PrivateLink keeps API calls to SageMaker/Bedrock within the AWS network — no public internet exposure

For the exam: 'protect data in S3' = Macie + KMS; 'private API access' = PrivateLink; 'access control' = IAM

AWS IAM
IAM (Identity and Access Management) controls WHO can access WHAT on AWS — it defines roles, policies, and permissions that govern which users and services can interact with AI resources like SageMaker notebooks, Bedrock models, and training data in S3.
AWS KMS
KMS (Key Management Service) handles ENCRYPTION — it creates and manages the cryptographic keys used to encrypt data at rest (S3 buckets, EBS volumes) and in transit. In AI contexts, KMS protects training datasets, model artifacts, and inference logs.
Amazon Macie
Macie uses ML to automatically DISCOVER and CLASSIFY sensitive data (PII, financial data, health records) stored in S3 buckets — it tells you where your sensitive training data is and flags any security risks around it.
AWS PrivateLink
PrivateLink creates private network connections between your VPC and AWS services — traffic never touches the public internet. In AI contexts, it ensures your model training jobs and API calls to SageMaker or Bedrock stay within AWS's private network.
AWS IAM
Purpose: Access control — who can do what | Security Layer: Identity & Access | AI/ML Use Case: Control which data scientists can access SageMaker, which models are available via Bedrock | Principle: Least Privilege — grant only the minimum permissions required
AWS KMS
Purpose: Encryption key management — protect data confidentiality | Security Layer: Data Encryption (at rest + in transit) | AI/ML Use Case: Encrypt S3 training data, encrypt model artifacts, encrypt Bedrock logs | Principle: Encryption at rest and in transit
Amazon Macie
Purpose: Sensitive data discovery and classification in S3 | Security Layer: Data Classification / Compliance | AI/ML Use Case: Find PII in training datasets that shouldn't be there | Principle: Data governance — know what sensitive data you have
AWS PrivateLink
Purpose: Private network connectivity — no public internet exposure | Security Layer: Network Security | AI/ML Use Case: Keep SageMaker training traffic private from internet | Principle: Network isolation — minimize attack surface
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#133 AI Security and Governance Services
Security aif_d5_security_governance_services 6 cols 10 rows
▼

Comparing AWS services for AI security, compliance, and governance

Feature IAM Amazon Macie AWS PrivateLink AWS Config AWS CloudTrail AWS Audit Manager
Primary focus Access control Sensitive data discovery Private service connectivity Configuration compliance API audit trail Audit evidence workflow
Best exam clue Roles, policies, permissions PII or sensitive data in S3 Private access path to services Track and evaluate config state Who changed what Collect compliance evidence
Great for Least privilege Data privacy posture Reducing public exposure Governance checks Forensics and auditing Compliance operations
Not primarily for Sensitive data discovery User permissioning Audit logs Detailed API event history Policy evidence packaging Runtime network isolation
Common trap Thinking auth alone solves data governance Treating it like a general-purpose SIEM Confusing it with VPN or Direct Connect Confusing it with CloudTrail Confusing it with Config state evaluation Assuming it replaces logs
Purpose Identity and access management — control who can access AWS resources and what actions they can perform Data security — automatically discovers, classifies, and protects sensitive data (PII, PHI) in S3 Network security — provides private connectivity to AWS services without traversing the public internet Configuration compliance — continuously monitors and records AWS resource configurations against rules Audit trail of all AWS API activity Audit automation — continuously collects evidence to help assess compliance with regulations and standards
Use Case Control access to SageMaker, Bedrock, S3 training data; enforce least-privilege for ML pipelines Scan S3 buckets for PII in training datasets; prevent sensitive data from entering ML pipelines Access SageMaker endpoints and Bedrock APIs privately from VPC without internet exposure Monitor SageMaker endpoint configurations; detect non-compliant encryption or logging settings Security audits, incident investigation Automate evidence collection for AI governance audits; map controls to SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR frameworks
What It Captures Authentication events (CloudTrail), authorization decisions, policy evaluations Sensitive data findings (PII types, locations), S3 bucket security posture, data access patterns VPC Flow Logs capture private endpoint traffic; no data content captured Resource configuration history, configuration changes, compliance status against rules User identity, API action, timestamp, source IP Automated evidence from CloudTrail, Config, Security Hub; manual evidence uploads for controls
Audience Security engineers, DevOps, platform teams — anyone managing AWS access and permissions Data protection officers, security teams, compliance officers managing sensitive data Network architects, security teams requiring private connectivity to AWS services Cloud architects, compliance teams monitoring infrastructure configuration drift Security teams, auditors Internal audit teams, compliance managers, risk assessors preparing for regulatory audits
Regulatory Value Foundational for all compliance frameworks — enforces access control required by SOC 2, HIPAA, PCI DSS, ISO 27001 GDPR data discovery, HIPAA PHI detection, PCI DSS cardholder data identification in S3 Meets network isolation requirements for HIPAA, PCI DSS, FedRAMP — data never traverses public internet Continuous compliance monitoring for CIS benchmarks, SOC 2, HIPAA; configuration audit trail Audit and compliance requirements Direct mapping to compliance frameworks (SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR, PCI DSS); automated evidence collection
💡 Mnemonics

MCI = Model Cards for documentation, CloudTrail for audit, IAM for access control

⚠️ Exam Tips

Model Cards are essential for AI governance and model transparency

CloudTrail provides the audit trail needed for AI compliance

Implement IAM roles with least privilege for all AI/ML services

Data lineage tracking is part of AI governance - use SageMaker ML Lineage Tracking

AWS Config rules can check AI resource configurations for compliance

IAM
A feature that creates standardized documentation for ML models, capturing purpose, performance metrics, training details, and relevant warnings for stakeholders.
Amazon Macie
Macie helps discover sensitive data, especially in Amazon S3. It is the privacy and data exposure clue in AI data governance scenarios.
AWS PrivateLink
PrivateLink provides private access to supported services without exposing traffic over the public internet. It is a strong answer when AI service access must stay private.
AWS Config
AWS Config records and evaluates resource configuration state over time. It is the governance service for configuration visibility and compliance checks.
AWS CloudTrail
A service that logs API calls and user activity across AWS, providing audit trails essential for AI governance and compliance requirements.
AWS Audit Manager
Audit Manager helps gather and organize evidence for audits and compliance workflows. It is the answer when the process is about audit readiness rather than raw event logging.
IAM
Primary focus: Access control | Best exam clue: Roles, policies, permissions | Great for: Least privilege | Not primarily for: Sensitive data discovery | Common trap: Thinking auth alone solves data governance | Purpose: Identity and access management — control who can access AWS resources and what actions they can perform | Use Case: Control access to SageMaker, Bedrock, S3 training data; enforce least-privilege for ML pipelines | What It Captures: Authentication events (CloudTrail), authorization decisions, policy evaluations | Audience: Security engineers, DevOps, platform teams — anyone managing AWS access and permissions | Regulatory Value: Foundational for all compliance frameworks — enforces access control required by SOC 2, HIPAA, PCI DSS, ISO 27001
Amazon Macie
Primary focus: Sensitive data discovery | Best exam clue: PII or sensitive data in S3 | Great for: Data privacy posture | Not primarily for: User permissioning | Common trap: Treating it like a general-purpose SIEM | Purpose: Data security — automatically discovers, classifies, and protects sensitive data (PII, PHI) in S3 | Use Case: Scan S3 buckets for PII in training datasets; prevent sensitive data from entering ML pipelines | What It Captures: Sensitive data findings (PII types, locations), S3 bucket security posture, data access patterns | Audience: Data protection officers, security teams, compliance officers managing sensitive data | Regulatory Value: GDPR data discovery, HIPAA PHI detection, PCI DSS cardholder data identification in S3
AWS PrivateLink
Primary focus: Private service connectivity | Best exam clue: Private access path to services | Great for: Reducing public exposure | Not primarily for: Audit logs | Common trap: Confusing it with VPN or Direct Connect | Purpose: Network security — provides private connectivity to AWS services without traversing the public internet | Use Case: Access SageMaker endpoints and Bedrock APIs privately from VPC without internet exposure | What It Captures: VPC Flow Logs capture private endpoint traffic; no data content captured | Audience: Network architects, security teams requiring private connectivity to AWS services | Regulatory Value: Meets network isolation requirements for HIPAA, PCI DSS, FedRAMP — data never traverses public internet
AWS Config
Primary focus: Configuration compliance | Best exam clue: Track and evaluate config state | Great for: Governance checks | Not primarily for: Detailed API event history | Common trap: Confusing it with CloudTrail | Purpose: Configuration compliance — continuously monitors and records AWS resource configurations against rules | Use Case: Monitor SageMaker endpoint configurations; detect non-compliant encryption or logging settings | What It Captures: Resource configuration history, configuration changes, compliance status against rules | Audience: Cloud architects, compliance teams monitoring infrastructure configuration drift | Regulatory Value: Continuous compliance monitoring for CIS benchmarks, SOC 2, HIPAA; configuration audit trail
AWS CloudTrail
Primary focus: API audit trail | Best exam clue: Who changed what | Great for: Forensics and auditing | Not primarily for: Policy evidence packaging | Common trap: Confusing it with Config state evaluation | Purpose: Audit trail of all AWS API activity | Use Case: Security audits, incident investigation | What It Captures: User identity, API action, timestamp, source IP | Audience: Security teams, auditors | Regulatory Value: Audit and compliance requirements
AWS Audit Manager
Primary focus: Audit evidence workflow | Best exam clue: Collect compliance evidence | Great for: Compliance operations | Not primarily for: Runtime network isolation | Common trap: Assuming it replaces logs | Purpose: Audit automation — continuously collects evidence to help assess compliance with regulations and standards | Use Case: Automate evidence collection for AI governance audits; map controls to SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR frameworks | What It Captures: Automated evidence from CloudTrail, Config, Security Hub; manual evidence uploads for controls | Audience: Internal audit teams, compliance managers, risk assessors preparing for regulatory audits | Regulatory Value: Direct mapping to compliance frameworks (SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR, PCI DSS); automated evidence collection
🎯 Why This Table Matters

AIF Domain 5 covers securing AI systems and handling governance, compliance, and auditability. This table keeps access control, data protection, private connectivity, configuration visibility, and audit trails from getting mashed together.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#134 AWS Security Services Comparison
Security SAA-014 4 cols 13 rows
▼

This table compares AWS security services for protecting infrastructure, data, and applications. GuardDuty provides intelligent threat detection, Macie discovers and protects sensitive data, WAF protects web applications, Shield provides DDoS protection, and KMS manages encryption keys.

Feature AWS Shield AWS WAF AWS GuardDuty Amazon Inspector
Protection Target Network availability (Layer 3/4) Web applications (Layer 7) AWS accounts, workloads, data Vulnerability assessment
Layer Network (L3/L4) and Application Application (L7) Account and workload EC2, Lambda, containers
Attack Types Blocked DDoS floods, SYN floods, amplification SQL injection, XSS, bot attacks Unusual API calls, compromised credentials Software vulnerabilities, CVEs
Deployment Automatic (Standard), subscription (Advanced) Web ACLs on CloudFront/ALB/API Gateway One-click enablement, no agents Managed service, S3 integration
Real-time Protection Yes - inline mitigation Yes - request filtering Near real-time alerts Scheduled scans
Pricing Tier Free (Standard), subscription (Advanced) Per rule + request count Per GB analyzed + findings Per instance scan duration
Integration Route 53, CloudFront, Global Accelerator CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, CloudWatch Security Hub, SNS, Lambda, EventBridge Security Hub, Systems Manager
Best For DDoS protection for web apps OWASP Top 10 protection Account compromise detection Pre-deployment security review
Security Function DDoS Protection Application Firewall Threat detection — continuously monitors for malicious activity and unauthorized behavior in AWS accounts Vulnerability assessment — automatically scans EC2 instances, Lambda functions, and container images for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure
Technology DDoS mitigation, traffic engineering Rule-based filtering, rate limiting ML-based anomaly detection analyzing VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail events, DNS logs, and EKS audit logs Agent-based and agentless scanning using CVE databases; network reachability analysis
Automation Automatic mitigation Managed rules, rate-based rules Auto-enabled per account; findings can trigger EventBridge rules → Lambda/SNS for automated remediation Continuous automated scanning; findings pushed to Security Hub and EventBridge for automated workflows
Compliance Relevance DDoS mitigation for compliance PCI DSS 6.6, OWASP Top 10 Supports compliance monitoring for PCI DSS, HIPAA; provides audit trail of threat findings Supports CIS benchmarks, CVE tracking; helps meet vulnerability management requirements for PCI DSS, SOC 2
Key Features Always-on detection, cost protection (Advanced) SQL injection, XSS protection, geo-blocking, bot control Intelligent threat detection, cryptocurrency mining detection, S3 protection, EKS runtime monitoring, malware scanning Automated CVE scanning, network reachability analysis, Lambda code scanning, SBOM export, risk scoring
💡 Mnemonics

Shield = DDoS Shield, WAF = Web Application Firewall, GuardDuty = Guard against threats, Inspector = Inspect vulnerabilities

Need DDoS protection? Shield. Need web app firewall? WAF. Need threat detection? GuardDuty. Need vulnerability scanning? Inspector.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Shield Standard is free and automatic for all AWS customers

Shield Advanced provides 24/7 DDoS response team (DRT) access

WAF protects against OWASP Top 10 (SQLi, XSS, etc.)

GuardDuty uses ML to detect anomalies in CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, DNS

Inspector scans EC2, Lambda, and container images for vulnerabilities

Use Shield + WAF together for comprehensive web app protection

AWS Shield
A managed DDoS protection service that safeguards applications running on AWS. Shield Standard provides automatic protection at no extra cost, while Shield Advanced offers enhanced detection, mitigation, and 24/7 access to the AWS DDoS Response Team.
AWS WAF
A web application firewall that helps protect web applications from common exploits like SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS). Provides rules to filter malicious web traffic before it reaches your application on CloudFront, ALB, or API Gateway.
AWS GuardDuty
A threat detection service that continuously monitors for malicious activity and unauthorized behavior. Analyzes CloudTrail logs, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs using machine learning and threat intelligence to detect compromised credentials, cryptocurrency mining, and other threats.
Amazon Inspector
An automated vulnerability management service that scans AWS workloads (EC2, Lambda, container images) for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure. Provides detailed findings with remediation recommendations integrated with Security Hub.
AWS Shield
Protection Target: Network availability (Layer 3/4) | Layer: Network (L3/L4) and Application | Attack Types Blocked: DDoS floods, SYN floods, amplification | Deployment: Automatic (Standard), subscription (Advanced) | Real-time Protection: Yes - inline mitigation | Pricing Tier: Free (Standard), subscription (Advanced) | Integration: Route 53, CloudFront, Global Accelerator | Best For: DDoS protection for web apps | Security Function: DDoS Protection | Technology: DDoS mitigation, traffic engineering | Automation: Automatic mitigation | Compliance Relevance: DDoS mitigation for compliance | Key Features: Always-on detection, cost protection (Advanced)
AWS WAF
Protection Target: Web applications (Layer 7) | Layer: Application (L7) | Attack Types Blocked: SQL injection, XSS, bot attacks | Deployment: Web ACLs on CloudFront/ALB/API Gateway | Real-time Protection: Yes - request filtering | Pricing Tier: Per rule + request count | Integration: CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, CloudWatch | Best For: OWASP Top 10 protection | Security Function: Application Firewall | Technology: Rule-based filtering, rate limiting | Automation: Managed rules, rate-based rules | Compliance Relevance: PCI DSS 6.6, OWASP Top 10 | Key Features: SQL injection, XSS protection, geo-blocking, bot control
AWS GuardDuty
Protection Target: AWS accounts, workloads, data | Layer: Account and workload | Attack Types Blocked: Unusual API calls, compromised credentials | Deployment: One-click enablement, no agents | Real-time Protection: Near real-time alerts | Pricing Tier: Per GB analyzed + findings | Integration: Security Hub, SNS, Lambda, EventBridge | Best For: Account compromise detection | Security Function: Threat detection — continuously monitors for malicious activity and unauthorized behavior in AWS accounts | Technology: ML-based anomaly detection analyzing VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail events, DNS logs, and EKS audit logs | Automation: Auto-enabled per account; findings can trigger EventBridge rules → Lambda/SNS for automated remediation | Compliance Relevance: Supports compliance monitoring for PCI DSS, HIPAA; provides audit trail of threat findings | Key Features: Intelligent threat detection, cryptocurrency mining detection, S3 protection, EKS runtime monitoring, malware scanning
Amazon Inspector
Protection Target: Vulnerability assessment | Layer: EC2, Lambda, containers | Attack Types Blocked: Software vulnerabilities, CVEs | Deployment: Managed service, S3 integration | Real-time Protection: Scheduled scans | Pricing Tier: Per instance scan duration | Integration: Security Hub, Systems Manager | Best For: Pre-deployment security review | Security Function: Vulnerability assessment — automatically scans EC2 instances, Lambda functions, and container images for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure | Technology: Agent-based and agentless scanning using CVE databases; network reachability analysis | Automation: Continuous automated scanning; findings pushed to Security Hub and EventBridge for automated workflows | Compliance Relevance: Supports CIS benchmarks, CVE tracking; helps meet vulnerability management requirements for PCI DSS, SOC 2 | Key Features: Automated CVE scanning, network reachability analysis, Lambda code scanning, SBOM export, risk scoring
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#135 AWS Shared Responsibility Model
Security SHARED-001 3 cols 7 rows
▼

The Shared Responsibility Model defines the security division between AWS and the customer. It is tested on ALL AWS certification exams. The core principle: AWS is responsible for security OF the cloud (hardware, facilities, hypervisor, managed services). YOU are responsible for security IN the cloud (your data, your configuration, your code, your access management).

Feature AWS Responsibility ('Of the Cloud') Customer Responsibility ('In the Cloud') Shared Responsibilities
Physical Security AWS — data centers, physical access, environmental controls Customer — own on-premises hardware (if any) Not shared — physical security is entirely AWS responsibility (data centers, hardware, networking infrastructure)
Hypervisor / Virtualization AWS Not applicable — hypervisor management is AWS responsibility; customer has no access to the hypervisor layer Not shared — hypervisor/virtualization layer is entirely AWS responsibility
Operating System (EC2) N/A (hardware only) CUSTOMER — OS patching, updates, hardening Not shared — OS patching and config is entirely customer responsibility on EC2; AWS handles it for managed services (Lambda, RDS)
Database Engine Patching (RDS) AWS patches the database engine software Customer sets maintenance windows and approves updates Shared — both contribute to patching process
Data Encryption AWS provides encryption tools (KMS) CUSTOMER must enable and configure encryption Shared — AWS provides tools, customer must use them
Network Traffic Encryption AWS secures inter-facility network links CUSTOMER must encrypt application traffic (SSL/TLS) Shared
IAM Configuration AWS provides IAM service and infrastructure CUSTOMER creates users, roles, policies, enforces MFA Shared
⚠️ Exam Tips

AWS is responsible for security OF the cloud (hardware, facilities, hypervisor, managed service infrastructure)

Customer is responsible for security IN the cloud (data, configuration, access management, encryption)

For managed services (RDS, S3, Lambda), AWS handles more of the stack — but you still own data and access

Patch management is SHARED: AWS patches managed services, you patch EC2 OS and applications

This concept is tested on EVERY AWS exam — know the boundary between AWS and customer responsibilities

AWS Responsibility ('Of the Cloud')
AWS secures everything that makes the cloud work — the physical data centers, the hardware servers, the network infrastructure, the hypervisor (virtualization layer), and the hardware/software of managed services like RDS, Lambda, and S3. Customers never see or touch any of this.
Customer Responsibility ('In the Cloud')
Customers are responsible for what they put INTO the cloud and how they configure it — their data and its encryption, who they grant access to (IAM), how they configure security groups and network settings, their application code, and any OS or software running on EC2 instances.
Shared Responsibilities
Some responsibilities are shared — both AWS and the customer contribute. Patch management is shared: AWS patches the RDS database engine, but you patch the OS on your EC2 instances. Configuration management and training are also shared — AWS provides the tools, you must use them correctly.
AWS Responsibility ('Of the Cloud')
Physical Security: AWS — data centers, physical access, environmental controls | Hypervisor / Virtualization: AWS | Operating System (EC2): N/A (hardware only) | Database Engine Patching (RDS): AWS patches the database engine software | Data Encryption: AWS provides encryption tools (KMS) | Network Traffic Encryption: AWS secures inter-facility network links | IAM Configuration: AWS provides IAM service and infrastructure
Customer Responsibility ('In the Cloud')
Physical Security: Customer — own on-premises hardware (if any) | Hypervisor / Virtualization: Not applicable — hypervisor management is AWS responsibility; customer has no access to the hypervisor layer | Operating System (EC2): CUSTOMER — OS patching, updates, hardening | Database Engine Patching (RDS): Customer sets maintenance windows and approves updates | Data Encryption: CUSTOMER must enable and configure encryption | Network Traffic Encryption: CUSTOMER must encrypt application traffic (SSL/TLS) | IAM Configuration: CUSTOMER creates users, roles, policies, enforces MFA
Shared Responsibilities
Physical Security: Not shared — physical security is entirely AWS responsibility (data centers, hardware, networking infrastructure) | Hypervisor / Virtualization: Not shared — hypervisor/virtualization layer is entirely AWS responsibility | Operating System (EC2): Not shared — OS patching and config is entirely customer responsibility on EC2; AWS handles it for managed services (Lambda, RDS) | Database Engine Patching (RDS): Shared — both contribute to patching process | Data Encryption: Shared — AWS provides tools, customer must use them | Network Traffic Encryption: Shared | IAM Configuration: Shared
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#136 Compliance and Audit Services Comparison
Security compliance_and_audit_services_comparison 4 cols 9 rows
▼

AWS provides comprehensive services for compliance monitoring, audit logging, and regulatory evidence collection. CloudTrail records API activity across your AWS infrastructure for security analysis and compliance auditing, Config tracks resource configurations and evaluates them against desired rules, Audit Manager automates evidence collection for compliance frameworks, and Artifact provides on-demand access to AWS compliance reports. Understanding when to use each service, their data retention policies, and integration patterns is essential for the exam. These services help organizations meet regulatory requirements, maintain security posture, and demonstrate compliance to auditors.

Feature CloudTrail Config Audit Manager Artifact
Primary Purpose Record and store AWS API call history for security analysis and compliance auditing Track resource configurations, evaluate compliance against rules, and monitor changes Automate evidence collection for compliance audits against industry frameworks Provide on-demand access to AWS compliance reports and agreements
What It Records API calls (management events), data events (S3, Lambda), network activity events Resource configuration snapshots, configuration changes, compliance evaluations Evidence from AWS services mapped to compliance framework controls AWS SOC, PCI DSS, ISO, FedRAMP reports and Business Associate Agreements (BAA)
Data Retention Event history: 90 days (free); Trails: indefinite in S3 (configurable lifecycle) Configuration history: 7 years; Snapshots: retained based on delivery frequency Evidence retained based on assessment settings; typically 1+ years Reports updated regularly; historical versions available
Pricing Model Management events: first trail free per region; Data events: $0.10 per 100,000 events $0.003 per configuration item recorded; $0.001 per rule evaluation $0.25 per evidence collected; varies by number of controls assessed Free - included with AWS account
Compliance Frameworks Supports compliance by providing audit trail; not framework-specific Managed rules for CIS, PCI DSS, HIPAA, NIST; custom rule support Pre-built frameworks: CIS, PCI DSS, HIPAA, FedRAMP, NIST, SOC 2, GDPR, custom SOC 1/2/3, PCI DSS, ISO 27001/27017/27018, FedRAMP, HIPAA, GDPR reports
Key Features Event history, multi-region trails, organization trails, Insights (anomaly detection) Configuration snapshots, change notifications, remediation actions, conformance packs Automated evidence collection, framework mapping, delegate admin, report generation NDA acceptance, report downloads, agreement management, multi-account access
Integration Points S3 (storage), CloudWatch Logs (monitoring), EventBridge (automation), SNS (notifications) SNS (notifications), Lambda (remediation), Security Hub (findings), CloudTrail (change tracking) CloudTrail, Config, Security Hub, manual evidence upload, S3 (evidence storage) IAM (access control), Organizations (multi-account access)
Automation Capabilities EventBridge rules for automated response; CloudWatch Alarms for anomalies Auto-remediation via Lambda; conformance packs for rule deployment Scheduled assessments; automatic evidence collection; automated report generation No automation - manual report download and NDA acceptance
Use Cases Security incident investigation, compliance auditing, operational troubleshooting Configuration drift detection, compliance monitoring, change management Preparing for external audits, continuous compliance monitoring, evidence collection Obtaining AWS compliance documentation for customer audits, BAA for HIPAA
⚠️ Exam Tips

CloudTrail = WHO did WHAT and WHEN (API call logging); Config = WHAT changed and IS IT compliant

Audit Manager = automated evidence collection for compliance frameworks (SOC 2, PCI, HIPAA)

Artifact = download AWS compliance reports (SOC, ISO, PCI) — it’s a document portal, not a monitoring tool

Enable CloudTrail in ALL regions and send logs to a centralized S3 bucket

Config Rules continuously evaluate resource compliance — use remediation actions for auto-fix

CloudTrail
Records API calls and management events across your AWS account. Provides a complete audit trail of who did what, when, and from where. Essential for security forensics and compliance auditing.
Config
Continuously records and evaluates AWS resource configurations against desired settings. Config Rules automatically check compliance. Provides a timeline of configuration changes for any resource.
Audit Manager
Automates evidence collection for audits against frameworks like SOC 2, PCI DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR. Maps AWS control activities to compliance requirements and generates audit-ready reports.
Artifact
A self-service portal for on-demand access to AWS compliance reports and agreements (SOC, PCI, ISO, BAA). Not a monitoring service — it provides documentation of AWS's own compliance certifications.
CloudTrail
Primary Purpose: Record and store AWS API call history for security analysis and compliance auditing | What It Records: API calls (management events), data events (S3, Lambda), network activity events | Data Retention: Event history: 90 days (free); Trails: indefinite in S3 (configurable lifecycle) | Pricing Model: Management events: first trail free per region; Data events: $0.10 per 100,000 events | Compliance Frameworks: Supports compliance by providing audit trail; not framework-specific | Key Features: Event history, multi-region trails, organization trails, Insights (anomaly detection) | Integration Points: S3 (storage), CloudWatch Logs (monitoring), EventBridge (automation), SNS (notifications) | Automation Capabilities: EventBridge rules for automated response; CloudWatch Alarms for anomalies | Use Cases: Security incident investigation, compliance auditing, operational troubleshooting
Config
Primary Purpose: Track resource configurations, evaluate compliance against rules, and monitor changes | What It Records: Resource configuration snapshots, configuration changes, compliance evaluations | Data Retention: Configuration history: 7 years; Snapshots: retained based on delivery frequency | Pricing Model: $0.003 per configuration item recorded; $0.001 per rule evaluation | Compliance Frameworks: Managed rules for CIS, PCI DSS, HIPAA, NIST; custom rule support | Key Features: Configuration snapshots, change notifications, remediation actions, conformance packs | Integration Points: SNS (notifications), Lambda (remediation), Security Hub (findings), CloudTrail (change tracking) | Automation Capabilities: Auto-remediation via Lambda; conformance packs for rule deployment | Use Cases: Configuration drift detection, compliance monitoring, change management
Audit Manager
Primary Purpose: Automate evidence collection for compliance audits against industry frameworks | What It Records: Evidence from AWS services mapped to compliance framework controls | Data Retention: Evidence retained based on assessment settings; typically 1+ years | Pricing Model: $0.25 per evidence collected; varies by number of controls assessed | Compliance Frameworks: Pre-built frameworks: CIS, PCI DSS, HIPAA, FedRAMP, NIST, SOC 2, GDPR, custom | Key Features: Automated evidence collection, framework mapping, delegate admin, report generation | Integration Points: CloudTrail, Config, Security Hub, manual evidence upload, S3 (evidence storage) | Automation Capabilities: Scheduled assessments; automatic evidence collection; automated report generation | Use Cases: Preparing for external audits, continuous compliance monitoring, evidence collection
Artifact
Primary Purpose: Provide on-demand access to AWS compliance reports and agreements | What It Records: AWS SOC, PCI DSS, ISO, FedRAMP reports and Business Associate Agreements (BAA) | Data Retention: Reports updated regularly; historical versions available | Pricing Model: Free - included with AWS account | Compliance Frameworks: SOC 1/2/3, PCI DSS, ISO 27001/27017/27018, FedRAMP, HIPAA, GDPR reports | Key Features: NDA acceptance, report downloads, agreement management, multi-account access | Integration Points: IAM (access control), Organizations (multi-account access) | Automation Capabilities: No automation - manual report download and NDA acceptance | Use Cases: Obtaining AWS compliance documentation for customer audits, BAA for HIPAA
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#137 Network Security Services Comparison
Security security_group_nacl_comparison 6 cols 12 rows
▼

AWS provides multiple layers of network security to protect applications from various attack vectors. WAF (Web Application Firewall) protects against Layer 7 application attacks like SQL injection and XSS, Shield provides DDoS protection at Layer 3/4, Network Firewall offers stateful inspection and intrusion prevention for VPC traffic, and Security Groups/NACLs provide foundational network access control. Understanding the OSI layer each service operates at, their deployment models, and use case differentiation is critical for the exam. These services work together to provide defense in depth for network security.

Feature WAF (Web Application Firewall) Shield Standard Shield Advanced Network Firewall Security Groups NACLs (Network ACLs)
OSI Layer Layer 7 (Application) - HTTP/HTTPS traffic only Layer 3 (Network) and Layer 4 (Transport) Layer 3 (Network) and Layer 4 (Transport) Layer 3-7 (Network through Application) Layer 4 (Transport) - TCP/UDP port level Layer 4 (Transport) - TCP/UDP port level
Primary Protection OWASP Top 10, SQL injection, XSS, bot control, rate limiting DDoS protection against common attacks (volumetric, state-exhaustion) Enhanced DDoS protection with 24/7 DRT, cost protection, detailed reporting Stateful inspection, intrusion prevention, domain filtering, TLS inspection Instance-level firewall controlling inbound/outbound traffic Subnet-level firewall controlling inbound/outbound traffic
Deployment Model Associated with CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, AppSync Automatically enabled for all AWS customers at no cost $3,000/month per organization + data transfer out fees Deployed in VPC subnets as a managed service; traffic routed through firewall endpoints Attached to ENIs (EC2 instances, Lambda, RDS, etc.) Associated with subnets; applies to all resources in subnet
Rule Evaluation Managed rule groups, custom rules, rate-based rules, regex patterns Automatic - no rules to configure Custom protections via WAF rules; DRT assistance Stateful rules (Suricata compatible), stateless rules, domain lists, IP lists Allow rules only (implicit deny); by protocol, port, source/destination Allow and Deny rules; by protocol, port, source/destination CIDR
Scope of Protection Application-specific (web applications behind CloudFront/ALB) All AWS resources globally (automatic) Resources you specify (EC2, ELB, CloudFront, Global Accelerator, Route 53) VPC-level protection for all outbound/inbound traffic Individual resource level (EC2, RDS, Lambda, etc.) Subnet level (all resources within subnet)
Statefulness Stateful - tracks request context Stateful - tracks connection state Stateful - tracks connection state Both stateful and stateless rules supported Stateful - return traffic automatically allowed Stateless - both directions must be explicitly allowed
Default Behavior Allow all by default; rules define what to block Always active; no configuration needed Must subscribe; then configure protected resources Allow all by default until rules are configured Deny all inbound; allow all outbound by default Allow all inbound and outbound by default
Logging & Monitoring Logs to CloudWatch, S3, Kinesis Data Firehose CloudWatch metrics for DDoS events Detailed reports, DRT access, cost protection metrics Flow logs to S3, CloudWatch, Kinesis; alert logs for rule matches VPC Flow Logs (must be enabled separately) VPC Flow Logs (must be enabled separately)
Key Limitations Only protects HTTP/HTTPS; requires CloudFront/ALB/API Gateway No advanced reporting or DRT access Expensive ($3,000/month); 1-year subscription commitment Adds latency; requires proper routing configuration Cannot deny traffic explicitly; limited to 5 SGs per ENI Processed after Security Groups; stateless requires bidirectional rules
Operating Level Layer 7 (Application) — inspects HTTP/HTTPS requests for SQL injection, XSS, and custom rules Layer 3/4 (Network/Transport) — automatic DDoS protection for all AWS resources at no extra cost Layers 3/4/7 — enhanced DDoS protection with 24/7 DDoS Response Team and cost protection Layers 3-7 — stateful inspection, IDS/IPS, domain filtering for VPC traffic Layer 4 (Transport) — stateful firewall at the ENI level filtering by protocol, port, and source/destination Layer 4 (Transport) — stateless firewall at the subnet level filtering by protocol, port, and CIDR
Association Attached to CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, AppSync, or Cognito User Pool Automatic — applies to all AWS resources (CloudFront, Route 53, ELB, etc.) at no extra cost Explicitly enabled per resource — CloudFront, Route 53, ALB, NLB, Elastic IP, Global Accelerator Deployed per VPC per AZ — associated with a firewall subnet; traffic routed through via route tables Attached to ENIs (EC2 instances, Lambda in VPC, RDS, ELB, etc.) — instance level Associated with subnets — one NACL per subnet; all resources in the subnet inherit the rules
Use Cases Block SQL injection, XSS, bot traffic; geo-blocking; rate limiting; IP reputation lists; custom web ACL rules Baseline DDoS protection — SYN flood, UDP reflection, and other common L3/L4 attacks; always-on, free High-value applications needing DDoS cost protection, 24/7 DRT access, advanced attack visibility, and SLA credits VPC traffic inspection, IDS/IPS, domain name filtering, TLS inspection, centralized egress filtering Control inbound/outbound traffic to EC2, RDS, Lambda; allow specific ports/protocols; reference other security groups Subnet-level defense-in-depth; explicitly deny specific IPs or CIDR ranges; complement security groups with deny rules
⚠️ Exam Tips

Security Groups are stateful (return traffic automatically allowed); NACLs are stateless (must allow both directions)

Security Groups operate at instance level; NACLs operate at subnet level

WAF protects web applications from common exploits (SQL injection, XSS) at Layer 7

Shield Standard is free DDoS protection; Shield Advanced provides 24/7 DRT support and cost protection

For the exam: 'instance-level firewall' = Security Group; 'subnet-level ACL' = NACL; 'web app protection' = WAF + Shield

Security Group
Virtual firewall that controls inbound and outbound traffic at the instance level, operating as a stateful filter that remembers connection state.
Network ACL (NACL)
Virtual firewall that controls traffic at the subnet level, operating as a stateless filter that evaluates each request independently using numbered rules.
WAF (Web Application Firewall)
OSI Layer: Layer 7 (Application) - HTTP/HTTPS traffic only | Primary Protection: OWASP Top 10, SQL injection, XSS, bot control, rate limiting | Deployment Model: Associated with CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, AppSync | Rule Evaluation: Managed rule groups, custom rules, rate-based rules, regex patterns | Scope of Protection: Application-specific (web applications behind CloudFront/ALB) | Statefulness: Stateful - tracks request context | Default Behavior: Allow all by default; rules define what to block | Logging & Monitoring: Logs to CloudWatch, S3, Kinesis Data Firehose | Key Limitations: Only protects HTTP/HTTPS; requires CloudFront/ALB/API Gateway | Operating Level: Layer 7 (Application) — inspects HTTP/HTTPS requests for SQL injection, XSS, and custom rules | Association: Attached to CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, AppSync, or Cognito User Pool | Use Cases: Block SQL injection, XSS, bot traffic; geo-blocking; rate limiting; IP reputation lists; custom web ACL rules
Shield Standard
OSI Layer: Layer 3 (Network) and Layer 4 (Transport) | Primary Protection: DDoS protection against common attacks (volumetric, state-exhaustion) | Deployment Model: Automatically enabled for all AWS customers at no cost | Rule Evaluation: Automatic - no rules to configure | Scope of Protection: All AWS resources globally (automatic) | Statefulness: Stateful - tracks connection state | Default Behavior: Always active; no configuration needed | Logging & Monitoring: CloudWatch metrics for DDoS events | Key Limitations: No advanced reporting or DRT access | Operating Level: Layer 3/4 (Network/Transport) — automatic DDoS protection for all AWS resources at no extra cost | Association: Automatic — applies to all AWS resources (CloudFront, Route 53, ELB, etc.) at no extra cost | Use Cases: Baseline DDoS protection — SYN flood, UDP reflection, and other common L3/L4 attacks; always-on, free
Shield Advanced
OSI Layer: Layer 3 (Network) and Layer 4 (Transport) | Primary Protection: Enhanced DDoS protection with 24/7 DRT, cost protection, detailed reporting | Deployment Model: $3,000/month per organization + data transfer out fees | Rule Evaluation: Custom protections via WAF rules; DRT assistance | Scope of Protection: Resources you specify (EC2, ELB, CloudFront, Global Accelerator, Route 53) | Statefulness: Stateful - tracks connection state | Default Behavior: Must subscribe; then configure protected resources | Logging & Monitoring: Detailed reports, DRT access, cost protection metrics | Key Limitations: Expensive ($3,000/month); 1-year subscription commitment | Operating Level: Layers 3/4/7 — enhanced DDoS protection with 24/7 DDoS Response Team and cost protection | Association: Explicitly enabled per resource — CloudFront, Route 53, ALB, NLB, Elastic IP, Global Accelerator | Use Cases: High-value applications needing DDoS cost protection, 24/7 DRT access, advanced attack visibility, and SLA credits
Network Firewall
OSI Layer: Layer 3-7 (Network through Application) | Primary Protection: Stateful inspection, intrusion prevention, domain filtering, TLS inspection | Deployment Model: Deployed in VPC subnets as a managed service; traffic routed through firewall endpoints | Rule Evaluation: Stateful rules (Suricata compatible), stateless rules, domain lists, IP lists | Scope of Protection: VPC-level protection for all outbound/inbound traffic | Statefulness: Both stateful and stateless rules supported | Default Behavior: Allow all by default until rules are configured | Logging & Monitoring: Flow logs to S3, CloudWatch, Kinesis; alert logs for rule matches | Key Limitations: Adds latency; requires proper routing configuration | Operating Level: Layers 3-7 — stateful inspection, IDS/IPS, domain filtering for VPC traffic | Association: Deployed per VPC per AZ — associated with a firewall subnet; traffic routed through via route tables | Use Cases: VPC traffic inspection, IDS/IPS, domain name filtering, TLS inspection, centralized egress filtering
Security Groups
OSI Layer: Layer 4 (Transport) - TCP/UDP port level | Primary Protection: Instance-level firewall controlling inbound/outbound traffic | Deployment Model: Attached to ENIs (EC2 instances, Lambda, RDS, etc.) | Rule Evaluation: Allow rules only (implicit deny); by protocol, port, source/destination | Scope of Protection: Individual resource level (EC2, RDS, Lambda, etc.) | Statefulness: Stateful - return traffic automatically allowed | Default Behavior: Deny all inbound; allow all outbound by default | Logging & Monitoring: VPC Flow Logs (must be enabled separately) | Key Limitations: Cannot deny traffic explicitly; limited to 5 SGs per ENI | Operating Level: Layer 4 (Transport) — stateful firewall at the ENI level filtering by protocol, port, and source/destination | Association: Attached to ENIs (EC2 instances, Lambda in VPC, RDS, ELB, etc.) — instance level | Use Cases: Control inbound/outbound traffic to EC2, RDS, Lambda; allow specific ports/protocols; reference other security groups
NACLs (Network ACLs)
OSI Layer: Layer 4 (Transport) - TCP/UDP port level | Primary Protection: Subnet-level firewall controlling inbound/outbound traffic | Deployment Model: Associated with subnets; applies to all resources in subnet | Rule Evaluation: Allow and Deny rules; by protocol, port, source/destination CIDR | Scope of Protection: Subnet level (all resources within subnet) | Statefulness: Stateless - both directions must be explicitly allowed | Default Behavior: Allow all inbound and outbound by default | Logging & Monitoring: VPC Flow Logs (must be enabled separately) | Key Limitations: Processed after Security Groups; stateless requires bidirectional rules | Operating Level: Layer 4 (Transport) — stateless firewall at the subnet level filtering by protocol, port, and CIDR | Association: Associated with subnets — one NACL per subnet; all resources in the subnet inherit the rules | Use Cases: Subnet-level defense-in-depth; explicitly deny specific IPs or CIDR ranges; complement security groups with deny rules
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

#138 Security Groups vs NACLs vs WAF vs AWS Shield
Security SAA-002_2 4 cols 6 rows
▼

AWS has layered network security — think of it like concentric walls around your infrastructure. Security Groups protect individual resources, NACLs protect subnets, WAF protects web applications at Layer 7, and Shield protects against DDoS attacks. Each layer solves a different type of threat. The exam loves scenario questions requiring you to pick the right security layer.

Feature Security Groups Network ACLs (NACLs) AWS WAF AWS Shield
Level of Operation Instance level (EC2, RDS, Lambda ENI) Subnet level Application layer — Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) Network layer — Layer 3/4 (DDoS protection)
Stateful vs Stateless STATEFUL — return traffic automatically allowed STATELESS — must define rules for both directions Stateless — inspects each request independently N/A — rate-based and signature detection
Default Behavior Deny all inbound, allow all outbound (by default) Default NACL: ALLOW all. Custom NACL: DENY all Allow all unless rules say block Standard: automatic for all. Advanced: opt-in
Rule Processing All rules evaluated — most permissive wins Rules evaluated in number order — first match wins Web ACL rules evaluated in priority order Automatic — no rule configuration needed for Standard
Protects Against Unauthorized instance access (port/protocol control) Subnet-level unauthorized traffic SQL injection, XSS, bot traffic, bad IP addresses DDoS attacks (volumetric, state-exhaustion, application layer)
Attaches To EC2, RDS, Lambda, ELB network interfaces VPC Subnets CloudFront, Application Load Balancer, API Gateway Route 53, CloudFront, ELB, EC2 Elastic IPs
⚠️ Exam Tips

Security Groups = stateful instance firewall; NACLs = stateless subnet firewall

WAF rules inspect HTTP/HTTPS requests — blocks SQL injection, XSS, and bot traffic

Shield Standard is automatic and free; Shield Advanced is paid with financial protection

Layer defense: Shield (L3/L4 DDoS) → WAF (L7 web attacks) → NACL (subnet) → Security Group (instance)

For the exam: 'block specific IP addresses' = NACL or WAF; 'allow specific port' = Security Group

Security Groups
Security Groups are stateful virtual firewalls that control inbound and outbound traffic at the INSTANCE level (EC2, RDS, Lambda). Because they are stateful, a return response to allowed inbound traffic is automatically permitted — you only need to define rules in one direction.
Network ACLs (NACLs)
NACLs are stateless packet filters that operate at the SUBNET level — all traffic entering or leaving a subnet is evaluated. Because they are stateless, you must explicitly define rules for BOTH directions (inbound and outbound). They process rules in number order and stop at the first match.
AWS WAF
WAF (Web Application Firewall) operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) and protects web applications from common exploits like SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), and bot attacks. It attaches to CloudFront, ALB, or API Gateway.
AWS Shield
Shield is AWS's managed DDoS protection service. Shield Standard is free and automatic for all AWS customers. Shield Advanced provides enhanced protection, 24/7 DDoS response team, and financial protection for Route 53, CloudFront, and Elastic Load Balancers.
Security Groups
Level of Operation: Instance level (EC2, RDS, Lambda ENI) | Stateful vs Stateless: STATEFUL — return traffic automatically allowed | Default Behavior: Deny all inbound, allow all outbound (by default) | Rule Processing: All rules evaluated — most permissive wins | Protects Against: Unauthorized instance access (port/protocol control) | Attaches To: EC2, RDS, Lambda, ELB network interfaces
Network ACLs (NACLs)
Level of Operation: Subnet level | Stateful vs Stateless: STATELESS — must define rules for both directions | Default Behavior: Default NACL: ALLOW all. Custom NACL: DENY all | Rule Processing: Rules evaluated in number order — first match wins | Protects Against: Subnet-level unauthorized traffic | Attaches To: VPC Subnets
AWS WAF
Level of Operation: Application layer — Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) | Stateful vs Stateless: Stateless — inspects each request independently | Default Behavior: Allow all unless rules say block | Rule Processing: Web ACL rules evaluated in priority order | Protects Against: SQL injection, XSS, bot traffic, bad IP addresses | Attaches To: CloudFront, Application Load Balancer, API Gateway
AWS Shield
Level of Operation: Network layer — Layer 3/4 (DDoS protection) | Stateful vs Stateless: N/A — rate-based and signature detection | Default Behavior: Standard: automatic for all. Advanced: opt-in | Rule Processing: Automatic — no rule configuration needed for Standard | Protects Against: DDoS attacks (volumetric, state-exhaustion, application layer) | Attaches To: Route 53, CloudFront, ELB, EC2 Elastic IPs
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#139 Security Monitoring Services Comparison
Security security_monitoring_services_comparison 8 cols 13 rows
▼

Comparing AWS services for security monitoring, logging, and compliance

AWS offers a comprehensive suite of security monitoring services that use machine learning and anomaly detection to identify threats and vulnerabilities. GuardDuty provides intelligent threat detection for accounts and workloads, Macie discovers and protects sensitive data in S3, Inspector assesses application vulnerabilities and network exposure, Security Hub provides a centralized security posture view across accounts, and Detective enables security incident investigation. Understanding the specific threats each service detects, their data sources, and integration patterns is crucial for the exam. These services work together to provide defense in depth for security monitoring.

Feature GuardDuty Macie Inspector Security Hub Detective Amazon CloudWatch AWS CloudTrail AWS Config
Primary Purpose Continuous threat detection for AWS accounts, workloads, and data using ML Discover, classify, and protect sensitive data (PII) in Amazon S3 Automated vulnerability assessment for EC2 and container workloads Centralized security posture management and compliance checking across accounts Security investigation and root cause analysis using visualizations Operational monitoring — collect metrics, logs, and events from AWS resources and applications API audit logging — records every API call made in your AWS account (who, what, when, where) Configuration compliance — continuously records resource configurations and evaluates against rules
What It Detects Account compromise, instance compromise, malicious reconnaissance, cryptocurrency mining Unencrypted or publicly accessible S3 buckets, PII/PHI/sensitive data exposure Software vulnerabilities (CVEs), network reachability issues, unintended network accessibility Security findings aggregation, compliance checks against standards (CIS, PCI DSS) Relationships between entities during security events, anomalous API activity patterns Custom metrics, application logs, events All AWS API calls (who, what, when) Resource configurations over time
Data Sources CloudTrail logs, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, EKS audit logs, S3 data events S3 bucket content and metadata, S3 CloudTrail events EC2 instance software inventory, network configuration, container images in ECR Findings from GuardDuty, Macie, Inspector, third-party products, Config rules CloudTrail logs, VPC Flow Logs, EKS audit logs, GuardDuty findings EC2 metrics, custom metrics, application logs, VPC Flow Logs, Lambda logs, container metrics API calls across all AWS services — management events (default), data events (opt-in), insight events Resource configuration snapshots from all supported AWS services; records configuration changes
Pricing Model CloudTrail analysis: free; VPC Flow Logs: $0.50-2.50/GB; DNS logs: free; EKS: $0.20-0.40/vCPU $0.10/GB for data classified; $0.0024 per S3 object assessed for public accessibility $0.09 per EC2 instance per assessment; $0.002 per container image scan $0.0010 per security check; $0.10 per finding ingested from third-party products $2.00-8.00 per terabyte of data processed (based on total volume) Per metric, per alarm, per GB logs ingested/stored, per dashboard; free tier includes basic metrics First management trail free; additional trails per 100K events; data events extra; S3 storage costs Per configuration item recorded + per Config rule evaluation; conformance packs available
Automation & Response EventBridge integration for automated response; Lambda for remediation EventBridge for alerts; can trigger Lambda for automatic remediation EventBridge integration; findings sent to Security Hub automatically Automated response via EventBridge; custom actions for remediation Manual investigation tool; integrates with Security Hub for finding context CloudWatch Alarms trigger SNS, Auto Scaling, Lambda, EC2 actions; Composite Alarms for complex conditions EventBridge integration — trigger Lambda, Step Functions, SNS on specific API calls; CloudTrail Insights for anomalies Auto-remediation via SSM Automation documents; Config rules trigger Lambda for custom remediation
Deployment Model Single-click enable per region; auto-scans all accounts in organization Enable per region; auto-scans all S3 buckets; supports organization-wide enablement Enable per region; continuous scanning or on-demand assessments Enable per region; master-member model for organizations; cross-region aggregation Enable per region; auto-analyzes data from enabled accounts Automatic for AWS services; CloudWatch Agent for OS/app metrics; cross-account and cross-region dashboards Organization trail for all accounts; per-account trails; multi-region enabled by default Per-region activation; aggregators for multi-account/multi-region views; organization-wide Config rules
Key Integrations Security Hub, Detective, EventBridge, Lambda, SNS, S3 (for findings export) Security Hub, EventBridge, Lambda, S3, IAM Access Analyzer Security Hub, EventBridge, EC2 Systems Manager, ECR GuardDuty, Macie, Inspector, Config, Firewall Manager, 50+ third-party integrations GuardDuty, Security Hub, CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs EventBridge, SNS, Lambda, Auto Scaling, Systems Manager, X-Ray, Grafana, third-party tools CloudWatch Logs, S3, EventBridge, Athena (for querying), Security Hub, Lake Formation Security Hub, Systems Manager, Lambda, S3 (delivery channel), SNS notifications, Organizations
Compliance Standards PCI DSS, SOC, ISO, FedRAMP (supports compliance but not certified) GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS (helps with compliance through data discovery) PCI DSS, CIS Benchmarks (provides compliance-related findings) CIS AWS Foundations, PCI DSS, AWS Foundational Security Best Practices, NIST Supports investigation for compliance incidents (not a compliance tool) Supports operational compliance monitoring; SOC, ISO, PCI DSS; log retention for audit requirements Essential for all compliance — PCI DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2, FedRAMP, ISO 27001 require API audit trails CIS Benchmarks, PCI DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2 — conformance packs map rules to compliance frameworks
