Unique Value Proposition ML-powered threat detection without deploying agents or managing infrastructure Only service that automatically discovers and classifies sensitive data at scale Agentless vulnerability scanning for EC2 and automated container image scanning Single pane of glass for security findings across all AWS accounts and services Visual investigation tool that reconstructs security events with entity relationships Single pane of glass for operational health — metrics, logs, traces, and dashboards in one service Immutable audit trail of all API activity — the definitive record of who did what in your AWS account Configuration time machine — query historical state of any resource and track configuration drift
Primary Function Intelligent threat detection — ML-based analysis of VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, CloudTrail for threats Sensitive data discovery — uses ML to find and classify PII/PHI in S3 buckets automatically Vulnerability scanning — automated CVE assessment of EC2, Lambda, and container images Central security dashboard — aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and third-party tools Security investigation — analyzes and visualizes security data to identify root cause of findings Monitor metrics, logs, set alarms Log API calls and user activity Track resource configuration changes
Retention 90 days in console; export findings to S3 via EventBridge for long-term retention 30 days for findings in console; export to S3 or Security Hub for longer retention Findings retained until resolved or 365 days (whichever is first); export via EventBridge to S3 90 days for findings; archive to S3 for compliance; integrate with SIEM for long-term storage Up to 1 year of behavior graph data built from VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail, and GuardDuty findings Customizable (days to years) 90 days (365 days in cloudtrail-docs bucket) Customizable (up to rules setting)
Use Case Detect compromised instances, unauthorized access, cryptocurrency mining, data exfiltration, port scanning Find PII in S3 before it enters ML pipelines; GDPR data subject requests; S3 security posture assessment Pre-deployment vulnerability scans; continuous CVE monitoring; container image scanning in ECR Consolidated security view across accounts; automated compliance checks (CIS, PCI DSS); ASFF standard findings Investigate GuardDuty findings — trace attack paths, analyze resource behaviors, identify blast radius Operational health, performance tuning Security auditing, incident investigation Compliance checking, drift detection
Proactive/Reactive Reactive with proactive elements — continuously monitors for threats; findings trigger automated response Proactive — scheduled scans discover sensitive data before breaches; reactive alerts on policy violations Proactive — continuously scans for vulnerabilities before exploitation; triggered on new CVEs and deployments Both — proactive compliance checks (security standards); reactive aggregation of findings from other services Reactive — used for post-incident investigation; analyzes historical data to understand security events Both (metrics + alarms) Reactive (after-the-fact logging) Proactive (configuration compliance)
💡 Mnemonics

CWCCAG = CloudWatch for health, CloudTrail for audit, Config for compliance, GuardDuty for threats

⚠️ Exam Tips

CloudTrail logs WHO did WHAT in AWS - essential for security auditing

CloudWatch monitors HOW systems are PERFORMING

Enable CloudTrail in ALL regions for complete audit coverage

GuardDuty uses machine learning to detect threats without rules

Config rules can automatically remediate non-compliant resources

Amazon CloudWatch
A monitoring and observability service providing data and actionable insights for AWS, hybrid, and on-premises applications through logs, metrics, and events.
AWS CloudTrail
A service that logs API calls and user activity across your AWS infrastructure, providing audit trails for security analysis and compliance.
AWS Config
A service that tracks resource inventory and changes, enabling compliance auditing and security analysis of AWS resource configurations.
Amazon GuardDuty
A threat detection service that continuously monitors for malicious activity and unauthorized behavior to protect AWS accounts and workloads.
GuardDuty
Primary Purpose: Continuous threat detection for AWS accounts, workloads, and data using ML | What It Detects: Account compromise, instance compromise, malicious reconnaissance, cryptocurrency mining | Data Sources: CloudTrail logs, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, EKS audit logs, S3 data events | Pricing Model: CloudTrail analysis: free; VPC Flow Logs: $0.50-2.50/GB; DNS logs: free; EKS: $0.20-0.40/vCPU | Automation & Response: EventBridge integration for automated response; Lambda for remediation | Deployment Model: Single-click enable per region; auto-scans all accounts in organization | Key Integrations: Security Hub, Detective, EventBridge, Lambda, SNS, S3 (for findings export) | Compliance Standards: PCI DSS, SOC, ISO, FedRAMP (supports compliance but not certified) | Unique Value Proposition: ML-powered threat detection without deploying agents or managing infrastructure | Primary Function: Intelligent threat detection — ML-based analysis of VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, CloudTrail for threats | Retention: 90 days in console; export findings to S3 via EventBridge for long-term retention | Use Case: Detect compromised instances, unauthorized access, cryptocurrency mining, data exfiltration, port scanning | Proactive/Reactive: Reactive with proactive elements — continuously monitors for threats; findings trigger automated response
Macie
Primary Purpose: Discover, classify, and protect sensitive data (PII) in Amazon S3 | What It Detects: Unencrypted or publicly accessible S3 buckets, PII/PHI/sensitive data exposure | Data Sources: S3 bucket content and metadata, S3 CloudTrail events | Pricing Model: $0.10/GB for data classified; $0.0024 per S3 object assessed for public accessibility | Automation & Response: EventBridge for alerts; can trigger Lambda for automatic remediation | Deployment Model: Enable per region; auto-scans all S3 buckets; supports organization-wide enablement | Key Integrations: Security Hub, EventBridge, Lambda, S3, IAM Access Analyzer | Compliance Standards: GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS (helps with compliance through data discovery) | Unique Value Proposition: Only service that automatically discovers and classifies sensitive data at scale | Primary Function: Sensitive data discovery — uses ML to find and classify PII/PHI in S3 buckets automatically | Retention: 30 days for findings in console; export to S3 or Security Hub for longer retention | Use Case: Find PII in S3 before it enters ML pipelines; GDPR data subject requests; S3 security posture assessment | Proactive/Reactive: Proactive — scheduled scans discover sensitive data before breaches; reactive alerts on policy violations
Inspector
Primary Purpose: Automated vulnerability assessment for EC2 and container workloads | What It Detects: Software vulnerabilities (CVEs), network reachability issues, unintended network accessibility | Data Sources: EC2 instance software inventory, network configuration, container images in ECR | Pricing Model: $0.09 per EC2 instance per assessment; $0.002 per container image scan | Automation & Response: EventBridge integration; findings sent to Security Hub automatically | Deployment Model: Enable per region; continuous scanning or on-demand assessments | Key Integrations: Security Hub, EventBridge, EC2 Systems Manager, ECR | Compliance Standards: PCI DSS, CIS Benchmarks (provides compliance-related findings) | Unique Value Proposition: Agentless vulnerability scanning for EC2 and automated container image scanning | Primary Function: Vulnerability scanning — automated CVE assessment of EC2, Lambda, and container images | Retention: Findings retained until resolved or 365 days (whichever is first); export via EventBridge to S3 | Use Case: Pre-deployment vulnerability scans; continuous CVE monitoring; container image scanning in ECR | Proactive/Reactive: Proactive — continuously scans for vulnerabilities before exploitation; triggered on new CVEs and deployments
Security Hub
Primary Purpose: Centralized security posture management and compliance checking across accounts | What It Detects: Security findings aggregation, compliance checks against standards (CIS, PCI DSS) | Data Sources: Findings from GuardDuty, Macie, Inspector, third-party products, Config rules | Pricing Model: $0.0010 per security check; $0.10 per finding ingested from third-party products | Automation & Response: Automated response via EventBridge; custom actions for remediation | Deployment Model: Enable per region; master-member model for organizations; cross-region aggregation | Key Integrations: GuardDuty, Macie, Inspector, Config, Firewall Manager, 50+ third-party integrations | Compliance Standards: CIS AWS Foundations, PCI DSS, AWS Foundational Security Best Practices, NIST | Unique Value Proposition: Single pane of glass for security findings across all AWS accounts and services | Primary Function: Central security dashboard — aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and third-party tools | Retention: 90 days for findings; archive to S3 for compliance; integrate with SIEM for long-term storage | Use Case: Consolidated security view across accounts; automated compliance checks (CIS, PCI DSS); ASFF standard findings | Proactive/Reactive: Both — proactive compliance checks (security standards); reactive aggregation of findings from other services
Detective
Primary Purpose: Security investigation and root cause analysis using visualizations | What It Detects: Relationships between entities during security events, anomalous API activity patterns | Data Sources: CloudTrail logs, VPC Flow Logs, EKS audit logs, GuardDuty findings | Pricing Model: $2.00-8.00 per terabyte of data processed (based on total volume) | Automation & Response: Manual investigation tool; integrates with Security Hub for finding context | Deployment Model: Enable per region; auto-analyzes data from enabled accounts | Key Integrations: GuardDuty, Security Hub, CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs | Compliance Standards: Supports investigation for compliance incidents (not a compliance tool) | Unique Value Proposition: Visual investigation tool that reconstructs security events with entity relationships | Primary Function: Security investigation — analyzes and visualizes security data to identify root cause of findings | Retention: Up to 1 year of behavior graph data built from VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail, and GuardDuty findings | Use Case: Investigate GuardDuty findings — trace attack paths, analyze resource behaviors, identify blast radius | Proactive/Reactive: Reactive — used for post-incident investigation; analyzes historical data to understand security events
Amazon CloudWatch
Primary Purpose: Operational monitoring — collect metrics, logs, and events from AWS resources and applications | What It Detects: Custom metrics, application logs, events | Data Sources: EC2 metrics, custom metrics, application logs, VPC Flow Logs, Lambda logs, container metrics | Pricing Model: Per metric, per alarm, per GB logs ingested/stored, per dashboard; free tier includes basic metrics | Automation & Response: CloudWatch Alarms trigger SNS, Auto Scaling, Lambda, EC2 actions; Composite Alarms for complex conditions | Deployment Model: Automatic for AWS services; CloudWatch Agent for OS/app metrics; cross-account and cross-region dashboards | Key Integrations: EventBridge, SNS, Lambda, Auto Scaling, Systems Manager, X-Ray, Grafana, third-party tools | Compliance Standards: Supports operational compliance monitoring; SOC, ISO, PCI DSS; log retention for audit requirements | Unique Value Proposition: Single pane of glass for operational health — metrics, logs, traces, and dashboards in one service | Primary Function: Monitor metrics, logs, set alarms | Retention: Customizable (days to years) | Use Case: Operational health, performance tuning | Proactive/Reactive: Both (metrics + alarms)
AWS CloudTrail
Primary Purpose: API audit logging — records every API call made in your AWS account (who, what, when, where) | What It Detects: All AWS API calls (who, what, when) | Data Sources: API calls across all AWS services — management events (default), data events (opt-in), insight events | Pricing Model: First management trail free; additional trails per 100K events; data events extra; S3 storage costs | Automation & Response: EventBridge integration — trigger Lambda, Step Functions, SNS on specific API calls; CloudTrail Insights for anomalies | Deployment Model: Organization trail for all accounts; per-account trails; multi-region enabled by default | Key Integrations: CloudWatch Logs, S3, EventBridge, Athena (for querying), Security Hub, Lake Formation | Compliance Standards: Essential for all compliance — PCI DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2, FedRAMP, ISO 27001 require API audit trails | Unique Value Proposition: Immutable audit trail of all API activity — the definitive record of who did what in your AWS account | Primary Function: Log API calls and user activity | Retention: 90 days (365 days in cloudtrail-docs bucket) | Use Case: Security auditing, incident investigation | Proactive/Reactive: Reactive (after-the-fact logging)
AWS Config
Primary Purpose: Configuration compliance — continuously records resource configurations and evaluates against rules | What It Detects: Resource configurations over time | Data Sources: Resource configuration snapshots from all supported AWS services; records configuration changes | Pricing Model: Per configuration item recorded + per Config rule evaluation; conformance packs available | Automation & Response: Auto-remediation via SSM Automation documents; Config rules trigger Lambda for custom remediation | Deployment Model: Per-region activation; aggregators for multi-account/multi-region views; organization-wide Config rules | Key Integrations: Security Hub, Systems Manager, Lambda, S3 (delivery channel), SNS notifications, Organizations | Compliance Standards: CIS Benchmarks, PCI DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2 — conformance packs map rules to compliance frameworks | Unique Value Proposition: Configuration time machine — query historical state of any resource and track configuration drift | Primary Function: Track resource configuration changes | Retention: Customizable (up to rules setting) | Use Case: Compliance checking, drift detection | Proactive/Reactive: Proactive (configuration compliance)

#140 Threat Detection and Application Protection
Security saa_d1_security_services 4 cols 6 rows
▼
Feature Amazon GuardDuty Amazon Macie AWS WAF AWS Shield
Protects against / detects Suspicious account and workload activity Sensitive data exposure in S3 Malicious web requests and Layer 7 patterns DDoS attacks
Best fit Threat detection across accounts and workloads Data discovery and classification Protecting web apps and APIs Hardening public endpoints against flooding attacks
Typical exam clue Compromised credentials, anomalous behavior PII in S3, data governance Block SQL injection, bot-like or abusive HTTP requests Need DDoS mitigation
Traffic path dependency No web proxy role No request filtering role Evaluates web requests Protects internet-facing availability
Data awareness Behavior-focused Content-focused Request-pattern-focused Attack-volume-focused
Common mix-up Not a firewall Not a threat detector for APIs Not a data classification tool Not a replacement for WAF
⚠️ Exam Tips

GuardDuty = intelligent threat detection using ML on VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, and CloudTrail events

Inspector = automated vulnerability scanning for EC2, Lambda, and container images

Detective = security investigation and root cause analysis using graph models

Security Hub = centralized security findings dashboard aggregating from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, etc.

Enable GuardDuty in ALL accounts and regions as the first line of automated threat detection

Amazon GuardDuty
GuardDuty is a threat detection service that analyzes signals like logs and activity patterns to flag suspicious behavior. For the exam, think compromised credentials, unusual API activity, or potentially malicious network behavior.
Amazon Macie
Macie focuses on discovering and helping protect sensitive data in Amazon S3, especially PII and data classification use cases. When the question smells like 'find sensitive data in buckets,' Macie is the answer.
AWS WAF
AWS WAF filters web requests at the application layer and is commonly used against things like SQL injection and bad HTTP patterns. It is your exam-friendly Layer 7 web filter.
AWS Shield
AWS Shield is about DDoS protection, especially for internet-facing resources. When the attack is volumetric or availability-focused, Shield enters the scene.
Amazon GuardDuty
Protects against / detects: Suspicious account and workload activity | Best fit: Threat detection across accounts and workloads | Typical exam clue: Compromised credentials, anomalous behavior | Traffic path dependency: No web proxy role | Data awareness: Behavior-focused | Common mix-up: Not a firewall
Amazon Macie
Protects against / detects: Sensitive data exposure in S3 | Best fit: Data discovery and classification | Typical exam clue: PII in S3, data governance | Traffic path dependency: No request filtering role | Data awareness: Content-focused | Common mix-up: Not a threat detector for APIs
AWS WAF
Protects against / detects: Malicious web requests and Layer 7 patterns | Best fit: Protecting web apps and APIs | Typical exam clue: Block SQL injection, bot-like or abusive HTTP requests | Traffic path dependency: Evaluates web requests | Data awareness: Request-pattern-focused | Common mix-up: Not a data classification tool
AWS Shield
Protects against / detects: DDoS attacks | Best fit: Hardening public endpoints against flooding attacks | Typical exam clue: Need DDoS mitigation | Traffic path dependency: Protects internet-facing availability | Data awareness: Attack-volume-focused | Common mix-up: Not a replacement for WAF
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 1 calls out security services for workloads and applications. This table helps you quickly map whether the threat is about malicious traffic, DDoS, sensitive data discovery, or threat detection.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

Identity & Access 5 tables
#141 AWS IAM Security Components
Identity & Access SAA-013 4 cols 14 rows
▼

AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) is the foundation of AWS security, providing centralized control over authentication and authorization. Understanding the differences between IAM Users, Groups, Roles, and Policies is critical for the exam. Users represent individual identities with long-term credentials, Groups are collections of users for easier permission management, Roles provide temporary credentials for cross-account access and service delegation, while Policies (both identity-based and resource-based) define the actual permissions. The exam frequently tests scenarios where you must choose the appropriate IAM component based on use case, security best practices, and operational requirements.

Feature IAM Users IAM Groups IAM Roles IAM Policies
Identity Type Individual identity Collection of users Assumable identity Permission document
Access Keys Yes (for programmatic access) N/A - assigned to users Temporary credentials via STS N/A - attached to identities
Password Yes (for console access) N/A - assigned to users N/A - assumed by principals Not applicable — IAM Policies are permission documents, not authentication identities
Use Cases Individual developers, service accounts needing programmatic access, legacy applications Organize users by job function (Developers, Admins, ReadOnly) Cross-account access, EC2/ Lambda service roles, SAML/OIDC federation, third-party access Cross-account access to single resource (S3, SNS, SQS), service-specific permissions
Best Practice Avoid for human access; use IAM Identity Center or federation instead Use for organizing users by function; cannot be referenced in resource policies Preferred over users for applications; rotate credentials automatically via STS Use managed policies for reusability; inline for specific exceptions
MFA Support Yes (recommended) N/A - configured on users Yes (when assumed) Can enforce MFA via condition keys (aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent) in policy statements
Maximum Attachments 10 managed policies + inline policies 10 managed policies + inline policies 10 managed policies + inline policies, 1 trust policy N/A - attached to identities
Credential Rotation Long-term credentials (password, access keys, MFA devices) No credentials - purely organizational construct Temporary credentials (STS tokens) with configurable expiration (15 min - 12 hours) No credentials - policy document only
Cross-Account Access Not possible Not possible Yes (via trust policy) Cross-account resource policies
Primary Purpose Represent individual human identities or applications needing long-term AWS access Collection of IAM users to simplify permission management at scale Provide temporary credentials for cross-account access, service delegation, or federated users Define permissions — JSON documents specifying allowed/denied actions on AWS resources
Trust Relationship Not applicable — IAM Users authenticate directly; trust relationships apply to Roles Not applicable — Groups are collections of users; trust relationships apply to Roles Requires trust policy specifying who can assume the role Not applicable — Policies define permissions; trust relationships are configured on Roles
Permission Boundaries Can have permission boundaries set Members inherit boundaries from user settings Can have permission boundaries set Can be used AS permission boundaries — an IAM policy attached as a boundary limits maximum permissions
Policy Evaluation Explicit DENY > Explicit ALLOW > Implicit DENY Permissions aggregated from all group memberships Session policies can further restrict permissions during assume role Deny by default; explicit Deny overrides Allow; evaluated across identity-based, resource-based, SCPs, and boundaries
Service Integration Console login, CLI, SDK access Cannot be used in resource policies or as principals EC2 instance profiles, Lambda execution roles, ECS task roles, CodeBuild/CodePipeline All AWS services — policies can reference any AWS service action and resource ARN
💡 Mnemonics

Users = Individual identities, Groups = User collections, Roles = Temporary assumable identity, Policies = Permission rules

Need individual access? User. Need team permissions? Group. Need service access? Role. Need to define permissions? Policy.

⚠️ Exam Tips

Always use IAM Roles for EC2 instances, Lambda, and other AWS services

Groups simplify permission management for teams

Roles provide temporary credentials via STS (more secure)

Policies follow least privilege - deny > allow evaluation

Cross-account access requires roles with trust policies

Enable MFA for all IAM users, especially root account

IAM Users
An identity within AWS that represents a person or application that interacts with AWS services. Users can have passwords for console access and access keys for programmatic access. Best practice is to use AWS IAM Identity Center for human users instead.
IAM Groups
A collection of IAM users that share common permissions. Groups simplify permission management by allowing you to attach policies to the group rather than individual users. Users inherit all permissions from all groups they belong to.
IAM Roles
An identity with specific permissions that can be assumed by trusted principals (users, applications, or AWS services). Roles provide temporary security credentials via AWS STS, making them ideal for cross-account access and AWS service permissions.
IAM Policies
JSON documents that define permissions for AWS identities and resources. Specify what actions are allowed or denied on which resources under what conditions. Can be managed (standalone) or inline (embedded in identity).
IAM Users
Identity Type: Individual identity | Access Keys: Yes (for programmatic access) | Password: Yes (for console access) | Use Cases: Individual developers, service accounts needing programmatic access, legacy applications | Best Practice: Avoid for human access; use IAM Identity Center or federation instead | MFA Support: Yes (recommended) | Maximum Attachments: 10 managed policies + inline policies | Credential Rotation: Long-term credentials (password, access keys, MFA devices) | Cross-Account Access: Not possible | Primary Purpose: Represent individual human identities or applications needing long-term AWS access | Trust Relationship: Not applicable — IAM Users authenticate directly; trust relationships apply to Roles | Permission Boundaries: Can have permission boundaries set | Policy Evaluation: Explicit DENY > Explicit ALLOW > Implicit DENY | Service Integration: Console login, CLI, SDK access
IAM Groups
Identity Type: Collection of users | Access Keys: N/A - assigned to users | Password: N/A - assigned to users | Use Cases: Organize users by job function (Developers, Admins, ReadOnly) | Best Practice: Use for organizing users by function; cannot be referenced in resource policies | MFA Support: N/A - configured on users | Maximum Attachments: 10 managed policies + inline policies | Credential Rotation: No credentials - purely organizational construct | Cross-Account Access: Not possible | Primary Purpose: Collection of IAM users to simplify permission management at scale | Trust Relationship: Not applicable — Groups are collections of users; trust relationships apply to Roles | Permission Boundaries: Members inherit boundaries from user settings | Policy Evaluation: Permissions aggregated from all group memberships | Service Integration: Cannot be used in resource policies or as principals
IAM Roles
Identity Type: Assumable identity | Access Keys: Temporary credentials via STS | Password: N/A - assumed by principals | Use Cases: Cross-account access, EC2/ Lambda service roles, SAML/OIDC federation, third-party access | Best Practice: Preferred over users for applications; rotate credentials automatically via STS | MFA Support: Yes (when assumed) | Maximum Attachments: 10 managed policies + inline policies, 1 trust policy | Credential Rotation: Temporary credentials (STS tokens) with configurable expiration (15 min - 12 hours) | Cross-Account Access: Yes (via trust policy) | Primary Purpose: Provide temporary credentials for cross-account access, service delegation, or federated users | Trust Relationship: Requires trust policy specifying who can assume the role | Permission Boundaries: Can have permission boundaries set | Policy Evaluation: Session policies can further restrict permissions during assume role | Service Integration: EC2 instance profiles, Lambda execution roles, ECS task roles, CodeBuild/CodePipeline
IAM Policies
Identity Type: Permission document | Access Keys: N/A - attached to identities | Password: Not applicable — IAM Policies are permission documents, not authentication identities | Use Cases: Cross-account access to single resource (S3, SNS, SQS), service-specific permissions | Best Practice: Use managed policies for reusability; inline for specific exceptions | MFA Support: Can enforce MFA via condition keys (aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent) in policy statements | Maximum Attachments: N/A - attached to identities | Credential Rotation: No credentials - policy document only | Cross-Account Access: Cross-account resource policies | Primary Purpose: Define permissions — JSON documents specifying allowed/denied actions on AWS resources | Trust Relationship: Not applicable — Policies define permissions; trust relationships are configured on Roles | Permission Boundaries: Can be used AS permission boundaries — an IAM policy attached as a boundary limits maximum permissions | Policy Evaluation: Deny by default; explicit Deny overrides Allow; evaluated across identity-based, resource-based, SCPs, and boundaries | Service Integration: All AWS services — policies can reference any AWS service action and resource ARN
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#142 AWS Identity and Access Management Services Comparison
Identity & Access iam_services_comparison 4 cols 12 rows
▼

Comparing AWS identity and access management services

This table compares AWS identity and access management services. IAM provides core identity and access management, IAM Identity Center enables SSO across AWS accounts and applications, Cognito adds user authentication for web/mobile apps, and Directory Service provides managed Active Directory.

Feature AWS IAM IAM Identity Center Amazon Cognito AWS Directory Service
Primary Purpose Access control for AWS resources Single Sign-On (SSO) for AWS and applications User authentication for web/mobile apps Directory integration (Active Directory)
User Types IAM users, roles for services/people Workforce users, SSO users Application end users (customers) Directory users (AD)
Federation Support SAML 2.0, OIDC, Web Identity SAML 2.0, automatic AWS account federation Social (Google, Facebook), SAML, OIDC AD trust relationships
Multi-Account Support Cross-account roles Native multi-account access management Not applicable (app-level) Can be shared across accounts
Authentication Methods Access keys, passwords, MFA SSO portal, MFA support Username/password, social login, MFA Kerberos, LDAP, NTLM
Token Type AWS credentials (temporary or long-term) AWS credentials via SSO JWT tokens (ID, access, refresh) Kerberos tickets
Integration Points All AWS services AWS accounts, SAML applications API Gateway, ALB, mobile SDKs EC2, RDS, WorkSpaces, WorkDocs
Key Features Policies, roles, permission boundaries Permission sets, account assignments User pools (auth), identity pools (AWS access) Managed AD, AD Connector, Simple AD
Users Managed IAM users within AWS account Workforce users across multiple AWS accounts — centralized SSO for organization members Application end users (sign-up/in) Enterprise directory users — integrates Active Directory with AWS for corporate identity management
Use Case AWS API access, cross-account access Single sign-on to multiple AWS accounts and business applications; centralized permission management with permission sets Mobile apps, web apps, user pools Extend on-premises Active Directory to AWS; enable Windows workloads to use existing AD credentials
Protocol IAM roles, policies, STS tokens SAML 2.0 and OIDC for SSO; SCIM for automatic user provisioning from external IdPs OAuth 2.0, SAML 2.0, OpenID Connect LDAP, Kerberos, NTLM — standard Active Directory protocols for authentication and authorization
External Identity Federation via STS Yes — supports external IdPs (Okta, Azure AD, Ping) via SAML 2.0 and SCIM provisioning Social (Google, Facebook, Apple), SAML Yes — AD Connector proxies to on-premises AD; Managed AD supports trust relationships with external domains
💡 Mnemonics

IIC = IAM for Infrastructure, Identity Center for corporate access, Cognito for Customer apps

⚠️ Exam Tips

IAM is for AWS service/programmatic access, NOT for end-user applications

Cognito is for mobile/web apps where end users sign up and sign in

IAM Identity Center (formerly SSO) is for enterprise single sign-on to AWS

Always follow principle of least privilege with IAM policies

Cognito supports both user pools (managed users) and identity pools (AWS credentials)

AWS IAM
Foundational service for securely controlling access to AWS services and resources through users, groups, roles, and policies with fine-grained permissions.
IAM Identity Center
Cloud-based single sign-on service that centrally manages access to multiple AWS accounts and business applications with SAML 2.0 integration.
Amazon Cognito
Authentication and authorization service for web and mobile applications that provides user sign-up, sign-in, and access control with social and enterprise identity federation.
AWS Directory Service
Managed service for connecting AWS resources with an existing on-premises Microsoft Active Directory or setting up a new directory in the AWS Cloud.
AWS IAM
Primary Purpose: Access control for AWS resources | User Types: IAM users, roles for services/people | Federation Support: SAML 2.0, OIDC, Web Identity | Multi-Account Support: Cross-account roles | Authentication Methods: Access keys, passwords, MFA | Token Type: AWS credentials (temporary or long-term) | Integration Points: All AWS services | Key Features: Policies, roles, permission boundaries | Users Managed: IAM users within AWS account | Use Case: AWS API access, cross-account access | Protocol: IAM roles, policies, STS tokens | External Identity: Federation via STS
IAM Identity Center
Primary Purpose: Single Sign-On (SSO) for AWS and applications | User Types: Workforce users, SSO users | Federation Support: SAML 2.0, automatic AWS account federation | Multi-Account Support: Native multi-account access management | Authentication Methods: SSO portal, MFA support | Token Type: AWS credentials via SSO | Integration Points: AWS accounts, SAML applications | Key Features: Permission sets, account assignments | Users Managed: Workforce users across multiple AWS accounts — centralized SSO for organization members | Use Case: Single sign-on to multiple AWS accounts and business applications; centralized permission management with permission sets | Protocol: SAML 2.0 and OIDC for SSO; SCIM for automatic user provisioning from external IdPs | External Identity: Yes — supports external IdPs (Okta, Azure AD, Ping) via SAML 2.0 and SCIM provisioning
Amazon Cognito
Primary Purpose: User authentication for web/mobile apps | User Types: Application end users (customers) | Federation Support: Social (Google, Facebook), SAML, OIDC | Multi-Account Support: Not applicable (app-level) | Authentication Methods: Username/password, social login, MFA | Token Type: JWT tokens (ID, access, refresh) | Integration Points: API Gateway, ALB, mobile SDKs | Key Features: User pools (auth), identity pools (AWS access) | Users Managed: Application end users (sign-up/in) | Use Case: Mobile apps, web apps, user pools | Protocol: OAuth 2.0, SAML 2.0, OpenID Connect | External Identity: Social (Google, Facebook, Apple), SAML
AWS Directory Service
Primary Purpose: Directory integration (Active Directory) | User Types: Directory users (AD) | Federation Support: AD trust relationships | Multi-Account Support: Can be shared across accounts | Authentication Methods: Kerberos, LDAP, NTLM | Token Type: Kerberos tickets | Integration Points: EC2, RDS, WorkSpaces, WorkDocs | Key Features: Managed AD, AD Connector, Simple AD | Users Managed: Enterprise directory users — integrates Active Directory with AWS for corporate identity management | Use Case: Extend on-premises Active Directory to AWS; enable Windows workloads to use existing AD credentials | Protocol: LDAP, Kerberos, NTLM — standard Active Directory protocols for authentication and authorization | External Identity: Yes — AD Connector proxies to on-premises AD; Managed AD supports trust relationships with external domains
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#143 IAM vs Amazon Cognito vs AWS Organizations + SCP
Identity & Access SAA-001 3 cols 6 rows
▼

AWS identity services handle authentication and authorization but serve very different audiences. IAM is for AWS resources and internal users. Cognito is for your APPLICATION's end users. Organizations/SCP is for multi-account governance. Mixing these up is one of the most common mistake points on the SAA-C03 exam.

Feature AWS IAM Amazon Cognito AWS Organizations + SCP
Who It Manages AWS users, roles, services (internal — employees and AWS resources) Application end users (customers, mobile app users, web app users) Multiple AWS accounts under a central management structure
Core Concept Users, Groups, Roles, Policies User Pools (authentication) + Identity Pools (AWS access via federation) Management account, member accounts, Organizational Units (OUs), SCPs
SCP Override IAM? N/A — IAM permissions can be overridden by SCPs Not applicable — SCPs do not apply to Cognito user pool identities; SCPs only affect IAM principals within AWS Organizations member accounts YES — SCP can DENY even if IAM policy ALLOWS. SCP sets the ceiling.
Federation Support SAML 2.0 (corporate SSO, Active Directory federation) Social (Google, Facebook, Apple), SAML, OIDC N/A — account management, not identity
Exam Trigger Keywords Employees, roles, least privilege, cross-account roles, resource policies Mobile app users, web app users, social login, sign-up/sign-in, User Pool Multi-account, prevent all accounts from using a service, restrict region usage
MFA Support Yes — for AWS console and API access Yes — for application user login Applies at account level via IAM
⚠️ Exam Tips

IAM = core access control for AWS resources (users, roles, policies)

Cognito = user authentication for web/mobile applications (sign-up, sign-in, MFA)

Organizations + SCPs = centralized governance across multiple AWS accounts

SCPs set permission guardrails for entire accounts — they restrict what IAM policies can grant

For the exam: 'application user login' = Cognito; 'cross-account governance' = Organizations + SCP

AWS IAM
IAM manages access for AWS users, roles, and services — it controls who (employees, apps, AWS services) can perform which actions on which AWS resources. It is the foundational access control layer for everything inside your AWS account.
Amazon Cognito
Cognito handles authentication and authorization for YOUR application's end users (customers, app users) — it is essentially a user directory as a service. It supports social login (Google, Facebook), SAML federation, and provides JWT tokens your app can use.
AWS Organizations + SCP
AWS Organizations lets you manage multiple AWS accounts centrally, and Service Control Policies (SCPs) set permission guardrails across ALL accounts in the organization. Even if an IAM policy grants access, an SCP can override and deny it.
AWS IAM
Who It Manages: AWS users, roles, services (internal — employees and AWS resources) | Core Concept: Users, Groups, Roles, Policies | SCP Override IAM?: N/A — IAM permissions can be overridden by SCPs | Federation Support: SAML 2.0 (corporate SSO, Active Directory federation) | Exam Trigger Keywords: Employees, roles, least privilege, cross-account roles, resource policies | MFA Support: Yes — for AWS console and API access
Amazon Cognito
Who It Manages: Application end users (customers, mobile app users, web app users) | Core Concept: User Pools (authentication) + Identity Pools (AWS access via federation) | SCP Override IAM?: Not applicable — SCPs do not apply to Cognito user pool identities; SCPs only affect IAM principals within AWS Organizations member accounts | Federation Support: Social (Google, Facebook, Apple), SAML, OIDC | Exam Trigger Keywords: Mobile app users, web app users, social login, sign-up/sign-in, User Pool | MFA Support: Yes — for application user login
AWS Organizations + SCP
Who It Manages: Multiple AWS accounts under a central management structure | Core Concept: Management account, member accounts, Organizational Units (OUs), SCPs | SCP Override IAM?: YES — SCP can DENY even if IAM policy ALLOWS. SCP sets the ceiling. | Federation Support: N/A — account management, not identity | Exam Trigger Keywords: Multi-account, prevent all accounts from using a service, restrict region usage | MFA Support: Applies at account level via IAM
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#144 Identity Federation Services Comparison
Identity & Access identity_federation_services_comparison 5 cols 9 rows
▼

AWS offers multiple identity federation solutions to enable users to access AWS resources using their existing corporate credentials or social identities. IAM Identity Center (formerly SSO) provides centralized workforce access to multiple AWS accounts and cloud applications, Cognito offers authentication for web and mobile applications with User Pools (authentication) and Identity Pools (authorization), while SAML and OIDC are industry-standard protocols for federation. Understanding the differences between these services, their authentication flows, and appropriate use cases is critical for the exam. The choice depends on whether you're building customer-facing applications (Cognito) or managing workforce access (Identity Center), and which identity provider you're integrating with.

Feature IAM Identity Center (SSO) Cognito User Pools Cognito Identity Pools SAML 2.0 OIDC (OpenID Connect)
Primary Purpose Centralized workforce access to AWS accounts and cloud applications User directory and authentication for web/mobile applications Provide temporary AWS credentials to authenticated users Enterprise identity federation protocol for SSO Modern authentication protocol built on OAuth 2.0
Target Users Enterprise workforce employees Application end-users (customers, partners) Application end-users needing AWS resource access Enterprise users accessing AWS via corporate IdP Users accessing AWS via social IdPs or modern authentication
Identity Provider Support Built-in directory, Active Directory, external IdP (SAML 2.0) Built-in directory, SAML, OIDC, social IdPs (Google, Facebook, Amazon) Cognito User Pools, SAML, OIDC, social IdPs, anonymous identities Active Directory Federation Services, Okta, Azure AD, Ping, OneLogin Google, Amazon, Auth0, Okta, any OIDC-compliant IdP
Authentication Flow SP-initiated or IdP-initiated SSO with SAML 2.0 OAuth 2.0 flows (Authorization Code, Implicit, Client Credentials) Exchange identity token for temporary AWS credentials via STS XML-based assertions exchanged between IdP and SP JWT tokens via OAuth 2.0 flows
AWS Access Method Temporary credentials via identity-aware sessions; AWS access portal Provides JWT tokens; does NOT provide AWS credentials directly Temporary AWS credentials via STS (IAM roles) Temporary credentials via STS AssumeRoleWithSAML API Temporary credentials via STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity API
User Management SCIM provisioning from external IdP; manual user creation Self-registration, admin-created users, Lambda triggers for customization No user management - only credential vending Managed by external IdP; no user storage in AWS Managed by external IdP; no user storage in AWS
Security Features MFA, conditional access, session management, access reviews MFA, password policies, account recovery, device tracking, advanced security IAM policy-based access control, unauthenticated role support Digital signatures, encryption, IdP-initiated SSO protection PKCE for mobile apps, state parameter, nonce for replay protection
Pricing Model Free - included with AWS Organizations First 50,000 MAUs free; $0.0055 per MAU thereafter Free - included with Cognito service Free - protocol implementation in IAM Free - protocol implementation in IAM
Best Use Cases Multi-account AWS access, workforce SSO, cloud application access Customer-facing app authentication, mobile app sign-up/sign-in Granting app users direct AWS resource access (S3, DynamoDB) Enterprise SSO with existing corporate identity infrastructure Modern app authentication, mobile apps, social login integration
⚠️ Exam Tips

IAM Identity Center is the recommended way to manage SSO across AWS accounts and business apps

Cognito User Pools handle sign-up/sign-in for customer-facing applications

Cognito Identity Pools grant temporary AWS credentials to authenticated users for direct service access

SAML 2.0 is the enterprise standard for federation with Active Directory and corporate IdPs

For the exam: 'employee access to multiple accounts' = Identity Center; 'mobile app login' = Cognito User Pools

IAM Identity Center (SSO)
Centralized SSO and identity management for multiple AWS accounts and business applications. Integrates with SAML 2.0 identity providers and AWS Organizations for multi-account access management.
Cognito User Pools
Managed user directory for web and mobile application authentication. Provides sign-up, sign-in, MFA, and account recovery. Supports social identity providers (Google, Facebook, Apple) and SAML/OIDC.
Cognito Identity Pools
Provides temporary AWS credentials to authenticated or guest users for accessing AWS services directly from client applications. Maps identity provider tokens to IAM roles.
SAML 2.0
Industry standard protocol for exchanging authentication and authorization data between identity providers and service providers. Used for enterprise federation with Active Directory and other corporate IdPs.
OIDC (OpenID Connect)
Modern authentication protocol built on top of OAuth 2.0. Used for web and mobile app authentication with social identity providers and custom OIDC-compliant identity providers.
IAM Identity Center (SSO)
Primary Purpose: Centralized workforce access to AWS accounts and cloud applications | Target Users: Enterprise workforce employees | Identity Provider Support: Built-in directory, Active Directory, external IdP (SAML 2.0) | Authentication Flow: SP-initiated or IdP-initiated SSO with SAML 2.0 | AWS Access Method: Temporary credentials via identity-aware sessions; AWS access portal | User Management: SCIM provisioning from external IdP; manual user creation | Security Features: MFA, conditional access, session management, access reviews | Pricing Model: Free - included with AWS Organizations | Best Use Cases: Multi-account AWS access, workforce SSO, cloud application access
Cognito User Pools
Primary Purpose: User directory and authentication for web/mobile applications | Target Users: Application end-users (customers, partners) | Identity Provider Support: Built-in directory, SAML, OIDC, social IdPs (Google, Facebook, Amazon) | Authentication Flow: OAuth 2.0 flows (Authorization Code, Implicit, Client Credentials) | AWS Access Method: Provides JWT tokens; does NOT provide AWS credentials directly | User Management: Self-registration, admin-created users, Lambda triggers for customization | Security Features: MFA, password policies, account recovery, device tracking, advanced security | Pricing Model: First 50,000 MAUs free; $0.0055 per MAU thereafter | Best Use Cases: Customer-facing app authentication, mobile app sign-up/sign-in
Cognito Identity Pools
Primary Purpose: Provide temporary AWS credentials to authenticated users | Target Users: Application end-users needing AWS resource access | Identity Provider Support: Cognito User Pools, SAML, OIDC, social IdPs, anonymous identities | Authentication Flow: Exchange identity token for temporary AWS credentials via STS | AWS Access Method: Temporary AWS credentials via STS (IAM roles) | User Management: No user management - only credential vending | Security Features: IAM policy-based access control, unauthenticated role support | Pricing Model: Free - included with Cognito service | Best Use Cases: Granting app users direct AWS resource access (S3, DynamoDB)
SAML 2.0
Primary Purpose: Enterprise identity federation protocol for SSO | Target Users: Enterprise users accessing AWS via corporate IdP | Identity Provider Support: Active Directory Federation Services, Okta, Azure AD, Ping, OneLogin | Authentication Flow: XML-based assertions exchanged between IdP and SP | AWS Access Method: Temporary credentials via STS AssumeRoleWithSAML API | User Management: Managed by external IdP; no user storage in AWS | Security Features: Digital signatures, encryption, IdP-initiated SSO protection | Pricing Model: Free - protocol implementation in IAM | Best Use Cases: Enterprise SSO with existing corporate identity infrastructure
OIDC (OpenID Connect)
Primary Purpose: Modern authentication protocol built on OAuth 2.0 | Target Users: Users accessing AWS via social IdPs or modern authentication | Identity Provider Support: Google, Amazon, Auth0, Okta, any OIDC-compliant IdP | Authentication Flow: JWT tokens via OAuth 2.0 flows | AWS Access Method: Temporary credentials via STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity API | User Management: Managed by external IdP; no user storage in AWS | Security Features: PKCE for mobile apps, state parameter, nonce for replay protection | Pricing Model: Free - protocol implementation in IAM | Best Use Cases: Modern app authentication, mobile apps, social login integration
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#145 Identity and Access Services
Identity & Access saa_d1_identity_access 4 cols 6 rows
▼
Feature IAM IAM Identity Center STS Amazon Cognito
Primary purpose Define permissions for AWS resources Centralize workforce sign-in and account access Issue temporary credentials for assumed roles Authenticate and manage application end users
Typical user Admins, services, workloads Employees and internal users Workloads or users needing delegated temporary access Customers, mobile users, web app users
Best exam clue Least privilege, policies, users, roles Multi-account SSO, workforce federation AssumeRole, cross-account, temporary creds User pools, identity pools, app authentication
Credential model Long-lived users or roles Federated workforce sessions Short-lived temporary credentials App-facing identity tokens and federation
Multi-account fit Possible but harder to manage at scale Excellent Excellent when paired with roles Not the main tool for AWS account administration
Common mistake to avoid Using many IAM users when federation is cleaner Using it as a customer login product Treating it as a full identity store Using it for admin SSO across AWS accounts
⚠️ Exam Tips

IAM is free and foundational — every AWS account uses it for authentication and authorization

IAM Identity Center replaces manual IAM user management for organizations with multiple accounts

Cognito supports millions of users for consumer applications with social login support

Always prefer IAM Roles over long-lived access keys for security best practices

For the exam: 'least privilege' means giving exactly the permissions needed, nothing more

IAM
IAM is the foundational AWS permissions system for users, groups, roles, and policies inside AWS accounts. On the exam, think of it as the core authorization layer for AWS resources.
IAM Identity Center
IAM Identity Center is for workforce access and centralized SSO across multiple AWS accounts and business apps. It shines when you need human user sign-in at scale without juggling long-lived IAM users everywhere.
STS
AWS STS issues temporary credentials, which is the exam clue for role assumption, cross-account access, and short-lived sessions. It is the engine underneath many secure delegation patterns.
Amazon Cognito
Amazon Cognito is mainly about customer and app user identity, not workforce admin access. On scenario questions, it usually appears when an application needs sign-up, sign-in, or token-based identity for end users.
IAM
Primary purpose: Define permissions for AWS resources | Typical user: Admins, services, workloads | Best exam clue: Least privilege, policies, users, roles | Credential model: Long-lived users or roles | Multi-account fit: Possible but harder to manage at scale | Common mistake to avoid: Using many IAM users when federation is cleaner
IAM Identity Center
Primary purpose: Centralize workforce sign-in and account access | Typical user: Employees and internal users | Best exam clue: Multi-account SSO, workforce federation | Credential model: Federated workforce sessions | Multi-account fit: Excellent | Common mistake to avoid: Using it as a customer login product
STS
Primary purpose: Issue temporary credentials for assumed roles | Typical user: Workloads or users needing delegated temporary access | Best exam clue: AssumeRole, cross-account, temporary creds | Credential model: Short-lived temporary credentials | Multi-account fit: Excellent when paired with roles | Common mistake to avoid: Treating it as a full identity store
Amazon Cognito
Primary purpose: Authenticate and manage application end users | Typical user: Customers, mobile users, web app users | Best exam clue: User pools, identity pools, app authentication | Credential model: App-facing identity tokens and federation | Multi-account fit: Not the main tool for AWS account administration | Common mistake to avoid: Using it for admin SSO across AWS accounts
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 1 emphasizes designing secure access across accounts, using federated identity, roles, and least privilege. This table helps separate who authenticates users, who grants permissions, and who issues temporary credentials.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

Encryption & Secrets 4 tables
#146 Encryption Services Comparison
Encryption & Secrets encryption_services_comparison 6 cols 9 rows
▼

AWS provides multiple encryption services to protect data at rest and in transit. Understanding the differences between KMS key types (Customer Managed, AWS Managed, AWS Owned), CloudHSM for dedicated HSMs, Secrets Manager for credential storage, and Certificate Manager for TLS/SSL certificates is essential. The exam tests your ability to select the appropriate encryption solution based on compliance requirements, key control needs, automation requirements, and integration patterns. Key considerations include who manages the keys, FIPS 140-2 validation levels, automatic rotation capabilities, and service integrations.

Feature KMS Customer Managed Keys (CMK) KMS AWS Managed Keys KMS AWS Owned Keys CloudHSM Secrets Manager Certificate Manager (ACM)
Key Ownership & Control Customer owns and fully controls the key lifecycle AWS owns and manages; customer has limited control AWS owns and manages; no customer visibility Customer owns keys in dedicated HSM; AWS has no access AWS manages encryption keys; customer manages secret values AWS manages private keys for TLS certificates
FIPS 140-2 Level Level 2 (validated HSMs) Level 2 (validated HSMs) Level 2 (validated HSMs) Level 3 (dedicated HSM hardware) Level 2 (uses KMS for encryption) Level 2 (uses KMS for key storage)
Key Rotation Automatic yearly rotation (optional, enabled via API) Automatic yearly rotation (AWS managed) Automatic rotation (AWS managed, not visible) Customer responsible for rotation Automatic rotation supported via Lambda (configurable interval) Automatic renewal before expiration
Pricing Model $1/month per key + $0.03 per 10,000 API calls Free (included in service cost) Free (included in service cost) $1.45/hour per HSM (~$1,053/month) + upfront provisioning $0.40/month per secret + $0.05 per 10,000 API calls Free for ACM-issued public certificates
Primary Use Cases Compliance requirements, key deletion capability, granular access control Default encryption when no specific key required Automatic encryption (S3 SSE-S3, DynamoDB default) Regulatory requirements for dedicated HSM, custom key storage Database credentials, API keys, OAuth tokens, automatic rotation TLS/SSL certificates for CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway
Deletion Capability Customer can schedule deletion (7-30 day waiting period) Cannot be deleted by customer Cannot be deleted by customer Customer can delete keys immediately Customer can delete secrets immediately Customer can delete certificates
Regional Scope Region-specific; multi-Region keys available Region-specific May span multiple regions (AWS managed) Region-specific; cluster can span AZs Region-specific; can replicate to other regions Region-specific for ELB; global for CloudFront
Service Integrations S3, EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, SQS, SNS, Lambda, 100+ services Same services as CMK (default encryption) S3 SSE-S3, DynamoDB, EFS, Aurora Custom applications via PKCS#11, JCE, OpenSSL, Windows CSP RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB, custom secrets via Lambda CloudFront, ALB, NLB, API Gateway
Audit & Compliance Full CloudTrail logging of all API calls CloudTrail logs key usage (not management) No CloudTrail visibility HSM audit logs to CloudWatch Logs CloudTrail logs all API calls CloudTrail logs management operations
⚠️ Exam Tips

KMS CMK = you control key policy, rotation, and deletion; best for data sovereignty requirements

KMS AWS Managed Keys = AWS manages everything; simpler but less customer control

CloudHSM = dedicated HSM hardware for FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or regulatory requirements

Secrets Manager rotates credentials automatically; Parameter Store (free tier) does not auto-rotate

ACM provides free SSL/TLS certificates for ALB, CloudFront, and API Gateway with automatic renewal

For the exam: 'FIPS 140-2 Level 3' = CloudHSM; 'automatic credential rotation' = Secrets Manager

KMS Customer Managed Keys (CMK)
Customer-created and controlled encryption keys in KMS. You define key policies, enable/disable keys, and manage rotation. Full audit trail via CloudTrail. Best for data requiring customer-controlled encryption.
KMS AWS Managed Keys
Keys created, managed, and used by AWS services on your behalf (e.g., aws/s3, aws/ebs). Automatically rotated every year. You cannot change key policies or manage these keys directly.
KMS AWS Owned Keys
Keys that AWS owns and manages across multiple accounts. Used internally by AWS services. You have no visibility into or control over these keys. No charges and no CloudTrail logging.
CloudHSM
Dedicated hardware security modules in the AWS cloud. FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated. You have exclusive control of encryption keys. Required for regulatory compliance needing dedicated HSM hardware.
Secrets Manager
Securely stores, rotates, and retrieves database credentials, API keys, and other secrets. Built-in automatic rotation for RDS, Redshift, and DocumentDB credentials without application downtime.
Certificate Manager (ACM)
Provisions, manages, and deploys public and private SSL/TLS certificates for AWS services and internal resources. Free public certificates with automatic renewal for use with ALB, CloudFront, and API Gateway.
KMS Customer Managed Keys (CMK)
Key Ownership & Control: Customer owns and fully controls the key lifecycle | FIPS 140-2 Level: Level 2 (validated HSMs) | Key Rotation: Automatic yearly rotation (optional, enabled via API) | Pricing Model: $1/month per key + $0.03 per 10,000 API calls | Primary Use Cases: Compliance requirements, key deletion capability, granular access control | Deletion Capability: Customer can schedule deletion (7-30 day waiting period) | Regional Scope: Region-specific; multi-Region keys available | Service Integrations: S3, EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, SQS, SNS, Lambda, 100+ services | Audit & Compliance: Full CloudTrail logging of all API calls
KMS AWS Managed Keys
Key Ownership & Control: AWS owns and manages; customer has limited control | FIPS 140-2 Level: Level 2 (validated HSMs) | Key Rotation: Automatic yearly rotation (AWS managed) | Pricing Model: Free (included in service cost) | Primary Use Cases: Default encryption when no specific key required | Deletion Capability: Cannot be deleted by customer | Regional Scope: Region-specific | Service Integrations: Same services as CMK (default encryption) | Audit & Compliance: CloudTrail logs key usage (not management)
KMS AWS Owned Keys
Key Ownership & Control: AWS owns and manages; no customer visibility | FIPS 140-2 Level: Level 2 (validated HSMs) | Key Rotation: Automatic rotation (AWS managed, not visible) | Pricing Model: Free (included in service cost) | Primary Use Cases: Automatic encryption (S3 SSE-S3, DynamoDB default) | Deletion Capability: Cannot be deleted by customer | Regional Scope: May span multiple regions (AWS managed) | Service Integrations: S3 SSE-S3, DynamoDB, EFS, Aurora | Audit & Compliance: No CloudTrail visibility
CloudHSM
Key Ownership & Control: Customer owns keys in dedicated HSM; AWS has no access | FIPS 140-2 Level: Level 3 (dedicated HSM hardware) | Key Rotation: Customer responsible for rotation | Pricing Model: $1.45/hour per HSM (~$1,053/month) + upfront provisioning | Primary Use Cases: Regulatory requirements for dedicated HSM, custom key storage | Deletion Capability: Customer can delete keys immediately | Regional Scope: Region-specific; cluster can span AZs | Service Integrations: Custom applications via PKCS#11, JCE, OpenSSL, Windows CSP | Audit & Compliance: HSM audit logs to CloudWatch Logs
Secrets Manager
Key Ownership & Control: AWS manages encryption keys; customer manages secret values | FIPS 140-2 Level: Level 2 (uses KMS for encryption) | Key Rotation: Automatic rotation supported via Lambda (configurable interval) | Pricing Model: $0.40/month per secret + $0.05 per 10,000 API calls | Primary Use Cases: Database credentials, API keys, OAuth tokens, automatic rotation | Deletion Capability: Customer can delete secrets immediately | Regional Scope: Region-specific; can replicate to other regions | Service Integrations: RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB, custom secrets via Lambda | Audit & Compliance: CloudTrail logs all API calls
Certificate Manager (ACM)
Key Ownership & Control: AWS manages private keys for TLS certificates | FIPS 140-2 Level: Level 2 (uses KMS for key storage) | Key Rotation: Automatic renewal before expiration | Pricing Model: Free for ACM-issued public certificates | Primary Use Cases: TLS/SSL certificates for CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway | Deletion Capability: Customer can delete certificates | Regional Scope: Region-specific for ELB; global for CloudFront | Service Integrations: CloudFront, ALB, NLB, API Gateway | Audit & Compliance: CloudTrail logs management operations
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimi-agsw].json

#147 Encryption and Secrets Management Comparison
Encryption & Secrets encryption_and_secrets_management_comparison 3 cols 5 rows
▼

Comparing AWS services for data encryption and secrets management

Feature AWS KMS AWS Secrets Manager AWS Certificate Manager
What It Manages Encryption keys (symmetric and asymmetric) Application secrets (passwords, API keys) SSL/TLS certificates — provision, manage, and deploy public and private certificates for AWS services
Use Case Encrypt data at rest in AWS services Store DB credentials, rotate passwords HTTPS for ALB, CloudFront, API Gateway; private CA for internal services; automatic certificate renewal
Rotation Manual or automatic (via Lambda) Automatic rotation built-in Automatic renewal for ACM-issued public certificates; private CA certificates configurable for renewal
Integration S3, EBS, RDS, Lambda, etc. RDS, DocumentDB, Redshift, other services ALB, NLB, CloudFront, API Gateway, Elastic Beanstalk; private certificates via ACM Private CA
Cost Free tier + per-key fees Per-secret per-month + API calls Free for public certificates; ACM Private CA costs $400/month per CA + per-certificate fees
💡 Mnemonics

KSA = KMS for Keys, Secrets for secrets, ACM for Certificates

⚠️ Exam Tips

KMS is for encryption keys; Secrets Manager is for application secrets

Use KMS customer managed keys (CMK) for production encryption

Secrets Manager can automatically rotate credentials for RDS

ACM certificates auto-renew; no need to manually update

Never store secrets in code, environment variables, or S3 buckets - use Secrets Manager

AWS KMS
Key Management Service providing secure, compliant key storage and management for data encryption, integrated with most AWS services for at-rest encryption.
AWS Secrets Manager
A secrets management service that securely stores, encrypts, and rotates database credentials, API keys, and other secrets throughout their lifecycle.
AWS Certificate Manager
A service that provisions, manages, and deploys SSL/TLS certificates for use with AWS services and internal resources, eliminating manual certificate management.
AWS KMS
What It Manages: Encryption keys (symmetric and asymmetric) | Use Case: Encrypt data at rest in AWS services | Rotation: Manual or automatic (via Lambda) | Integration: S3, EBS, RDS, Lambda, etc. | Cost: Free tier + per-key fees
AWS Secrets Manager
What It Manages: Application secrets (passwords, API keys) | Use Case: Store DB credentials, rotate passwords | Rotation: Automatic rotation built-in | Integration: RDS, DocumentDB, Redshift, other services | Cost: Per-secret per-month + API calls
AWS Certificate Manager
What It Manages: SSL/TLS certificates — provision, manage, and deploy public and private certificates for AWS services | Use Case: HTTPS for ALB, CloudFront, API Gateway; private CA for internal services; automatic certificate renewal | Rotation: Automatic renewal for ACM-issued public certificates; private CA certificates configurable for renewal | Integration: ALB, NLB, CloudFront, API Gateway, Elastic Beanstalk; private certificates via ACM Private CA | Cost: Free for public certificates; ACM Private CA costs $400/month per CA + per-certificate fees
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax-dr].json

#148 KMS vs CloudHSM vs Secrets Manager vs Parameter Store
Encryption & Secrets SAA-003_2 4 cols 6 rows
▼

AWS provides multiple services for managing encryption keys and sensitive configuration values. The exam will describe what you need to protect (keys, passwords, config) and the compliance requirements, then ask you to choose the right service. The key differentiators are: ownership of the key hardware (KMS vs CloudHSM) and storing secrets vs config values (Secrets Manager vs Parameter Store).

Feature AWS KMS AWS CloudHSM AWS Secrets Manager SSM Parameter Store
Primary Use Create and manage encryption keys for AWS services Dedicated hardware for highest-compliance key storage Store and automatically rotate secrets (passwords, API keys) Store configuration values and basic secrets
Key Ownership AWS manages hardware; YOU own/control keys YOU own the hardware AND keys — AWS has NO access N/A — stores secrets, uses KMS for encryption N/A — stores values, uses KMS for SecureString encryption
Automatic Rotation Yes — annual key rotation (configurable) Manual — you manage the hardware YES — built-in automatic rotation with Lambda No — manual rotation required
Cost Per key + per API call High — dedicated hardware instance (~$1.60/hour) Per secret per month + API calls Free for standard; $0.05/advanced parameter/month
Compliance Level FIPS 140-2 Level 2 FIPS 140-2 Level 3 (highest) Uses KMS — inherits FIPS 140-2 Level 2 Uses KMS for encrypted params
Exam Trigger Keywords 'Encrypt data at rest,' 'managed key,' 'S3 encryption,' 'EBS encryption' 'FIPS Level 3,' 'dedicated hardware,' 'single-tenant HSM,' 'bring your own key (BYOK)' 'Rotate database password,' 'automatic rotation,' 'store API keys,' 'RDS password rotation' 'Store config values,' 'free secret storage,' 'environment variables,' 'non-rotating secrets'
⚠️ Exam Tips

KMS = managed encryption key service for most encryption needs; CloudHSM = dedicated hardware

Secrets Manager = designed for secrets with automatic rotation support (costs $0.40/secret/month)

Parameter Store = configuration data and secrets (free tier, no auto-rotation for secrets)

CloudHSM gives you exclusive control of HSM hardware — required for specific compliance standards

For the exam: 'rotate database credentials automatically' = Secrets Manager; 'FIPS 140-2 Level 3' = CloudHSM

AWS KMS
KMS (Key Management Service) is AWS's managed key management service — AWS manages the hardware but you control and own the keys. It integrates natively with almost every AWS service for encryption and is the standard choice for encrypting data in S3, EBS, RDS, and more.
AWS CloudHSM
CloudHSM (Hardware Security Module) gives you a dedicated hardware device in the cloud where YOU fully own and control the keys — AWS cannot access them. It is required when regulations (FIPS 140-2 Level 3) mandate that no third party can ever access your keys.
AWS Secrets Manager
Secrets Manager is purpose-built for storing, rotating, and retrieving SECRETS (database passwords, API keys, OAuth tokens). It automatically rotates credentials on a schedule, which is critical for security compliance.
SSM Parameter Store
Parameter Store (part of AWS Systems Manager) stores configuration data and secrets as key-value pairs. It is simpler and free for standard parameters — often used for application configuration like environment variables, endpoint URLs, and non-sensitive settings.
AWS KMS
Primary Use: Create and manage encryption keys for AWS services | Key Ownership: AWS manages hardware; YOU own/control keys | Automatic Rotation: Yes — annual key rotation (configurable) | Cost: Per key + per API call | Compliance Level: FIPS 140-2 Level 2 | Exam Trigger Keywords: 'Encrypt data at rest,' 'managed key,' 'S3 encryption,' 'EBS encryption'
AWS CloudHSM
Primary Use: Dedicated hardware for highest-compliance key storage | Key Ownership: YOU own the hardware AND keys — AWS has NO access | Automatic Rotation: Manual — you manage the hardware | Cost: High — dedicated hardware instance (~$1.60/hour) | Compliance Level: FIPS 140-2 Level 3 (highest) | Exam Trigger Keywords: 'FIPS Level 3,' 'dedicated hardware,' 'single-tenant HSM,' 'bring your own key (BYOK)'
AWS Secrets Manager
Primary Use: Store and automatically rotate secrets (passwords, API keys) | Key Ownership: N/A — stores secrets, uses KMS for encryption | Automatic Rotation: YES — built-in automatic rotation with Lambda | Cost: Per secret per month + API calls | Compliance Level: Uses KMS — inherits FIPS 140-2 Level 2 | Exam Trigger Keywords: 'Rotate database password,' 'automatic rotation,' 'store API keys,' 'RDS password rotation'
SSM Parameter Store
Primary Use: Store configuration values and basic secrets | Key Ownership: N/A — stores values, uses KMS for SecureString encryption | Automatic Rotation: No — manual rotation required | Cost: Free for standard; $0.05/advanced parameter/month | Compliance Level: Uses KMS for encrypted params | Exam Trigger Keywords: 'Store config values,' 'free secret storage,' 'environment variables,' 'non-rotating secrets'
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#149 Keys, Secrets, and Certificates
Encryption & Secrets saa_d1_keys_secrets_certs 3 cols 6 rows
▼
Feature AWS KMS AWS Secrets Manager AWS Certificate Manager
Main job Manage encryption keys Store and rotate secrets Provision and manage TLS certificates
Protects Data at rest Credentials and secrets Data in transit
Typical exam clue Envelope encryption, CMKs, key policies Automatic credential rotation HTTPS listeners, certificate renewal
Used by other services Very often Frequently For supported endpoints and load balancers
Human-readable secret content No, not its primary role Yes No, certificate lifecycle not app secrets
Common mistake to avoid Using it as a secret vault Thinking it replaces TLS certificates Thinking it manages database passwords
⚠️ Exam Tips

KMS generates and manages encryption keys; use it for encrypting S3, EBS, RDS, and more

Secrets Manager securely stores secrets and rotates RDS/Redshift/DocumentDB credentials automatically

Parameter Store can store secrets too (encrypted with KMS) but lacks built-in rotation

ACM handles SSL/TLS certificate provisioning and renewal at no extra cost for supported services

For the exam: encryption at rest = KMS; API keys/passwords = Secrets Manager; HTTPS certificates = ACM

AWS KMS
AWS KMS is the managed key service used to control encryption keys for data at rest across AWS services. In exam language, it is the default answer when the requirement says customer-managed encryption keys or key access policies.
AWS Secrets Manager
Secrets Manager stores and rotates secrets such as database passwords, API keys, and credentials. It is not just storage; the key exam clue is secret lifecycle and rotation.
AWS Certificate Manager
ACM provisions and manages TLS certificates for encryption in transit on supported AWS services. If the requirement is HTTPS, TLS, or certificate renewal, ACM is the favorite.
AWS KMS
Main job: Manage encryption keys | Protects: Data at rest | Typical exam clue: Envelope encryption, CMKs, key policies | Used by other services: Very often | Human-readable secret content: No, not its primary role | Common mistake to avoid: Using it as a secret vault
AWS Secrets Manager
Main job: Store and rotate secrets | Protects: Credentials and secrets | Typical exam clue: Automatic credential rotation | Used by other services: Frequently | Human-readable secret content: Yes | Common mistake to avoid: Thinking it replaces TLS certificates
AWS Certificate Manager
Main job: Provision and manage TLS certificates | Protects: Data in transit | Typical exam clue: HTTPS listeners, certificate renewal | Used by other services: For supported endpoints and load balancers | Human-readable secret content: No, certificate lifecycle not app secrets | Common mistake to avoid: Thinking it manages database passwords
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 1 includes encryption at rest, encryption in transit, and secure secret handling. This is a classic exam tripwire because KMS, Secrets Manager, and ACM solve different problems.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json

Cost Management 1 tables
#150 AWS Cost Management Tools Comparison
Cost Management SAA-007_2 4 cols 16 rows
▼

Comparison of AWS cost management services for monitoring, alerting, and anomaly detection

Comparing AWS cost optimization tools - critical for Domain 4 (Cost-Optimized Architectures)

Feature AWS Cost Explorer AWS Budgets AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR) Cost Anomaly Detection
Primary Purpose Historical analysis, visualization, and trend identification Setting spending limits with proactive alerts Detailed cost data export Automatic detection of unexpected spending spikes
Granularity Daily, monthly Monthly, quarterly, yearly Hourly (most detailed) Daily detection
Alert Mechanism Manual review Automated alerts (email/SNS) S3 notifications Automated alerts
Forecasting Yes - includes cost forecasting Yes - forecasts to predict if budget will be exceeded No No - focuses on anomaly detection not forecasting
Data Export CSV download No export Detailed CSV to S3 Report available
Reservation Analysis RI recommendations Track RI/Savings Plans RI utilization data No
Best For Understanding cost trends Proactive cost control Detailed analysis, chargeback Catching unexpected costs
Cost Free Free (first 2 budgets) Free Free
Integration Standalone, APIs SNS, Chatbot Athena, QuickSight SNS alerts
Proactive vs Reactive Reactive - analyze what already happened Proactive - alert before thresholds exceeded Reactive — provides detailed historical billing data for analysis after costs are incurred Reactive but automated - catches issues early
Machine Learning Used for anomaly detection feature No ML - rule-based threshold alerts No ML features — raw data export for external analysis tools (Athena, QuickSight, third-party) Yes - ML models detect statistical anomalies
Budget Types N/A - it's an analysis tool Cost, usage, RI coverage, savings plan utilization Not applicable — CUR is a reporting tool, not a budgeting tool N/A - detection feature in Cost Explorer
Alert Recipients Viewers can explore data Alert emails to billing alerts contacts Not applicable — CUR does not send alerts; use AWS Budgets for alert notifications Alert emails to subscribed recipients
Real-time Alerts No - data refreshed daily (up to 24 hours lag) Yes - alerts trigger based on threshold % No — CUR data is delivered to S3 up to 3x daily; not real-time Yes - alerts when anomaly detected (usually daily)
Use Case Monthly cost reviews, trend analysis, identifying optimization opportunities Preventing budget overruns, tracking RI/SP savings Deep-dive cost analysis, chargeback/showback, custom dashboards via Athena + QuickSight, third-party cost tools Catching unexpected costs from misconfigurations or attacks
Pricing Free for basic features; charge for anomaly detection + RI recommendations First 2 budgets free; \$0.50 per budget per month after Free to generate; pay only for S3 storage and any query tools (Athena, QuickSight) Included with Cost Explorer
💡 Mnemonics

CE = Cost Explorer (visualize), Budgets = Budget alerts, CUR = Cost Usage Report (detailed), CAD = Cost Anomaly Detection

Need to see costs? Cost Explorer. Need alerts when over budget? Budgets. Need detailed data for analysis? CUR. Need to catch unexpected spikes? Anomaly Detection.

E.B.A - Explorer shows trends, Budgets prevent overruns, Anomaly detects surprises

⚠️ Exam Tips

Cost Explorer provides forecasts and RI/Savings Plan recommendations

AWS Budgets alerts BEFORE you exceed your spending limit

CUR is the most detailed - includes hourly data and all tags

Cost Anomaly Detection uses ML to identify unusual spending patterns

CUR can be analyzed with Athena and visualized with QuickSight

Cost allocation tags are essential for cost tracking across all tools

Use AWS Budgets FIRST to set spending guardrails - this is proactive cost management

Cost Explorer shows historical data and trends - use for analysis after-the-fact

Cost Anomaly Detection uses ML to find unusual patterns - set up for unexpected cost alerts

All three tools work together: Budgets for guardrails, Explorer for analysis, Anomaly for surprises

For SAA-C03, understand when to recommend each tool based on cost management needs

AWS Cost Explorer
A tool that enables visualization and analysis of AWS costs and usage over time. Provides pre-configured views of cost data, forecasting capabilities, and recommendations for Reserved Instances and Savings Plans based on usage patterns.
AWS Budgets
A service that enables setting custom budgets to track costs and usage, with alerts when actual or forecasted costs exceed budgeted amounts. Supports cost budgets, usage budgets, and RI/Savings Plans coverage budgets.
AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR)
The most comprehensive cost and usage data available, published to S3 in CSV format. Includes hourly granularity, resource-level data, and all cost allocation tags, ideal for detailed analysis with Athena or BI tools.
Cost Anomaly Detection
A service that uses machine learning to detect unusual spending patterns in AWS accounts. Continuously monitors costs and alerts on anomalies, helping identify unexpected charges before they become significant issues.
AWS Cost Explorer
Primary Purpose: Historical analysis, visualization, and trend identification | Granularity: Daily, monthly | Alert Mechanism: Manual review | Forecasting: Yes - includes cost forecasting | Data Export: CSV download | Reservation Analysis: RI recommendations | Best For: Understanding cost trends | Cost: Free | Integration: Standalone, APIs | Proactive vs Reactive: Reactive - analyze what already happened | Machine Learning: Used for anomaly detection feature | Budget Types: N/A - it's an analysis tool | Alert Recipients: Viewers can explore data | Real-time Alerts: No - data refreshed daily (up to 24 hours lag) | Use Case: Monthly cost reviews, trend analysis, identifying optimization opportunities | Pricing: Free for basic features; charge for anomaly detection + RI recommendations
AWS Budgets
