Primary Purpose: Setting spending limits with proactive alerts | Granularity: Monthly, quarterly, yearly | Alert Mechanism: Automated alerts (email/SNS) | Forecasting: Yes - forecasts to predict if budget will be exceeded | Data Export: No export | Reservation Analysis: Track RI/Savings Plans | Best For: Proactive cost control | Cost: Free (first 2 budgets) | Integration: SNS, Chatbot | Proactive vs Reactive: Proactive - alert before thresholds exceeded | Machine Learning: No ML - rule-based threshold alerts | Budget Types: Cost, usage, RI coverage, savings plan utilization | Alert Recipients: Alert emails to billing alerts contacts | Real-time Alerts: Yes - alerts trigger based on threshold % | Use Case: Preventing budget overruns, tracking RI/SP savings | Pricing: First 2 budgets free; \$0.50 per budget per month after
AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR)
Primary Purpose: Detailed cost data export | Granularity: Hourly (most detailed) | Alert Mechanism: S3 notifications | Forecasting: No | Data Export: Detailed CSV to S3 | Reservation Analysis: RI utilization data | Best For: Detailed analysis, chargeback | Cost: Free | Integration: Athena, QuickSight | Proactive vs Reactive: Reactive — provides detailed historical billing data for analysis after costs are incurred | Machine Learning: No ML features — raw data export for external analysis tools (Athena, QuickSight, third-party) | Budget Types: Not applicable — CUR is a reporting tool, not a budgeting tool | Alert Recipients: Not applicable — CUR does not send alerts; use AWS Budgets for alert notifications | Real-time Alerts: No — CUR data is delivered to S3 up to 3x daily; not real-time | Use Case: Deep-dive cost analysis, chargeback/showback, custom dashboards via Athena + QuickSight, third-party cost tools | Pricing: Free to generate; pay only for S3 storage and any query tools (Athena, QuickSight)
Cost Anomaly Detection
Primary Purpose: Automatic detection of unexpected spending spikes | Granularity: Daily detection | Alert Mechanism: Automated alerts | Forecasting: No - focuses on anomaly detection not forecasting | Data Export: Report available | Reservation Analysis: No | Best For: Catching unexpected costs | Cost: Free | Integration: SNS alerts | Proactive vs Reactive: Reactive but automated - catches issues early | Machine Learning: Yes - ML models detect statistical anomalies | Budget Types: N/A - detection feature in Cost Explorer | Alert Recipients: Alert emails to subscribed recipients | Real-time Alerts: Yes - alerts when anomaly detected (usually daily) | Use Case: Catching unexpected costs from misconfigurations or attacks | Pricing: Included with Cost Explorer
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[minimax].json

Disaster Recovery 2 tables
#151 Disaster Recovery Strategies — Backup/Restore vs Pilot Light vs Warm Standby vs Active-Active
Disaster Recovery SAA-006_2 4 cols 6 rows
▼

The exam presents a business scenario with RPO (Recovery Point Objective) and RTO (Recovery Time Objective) requirements and asks you to identify the correct DR strategy. RPO = how much DATA you can afford to lose (time since last backup). RTO = how long the business can be DOWN. Shorter RTO/RPO = higher cost.

Feature Backup and Restore Pilot Light Warm Standby Active-Active (Multi-Site)
RPO (Data Loss Tolerance) Hours to days Minutes Seconds to minutes Near zero — real-time replication
RTO (Downtime Tolerance) Hours to days 10 minutes to 1 hour Minutes Near zero — instant failover
Cost Lowest — only storage costs Low — minimal running resources Medium — scaled-down duplicate running 24/7 Highest — full duplicate environment running 24/7
What Runs in Standby Region Nothing — just data backups in S3 Core data tier only (database replication active) Scaled-down full stack (database + app servers at reduced capacity) Full production stack at full capacity
Recovery Action Required Restore backup, provision servers, reconfigure — high manual effort Scale up app tier (pre-configured AMIs help speed this up) Scale up capacity — mostly automated DNS/load balancer reroute — mostly automated
AWS Services Used AWS Backup, S3, S3 Glacier RDS cross-region read replica, pre-baked AMIs Auto Scaling, RDS Multi-AZ or replica, Route 53 health checks Route 53 latency/weighted routing, DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database
⚠️ Exam Tips

Backup/Restore = cheapest, highest RTO/RPO — restore from backups when disaster occurs

Pilot Light = core infrastructure running minimally, scale up when needed

Warm Standby = scaled-down working copy of production, ready to scale up quickly

Active-Active (Multi-Site) = full production in multiple regions, instant failover, highest cost

RTO/RPO decreases as cost increases: Backup/Restore > Pilot Light > Warm Standby > Active-Active

For the exam: match the RTO/RPO requirement to the appropriate strategy

Backup and Restore
The simplest and cheapest strategy — your data is regularly backed up to S3 or another durable store, and in a disaster you restore from that backup. Recovery takes hours to days, so RPO and RTO are highest (worst). Good for non-critical systems.
Pilot Light
A minimal version of your environment is always running in another region — typically just the database replication is live (the 'pilot light'). In a disaster, you scale up the dormant compute resources to handle traffic. Faster than Backup/Restore but still requires some manual scale-up.
Warm Standby
A scaled-down but FULLY FUNCTIONAL version of your production environment runs continuously in another region. In a disaster, you simply scale it up to full production capacity. It is ready to serve limited traffic immediately and can go full-scale quickly.
Active-Active (Multi-Site)
Your FULL production environment runs simultaneously in MULTIPLE regions, all serving live traffic. There is virtually no downtime or data loss because traffic is simply rerouted from the failed region. This is the most expensive but offers the lowest possible RTO and RPO.
Backup and Restore
RPO (Data Loss Tolerance): Hours to days | RTO (Downtime Tolerance): Hours to days | Cost: Lowest — only storage costs | What Runs in Standby Region: Nothing — just data backups in S3 | Recovery Action Required: Restore backup, provision servers, reconfigure — high manual effort | AWS Services Used: AWS Backup, S3, S3 Glacier
Pilot Light
RPO (Data Loss Tolerance): Minutes | RTO (Downtime Tolerance): 10 minutes to 1 hour | Cost: Low — minimal running resources | What Runs in Standby Region: Core data tier only (database replication active) | Recovery Action Required: Scale up app tier (pre-configured AMIs help speed this up) | AWS Services Used: RDS cross-region read replica, pre-baked AMIs
Warm Standby
RPO (Data Loss Tolerance): Seconds to minutes | RTO (Downtime Tolerance): Minutes | Cost: Medium — scaled-down duplicate running 24/7 | What Runs in Standby Region: Scaled-down full stack (database + app servers at reduced capacity) | Recovery Action Required: Scale up capacity — mostly automated | AWS Services Used: Auto Scaling, RDS Multi-AZ or replica, Route 53 health checks
Active-Active (Multi-Site)
RPO (Data Loss Tolerance): Near zero — real-time replication | RTO (Downtime Tolerance): Near zero — instant failover | Cost: Highest — full duplicate environment running 24/7 | What Runs in Standby Region: Full production stack at full capacity | Recovery Action Required: DNS/load balancer reroute — mostly automated | AWS Services Used: Route 53 latency/weighted routing, DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database
📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[sonnet46].json

#152 Disaster Recovery Strategy Comparison
Disaster Recovery saa_d2_dr_strategies 4 cols 12 rows
▼

This table compares disaster recovery strategies on AWS based on Recovery Point Objective (RPO) and Recovery Time Objective (RTO). The strategies range from simple backup and restore to active-active multi-region deployments with varying cost and complexity trade-offs.

Feature Backup and Restore Pilot Light Warm Standby Active-Active
Relative cost Lowest (storage costs only) Low to Medium (minimal compute + storage) Medium (reduced capacity always running) Highest (full capacity x regions)
Recovery speed Slowest Faster Fast Fastest
Data / environment readiness Backups only Core systems ready Scaled-down running stack Fully running in multiple locations
Best exam clue Non-critical workloads, development environments Need better recovery without full duplicate stack Need quick recovery but can keep smaller standby Mission-critical, zero-downtime requirements
Failover Complexity Restore from backup to new infrastructure Scale up pilot light resources Scale up standby or redirect traffic Route traffic away from failed region
Common trap Picking it for low RTO workloads Assuming it is instantly full-scale Forgetting ongoing standby cost Choosing it when business need does not justify complexity
RPO (Data Loss) Hours — data loss equals time since last backup; depends on backup frequency Minutes to hours Seconds to minutes Near zero — continuous synchronous replication; minimal to no data loss
RPO (Recovery Point Objective) Hours — determined by backup schedule (daily, hourly); longest RPO of all DR strategies 10s of minutes to hours Seconds to minutes Near zero / real-time — both sites process traffic simultaneously with synchronous data replication
Infrastructure Minimal — only backup storage (S3, AWS Backup) during normal operation; full infra provisioned during recovery Core infrastructure running Scaled-down production Full production — identical infrastructure running in both regions simultaneously; highest cost
Data Replication Periodic — scheduled backups to S3 or AWS Backup; snapshots of EBS, RDS at defined intervals Continuous replication (async/sync) Continuous replication (typically async) Continuous synchronous — DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database, S3 Cross-Region Replication
Database State Cold — database restored from snapshots during recovery; requires provisioning and restore time Core components running (database, critical services) Scaled-down but functional environment Hot — databases actively serving reads/writes in both regions simultaneously
Example AWS Services AWS Backup, S3 Cross-Region Replication, EBS Snapshots, RDS Snapshots, AMIs, CloudFormation for infra rebuild RDS Read Replicas, S3 CRR, minimal EC2/ECS Auto Scaling, ALB, RDS cross-region, Route 53 Route 53 (multi-region routing), DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database, S3 CRR, Global Accelerator
💡 Mnemonics

B&R = Backup & Restore (slowest), Pilot = Minimal core, Warm = Scaled-down standby, Active/Active = Full duplication

Low budget? Backup & Restore. Need DB ready? Pilot Light. Need faster recovery? Warm Standby. Need zero downtime? Multi-Site.

⚠️ Exam Tips

RPO = Recovery Point Objective (how much data you can lose)

RTO = Recovery Time Objective (how fast you need to recover)

Pilot Light keeps database replicated; other resources scaled down

Warm Standby runs a scaled-down version of production

Multi-Site is most expensive but provides instant failover

Aurora Global Database supports cross-region read replicas for DR

Backup and Restore
The simplest and most cost-effective DR strategy where data is regularly backed up and can be restored in a new environment when needed. Suitable for non-critical applications with tolerance for hours to days of downtime.
Pilot Light
A DR strategy where core infrastructure components (typically the database) are always running in the DR region with replicated data, while other resources are provisioned only during failover. Provides faster recovery than backup/restore with moderate cost.
Warm Standby
A DR approach where a scaled-down version of the full production environment runs continuously in the DR region. During failover, resources are scaled up to production capacity, enabling recovery in minutes rather than hours.
Active-Active
The most robust DR strategy where full production environments run in multiple regions simultaneously, with traffic distributed across all sites. Provides zero or near-zero RPO and RTO but at the highest cost.
Backup and Restore
Relative cost: Lowest (storage costs only) | Recovery speed: Slowest | Data / environment readiness: Backups only | Best exam clue: Non-critical workloads, development environments | Failover Complexity: Restore from backup to new infrastructure | Common trap: Picking it for low RTO workloads | RPO (Data Loss): Hours — data loss equals time since last backup; depends on backup frequency | RPO (Recovery Point Objective): Hours — determined by backup schedule (daily, hourly); longest RPO of all DR strategies | Infrastructure: Minimal — only backup storage (S3, AWS Backup) during normal operation; full infra provisioned during recovery | Data Replication: Periodic — scheduled backups to S3 or AWS Backup; snapshots of EBS, RDS at defined intervals | Database State: Cold — database restored from snapshots during recovery; requires provisioning and restore time | Example AWS Services: AWS Backup, S3 Cross-Region Replication, EBS Snapshots, RDS Snapshots, AMIs, CloudFormation for infra rebuild
Pilot Light
Relative cost: Low to Medium (minimal compute + storage) | Recovery speed: Faster | Data / environment readiness: Core systems ready | Best exam clue: Need better recovery without full duplicate stack | Failover Complexity: Scale up pilot light resources | Common trap: Assuming it is instantly full-scale | RPO (Data Loss): Minutes to hours | RPO (Recovery Point Objective): 10s of minutes to hours | Infrastructure: Core infrastructure running | Data Replication: Continuous replication (async/sync) | Database State: Core components running (database, critical services) | Example AWS Services: RDS Read Replicas, S3 CRR, minimal EC2/ECS
Warm Standby
Relative cost: Medium (reduced capacity always running) | Recovery speed: Fast | Data / environment readiness: Scaled-down running stack | Best exam clue: Need quick recovery but can keep smaller standby | Failover Complexity: Scale up standby or redirect traffic | Common trap: Forgetting ongoing standby cost | RPO (Data Loss): Seconds to minutes | RPO (Recovery Point Objective): Seconds to minutes | Infrastructure: Scaled-down production | Data Replication: Continuous replication (typically async) | Database State: Scaled-down but functional environment | Example AWS Services: Auto Scaling, ALB, RDS cross-region, Route 53
Active-Active
Relative cost: Highest (full capacity x regions) | Recovery speed: Fastest | Data / environment readiness: Fully running in multiple locations | Best exam clue: Mission-critical, zero-downtime requirements | Failover Complexity: Route traffic away from failed region | Common trap: Choosing it when business need does not justify complexity | RPO (Data Loss): Near zero — continuous synchronous replication; minimal to no data loss | RPO (Recovery Point Objective): Near zero / real-time — both sites process traffic simultaneously with synchronous data replication | Infrastructure: Full production — identical infrastructure running in both regions simultaneously; highest cost | Data Replication: Continuous synchronous — DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database, S3 Cross-Region Replication | Database State: Hot — databases actively serving reads/writes in both regions simultaneously | Example AWS Services: Route 53 (multi-region routing), DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database, S3 CRR, Global Accelerator
🎯 Why This Table Matters

SAA Domain 2 heavily tests tradeoffs between recovery speed, recovery point, and cost. This table is pure exam gold because DR strategy questions are often about balancing RTO/RPO against spend.

📎 Sources

aws_exam_comparison_tables[gpt5].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[glm5-agent].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v1].json, aws_exam_comparison_tables[kimicode-v2].json

Cloud Fundamentals 1 tables
#153 Cloud Service Models: IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS
Cloud Fundamentals cloud_service_models_comparison 3 cols 8 rows
▼

This table compares the three primary cloud service models. Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) provides fundamental computing resources, Platform as a Service (PaaS) offers managed development platforms, and Software as a Service (SaaS) delivers complete applications over the internet.

Feature IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) PaaS (Platform as a Service) SaaS (Software as a Service)
User Manages Applications, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS Applications, Data None (vendor manages everything)
Vendor Manages Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking Runtime, Middleware, OS, Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking Everything (Application, Data, Runtime, OS, Infrastructure)
AWS Examples EC2, VPC, EBS, S3 Elastic Beanstalk, Lambda, ECS, EKS Amazon Chime, Amazon WorkDocs, QuickSight
Control Level High (full infrastructure control) Medium (focus on application code) Low (use as-is)
Customization Maximum flexibility Limited to platform capabilities Minimal (configuration only)
Use Cases Lift-and-shift, custom infrastructure, maximum control Application development, microservices, rapid deployment Email, CRM, productivity tools, out-of-box solutions
Technical Expertise High (system administrators) Medium (developers) Low (end users)
Deployment Speed Slower (configure infrastructure first) Fast (deploy code directly) Immediate (sign up and use)
⚠️ Exam Tips

IaaS = Infrastructure as a Service (EC2, VPC) — you manage OS, middleware, and application

PaaS = Platform as a Service (Elastic Beanstalk, RDS) — you manage application and data only

SaaS = Software as a Service (Amazon Q, QuickSight) — you just use it, AWS manages everything

The more managed (SaaS > PaaS > IaaS), the less infrastructure you manage, but the less control you have

For the exam: understand the Shared Responsibility Model changes based on the service model

IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)
Provides virtualized computing resources including servers, storage, and networking over the internet, giving users full control over the infrastructure.
PaaS (Platform as a Service)
Offers a managed development and deployment environment with built-in tools, databases, and middleware, allowing developers to focus on code.
SaaS (Software as a Service)
Delivers complete software applications over the internet, fully managed by the vendor, requiring no installation or maintenance by users.
IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)
User Manages: Applications, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS | Vendor Manages: Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | AWS Examples: EC2, VPC, EBS, S3 | Control Level: High (full infrastructure control) | Customization: Maximum flexibility | Use Cases: Lift-and-shift, custom infrastructure, maximum control | Technical Expertise: High (system administrators) | Deployment Speed: Slower (configure infrastructure first)
PaaS (Platform as a Service)
User Manages: Applications, Data | Vendor Manages: Runtime, Middleware, OS, Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | AWS Examples: Elastic Beanstalk, Lambda, ECS, EKS | Control Level: Medium (focus on application code) | Customization: Limited to platform capabilities | Use Cases: Application development, microservices, rapid deployment | Technical Expertise: Medium (developers) | Deployment Speed: Fast (deploy code directly)
SaaS (Software as a Service)
User Manages: None (vendor manages everything) | Vendor Manages: Everything (Application, Data, Runtime, OS, Infrastructure) | AWS Examples: Amazon Chime, Amazon WorkDocs, QuickSight | Control Level: Low (use as-is) | Customization: Minimal (configuration only) | Use Cases: Email, CRM, productivity tools, out-of-box solutions | Technical Expertise: Low (end users) | Deployment Speed: Immediate (sign up and use)
